    • Affine Processes and Optimal Stopping

    Advanced Topics: Affine Processes & Optimal Stopping (Festschrift)¶

    Source

    From Stochastic Calculus to Mathematical Finance: The Shiryaev Festschrift edited by Yu. Kabanov, R. Liptser, and J. Stoyanov, Springer, 2006. These notes are used for educational purposes.

    Affine Processes and Optimal Stopping¶

    COGARCH versus OrnsteinвАУUhlenbeck models 415 / ѕЙ : 0<sвЙ§t0 |вИЖLs(ѕЙ)| < ќµ 0 . Then P(Dќµ,k) > 0, and with c1 and c2 as before it is the case that, on Dќµ,k, t0 0 eвИТXsвИТdLs =

    0 0

    0 when ќµ < ќ≥0c1t0/c2. The following theorem now gives the Pareto type tail behaviour of Gt. We need slightly more stringent moment conditions than in Theorem 5.2, and assume that the driving L¬іevy process is of finite variation. Theorem 5.3. [Tail behaviour of G] Suppose there is ќЇ > 0 and d > 4ќЇ such that E|L1|d < вИЮ and ќ®(ќЇ) = 0. (5.6) Suppose further that (Lt)tвЙ•0 is of finite variation. Let (ѕГ2 t )tвЙ•0 be the stationary version of the volatility process, and Gt = t 0 ѕГs dLs the corresponding CO- GARCH process. Then if (вИТLt)tвЙ•0 is not a subordinator, for every t > 0 there exists a positive constant C1,t such that lim xвЖТвИЮx2ќЇP(Gt > x) = C1,t, and if (вИТLt)tвЙ•0 is a subordinator, then Gt вЙ§0 a.s. Similarly, if (Lt)tвЙ•0 is not a subordinator, then there exists C2,t > 0 such that lim xвЖТвИЮx2ќЇP(Gt вЙ§вИТx) = C2,t, and if (Lt)tвЙ•0 is a subordinator, then Gt вЙ•0 a.s. Proof. For s вЙ§t, define As := eвИТXsвИТ, Bs := ќ≤ s 0 eXuвИТXsвИТdu. Then from (2.7) ѕГs = H AsѕГ2 0 + Bs =

    AsѕГ0 + Bs

    AsѕГ2 0 + Bs +

    AsѕГ2 0 . Defining Yt := ѕГ0 t 0

    As dLs, ќґs := Bs

    AsѕГ2 0 + Bs +

    AsѕГ2 0 , and Zt := t 0 ќґs dLs,

    416 C. Kl¬®uppelberg et al. we obtain Gt = t 0 ѕГsdLs = Yt + Zt, t > 0. From Theorem 5.2 we know that limxвЖТвИЮx2ќЇP(ѕГ0 > x) = C for some positive constant C. Suppose we show that there is an dвА≤ > 2ќЇ such that E

    t 0 вИЪAs dLs

    dвА≤ < вИЮ. Then a classical result of Breiman [9], using the in- dependence of ѕГ0 and t 0 вИЪAs dLs, yields the existence of strictly positive constants C1,t, C2,t such that lim xвЖТвИЮx2ќЇP(Yt > x) = C1,t, lim xвЖТвИЮx2ќЇP(Yt вЙ§вИТx) = C2,t, (5.7) provided P t 0 вИЪAs dLs > 0

    0 and P t 0 вИЪAs dLs < 0

    0, respectively. We shall verify the required moment condition with dвА≤ := d/2. Note that

    t 0

    As dLs вЙ§sup 0вЙ§sвЙ§t eвИТXs/2 вИ•LtвИ•TV, where вИ•LtвИ•TV denotes the total variation of (Ls)0вЙ§sвЙ§t on [0, t], and we also have that E sup0вЙ§sвЙ§t eвИТdвА≤Xs < вИЮsince EeвИТdвА≤X1 < вИЮ(as in the proof of Theorem 5.2), and that EвИ•LtвИ•2dвА≤ TV is finite since E|L1|2dвА≤ is finite by assumption (see Sato [24], Theorem 21.9); also, it follows from H¬®olderвАЩs inequality that E

    sup 0вЙ§sвЙ§t eвИТXs/2вИ•LtвИ•TV dвА≤ вЙ§

    E sup 0вЙ§sвЙ§t eвИТdвА≤Xs 1/2 EвИ•LtвИ•2dвА≤ TV 1/2 < вИЮ. So the moment condition is established, with dвА≤ = d/2 > 2ќЇ. To get an estimate for Zt, note that Xu вЙ§вИТu log ќі by (2.6), so that for 0 вЙ§s вЙ§t, ќґs вЙ§

    Bs =

    ќ≤

    As

    s 0 eXu du вЙ§

    ќ≤ вИЪ tќівИТt As. This implies, with dвА≤ as above, E|Zt|dвА≤ вЙ§ќ≤dвА≤/2tdвА≤/2ќівИТdвА≤t/2E sup 0вЙ§sвЙ§t eвИТXs/2вИ•LtвИ•TV

    dвА≤ < вИЮ, as already shown. Now if P t 0 вИЪAs dLs > 0

    0, i.e. (вИТLt)tвЙ•0 is not a subordinator by Lemma 5.2, an application of Lemma 5.1 to (5.7) gives the result. On the other hand, if P t 0 вИЪAs dLs > 0

    = 0, i.e. if (вИТLt)tвЙ•0 is a subordinator, then also Gt = t 0 ѕГs dLs вЙ§0 a.s. The assertion for the left tail behaviour of Gt follows similarly.

    COGARCH versus OrnsteinвАУUhlenbeck models 417 Examples for the application of Theorem 5.3, similar to Example 1(a) in the case when all moments of L1 exist, or Example 1(b) can be easily stated. We conclude this section with the observation that with the same methods of proof the tail behaviour of the integrated squared volatility can be determined. Here, a weaker moment condition is sufficient: Proposition 5.2. [Tail behaviour of the integrated squared volatility] Let the conditions of Theorem 5.2 be satisfied. In addition assume that there is d > 2ќЇ such that E|L1|d < вИЮ. Let (ѕГ2 t )tвЙ•0 be the stationary version. Then, for any t > 0 there is a constant Ct > 0 such that lim xвЖТвИЮxќЇP t 0 ѕГ2 sds > x

    = Ct. 6 Conclusion We have compared the probabilistic properties of both the stochastic volatil- ity model of Barndorff-Nielsen and Shephard and the COGARCH process. Both volatility models are positive Markov processes, which exhibit jumps and decrease exponentially between jumps. Although the log price process is defined in terms of an independent Brownian motion for the OU model and in terms of the same driving L¬іevy process for the COGARCH process, the autocorrelation structure of the returns is similar for both processes. Further- more, we have seen that the tail behaviour in the OU model depends heavily on the driving L¬іevy process, while for the COGARCH model Pareto like tails occur in most cases under weak regularity conditions. Acknowledgements We thank Marc Yor and Victor Rivero for interesting discussions and their considerable efforts concerning the tail behaviour of the COGARCH model. Further, we thank Gennady Samorodnitsky for answering the question if the stationary distribution of the COGARCH process is self-decomposable, and for his generosity in allowing us to include this result in Theorem 5.1. Thanks also to Ole Barndorff-Nielsen for helpful comments on the paper, and in particular for drawing our attention to the quadratic variation of the CO- GARCH process, which led to Proposition 3.3. Parts of this research were carried out while A. Lindner was visiting the Cen- tre for Mathematical Analysis and the School of Finance & Applied Statistics at ANU in Canberra. He takes pleasure in thanking both for their hospitality. This research was partially supported by ARC grant DP0210572. A. Lindner was supported by the German Science Foundation (Deutsche Forschungsge- meinschaft).

Oxford: Oxford Univer- sity Press 2004 26. Rivero, V.: Recurrent extensions of self-similar Markov processes and Cram¬іerвАЩs condition. Bernoulli (to appear) (2005). 27.     Tail Distributions of Supremum and Quadratic Variation of Local Martingales Robert LIPTSER1 and Alexander NOVIKOV2 1 Electrical Engineering-Systems, Tel Aviv University, 69978 Tel Aviv Israel, Institute of Information Transmission, Moscow, Russia. liptser@eng.tau.ac.il 2 School of Mathematical Sciences, UTS, NSW 2007, Australia. prob@maths.uts.edu.au Summary. We extend some known results concerning the tail distribution of supre- mum and quadratic variation of a continuous local martingale to the case of locally square integrable martingales with bounded jumps. The predictable and optional quadratic variations are involved in the main result. Key words: tail distribution, martingale supremum, quadratic variation Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 60G44, 60HXX, 40E05 1 Introduction and main result Let M = (Mt)tвЙ•0 be a local martingale starting from zero and with paths in the Skorohod space D[0,вИЮ). We assume that it is defined on a stochastic basis (вД¶, F, (Ft)tвЙ•0, P) with usual conditions. We shall use the standard notation Mloc for the class of local martingales and M2 loc Mc, M, M2 for its subclasses. Recall that a adapted process X with paths in D[0,вИЮ) defined on this stochastic basis belongs to the class D if the family (XѕД, ѕД вИИT ), where T is the set of stopping times ѕД, is uniformly integrable. Henceforth вЦ≥Mt := Mt вИТMtвИТ, вЯ®MвЯ©t and [M, M]t denote the jumps, predictable quadratic variation and optional quadratic variation of M. It is well-known (see, e.g., [9], [7] and references therein) that for any M вИИM2 loc: вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮa.s. вЗТ

    [M, M]вИЮ< вИЮa.s. lim tвЖТвИЮMt = MвИЮвИИR a.s. (1.1)

    422 R. Liptser and A. Novikov There are many other remarkable relations between MвИЮand вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ(e.g., BurkholderвАУGundyвАУDavisвАЩs inequalities, law of large numbers for martingales, etc.). For M вИИM вИ©D we have the Wald equality EMвИЮ= 0, which plays a fundamental role in many applications of the stochastic calculus. Recall that the condition EвЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮimplies that M вИИM2 and notice that вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮћЄвЗТM вИИM. However, the condition вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮ, implying the existence of the limit value MвИЮ(see, (1.1)), jointly with EMвИЮ= 0 ensures M вИИM. One may ask which condition on вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮcan provide the equality EMвИЮ= 0? A positive answer for M вИИMc loc with вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮis known from Novikov, [10], and Elworthy, Li and Yor, [2], under the additional assumption: EeќµM+ вИЮ< вИЮfor sufficiently small ќµ > 0, lim ќївЖТвИЮќїP

    вЯ®MвЯ©1/2 вИЮ> ќї

    = 0. More precisely, the following statement is valid. Theorem. ([10]) Let M вИИMc loc and вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮ. Assume supt>0 EeќµMt < вИЮ for some sufficiently small ќµ > 0. Then: 0 вЙ§EMвИЮвЙ§EM + вИЮ< вИЮ, lim ќївЖТвИЮќїP

    вЯ®MвЯ©1/2 вИЮ> ќї

    =

    2 ѕА EMвИЮ. For related topics see Az¬іema, Gundy and Yor [1], Gundy [5], Galtchouk and Novikov [6], Takaoka, [14], Peskir and Shiryaev [13], and VondraЋШcek [15]). The aim of this paper is to extend the statement of this Theorem for local martingales with bounded jumps. Theorem 1.1. Let M вИИM2 loc, вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮand M + вИИD. Then (i) MвИЮ= limtвЖТвИЮMt possesses the following properties: 0 вЙ§EMвИЮвЙ§EM + вИЮ< вИЮ; (ii) the uniform integrability of (|вЦ≥Mt|)t>0 and (i) imply lim ќївЖТвИЮќїP

    sup tвЙ•0 M вИТ t > ќї

    = EMвИЮ; (iii) |вЦ≥M| вЙ§K and EeќµMвИЮ< вИЮfor some K > 0 and sufficiently small ќµ > 0 imply lim ќївЖТвИЮќїP

    вЯ®MвЯ©1/2 вИЮ> ќї

    = lim ќївЖТвИЮќїP

    [M, M]1/2 вИЮ> ќї

    =

    2 ѕА EMвИЮ.

    Tail Distributions of Supremum and Quadratic Variation 423 For M + вИИD, Theorem 1.1 gives necessary and sufficient conditions for M вИИM expressed in terms of suptвЙ•0 M вИТ t , вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ, and [M, M]вИЮ. Concerning an effectiveness of these conditions see Jacod and Shiryaev [8]. Corollary 1.1. Under the assumptions of Theorem 1.1, the process M вИИM iffany of the following conditions hold: lim ќївЖТвИЮќїP

    sup tвЙ•0 M вИТ t > ќї

    = 0, lim ќївЖТвИЮќїP

    вЯ®MвЯ©1/2 вИЮ> ќї

    = 0, lim ќївЖТвИЮќїP

    [M, M]1/2 вИЮ> ќї

    = 0. The proofs of statements (i) and (ii) of Theorem 1.1 are obvious and might even be known. The proof of (iii) exploits a combination of techniques: вАЬStochastic exponential + Tauberian theoremвАЭ used by Novikov in [11] and [12]. The necessary information on the stochastic exponential is gathered in Section 2. The proof of Theorem 1.1 is given in Section 3. We mention also a result, formulating in Theorem 3.1 (Section 3), presenting conditions alterna- tive to |вЦ≥M| вЙ§K. 2 Stochastic exponential We start with recalling necessary notions and objects (for details see, e.g., [9] or [7]). For any M вИИM2 loc we have the decomposition M = M c + M d where M c, M d вИИM2 loc are continuous and purely discontinuous martingales, re- spectively. Since вЯ®MвЯ©= вЯ®M cвЯ©+ вЯ®M dвЯ©, the assumption вЯ®MвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮimplies вЯ®M cвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮ, вЯ®M dвЯ©вИЮ< вИЮ. The jump process вЦ≥M вЙ°вЦ≥M d generates the integer-valued measure ¬µ = ¬µ(dt, dz) with ¬µ((0, t] √Ч A) = sвЙ§t I(вЦ≥Ms вИИA). We denote by ќљ = ќљ(dt, dz) the compensator of ¬µ. The condition |вЦ≥M| вЙ§K guarantees the existence of a version ќљ such that ќљ(R+ √Ч{|z| > K}) = 0. This version of ќљ is used in the sequel. The purely discontinuous martingale M d can be represented as the ItЋЖo integral with respect to ¬µ вИТќљ: M d t = t 0

    |z|вЙ§K zќљ({t}, dz) = 0 and, so that,

    ќљ({t}, dz). We emphasize that Gt(ќї) increases in t вЖСto GвИЮ(ќї) := limtвЖТвИЮGt(ќї) < вИЮ and вЦ≥Gt(ќї) вЙ•0. The process Et(ќї) = exp ќї2 2 вЯ®M cвЯ©t + Gt(ќї) ( 0<sвЙ§t

    1 + вЦ≥Gs(ќї)

    eвИТвЦ≥Gs(ќї) is called вАЬstochastic exponentialвАЭ for the martingale M. Since вЦ≥G(ќї) вЙ•0, the stochastic exponential is nonnegative. A remarkable property of Et(ќї) is that the process zt(ќї) = eќїMtвИТlog Et(ќї) (2.1) is a positive local martingale with respect to the filtration (Ft)tвЙ•0. This prop- erty is readily verified with the help of ItЋЖoвАЩs formula applied to (2.1): dzt(ќї) = ќїzt(ќї)dM c t +

    |z|вЙ§K ztвИТ(ќї)

    eќїz вИТ1

    1 + вЦ≥Gt(ќї)(¬µ вИТќљ)(dt, dz). As any nonnegative local martingale, zt(ќї) is also a supermartingale (see, e.g., Problem 1.4.4 in Liptser and Shiryaev [9]) and, therefore, has a finite limit at infinity zвИЮ(ќї) := lim tвЖТвИЮzt(ќї) вИИR+ and EzѕД(ќї) вЙ§1 for any stopping time ѕД. In particular, EzвИЮвЙ§1. Proposition 2.1. Under the conditions from statement (iii) of Theorem 1.1 we have: 1) EzвИЮ(ќї) = 1. 2) EвИЮ(ќї) = lim tвЖТвИЮEt(ќї) вИИ(0, вИЮ). Proof. 1) Let (ѕДn) be a sequence of stopping times increasing to infinity and such that (MtвИІѕДn)tвЙ•0 and (ztвИІѕДn(ќї))tвЙ•0 are uniformly integrable martingales for any n. Then EzѕДn(ќї) вЙ°1. By JensenвАЩs inequality, E

    eќїM+ вИЮ|FѕДn

    вЙ•eќїE(M + вИЮ|FѕДn) вЙ•eќїM+ ѕДn вЙ•zѕДn(ќї).

    Tail Distributions of Supremum and Quadratic Variation 425 In other words, the martingale

    zѕДn(ќї), FѕДn

    nвЙ•1 is majorized by the uniformly integrable martingale

    , FѕДn

    nвЙ•1, that is,

    zѕДn(ќї), FѕДn

    nвЙ•1 is the uniformly martingale itself. Consequently, 1 = limnвЖТвИЮEzѕДn(ќї) = EzвИЮ(ќї). 2) Notice that |MвИЮ| < вИЮ, EвИЮ(ќї) < вИЮand zвИЮ(ќї) = eќїMвИЮвИТlog EвИЮ(ќї) imply that 1 вЙ•EI(EвИЮ(ќї) = 0)zвИЮ(ќї) вЙ•NP(EвИЮ(ќї) = 0) for any N > 0. Hence, P(EвИЮ(ќї) = 0) = 0. 3 The proof of Theorem 1.1 3.1 The proof of (i) and (ii) (i) Let (ѕДn)nвЙ•1 be an increasing sequence of stopping times with tending to infinity and such that (MѕДn)nвЙ•1 вИИM. Therefore, EM вИТ ѕДn вИТEM + ѕДn = 0, n вЙ•1. By M + вИИD, we have lim nвЖТвИЮEM + ѕДn = EM + вИЮ< вИЮ. Further, by the Fatou lemma limnвЖТвИЮEM вИТ ѕДn вЙ•EM вИТ вИЮ, so that EM + вИЮвИТEM вИТ вИЮвЙ•0. Hence, EMвИЮ= (EM + вИЮвИТEM вИТ вИЮ) вЙ•0. (ii) Notice that {suptвЙ•0 M вИТ t > ќї} = {Sќї < вИЮ}, where Sќї = inf{t : M вИТ t вЙ•ќї}, inf{вИЕ} = вИЮ. Since (|вЦ≥Mt|)t>0 is uniformly integrable process and M + вИИD, we have (MtвИІSќї)tвЙ•0 вИИM, that is, 0 = EMSќї = EMвИЮI{Sќї=вИЮ} + EMSќїI{Sќї<вИЮ}. We derive the desired statement from the relations lim ќївЖТвИЮEMвИЮI{Sќї=вИЮ} = EMвИЮ, lim ќївЖТвИЮEMSќїI{Sќї<вИЮ} = вИТќїP

    sup tвЙ•0 M вИТ t > ќї

    . (3.1) By (i), EM вИТ вИЮвЙ§EM + вИЮ< вИЮ. Consequently, M вИТ вИЮ< вИЮand, therefore, we have limќївЖТвИЮSќї = вИЮ. The first part of (3.1) is implied by the inequality EMвИЮI{Sќї=вИЮ} вИТEMвИЮ вЙ§E|MвИЮ|I{Sќї<вИЮ} and the Lebesgue dominated theorem. The second part in (3.1) follows from MSќїI{Sќї<вИЮ} = вИТќїI{Sќї<вИЮ} + (MSќї + ќї)I{Sќї<вИЮ} since E|MSќї + ќї|I{Sќї<вИЮ} вЙ§E|вЦ≥MSќї|I{Sќї<вИЮ} вЙ§KP(Sќї < вИЮ) вИТвИТвИТвИТвЖТ ќївЖТвИЮ0.

    . A helpful tool here is the inequality: for some C > 0, any stopping time ѕД and K being a bound for |вЦ≥M|, E sup tвЙ§ѕД |Lt|2 вЙ§CK2EвЯ®MвЯ©ѕД. (3.7) In order to establish (3.7), we use the following facts: - L is the purely discontinuous local martingale with [L, L]t =

    |z|вЙ§K z2ќљ({ds, dz) вЙ§K2вЯ®MвЯ©t, - K2вЯ®MвЯ©вИТвЯ®LвЯ©is the increasing process. Now, we refer to the BurkholderвАУGundy inequality (see, e.g., Theorem 1.9.7 in [9]): for any stopping time ѕД, E sup tвЙ§ѕД |Lt|2 вЙ§CE[L, L]ѕД. Due to the relations E[L, L]ѕД = EвЯ®LвЯ©ѕД and K2вЯ®MвЯ©ѕД вЙ•вЯ®LвЯ©ѕД (recall that K2вЯ®MвЯ©вЙ•вЯ®LвЯ©), we have EвЯ®LвЯ©ѕД вЙ§K2EвЯ®MвЯ©ѕД, that is, (3.7) is valid. Following [10], we show that this condition can be replaced by one seems to be more suitable for applications. Theorem 3.1.
