    Stochastic Differential Equations: A Wiener Chaos Approach Sergey LOTOTSKY1 вИЧand Boris ROZOVSKII2вА† 1 Department of Mathematics, USC Los Angeles, CA 90089 USA. lototsky@math.usc.edu 2 Department of Mathematics, USC Los Angeles, CA 90089 USA. rozovski@math.usc.edu Summary. A new method is described for constructing a generalized solution for stochastic differential equations. The method is based on the CameronвАУMartin ver- sion of the Wiener Chaos expansion and provides a unified framework for the study of ordinary and partial differential equations driven by finite- or infinite-dimensional noise with either adapted or anticipating input. Existence, uniqueness, regularity, and probabilistic representation of this Wiener Chaos solution is established for a large class of equations. A number of examples are presented to illustrate the general constructions. A detailed analysis is presented for the various forms of the passive scalar equation and for the first-order ItЋЖo stochastic partial differential equa- tion. Applications to nonlinear filtering of diffusion processes and to the stochastic NavierвАУStokes equation are also discussed. Key words: anticipating equations, generalized random elements, degenerate par- abolic equations, Malliavin calculus, passive scalar equation, Skorohod integral, S- transform, weighted spaces Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 60H15, 35R60, 60H40 Contents 1. Introduction . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 434 2. Traditional Solutions of Linear Parabolic Equations . . . . . . 437 3. White Noise Solutions of Stochastic Parabolic Equations . 441 4. Generalized Functions on the Wiener Chaos Space . . . . . . . 446 5. The Malliavin Derivative and its Adjoint . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 449 6. The Wiener Chaos Solution and the Propagator. . . . . . . . . .452 вИЧThe work of S. Lototsky was partially supported by the Sloan Research Fellow- ship, by the NSF CAREER award DMS-0237724, and by the ARO Grant DAAD19- 02-1-0374 вА†The work of B. Rozovskii was partially supported by the ARO Grant DAAD19- 02-1-0374 and ONR Grant N0014-03-1-0027.

    434 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii 7. Weighted Wiener Chaos Spaces and S-Transform . . . . . . . . 460 8. General Properties of the Wiener Chaos Solutions . . . . . . .466 9. Regularity of the Wiener Chaos Solution . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 469 10. Probabilistic Representation of Wiener Chaos Solutions . 480 11. Wiener Chaos and Nonlinear Filtering . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 486 12. Passive Scalar in a Gaussian Field . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 491 13. Stochastic Navier-Stokes Equation . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 497 14. First-Order ItЋЖo Equations . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . 502 1 Introduction Consider a stochastic evolution equation du(t) = (Au(t) + f(t))dt + (Mu(t) + g(t))dW(t), (1.1) where A and M are differential operators, and W is a noise process on a stochastic basis F = (вД¶, F, {Ft}tвЙ•0, P). Traditionally, this equation is studied under the following assumptions: (i) The operator A is elliptic, the order of the operator M is at most half the order of A, and a special parabolicity condition holds. (ii) The functions f and g are predictable with respect to the filtration {Ft}tвЙ•0, and the initial condition is F0-measurable. (iii) The noise process W is sufficiently regular. Under these assumptions, there exists a unique predictable solution u of (1.1) such that u belongs to L2(вД¶√Ч (0, T); H) for T > 0 and a suitable function space H (see, for example, Chapter 3 of [42]). Moreover, there are examples showing that the parabolicity condition and the regularity of noise are necessary to have a square integrable solution of (1.1). The objective of the current paper is to study stochastic differential equa- tions of the type (1.1) without making the above assumptions (i)вАУ(iii). We show that, with a suitable definition of the solution, solvability of the stochas- tic equation is essentially equivalent to solvability of a deterministic evolution equation dv = (Av + ѕХ)dt for certain functions ѕХ; the operator A does not even have to be elliptic. Generalized solutions have been introduced and studied for stochastic dif- ferential equations, both ordinary and with partial derivatives, and definitions of such solutions relied on various forms of the Wiener Chaos decomposition. For stochastic ordinary differential equations, Krylov and Veretennikov [20] used multiple Wiener integral expansion to study ItЋЖo diffusions with non- smooth coefficients, and more recently, LeJan and Raimond [22] used a sim- ilar approach in the construction of stochastic flows. Various versions of the Wiener Chaos appear in a number of papers on nonlinear filtering and related

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 435 topics [2, 25, 33, 39, 46, etc.] The book by Holden et al. [12] presents a sys- tematic approach to the stochastic differential equations based on the white noise theory. See also [10], [40] and the references therein. For stochastic partial differential equations, most existing constructions of the generalized solution rely on various modifications of the Fourier trans- form in the infinite-dimensional Wiener Chaos space L2(W) = L2(вД¶, FW T , P). The two main modifications are known as the S-transform [10] and the Her- mite transform [12]. The key elements in the development of the theory are the spaces of the test functions and the corresponding distributions. Several constructions of these spaces were suggested by Hida [10], Kondratiev [17], and Nualart and Rozovskii [38]. Both S- and Hermite transforms establish a bijection between the space of generalized random elements and a suitable space of analytic functions. Using the S-transform, Mikulevicius and Rozovskii [33] studied stochastic parabolic equations with non-smooth coefficients, while Nualart and Rozovskii [38] and Potthoffet al. [40] constructed generalized so- lutions for the equations driven by space-time white noise in more than one spatial dimension. Many other types of equations have been studied, and the book [12] provides a good overview of literature on the corresponding results. In this paper, generalized solutions of (1.1) are defined in the spaces that are even larger than of Hida or Kondratiev distributions. The Wiener Chaos space is a separable Hilbert space with a CameronвАУMartin basis [3]. The elements of the space with a finite Fourier series expansion provide the nat- ural collection of test functions D(L2(W)), an analog of the space D(Rd) of smooth compactly supported functions on Rd. The corresponding space of distributions DвА≤(L2(W)) is the collection of generalized random elements rep- resented by formal Fourier series. A generalized solution u = u(t, x) of (1.1) is constructed as an element of DвА≤(L2(W)) such that the generalized Fourier coefficients satisfy a system of deterministic evolution equations, known as the propagator. If the equation is linear the propagator is a lower-triangular system. We call this solution a Wiener Chaos solution. The propagator was first introduced by Mikulevicius and Rozovskii in [32], and further studied in [25], as a numerical tool for solving the nonlinear filtering problem. The propagator can also be derived for certain nonlinear equations; in particular, it was used in [31, 34, 35] to study the stochastic NavierвАУStokes equation. The propagator approach to defining the solution of (1.1) has two advan- tages over the S-transform approach. First, the resulting construction is more general: there are equations for which the Wiener Chaos solution is not in the domain of the S-transform. Indeed, it is shown in Section 14 that, for certain initial conditions, equation du = uxdWt has a Wiener Chaos solution for which the S-transform is not defined. On the other hand, by Theorem 8.1 below, if the generalized solution of (1.1) can be defined using the S-transform, then this solution is also a Wiener Chaos solution. Second, there is no problem of inversion: the propagator provides a direct approach to studying the prop-

    436 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii erties of Wiener Chaos solution and computing both the sample trajectories and statistical moments. Let us emphasize also the following important features of the Wiener Chaos approach: вАҐ The Wiener Chaos solution is a strong solution in the probabilistic sense, that is, it is uniquely determined by the coefficients, free terms, initial condition, and the Wiener process. вАҐ The solution exists under minimal regularity conditions on the coefficients in the stochastic part of the equation and no special measurability restric- tion on the input. вАҐ The Wiener Chaos solution often serves as a convenient first step in the in- vestigation of the traditional solutions or solutions in weighted stochastic Sobolev spaces that are much smaller then the spaces of Hida or Kon- dratiev distributions. To better understand the connection between the Wiener Chaos solution and other notions of the solution, recall that, traditionally, by a solution of a stochastic equation we understand a random process or field satisfying the equation for almost all elementary outcomes. This solution can be either strong or weak in the probabilistic sense. Probabilistically strong solution is constructed on a prescribed probability space with a specific noise process. Existence of strong solutions requires cer- tain regularity of the coefficients and the noise in the equation. The tools for constructing strong solutions often come from the theory of the corresponding deterministic equations. Probabilistically weak solution includes not only the solution process but also the stochastic basis and the noise process. This freedom to choose the probability space and the noise process makes the conditions for existence of weak solutions less restrictive than the similar conditions for strong solutions. Weak solutions can be obtained either by considering the corresponding mar- tingale problem or by constructing a suitable Hunt process using the theory of the Dirichlet forms. There exist equations that have neither weak nor strong solutions in the traditional sense. An example is the bi-linear stochastic heat equation driven by a multiplicative space-time white noise in two or more spatial dimensions: the irregular nature of the noise prevents the existence of a random field that would satisfy the equation for individual elementary outcomes. For such equa- tions, the solution must be defined as a generalized random element satisfying the equation after the randomness has been averaged out. White noise theory provides one approach for constructing these gener- alized solutions. The approach is similar to the Fourier integral method for deterministic equations. The white noise solution is constructed on a special white noise probability space by inverting an integral transform; the special structure of the probability space is essential to carry out the inversion. We

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 437 can therefore say that the white noise solution extends the notion of the prob- abilistically weak solution. Still, this extension is not a true generalization: when the equation satisfies the necessary regularity conditions, the connec- tion between the white noise and the traditional weak solution is often not clear. The Wiener chaos approach provides the means for constructing a gener- alized solution on a prescribed probability space. The Wiener Chaos solution is a formal Fourier series in the corresponding CameronвАУMartin basis. The coefficients in the series are uniquely determined by the equation via the propagator system. This representation provides a convenient way for com- puting numerically the solution and its statistical moments. As a result, the Wiener Chaos solution extends the notion of the probabilistically strong so- lution. Unlike the white noise approach, this is a bona fide extension: when the equation satisfies the necessary regularity conditions, the Wiener Chaos solution coincides with the traditional strong solution. After a general discussion of the Wiener Chaos space in Sections 4 and 5, the Wiener Chaos solution for equation (1.1) and the main properties of the solution are studied in Section 6. Several examples illustrate how the Wiener Chaos solution provides a uniform treatment of various types of equations: traditional parabolic, non-parabolic, and anticipating. In particular, for equa- tions with non-predictable input, the Wiener Chaos solution corresponds to the Skorohod integral interpretation of the equation. The initial solution space DвА≤(W) is too large to provide much of interesting information about the solu- tion. Accordingly, Section 7 discusses various weighted Wiener Chaos spaces. These weighted spaces provide the necessary connection between the Wiener Chaos, white noise, and traditional solutions. This connection is studied in Section 8. In Section 9, the Wiener Chaos solution is constructed for degener- ate linear parabolic equations and new regularity results are obtained for the solution. Probabilistic representation of the Wiener Chaos solution is studied in Section 10, where a FeynmannвАУKac type formula is derived. Sections 11 вАУ 14 discuss the applications of the general results to particular equations: the Zakai filtering equation, the stochastic transport equation, the stochastic NavierвАУStokes equation, and a first-order ItЋЖo SPDE. The following notation will be in force throughout the paper: вИЖis the Laplace operator, Di = вИВ/вИВxi, i = 1, . . . , d, and summation over the repeated indices is assumed. The space of continuous functions is denoted by C, and Hќ≥ 2 , ќ≥ вИИR, is the Sobolev space

    f :

    R | ЋЖf(y)|2(1 + |y|2)ќ≥dy < вИЮ

    , where ЋЖf is the Fourier transform of f. 2 Traditional Solutions of Linear Parabolic Equations Below is a summary of the Hilbert space theory of linear stochastic parabolic equations. The details can be found in the books [41] and [42]; see also [19].

    438 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii For a Hilbert space X, (¬Ј, ¬Ј)X and вИ•¬Ј вИ•X denote the inner product and the norm in X. Definition 2.1 The triple (V, H, V вА≤) of Hilbert spaces is called normal if and only if 1. V ÷ТвЖТH ÷ТвЖТV вА≤ and both embeddings V ÷ТвЖТH and H ÷ТвЖТV вА≤ are dense and continuous; 2. The space V вА≤ is the dual of V relative to the inner product in H; 3. There exists a constant C > 0 such that |(h, v)H| вЙ§CвИ•vвИ•V вИ•hвИ•V вА≤ for all v вИИV and h вИИH. E.g., the Sobolev spaces (HвДУ+ќ≥ 2 (Rd), HвДУ 2(Rd), HвДУвИТќ≥ 2 (Rd)), ќ≥ > 0, вДУвИИR, form a normal triple. Denote by вЯ®vвА≤, vвЯ©, vвА≤ вИИV вА≤, v вИИV , the duality between V and V вА≤ relative to the inner product in H. The properties of the normal triple imply that |вЯ®vвА≤, vвЯ©| вЙ§CвИ•vвИ•V вИ•vвА≤вИ•V вА≤, and, if vвА≤ вИИH and v вИИV , then вЯ®vвА≤, vвЯ©= (vвА≤, v)H; Let F = (вД¶, F, {Ft}tвЙ•0, P) be a stochastic basis with the usual assump- tions. In particular, the ѕГ-algebras F and F0 are P-complete, and the filtration {Ft}tвЙ•0 is right-continuous; for details, see [23, Definition I.1.1]. We assume that F is rich enough to carry a collection wk = wk(t), k вЙ•1, t вЙ•0, of independent standard Wiener processes. Given a normal triple (V, H, V вА≤) and a family of linear bounded operators A(t) : V вЖТV вА≤, Mk(t) : V вЖТH, t вИИ[0, T], consider the following equation: u(t) = u0 + t 0 (Au(s) + f(s))ds + t 0 (Mku(s) + gk(s))dwk(s), t вИИ[0, T], (2.1) where T < вИЮis fixed and non-random and the summation convention is in force. Assume that, for all v вИИV ,

    kвЙ•1 вИ•Mk(t)vвИ•2 H < вИЮ, t вИИ[0, T]. (2.2) The input data u0, f, and gk are chosen so that E п£Ђ п£≠вИ•u0вИ•2 H + T 0 вИ•f(t)вИ•2 V вА≤dt +

    kвЙ•1 T 0 вИ•gk(t)вИ•2 Hdt п£ґ п£Є< вИЮ, (2.3) u0 is F0-measurable, and the processes f, gk are Ft-adapted, that is, f(t) and each gk(t) are Ft-measurable for each t вЙ•0. Definition 2.2 An Ft-adapted process u вИИL2(F; L2((0, T); V )) is called a traditional, or square-integrable, solution of equation (2.1) if, for every v вИИV , there exists a measurable subset вД¶вА≤ of вД¶with P(вД¶вА≤) = 1, such that the equality

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 439 (u(t), v)H = (u0, v)H+ t 0 вЯ®Au(s)+f(s), vвЯ©ds+

    kвЙ•1 (Mku(s)+gk(s), v)Hdwk(s) (2.4) holds on вД¶вА≤ for all t вИИ[0, T]. Existence and uniqueness of the traditional solution for (2.1) can be es- tablished when the equation is parabolic. Definition 2.3 Equation (2.1) is called strongly parabolic if there exists a positive number ќµ and a real number C0 such that, for all v вИИV and t вИИ[0, T], 2вЯ®A(t)v, vвЯ©+

    kвЙ•1 вИ•M(t)kvвИ•2 H + ќµвИ•vвИ•2 V вЙ§C0вИ•vвИ•2 H. (2.5) Equation (2.1) is called weakly parabolic (or degenerate parabolic) if con- dition (2.5) holds with ќµ = 0. Theorem 2.1. If (2.3) and (2.5) hold, then there exists a unique traditional solution of (2.1). The solution process u is an element of the space L2(F; L2((0, T); V )) ? L2(F; C((0, T), H)) and satisfies E

    sup 0<t<T вИ•u(t)вИ•2 H + T 0 вИ•u(t)вИ•2 V dt

    вЙ§C(C0, ќі, T)E п£Ђ п£≠вИ•u0вИ•2 H + T 0 вИ•f(t)вИ•2 V вА≤dt +

    kвЙ•1 T 0 вИ•gk(t)вИ•2 Hdt п£ґ п£Є. (2.6) Proof. This follows, for example, from Theorem 3.1.4 in [42]. A somewhat different solvability result holds for weakly parabolic equa- tions [42, Section 3.2]. As an application of Theorem 2.1, consider the equation du(t, x) = (aij(t, x)DiDju(t, x) + bi(t, x)Diu(t, x) + c(t, x)u(t, x) + f(t, x))dt + (ѕГik(t, x)Diu(t, x) + ќљk(t, x)u(t, x) + gk(t, x))dwk(t) (2.7) with 0 < t вЙ§T, x вИИRd, and initial condition u(0, x) = u0(x). Assume that (CL1) The functions aij are bounded and Lipschitz continuous, the functions bi, c, ѕГik, and ќљ are bounded measurable. (CL2) There exists a positive number ќµ > 0 such that (2aij(x) вИТѕГik(x)ѕГjk(x))yiyj вЙ•ќµ|y|2, x, y вИИRd, t вИИ[0, T].

    440 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii (CL3) There is a positive number K such that, for all x вИИRd, kвЙ•1 |ќљk(x)|2 вЙ§K. (CL4) The initial condition u0 вИИL2(вД¶; L2(Rd)) is F0-measurable, the processes f вИИL2(вД¶√Ч [0, T]; HвИТ1 2 (Rd)) and gk вИИL2(вД¶√Ч [0, T]; L2(Rd)) are Ft- adapted, and kвЙ•1 T 0 EвИ•gkвИ•2 L2(Rd)(t)dt < вИЮ. Theorem 2.2. Under assumptions (CL1)вАУ(CL4), equation (2.7) has a unique traditional solution u вИИL2(F; L2((0, T); H1 2(Rd))) ? L2(F; C((0, T), L2(Rd))), and the solution satisfies E

    sup 0<t<T вИ•uвИ•2 L2(Rd)(t) + T 0 вИ•uвИ•2 H1 2(Rd)(t)dt

    вЙ§C(K, ќµ, T)E п£Ђ п£≠вИ•u0вИ•2 L2(Rd) + T 0 вИ•fвИ•2 HвИТ1 2 (Rd)(t)dt+

    kвЙ•1 T 0 вИ•gkвИ•2 L2(Rd)(t)dt п£ґ п£Є. (2.8) Proof. Apply Theorem 2.1 to the normal triple (H1 2(Rd), L2(Rd), HвИТ1 2 (Rd)); condition (2.5) in this case is equivalent to assumption (CL2). The details of the proof are in [42, Section 4.1]. Condition (2.5) essentially means that the deterministic part of the equa- tion dominates the stochastic part. Accordingly, there are two main ways to violate (2.5): 1. The order of the operator M is more than half the order of the operator A. The equation du = uxdw(t) is an example. 2. The value of k вИ•Mk(t)vвИ•2 H is too large. This value can be either finite, as for the equation du(t, x) = uxx(t, x)dt + 5ux(t, x)dw(t), or infinite, as for the equation du(t, x) = вИЖu(t, x)dt + ѕГk(x)udwk, ѕГk вИТCONS in L2(Rd), d вЙ•2. (2.9) Indeed, it is shown in [38] that, for equation (2.9), we have

    kвЙ•1 вИ•Mk(t)vвИ•2 H = вИЮ in every Sobolev space Hќ≥. Without condition (2.5), analysis of equation (2.1) requires new technical tools and a different notion of solution. The white noise theory provides one possible collection of such tools.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 441 3 White Noise Solutions of Stochastic Parabolic Equations The central part of the white noise theory is the mathematical model for the derivative of the Brownian motion. In particular, the ItЋЖo integral t 0 f(s)dw(s) is replaced with the integral t 0 f(s) вЛДЋЩW(s)ds, where ЋЩW is the white noise process and вЛДis the Wick product. The white noise formulation is very dif- ferent from the Hilbert space approach of the previous section, and requires several new constructions. The book [10] is a general reference about the white noise theory, while [12] presents the white noise analysis of stochastic partial differential equations. Below is the summary of the main definitions and results. Denote by S = S(RвДУ) the Schwartz space of rapidly decreasing functions and by SвА≤ = SвА≤(RвДУ), the Schwartz space of tempered distributions. For the properties of the spaces S and SвА≤ see [43]. Definition 3.1 The white noise probability space is the triple S = (SвА≤, B(SвА≤), ¬µ), where B(SвА≤) is the Borel ѕГ-algebra of subsets of SвА≤, and ¬µ is the normalized Gaussian measure on B(SвА≤). The measure ¬µ is characterized by the property

    SвА≤ e вИЪвИТ1вЯ®ѕЙ,ѕХвЯ©d¬µ(ѕЙ) = e вИТ1 2 вИ•ѕХвИ•2 L2(Rd), where вЯ®ѕЙ, ѕХвЯ©, ѕЙ вИИSвА≤, ѕХ вИИS, is the duality between S and SвА≤. Existence of this measure follows from the BochnerвАУMinlos theorem [12, Appendix A]. Let {ќЈk, k вЙ•1} be the Hermite basis in L2(RвДУ), consisting of the normal- ized eigenfunctions of the operator ќЫ = вИТвИЖ+ |x|2, x вИИRвДУ. (3.1) Each ќЈk is an element of S [12, Section 2.2]. Consider the collection of multi-indices J1 = / ќ± = (ќ±i, i вЙ•1) : ќ±i вИИ{0, 1, 2, . . .},

    i ќ±i < вИЮ 0 . The set J1 is countable, and, for every ќ± вИИJ , only finitely many of ќ±i are not equal to zero. For ќ± вИИJ1, write ќ±! = K i ќ±i! and define ќЊќ±(ѕЙ) = 1 вИЪ ќ±! ( i Hќ±i(вЯ®ѕЙ, ќЈiвЯ©), ѕЙ вИИSвА≤, (3.2) where вЯ®¬Ј, ¬ЈвЯ©is the duality between S and SвА≤, and

    442 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Hn(t) = (вИТ1)net2/2 dn dtn eвИТt2/2 (3.3) is nth Hermite polynomial. In particular, H1(t) = 1, H1(t) = t, H2(t) = t2вИТ1. If, for example, ќ± = (0, 2, 0, 1, 3, 0, 0, . . .) has three non-zero entries, then ќЊќ±(ѕЙ) = H2(вЯ®ѕЙ, ќЈ2вЯ©) 2! ¬Ј вЯ®ѕЙ, ќЈ4вЯ©¬Ј H3(вЯ®ѕЙ, ќЈ5вЯ©) 3! . Theorem 3.1. The collection {ќЊќ±, ќ± вИИJ1} is an orthonormal basis in L2(S). Proof. This is a version of the classical result of Cameron and Martin [3]. In this particular form, the result is stated and proved in [12, Theorem 2.2.3]. By Theorem 3.1, every element ѕХ of L2(S) is represented as a Fourier series ѕХ = ќ± ѕХќ±ќЊќ±, where ѕХќ± =

    SвА≤ ѕХ(ѕЙ)ќЊќ±(ѕЙ)d¬µ, and вИ•ѕХвИ•2 L2(S) = ќ±вИИJ1 |ѕХќ±|2. For ќ± вИИJ1 and q вИИR, we write (2N)qќ± = ( j (2j)qќ±j. Definition 3.2 For ѕБ вИИ[0, 1] and q вЙ•0, 1. the space (S)ѕБ,q is the collection of elements ѕХ from L2(S) such that вИ•ѕХвИ•2 ѕБ,q =

    ќ±вИИJ1 (ќ±!)ѕБ(2N)qќ±|ѕХќ±|2 < вИЮ; 2. the space (S)вИТѕБ,вИТq is the closure of L2(S) relative to the norm вИ•ѕХвИ•2 вИТѕБ,вИТq =

    ќ±вИИJ1 (ќ±!)вИТѕБ(2N)вИТqќ±|ѕХќ±|2; (3.4) 3. the space (S)ѕБ is the projective limit of (S)ѕБ,q as q changes over all non- negative integers; 4. the space (S)вИТѕБ is the inductive limit of (S)вИТѕБ,вИТq as q changes over all non-negative integers. It follows that вАҐ For each ѕБ вИИ[0, 1] and q вЙ•0, ((S)ѕБ,q, L2(S), (S)вИТѕБ,вИТq) is a normal triple of Hilbert spaces. вАҐ The space (S)ѕБ is a Frechet space with topology generated by the countable family of norms вИ•¬ЈвИ•ѕБ,n, n = 0, 1, 2, . . ., and ѕХ вИИ(S)ѕБ if and only if ѕХ вИИ(S)ѕБ,q for every q вЙ•0. вАҐ The space (S)вИТѕБ is the dual of (S)ѕБ and ѕХ вИИ(S)вИТѕБ if and only if ѕХ вИИ (S)вИТѕБ,вИТq for some q вЙ•0. Every element ѕХ from (S)ѕБ is identified with a formal sum ќ±вИИJ1 ѕХќ±ќЊќ± such that (3.4) holds for some q вЙ•0.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 443 вАҐ For ѕБ вИИ(0, 1), (S)1 вКВ(S)ѕБ вКВ(S)0 вКВL2(S) вКВ(S)вИТ0 вКВ(S)вИТѕБ вКВ(S)вИТ1, with all inclusions strict. The spaces (S)0 and (S)1 are known as the spaces of Hida and Kon- dratiev test functions. The spaces (S)вИТ0 and (S)вИТ1 are known as the spaces of Hida and Kondratiev distributions. Sometimes, the spaces (S)ѕБ and (S)вИТѕБ, 0 < ѕБ вЙ§1, go under the name of Kondratiev test functions and Kondratiev distributions, respectively. Let h вИИS and hk =

    RвДУh(x)ќЈk(x)dx. Since the asymptotics of nth eigen- value of the operator ќЫ in (3.1) is n1/d [11, Chapter 21] and ќЫkh вИИS for every positive integer k, it follows that

    kвЙ•1 |hk|2kq < вИЮ (3.5) for every q вИИR. For ќ± вИИJ1 and hk as above, write hќ± = K j(hj)ќ±j, and define the stochas- tic exponential E(h) =

    ќ±вИИJ1 hќ± вИЪ ќ±! ќЊќ± (3.6) Lemma 3.1. The stochastic exponential E = E(h), h вИИS, has the following properties: вАҐ E(h) вИИ(S)ѕБ, 0 < ѕБ < 1; вАҐ For every q > 0, there exists ќі > 0 such that E(h) вИИ(S)1,q as long as

    kвЙ•1 |hk|2 < ќі. Proof. Both properties are verified by direct calculation [12, Chapter 2]. Definition 3.3 The S-transform SѕХ(h) of an element ѕХ = ќ±вИИJ ѕХќ±ќЊќ± from (S)вИТѕБ is the number SѕХ(h) =

    ќ±вИИJ1 hќ± вИЪ ќ±! ѕХќ±, (3.7) where h = kвЙ•1 hkќЈk вИИS and hќ± = K j(hj)ќ±j. The definition implies that if ѕХ вИИ(S)вИТѕБ,вИТq for some q вЙ•0, then SѕХ(h) = вЯ®ѕХ, E(h)вЯ©, where вЯ®¬Ј, ¬ЈвЯ©is the duality between (S)ѕБ,q and (S)вИТѕБ,вИТq for suitable q. Therefore, if ѕБ < 1, then SѕХ(h) is well-defined for all h вИИS, and, if ѕБ = 1, the SѕХ(h) is well-defined for h with sufficiently small L2(RвДУ) norm. To give a complete characterization of the S-transform, an additional construction is necessary. Let UѕБ, 0 вЙ§ѕБ < 1, be the collection of mappings F from S to the complex numbers such that

    444 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii 1. For every h1, h2 вИИS, the function F(h1 + zh2) is an analytic function of the complex variable z. 2. There exist positive numbers K1, K2 and an integer number n so that, for all h вИИS and all complex number z, |F(zh)| вЙ§K1 exp

    K2вИ•ќЫnhвИ• 2 1вИТѕБ L2(Rd)|z| 2 1вИТѕБ

    . For ѕБ = 1, let U1 be the collection of mappings F from S to the complex numbers such that 1вА≤. There exist ќµ > 0 and a positive integer n such that, for all h1, h2 вИИS with вИ•ќЫnh1вИ•L2(RвДУ) < ќµ, the function of a complex variable z вЖТF(h1 + h2z) is analytic at zero, and 2вА≤. There is a constant K > 0 such that, for all h вИИS with вИ•ќЫnhвИ•L2(RвДУ) < ќµ, |F(h)| вЙ§K. Two mappings F, G with properties 1вА≤ and 2вА≤ are identified with the same element of U1 if F = G on an open neighborhood of zero in S. The following result holds. Theorem 3.2. For every ѕБ вИИ[0, 1], the S-transform is a bijection from (S)вИТѕБ to UѕБ. In other words, for every ѕХ вИИ(S)вИТѕБ, the S-transform SѕХ is an element of UѕБ, and, for every F вИИUѕБ, there exists a unique ѕХ вИИ(S)вИТѕБ such that SѕХ = F. This result is proved in [10] when ѕБ = 0, and in [17] when ѕБ = 1. Definition 3.4 For ѕХ and ѕИ from (S)вИТѕБ, ѕБ вИИ[0, 1], the Wick product ѕХ вЛДѕИ is the unique element of (S)вИТѕБ whose S-transform is SѕХ ¬Ј SѕИ. If SвИТ1 is the inverse S-transform, then ѕХ вЛДѕИ = SвИТ1(SѕХ ¬Ј SѕИ), Note that, by Theorem 3.2, the Wick product is well-defined, because the space UѕБ, ѕБ вИИ[0, 1] is closed under the point-wise multiplication. Theorem 3.2 also ensures the correctness of the following definition of the white noise. Definition 3.5 The white noise ЋЩW on RвДУis the unique element of (S)0 whose S transform satisfies S ЋЩW(h) = h. Remark 3.1 If g вИИLp(S), p > 1, then g вИИ(S)вИТ0 [12, Corollary 2.3.8], and the Fourier transform ЋЖg(h) =

    SвА≤ exp вИЪ вИТ1вЯ®ѕЙ, hвЯ©

    g(ѕЙ)d¬µ(ѕЙ) is defined. Direct calculations [12, Section 2.9] show that, for those g, Sg( вИЪ вИТ1 h) = ЋЖg(h) e 1 2 вИ•hвИ•2 L2(RвДУ). As a result, the Wick product can be interpreted as a convolution on the infinite-dimensional space (S)вИТѕБ.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 445 In the study of stochastic parabolic equations, вДУ= d + 1, so that the generic point from Rd+1 is written as (t, x), t вИИR, x вИИRd. As was mentioned earlier, the terms of the type fdW(t) become f вЛДЋЩWdt. The precise connection between the ItЋЖo integral and Wick product is discussed, for example, in [12, Section 2.5]. As an example, consider the following equation: ut(t, x) = a(x)uxx(t, x) + b(x)ux(t, x) + ux(t, x) вЛДЋЩW(t, x), 0 < t < T, x вИИR, (3.8) with initial condition u(0, x) = u0(x). In (3.8), (WN1) ЋЩW is the white noise process on R2. (WN2) The initial condition u0 and the coefficients a, b are bounded and have continuous bounded derivatives up to second order. (WN3) There exists a positive number ќµ such that a(x) вЙ•ќµ, x вИИR. (WN4) The second-order derivative of a is uniformly H¬®older continuous. The equivalent ItЋЖo formulation of (3.8) is du(t, x) = (a(x)uxx(t, x) + b(x)ux(t, x))dt + ek(x)ux(t, x)dwk(x), (3.9) where {ek, k вЙ•1} is the Hermite basis in L2(R). With Mkv = ekvx, we see that condition (2.2) does not hold in any Sobolev space Hќ≥ 2 (R). In fact, no traditional solution exists in any normal triple of Sobolev spaces. On the other hand, with a suitable definition of solution, equation (3.8) is solvable in the space (S)вИТ0 of Hida distributions. Definition 3.6 A mapping u : Rd вЖТ(S)вИТѕБ is called weakly differentiable with respect to xi at a point xвИЧвИИRвДУif and only if there exists Ui(xвИЧ) вИИ(S)вИТѕБ so that, for all ѕХ вИИ(S)ѕБ, DiвЯ®u(x), ѕХвЯ©|x=xвИЧ= вЯ®Ui(xвИЧ), ѕХвЯ©. In that case, we write Ui(xвИЧ) = Diu(xвИЧ). Definition 3.7 A mapping u from [0, T] √Ч R to (S)вИТ0 is called a white noise solution of (3.8) if and only if 1. The weak derivatives ut, ux, and uxx exist, in the sense of Definition 3.6, for all (t, x) вИИ(0, T) √Ч R. 2. Equality (3.8) holds for all (t, x) вИИ(0, T) √Ч Rd. 3. limtвЖУ0 u(t, x) = u0(x) in the topology of (S)вИТ0. Theorem 3.3. Under assumptions (WN1)вАУ(WN4), there exists a white noise solution of (3.8). This solution is unique in the class of weakly measurable mappings v from (0, T) √Ч R to (S)вИТ0, for which there exists a non-negative integer q and a positive number K such that T 0

    R вИ•v(t, x)вИ•вИТ0,вИТqeвИТKx2dxdt < вИЮ.

    446 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Proof. Consider the S-transformed equation Ft(t, x; h) = a(x)Fxx(t, x; h) + b(x)Fx(t, x; h) + Fx(t, x; h)h, (3.10) 0 < t < T, x вИИR, h вИИS(R), with initial condition F(0, x; h) = u0(x). This a deterministic parabolic equation, and one can show, using the probabilistic representation of F, that F, Ft, Fx, and Fxx belong to U0. Then the inverse S-transform of F is a solution of (3.8), and the uniqueness follows from the uniqueness for equation (3.10). The details of the proof are in [40], where a similar equation is considered for x вИИRd. Even though the initial condition in (3.8) is deterministic, there are no measurability restrictions on u0 for the white noise solution to exist; see [12] for more details. With appropriate modifications, the white noise solution can be defined for equations more general than (3.8). The solution F = F(t, x; h) of the corresponding S-transformed equation determines the regularity of the white noise solution [12, Section 4.1]. Two main advantages of the white noise approach over the Hilbert space approach are: 1. No need for parabolicity condition. 2. No measurability restrictions on the input data. Still, there are substantial limitations: 1. There seems to be little or no connection between the white noise solution and the traditional solution. While the white noise solution can, in princi- ple, be constructed for equation (2.7), this solution will be very different from the traditional solution. 2. There are no clear ways of computing the solution numerically, even with available representations of the Feynmann-Kac type [12, Chapter 4]. 3. The white noise solution, being constructed on a special white noise prob- ability space, is weak in the probabilistic sense. Path-wise uniqueness does not apply to such solutions because of the вАЭaveragingвАЭ nature of the so- lution spaces. 4 Generalized Functions on the Wiener Chaos Space The objective of this section is to introduce the space of generalized random elements on an arbitrary stochastic basis. Let F = (вД¶, F, {Ft}tвЙ•0, P) be a stochastic basis with the usual assump- tions and Y , a separable Hilbert space with inner product (¬Ј, ¬Ј)Y and an or- thonormal basis {yk, k вЙ•1}. On F and Y , consider a cylindrical Brownian motion W, that is, a family of continuous Ft-adapted Gaussian martingales Wy(t), y вИИY , such that Wy(0) = 0 and E(Wy1(t)Wy2(s)) = min(t, s)(y1, y2)Y . In particular,

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 447 wk(t) = Wyk(t), k вЙ•1, t вЙ•0, (4.1) are independent standard Wiener processes on F. Equivalently, instead of the process W, the starting point can be a system of independent standard Wiener processes {wk, k вЙ•1} on F. Then, given a separable Hilbert space Y with an orthonormal basis {yk, k вЙ•1}, the corresponding cylindrical Brownian motion W is defined by Wy(t) =

    kвЙ•1 (y, yk)Y wk(t). (4.2) Fix a non-random T вИИ(0, вИЮ) and denote by FW T the ѕГ-algebra generated by wk(t), k вЙ•1, t < T. Denote by L2(W) the collection of FW T -measurable square integrable random variables. We now review construction of the CameronвАУMartin basis in the Hilbert space L2(W). Let m = {mk, k вЙ•1} be an orthonormal basis in L2((0, T)) such that each mk belongs to LвИЮ((0, T)). Define the independent standard Gaussian random variables ќЊik = T 0 mi(s)dwk(s). Consider the collection of multi-indices J = / ќ± = (ќ±k i , i, k вЙ•1) : ќ±k i вИИ{0, 1, 2, . . .},

    i,k ќ±k i < вИЮ 0 . The set J is countable, and, for every ќ± вИИJ , only finitely many of ќ±k i are not equal to zero. The upper and lower indices in ќ±k i represent, respectively, the space and time components of the noise process W. For ќ± вИИJ , define |ќ±| =

    i,k ќ±k i , ќ±! = ( i,k ќ±k i !, and ќЊќ± = 1 вИЪ ќ±! ( i,k Hќ±k i (ќЊik), (4.3) where Hn is nth Hermite polynomial. For example, if ќ± = п£Ђ п£ђ п£ђ п£ђ п£≠ 0 1 0 3 0 0 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј 2 0 0 0 4 0 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј 0 0 0 0 0 0 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ... ... ... ... ... ... ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј п£ґ п£Ј п£Ј п£Ј п£Є with four non-zero entries ќ±1 2 = 1; ќ±1 4 = 3; ќ±2 1 = 2; ќ±2 5 = 4, then ќЊќ± = ќЊ2,1 ¬Ј H3(ќЊ4,1) вИЪ 3! ¬Ј H2(ќЊ1,2) вИЪ 2! ¬Ј H4(ќЊ5,2) вИЪ 4! . There are two main differences between (3.2) and (4.3):

    448 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii 1. The basis (4.3) is constructed on an arbitrary probability space. 2. In (4.3), there is a clear separation of the time and space components of the noise, and explicit presence of the time-dependent functions mi facilitates the analysis of evolution equations. Definition 4.1 The space L2(W) is called the Wiener Chaos space. The N-th Wiener Chaos is the linear subspace of L2(W), generated by ќЊќ±, |ќ±| = N. The following is another version of the classical results of Cameron and Martin [3]. Theorem 4.1. The collection ќЮ = {ќЊќ±, ќ± вИИJ } is an orthonormal basis in L2(W). We refer to ќЮ as the CameronвАУMartin basis in L2(W). By Theorem 4.1, every element v of L2(W) can be written as v =

    ќ±вИИJ vќ±ќЊќ±, where vќ± = E(vќЊќ±). We now define the space D(L2(W)) of test functions and the space DвА≤(L2(W); X) of X-valued generalized random elements. Definition 4.2 (1) The space D(L2(W)) is the collection of elements from L2(W) that can be written in the form v =

    ќ±вИИJv vќ±ќЊќ± for some vќ± вИИR and a finite subset Jv of J . (2) A sequence vn converges to v in D(L2(W)) if and only if Jvn вКЖJv for all n and lim nвЖТвИЮ|vn,ќ± вИТvќ±| = 0 for all ќ±. Definition 4.3 For a linear topological space X define the space DвА≤(L2(W); X) of X-valued generalized random elements as the collection of continuous linear maps from the linear topological space D(L2(W)) to X. Similarly, the elements of DвА≤(L2(W); L1((0, T); X)) are called X-valued generalized random processes. The element u of DвА≤(L2(W); X) can be identified with a formal Fourier series u =

    ќ±вИИJ uќ±ќЊќ±, where uќ± вИИX are the generalized Fourier coefficients of u. For such a series and v вИИD(L2(W)), we have u(v) =

    ќ±вИИJv vќ±uќ±.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 449 Conversely, for u вИИDвА≤(L2(W); X), we define the formal Fourier series of u by setting uќ± = u(ќЊќ±). If u вИИL2(W), then u вИИDвА≤(L2(W); R) and u(v) = E(uv). By Definition 4.3, a sequence {un, n вЙ•1} converges to u in DвА≤(L2(W); X) if and only if un(v) converges to u(v) in the topology of X for every v вИИD(W). In terms of generalized Fourier coefficients, this is equivalent to lim nвЖТвИЮun,ќ± = uќ± in the topology of X for every ќ± вИИJ . The construction of the space DвА≤(L2(W); X) can be extended to Hilbert spaces other than L2(W). Let H be a real separable Hilbert space with an orthonormal basis {ek, k вЙ•1}. Define the space D(H) = / v вИИH : v =

    kвИИJv vkek, vk вИИR, Jv вИТa finite subset of {1, 2, . . .} 0 . By definition, vn converges to v in D(H) as n вЖТвИЮif and only if Jvn вКЖJv for all n and lim nвЖТвИЮ|vn,k вИТvk| = 0 for all k. For a linear topological space X, DвА≤(H; X) is the space of continuous linear maps from D(H) to X. An element g of DвА≤(H; X) can be identified with a formal series kвЙ•1 gk вКЧek such that gk = g(ek) вИИX and, for v вИИD(H), g(v) = kвИИJv gkvk. If X = R and kвЙ•1 g2 k < вИЮ, then g = kвЙ•1 gkek вИИ H and g(v) = (g, v)H, the inner product in H. The space X is naturally imbedded into DвА≤(H; X): if u вИИX, then kвЙ•1 u вКЧek вИИDвА≤(H; X). A sequence gn = kвЙ•1 gn,k вКЧek, n вЙ•1, converges to g = kвЙ•1 gk вКЧek in DвА≤(H; X) if and only if, for every k вЙ•1, lim nвЖТвИЮgn,k = gk in the topology of X. A collection {Lk, k вЙ•1} of linear operators from X1 to X2 naturally defines a linear operator L from DвА≤(H; X1) to DвА≤(H; X2): L п£Ђ п£≠ kвЙ•1 gk вКЧek п£ґ п£Є=

    kвЙ•1 Lk(gk) вКЧek. Similarly, a linear operator L : DвА≤(H; X1) вЖТDвА≤(H; X2) can be identified with a collection {Lk, k вЙ•1} of linear operators from X1 to X2 by setting Lk(u) = L(u вКЧek). Introduction of spaces DвА≤(H; X) and the corresponding operators makes it possible to avoid conditions of the type (2.2). 5 The Malliavin Derivative and its Adjoint In this section, we define an analog of the ItЋЖo stochastic integral for generalized random processes. All notations from the previous section will remain in force. In particular, Y is a separable Hilbert space with a fixed orthonormal basis {yk, k вЙ•1}, and ќЮ = {ќЊќ±, ќ± вИИJ }, the CameronвАУMartin basis in L2(W) defined in (4.3). We start with a brief review of the Malliavin calculus [37]. The Malliavin derivative D is a continuous linear operator from

    450 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii L1 2(W) = / u вИИL2(W) :

    ќ±вИИJ |ќ±|u2 ќ± < вИЮ 0 (5.1) to L2 (W; (L2((0, T)) √Ч Y )). In particular, (DќЊќ±)(t) =

    i,k H ќ±k i ќЊќ±вИТ(i,k)mi(t)yk, (5.2) where ќ±вИТ(i, k) is the multi-index with the components

    ќ±вИТ(i, k) l j = max(ќ±k i вИТ1, 0), if i = j and k = l, ќ±l j, otherwise. Note that, for each t вИИ[0, T], DќЊќ±(t) вИИD(L2(W)√ЧY ). Using (5.2), we extend the operator D by linearity to the space DвА≤(L2(W)): D

    ќ±вИИJ uќ±ќЊќ±

    =

    ќ±вИИJ п£Ђ п£≠uќ±

    i,k H ќ±k i ќЊќ±вИТ(i,k)mi(t)yk п£ґ п£Є. For the sake of completeness and to justify further definitions, let us es- tablish connection between the Malliavin derivative and the stochastic ItЋЖo integral. If u is an arbitrary FW t -adapted process from L2 (W; L2((0, T); Y )), then u(t) = kвЙ•1 uk(t)yk, where the random variable uk(t) is FW t -measurable for each t and k, and

    kвЙ•1 T 0 E|uk(t)|2dt < вИЮ. We define the stochastic ItЋЖo integral U(t) = t 0 (u(s), dW(s))Y =

    kвЙ•1 t 0 uk(s)dwk(s). (5.3) Note that U(t) is FW t -measurable and E|U(t)|2 = kвЙ•1 t 0 E|uk(s)|2ds. The next result establishes a connection between the Malliavin derivative and the stochastic ItЋЖo integral. Lemma 5.1. Suppose that u is an FW t -adapted process from L2 (W; L2((0, T); Y )), and define the process U according to (5.3). Then, for every t вЙ§T and ќ± вИИJ , E(U(t)ќЊќ±) = E t 0 (u(s), (DќЊќ±)(s))Y ds. (5.4)

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 451 Proof. Define ќЊќ±(t) = E(ќЊќ±|FW t ). It is known (see [33] or Remark 8.2 below) that dќЊќ±(t) =

    i,k H ќ±k i ќЊќ±вИТ(i,k)(t)mi(t)dwk(t). (5.5) Due to FW t -measurability of uk(t), we have uk,ќ±(t) = E

    uk(t)E(ќЊќ±|FW t )

    = E(uk(t)ќЊќ±(t)). (5.6) The definition of U implies dU(t) = kвЙ•1 uk(t)dwk(t), so that, by (5.5), (5.6), and the ItЋЖo formula, Uќ±(t) = E(U(t)ќЊќ±) = t 0

    i,k H ќ±k i uk,ќ±вИТ(i,k)(s)mi(s)ds. (5.7) Together with (5.2), the last equality implies (5.4). Lemma 5.1 is proved. Note that the coefficients uk,ќ± of u вИИL2(W; L2((0, T); H)) belong to L2((0, T)). We therefore define uk,ќ±,i = T 0 uk,ќ±(t)mi(t)dt. Then, by (5.7), Uќ±(T) =

    i,k H ќ±k i uk,ќ±вИТ(i,k),i. (5.8) Since U(T) = ќ±вИИJ Uќ±(T)ќЊќ±, we shift the summation index in (5.8) and conclude that U(T) =

    ќ±вИИJ

    i,k H ќ±k i + 1uk,ќ±,iќЊќ±+(i,k), (5.9) where

    ќ±+(i, k) l j = ќ±k i + 1, if i = j and k = l, ќ±l j, otherwise. (5.10) As a result, U(T) = ќі(u), where ќі is the adjoint of the Malliavin deriva- tive, also known as the Skorohod integral (called also the SkorohodвАУHitsuda integral); see [10], [37] or [38] for details. Lemma 5.1 suggests the following definition. For an FW t -adapted process u from L2 (W; L2((0, T))), let DвИЧ ku be the FW t -adapted process from L2 (W; L2((0, T))) such that (DвИЧ ku)ќ±(t) = t 0

    i H ќ±k i uќ±вИТ(i,k)(s)mi(s)ds. (5.11) If u вИИL2 (W; L2((0, T); Y )) is FW t -adapted, then u is in the domain of the operator ќі and ќі(uI(s < t)) = kвЙ•1(DвИЧ kuk)(t). We now extend the operators DвИЧ k to the generalized random processes. Let X be a Banach space with norm вИ•¬Ј вИ•X.

    452 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Definition 5.1 If u is an X-valued generalized random process, then DвИЧ ku is the X-valued generalized random process such that (DвИЧ ku)ќ±(t) =

    i t 0 uќ±вИТ(i,k)(s) H ќ±k i mi(s)ds. (5.12) If g вИИDвА≤ Y ; DвА≤ (L2(W); L1((0, T); X))

    , then DвИЧg is the X-valued generalized random process such that, for g = kвЙ•1 gkвКЧyk, gk вИИDвА≤(L2(W); L1((0, T); X)), (DвИЧg)ќ±(t) =

    k (DвИЧ kgk)ќ±(t) =

    i,k t 0 gk,ќ±вИТ(i,k)(s) H ќ±k i mi(s)ds. (5.13) Using (5.2), we get a generalization of equality (5.4): (DвИЧg)ќ±(t) = t 0 g(DќЊќ±(s))(s)ds. (5.14) Indeed, by linearity, gk H ќ±k i mi(s)ќЊќ±вИТ(i,k)

    (s) = H ќ±k i mi(s)gk,ќ±вИТ(i,k))(s). Theorem 5.1. If T < вИЮ, then DвИЧ k and DвИЧare continuous linear operators. Proof. It is enough to show that, if u, un вИИDвА≤ L2(FW T ); L1((0, T); X)

    and limnвЖТвИЮвИ•uќ± вИТun,ќ±вИ•L1((0,T );X) = 0 for every ќ± вИИJ , then, for every k вЙ•1 and ќ± вИИJ , lim nвЖТвИЮвИ•(DвИЧ ku)ќ± вИТ(DвИЧ kun)ќ±вИ•L1((0,T );X) = 0. Using (5.12), we find that вИ•(DвИЧ ku)ќ± вИТ(DвИЧ kun)ќ±вИ•X(t) вЙ§

    i T 0 H ќ±k i вИ•uќ±вИТ(i,k) вИТun,ќ±вИТ(i,k)вИ•X(s)|mi(s)|ds. Note that the sum contains finitely many terms. By assumption, |mi(t)| вЙ§Ci, and so вИ•(DвИЧ ku)ќ±вИТ(DвИЧ kun)ќ±вИ•L1((0,T );X) вЙ§C(ќ±)

    i H ќ±k i вИ•uќ±вИТ(i,k)вИТun,ќ±вИТ(i,k)вИ•L1((0,T );X). Theorem 5.1 is proved. 6 The Wiener Chaos Solution and the Propagator In this section we build on the ideas from [25] to introduce the Wiener Chaos solution and the corresponding propagator for a general stochastic evolution

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 453 equation. The notations from Sections 4 and 5 will remain in force. It will be convenient to interpret the cylindrical Brownian motion W as a collection {wk, k вЙ•1} of independent standard Wiener processes. As before, T вИИ(0, вИЮ) is fixed and non-random. Introduce the following objects: вАҐ The Banach spaces A, X, and U such that U вКЖX. вАҐ Linear operators A : L1((0, T); A) вЖТL1((0, T); X), Mk : L1((0, T); A) вЖТL1((0, T); X). вАҐ Generalized random processes f вИИDвА≤ (L2(W); L1((0, T); X)) and gk вИИDвА≤ (L2(W); L1((0, T); X)) . вАҐ The initial condition u0 вИИDвА≤ (L2(W); U). Consider the deterministic equation v(t) = v0 + t 0 (Av)(s)ds + t 0 ѕХ(s)ds, (6.1) where v0 вИИU and ѕХ вИИL1((0, T); X). Definition 6.1 A function v is called a w(A, X) solution of (6.1) if and only if v вИИL1((0, T); A) and equality (6.1) holds in the space L1((0, T); A). Definition 6.2 An A-valued generalized random process u is called a w(A, X) Wiener Chaos solution of the stochastic differential equation du(t) = (Au(t)+f(t))dt+(Mku(t)+gk(t))dwk(t), t вЙ§T, u|t=0 = u0, (6.2) if and only if the equality u(t) = u0 + t 0 (Au + f)(s)ds +

    kвЙ•1 (DвИЧ k(Mku + gk))(t) (6.3) holds in DвА≤ (L2(W); L1((0, T); X)). Sometimes, to stress the dependence of the Wiener Chaos solution on the terminal time T, the notation wT (A, X) will be used. Equalities (6.3) (5.13) mean that, for every ќ± вИИJ , the generalized Fourier coefficient uќ± of u satisfies the equation uќ±(t) = u0,ќ± + t 0 (Au+f)ќ±(s)ds+ t 0

    i,k H ќ±k i (Mku+gk)ќ±вИТ(i,k)(s)mi(s)ds. (6.4) Definition 6.3 System (6.4) is called the propagator for equation (6.2).

    454 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii The propagator is a lower triangular system. Indeed, If ќ± = (0), that is, |ќ±| = 0, then the corresponding equation in (6.4) becomes u(0)(t) = u0,(0) + t 0 (Au(0)(s) + f(0)(s))ds. (6.5) If ќ± = (jвДУ), that is, ќ±вДУ j = 1 for some fixed j and вДУand ќ±k i = 0 for all other i, k вЙ•1, then the corresponding equation in (6.4) becomes u(jвДУ)(t) = u0,(jвДУ) + t 0 (Au(jвДУ)(s) + f(jвДУ)(s))ds + t 0 (Mku(0)(s) + gвДУ,(0)(s))mj(s)ds. (6.6) Continuing in this way, we conclude that (6.4) can be solved by induction on |ќ±| as long as the corresponding deterministic equation (6.1) is solvable. The precise result is as follows. Theorem 6.1. If, for every v0 вИИU and ѕХ вИИL1((0, T); X), equation (6.1) has a unique w(A, X) solution v(t) = V (t, v0, ѕХ), then equation (6.2) has a unique w(A, X) Wiener Chaos solution such that uќ±(t) = V (t, u0,ќ±, fќ±) +

    i,k H ќ±k i V (t, 0, miMkuќ±вИТ(i,k)) +

    i,k H ќ±k i V (t, 0, migk,ќ±вИТ(i,k)). (6.7) Proof. Using the assumptions of the theorem and linearity, we conclude that (6.7) is the unique solution of (6.4). To derive a more explicit formula for uќ±, we need some additional con- structions. For every multi-index ќ± with |ќ±| = n, define the characteristic set Kќ± of ќ± as Kќ± = {(iќ± 1 , kќ± 1 ), . . . , (iќ± n, kќ± n)}, iќ± 1 вЙ§iќ± 2 вЙ§. . . вЙ§iќ± n, and if iќ± j = iќ± j+1, then kќ± j вЙ§kќ± j+1. The first pair (iќ± 1 , kќ± 1 ) in Kќ± is the position numbers of the first nonzero element of ќ±. The second pair is the same as the first if the first nonzero element of ќ± is greater than one; otherwise, the second pair is the position numbers of the second nonzero element of ќ± and so on. As a result, if ќ±k i > 0, then exactly ќ±k i pairs in Kќ± are equal to (i, k). For example, if ќ± = п£Ђ п£ђ п£ђ п£ђ п£≠ 0 1 0 2 3 0 0 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј 1 2 0 0 0 1 0 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј 0 0 0 0 0 0 0 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ... ... ... ... ... ... ... ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј п£ґ п£Ј п£Ј п£Ј п£Є

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 455 with nonzero elements ќ±2 1 = ќ±1 2 = ќ±6 1 = 1, ќ±2 2 = ќ±1 4 = 2, ќ±1 5 = 3, then the characteristic set is Kќ± ={(1, 2), (2, 1), (2, 2), (2, 2), (4, 1), (4, 1), (5, 1), (5, 1), (5, 1), (6, 2)}. Theorem 6.2. Assume that: 1. For every v0 вИИU and ѕХ вИИL1((0, T); X), equation (6.1) has a unique w(A, X) solution v(t) = V (t, v0, ѕХ), 2. The input data in (6.4) satisfy gk = 0 and fќ± = u0,ќ± = 0 if |ќ±| > 0. Let u(0)(t) = V (t, u0, 0) be the solution of (6.4) for |ќ±| = 0. For ќ± вИИJ with |ќ±| = n вЙ•1 and the characteristic set Kќ±, define functions F n = F n(t; ќ±) by induction as follows: F 1(t; ќ±) = V (t, 0, miMku(0)) if Kќ± = {(i, k)}; F n(t; ќ±) = n

    j=1 V (t, 0, mijMkjF nвИТ1(¬Ј; ќ±вИТ(ij, kj))) if Kќ± = {(i1, k1), . . . , (in, kn)}. (6.8) Then uќ±(t) = 1 вИЪ ќ±! F n(t; ќ±). (6.9) Proof. If |ќ±| = 1, then representation (6.9) follows from (6.6). For |ќ±| > 1, observe that: вАҐ If ¬ѓuќ±(t) = вИЪ ќ±!uќ± and |ќ±| вЙ•1, then (6.4) implies the relation ¬ѓu(t) = t 0 A¬ѓuќ±(s)ds +

    i,k t 0 ќ±k i mi(s)Mk¬ѓuќ±вИТ(i,k)(s)ds. вАҐ If Kќ± = {(i1, k1), . . . , (in, kn)}, then, for every j = 1, . . . , n, the character- istic set Kќ±вИТ(ij,kj) of ќ±вИТ(ij, kj) is obtained from Kќ± by removing the pair (ij, kj). вАҐ By the definition of the characteristic set,

    i,k ќ±k i mi(s)Mk¬ѓuќ±вИТ(i,k)(s) = n

    j=1 mij(s)Mkj ¬ѓuќ±вИТ(ij,kj)(s). As a result, representation (6.9) follows by induction on |ќ±| using (6.7): if |ќ±| = n > 1, then

    456 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii ¬ѓuќ±(t) = n

    j=1 V (t, 0, mijMkj ¬ѓuќ±вИТ(ij,kj)) = n

    j=1 V (t, 0, mijMkjF (nвИТ1)(¬Ј; ќ±вИТ(ij, kj)) = F n(t; ќ±). (6.10) Theorem 6.2 is proved. Corollary 6.1 Assume that the operator A is a generator of a strongly con- tinuous semigroup ќ¶ = ќ¶t,s, t вЙ•s вЙ•0, in some Hilbert space H such that A вКВH, each Mk is a bounded operator from A to H, and the solution V (t, 0, ѕХ) of equation (6.1) is written as V (t, 0, ѕХ) = T 0 ќ¶t,sѕХ(s)ds, ѕХ вИИL2((0, T); H)). (6.11) Denote by Pn the permutation group of {1, . . . , n}. If u(0) вИИL2((0, T); H)), then, for |ќ±| = n > 1 with the characteristic set Kќ± = {(i1, k1), . . . , (in, kn)}, representation (6.9) becomes uќ±(t) = 1 вИЪ ќ±!

    ѕГвИИPn t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 ќ¶t,snMkѕГ(n) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ќ¶s2,s1MkѕГ(1)u(0)(s1)miѕГ(n)(sn) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј miѕГ(1)(s1)ds1 . . . dsn. (6.12) Also,

    |ќ±|=n uќ±(t)ќЊќ± =

    k1,...,knвЙ•1 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 ќ¶t,snMkn ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ќ¶s2,s1

    Mk1u(0) + gk1(s1)

    dwk1(s1) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј dwkn(sn), n вЙ•1, (6.13) and, for every Hilbert space X, the following energy equality holds:

    |ќ±|=n вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 X = вИЮ

    k1,...,kn=1 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 вИ•ќ¶t,snMkn ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ќ¶s2,s1Mk1u(0)(s1)вИ•2 Xds1 . . . dsn; (6.14) both sides in the last equality can be infinite. For n = 1, formulas (6.12) and (6.14) become u(ik)(t) = t 0 ќ¶t,sMku(0)(s) mi(s)ds; (6.15)

    |ќ±|=1 вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 X = вИЮ

    k=1 t 0 вИ•ќ¶t,sMku(0)(s)вИ•2 Xds. (6.16)

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 457 Proof. Using the semigroup representation (6.11), we conclude that (6.12) is just an expanded version of (6.9). Since {mi, i вЙ•1} is an orthonormal basis in L2(0, T), equality (6.16) follows from (6.15) and the Parcevall identity. Similarly, equality (6.14) will follow from (6.12) after an application of an appropriate ParcevallвАЩs identity. To carry out the necessary arguments when |ќ±| > 1, denote by J1 the collection of one-dimensional multi-indices ќ≤ = (ќ≤1, ќ≤2, . . .) such that each ќ≤i is a non-negative integer and |ќ≤| = iвЙ•1 ќ≤i < вИЮ. Given a ќ≤ вИИJ1 with |ќ≤| = n, we define Kќ≤ = {i1, . . . , in}, the characteristic set of ќ≤ and the function Eќ≤(s1, . . . , sn) = 1 вИЪќ≤!n!

    ѕГвИИPn mi1(sѕГ(1)) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј min(sѕГ(n)). (6.17) By construction, the collection {Eќ≤, ќ≤ вИИJ1, |ќ≤| = n} is an orthonormal basis in the subspace of symmetric functions in L2((0, T)n; X). Next, we rewrite (6.12) in a symmetrized form. To make the notations shorter, denote by s(n) the ordered set (s1, . . . , sn) and write dsn = ds1 . . . dsn. Fix t вИИ(0, T] and the set k(n) = {k1, . . . , kn} of the second components of the characteristic set Kќ±. Define the symmetric function G(t, k(n); s(n)) = 1 вИЪ n!

    ѕГвИИPn ќ¶t,sѕГ(n)Mkn ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ќ¶sѕГ(2),sѕГ(1)Mk1u(0)(sѕГ(1))1sѕГ(1)<¬Ј¬Ј¬Ј<sѕГ(n)<t(s(n)). (6.18) Then (6.12) becomes uќ±(t) =

    [0,T ]n G(t, k(n); s(n))Eќ≤(ќ±)(s(n))dsn, (6.19) where the multi-indices ќ± and ќ≤(ќ±) are related via their characteristic sets: if Kќ± = {(i1, k1), . . . , (in, kn)}, then Kќ≤(ќ±) = {i1, . . . , in}. Equality (6.19) means that, for fixed k(n), the function uќ± is a Fourier coef- ficient of the symmetric function G(t, k(n); s(n)) in the space L2((0, T)n; X). ParcevallвАЩs identity and summation over all possible k(n) yield the equality

    |ќ±|=n вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 X = 1 n! вИЮ

    k1,...,kn=1

    [0,T ]n вИ•G(t, k(n); s(n))вИ•2 Xdsn, which, due to (6.18), is the same as (6.14).

    458 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii To prove equality (6.13), relating the CameronвАУMartin and multiple ItЋЖo integral expansions of the solution, we use the following result [13, Theorem 3.1]: ќЊќ± = 1 вИЪ ќ±! T 0 sn 0 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј s2 0 Eќ≤(ќ±)(s(n))dwk1(s1) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј dwkn(sn); see also [37, pp. 12вАУ13]. Since the collection of all Eќ≤ is an orthonormal basis, equality (6.13) follows from (6.19) after summation over al k1, . . . , kn. Corollary 6.1 is proved. We now present several examples to illustrate the general results. Example 6.1 Consider the following equation: du(t, x) = (auxx(t, x) + f(t, x))dt + (ѕГux(t, x) + g(t, x))dw(t), x вИИR, (6.20) where a > 0, ѕГ вИИR, f вИИL2((0, T); HвИТ1 2 (R)), g вИИL2((0, T); L2(R)), and u|t=0 = u0 вИИL2(R). By Theorem 2.2, if ѕГ2 < 2a, then equation (6.20) has a unique traditional solution u вИИL2

    W; L2((0, T); H1 2(R))

    . By FW t -measurability of u(t), we have E(u(t)ќЊќ±) = E(u(t)E(ќЊќ±|FW t )). Using the relation (5.5) and the ItЋЖo formula, we find that uќ± satisfy duќ± = a(uќ±)xxdt +

    i вИЪќ±iѕГ(uќ±вИТ(i))xmi(t)dt, which is precisely the propagator for equation (6.20). In other words, in the case 2a > ѕГ2 the traditional solution of (6.20) coincides with the Wiener Chaos solution. On the other hand, the heat equation v(t, x) = v0(x) + t 0 vxx(s, x)ds + t 0 ѕХ(s, x)ds, v0 вИИL2(R), with ѕХ вИИL2((0, T); HвИТ1 2 (R)) has a unique w(H1 2(R), HвИТ1 2 (R)) solution. There- fore, by Theorem 6.1, the unique w(H1 2(R), HвИТ1 2 (R)) Wiener Chaos solution of (6.20) exists for all ѕГ вИИR. In the next example, the equation, although not parabolic, can be solved explicitly. Example 6.2 Consider the following equation: du(t, x) = ux(t, x)dw(t), t > 0, x вИИR; u(0, x) = x. (6.21) Clearly, u(t, x) = x + w(t) satisfies (6.21).

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 459 To find the Wiener Chaos solution of (6.21), note that, with one- dimensional Wiener process, ќ±k i = ќ±i, and the propagator in this case becomes uќ±(t, x) = xI(|ќ±| = 0) + t 0

    i вИЪќ±i(uќ±вИТ(i)(s, x))xmi(s)ds. Then uќ± = 0 if |ќ±| > 1, and u(t, x) = x +

    iвЙ•1 ќЊi t 0 mi(s)ds = x + w(t). (6.22) Even though Theorem 6.1 does not apply, the above arguments show that u(t, x) = x + w(t) is the unique w(A, X) Wiener Chaos solution of (6.21) for suitable spaces A and X, for example, X =

    f :

    R (1 + x2)вИТ2f 2(x)dx < вИЮ

    and A = {f : f, f вА≤ вИИX}. Section 14 provides a more detailed analysis of equation (6.21). If equation (6.2) is anticipating, that is, the initial condition is not de- terministic and/or the free terms f, g are not FW t -adapted, then the Wiener Chaos solution generalizes the Skorohod integral interpretation of the equa- tion. Example 6.3 Consider the equation du(t, x) = 1 2uxx(t, x)dt + ux(t, x)dw(t), x вИИR, (6.23) with initial condition u(0, x) = x2w(T). Since w(T) = вИЪ TќЊ1, we find that (uќ±)t(t, x) = 1 2(uќ±)xx(t, x) +

    i вИЪќ±imi(t)(uќ±вИТ(i))x(t, x) (6.24) with initial condition uќ±(0, x) = вИЪ Tx2I(|ќ±| = 1, ќ±1 = 1). By Theorem 6.1, there exists a unique w(A, X) Wiener Chaos solution of (6.23) for suitable spaces A and X. For example, we can take X =

    f :

    R (1 + x2)вИТ8f 2(x)dx < вИЮ

    and A = {f : f, f вА≤, f вА≤вА≤ вИИX}. System (6.24) can be solved explicitly. Indeed, uќ± вЙ°0 if |ќ±| = 0 or |ќ±| > 3 or if ќ±1 = 0. Otherwise, writing Mi(t) = t 0 mi(s)ds, we find:

    460 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii uќ±(t, x) = (t + x2) вИЪ T, if |ќ±| = 1, ќ±1 = 1; uќ±(t, x) = 2 вИЪ 2 xt, if |ќ±| = 2, ќ±1 = 2; uќ±(t, x) = 2 вИЪ T xMi(t), if |ќ±| = 2, ќ±1 = ќ±i = 1, 1 < i; uќ±(t, x) =

    6 T t2, if |ќ±| = 3, ќ±1 = 3; uќ±(t, x) = 2 вИЪ 2T M1(t)Mi(t), if |ќ±| = 3, ќ±1 = 2, ќ±i = 1, 1 < i; uќ±(t, x) = вИЪ 2T M 2 i (t), if |ќ±| = 3, ќ±1 = 1, ќ±i = 2, 1 < i; uќ±(t, x) = 2 вИЪ T Mi(t)Mj(t), if |ќ±| = 3, ќ±1 = ќ±i = ќ±j = 1, 1 < i < j. Then u(t, x) =

    ќ±вИИJ uќ±ќЊќ± = w(T)w2(t)вИТ2tw(t)+2(W(T)w(t)вИТt)x+x2w(T) (6.25) is the Wiener Chaos solution of (6.23). It can be verified using the properties of the Skorohod integral [37] that the function u defined by (6.25) satisfies u(t, x) = x2w(T) + 1 2 t 0 uxx(s, x)ds + t 0 ux(s, x)dw(s), t вИИ[0, T], x вИИR, where the stochastic integral is in the sense of Skorohod. 7 Weighted Wiener Chaos Spaces and S-Transform The space DвА≤(L2(W); X) is too big to provide any reasonable information about regularity of the Wiener Chaos solution. Introduction of weighted Wiener chaos spaces makes it possible to resolve this difficulty. As before, let ќЮ = {ќЊќ±, ќ± вИИJ } be the CameronвАУMartin basis in L2(W), and D(L2(W); X), the collection of finite linear combinations of ќЊќ± with co- efficients in a Banach space X. Definition 7.1 Given a collection {rќ±, ќ± вИИJ } of positive numbers, the space RL2(W; X) is the closure of D(L2(W); X) with respect to the norm вИ•vвИ•2 RL2(W;X) :=

    ќ±вИИJ r2 ќ±вИ•vќ±вИ•2 X. The operator R defined by (Rv)ќ± := rќ±vќ± is a linear homeomorphism from RL2(W; X) to L2(W; X). There are several special choices of the weight sequence R = {rќ±, ќ± вИИJ } and special notations for the corresponding weighted Wiener Chaos spaces.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 461 вАҐ If Q = {q1, q2, . . .} is a sequence of positive numbers, define qќ± = ( i,k qќ±k i k . The operator R, corresponding to rќ± = qќ±, is denotes by Q. The space QL2(W; X) is denoted by L2,Q(W; X) and is called a Q-weighted Wiener Chaos space. The significance of this choice of weights will be explained shortly (see, in particular, Proposition 7.2). вАҐ If r2 ќ± = (ќ±!)ѕБ ( i,k (2ik)ќ≥ќ±k i , ѕБ, ќ≥ вИИR, then the corresponding space RL2(W; X) is denoted by (S)ѕБ,ќ≥(X). As always, the argument X will be omitted if X = R. Note the analogy with Definition 3.2. The structure of weights in the spaces L2,Q and (S)ѕБ,ќ≥ is different, and in general these two classes of spaces are not related. There exist generalized random elements that belong to some L2,Q(W; X), but do not belong to any (S)ѕБ,ќ≥(X). For example, u = kвЙ•1 ek2ќЊ1,k belongs to L2,Q(W) with qk = eвИТ2k2, but to no (S)ѕБ,ќ≥, because the sum kвЙ•1 e2k2(k!)ѕБ(2k)ќ≥ diverges for every ѕБ, ќ≥ вИИR. Similarly, there exist generalized random elements that belong to some (S)ѕБ,ќ≥(X), but to no L2,Q(W; X). For example, u = nвЙ•1 вИЪ n!ќЊ(n), where (n) is the multi-index with ќ±1 1 = n and ќ±k i = 0 elsewhere, belongs to (S)вИТ1,вИТ1, but does not belong to any L2,Q(W), because the sum nвЙ•1 qnn! diverges for every q > 0. The next result is the space-time analog of Proposition 2.3.3 in [12]. Proposition 7.1 The sum

    ќ±вИИJ ( i,kвЙ•1 (2ik)вИТќ≥ќ±k i converges if and only if ќ≥ > 1. Proof. Note that

    ќ±вИИJ ( i,kвЙ•1 (2ik)вИТќ≥ќ±k i = ( i,kвЙ•1 п£Ђ п£≠ nвЙ•0 ((2ik)вИТќ≥)n п£ґ п£Є= ( i,k 1 (1 вИТ(2ik)вИТќ≥), ќ≥ > 0 (7.1) The infinite product on the right of (7.1) converges if and only if each of the sums iвЙ•1 iвИТќ≥, kвЙ•1 kвИТќ≥ converges, that is, if an only if ќ≥ > 1. Corollary 7.1 For every u вИИDвА≤(W; X), there exists an operator R such that Ru вИИL2(W; X).

    462 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Proof. Define r2 ќ± = 1 1 + вИ•uќ±вИ•2 X ( i,kвЙ•1 (2ik)вИТ2ќ±k i . Then вИ•RuвИ•2 L2(W;X) =

    ќ±вИИJ вИ•uќ±вИ•2 X 1 + вИ•uќ±вИ•2 X ( i,kвЙ•1 (2ik)вИТ2ќ±k i вЙ§

    ќ±вИИJ ( i,kвЙ•1 (2ik)вИТ2ќ±k i < вИЮ. The importance of the operator Q in the study of stochastic equations is due to the fact that the operator R maps a Wiener Chaos solution to a Wiener Chaos solution if and only R = Q for some sequence Q. Indeed, direct calculations show that the functions uќ±, ќ± вИИJ , satisfy the propagator (6.4) if and only if vќ± = (Ru)ќ± satisfy the equation vќ±(t) = (Ru0)ќ± + t 0 (Av + Rf)ќ±(s)ds + t 0

    i,k H ќ±k i ѕБќ± ѕБќ±вИТ(i,k) (MkRu + Rgk)ќ±вИТ(i,k)(s)mi(s)ds. (7.2) Therefore, the operator R preserves the structure of the propagator if and only if ѕБќ± ѕБќ±вИТ(i,k) = qk, that is, ѕБќ± = qќ± for some sequence Q. Below is the summary of the main properties of the operator Q. Proposition 7.2 1. If qk вЙ§q < 1 for all k вЙ•1, then L2,Q(W) вКВ(S)0,вИТќ≥ for some ќ≥ > 0. 2. If qk вЙ•q > 1 for all k, then L2,Q(W) вКВLn 2(W) for all n вЙ•1, that is, the elements of L2,Q(W) are infinitely differentiable in the Malliavin sense. 3. If u вИИL2,Q(W; X) with generalized Fourier coefficients uќ± satisfying the propagator (6.4), and v = Qu, then the corresponding system for the generalized Fourier coefficients of v is vќ±(t) = (Qu0)ќ± + t 0 (Av + Qf)ќ±(s)ds + t 0

    i,k H ќ±k i (Mkv + Qgk)ќ±вИТ(i,k)(s)qkmi(s)ds. (7.3) 4. The function u is a Wiener Chaos solution of u(t) = u0 + t 0 (Au(s) + f(s))dt + t 0 (Mu(s) + g(s), dW(s))Y (7.4)

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 463 if and only if v = Qu is a Wiener Chaos solution of v(t) = (Qu)0 + t 0 (Av(s) + Qf(s))dt + t 0 (Mv(s) + Qg(s), dW Q(s))Y , (7.5) where, for h вИИY , W Q h (t) = kвЙ•1(h, yk)Y qkwk(t). The following examples demonstrate how the operator Q helps with the analysis of various stochastic evolution equations. Example 7.1 Consider the w(H1 2(R), HвИТ1 2 (R)) Wiener Chaos solution u of equation du(t, x) = (auxx(t, x) + f(t, x))dt + ѕГux(t, x)dw(t), x вИИR, (7.6) with f вИИL2(вД¶√Ч (0, T); HвИТ1 2 (R)), g вИИL2(вД¶√Ч (0, T); L2(R)), and the initial data u|t=0 = u0 вИИL2(R). Assume that ѕГ > 0 and define the sequence Q such that qk = q for all k вЙ•1 and q < вИЪ 2a/ѕГ. By Theorem 2.2, the equation dv = (avxx + f)dt + (qѕГux + g)dw with v|t=0 = u0, has a unique traditional solution v вИИL2

    W; L2((0, T); H1 2(R))

    ? L2 (W; C((0, T); L2(R))) . By Proposition 7.2, the w(H1 2(R), HвИТ1 2 (R)) Wiener Chaos solution u of equa- tion (7.6) satisfies u = QвИТ1v and u вИИL2,Q

    W; L2((0, T); H1 2(R))

    ? L2,Q (W; C((0, T); L2(R))) . Note that if equation (7.6) is strongly parabolic, that is, 2a > ѕГ2, then the weight q can be taken bigger than one, and, according to the first statement of Proposition 7.2, regularity of the solution is better than the one guaranteed by Theorem 2.2. Example 7.2 The Wiener Chaos solutions can be constructed for stochastic ordinary differential equations. Consider, for example, u(t) = 1 + t 0

    kвЙ•1 u(s)dwk(s), (7.7) which clearly does not have a traditional solution. On the other hand, the unique w(R, R) Wiener Chaos solution of this equation belongs to L2,Q (W; L2((0, T)) for every Q satisfying k q2 k < вИЮ. Indeed, for (7.7), equa- tion (7.5) becomes v(t) = 1 + t 0

    k v(s)qkdwk(s). If k q2 k < вИЮ, then the traditional solution of this equation exists and belongs to L2 (W; L2((0, T))).

    464 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii There exist equations for which the Wiener Chaos solution does not be- long to any weighted Wiener chaos space L2,Q. An example is given below in Section 14. To define the S-transform, consider the following analog of the stochastic exponential (3.6). Lemma 7.1. If h вИИD (L2((0, T); Y )) and E(h) = exp T 0 (h(t), dW(t))Y вИТ1 2 T 0 вИ•h(t)вИ•2 Y dt

    , then вАҐ E(h) вИИL2,Q(W) for every sequence Q. вАҐ E(h) вИИ(S)ѕБ,ќ≥ for ѕБ вИИ[0, 1) and ќ≥ вЙ•0. вАҐ E(h) вИИ(S)1,ќ≥, ќ≥ вЙ•0, as long as вИ•hвИ•2 L2((0,T );Y ) is sufficiently small. Proof. Recall that, if h вИИD(L2((0, T); Y )), then h(t) = i,kвИИIh hk,imi(t)yk, where Ih is a finite set. Direct computations show that E(h) = ( i,k п£Ђ п£≠ nвЙ•0 Hn(ќЊik) n! (hk,i)n п£ґ п£Є=

    ќ±вИИJ hќ± вИЪ ќ±! ќЊќ± where hќ± = K i,k hќ±k i k,i. In particular, (E(h))ќ± = hќ± вИЪ ќ±! . (7.8) Consequently, for every sequence Q of positive numbers, вИ•E(h)вИ•2 L2,Q(W) = exp п£Ђ п£≠ i,kвИИIh h2 k,iq2 k п£ґ п£Є< вИЮ. (7.9) Similarly, for ѕБ вИИ[0, 1) and ќ≥ вЙ•0, вИ•E(h)вИ•2 (S)ѕБ,ќ≥ =

    ќ±вИИJ ( i,k ((2ik)ќ≥hk,i)2ќ±k i (ќ±k i !)1вИТѕБ = ( i,kвИИIh п£Ђ п£≠ nвЙ•0 ((2ik)ќ≥hk,i)2n (n!)1вИТѕБ п£ґ п£Є< вИЮ, (7.10) and, for ѕБ = 1, вИ•E(h)вИ•2 (S)1,ќ≥ =

    ќ±вИИJ ( i,k ((2ik)ќ≥hk,i)2ќ±k i = ( i,kвИИIh п£Ђ п£≠ nвЙ•0 ((2ik)ќ≥hk,i)2n п£ґ п£Є< вИЮ, (7.11) if 2

    max(m,n)вИИIh)(mn)ќ≥

    i,k h2 k,i < 1. Lemma 7.1 is proved.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 465 Remark 7.1 It is well-known (see, for example, [24, Proof of Theorem 5.5]) that the family {E(h), h вИИD (L2((0, T); Y ))} is dense in L2(W) and conse- quently in every L2,Q(W) and every (S)ѕБ,ќ≥, вИТ1 < ѕБ вЙ§1, ќ≥ вИИR. Definition 7.2 If u вИИL2,Q(W; X) for some Q, or if u вИИI qвЙ•0(S)вИТѕБ,вИТќ≥(X), 0 вЙ§ѕБ вЙ§1, then the deterministic function Su(h) =

    ќ±вИИJ uќ±hќ± вИЪ ќ±! вИИX (7.12) is called the S-transform of u. Similarly, for g вИИDвА≤ (Y ; L2,Q(W; X)) the S-transform Sg(h) вИИDвА≤(Y ; X) is defined by setting (Sg(h))k = (Sgk)(h). Note that if u вИИL2(W; X), then Su(h) = E(uE(h)). If u belongs to L2,Q(W; X) or to I qвЙ•0(S)вИТѕБ,вИТќ≥(X), 0 вЙ§ѕБ < 1, then Su(h) is defined for all h вИИD (L2((0, T); Y )) . If u вИИI ќ≥вЙ•0(S)вИТ1,вИТќ≥(X), then Su(h) is defined only for h sufficiently close to zero. By Remark 7.1, an element u from L2,Q(W; X) or I ќ≥вЙ•0(S)вИТѕБ,вИТќ≥(X), 0 вЙ§ ѕБ < 1, is uniquely determined by the collection of deterministic functions Su(h), h вИИD (L2((0, T); Y )) . Since E(h) > 0 for all h вИИD (L2((0, T); Y )), Remark 7.1 also suggests the following definition. Definition 7.3 An element u from L2,Q(W) or I ќ≥вЙ•0(S)вИТѕБ,вИТќ≥, 0 вЙ§ѕБ < 1, is called non-negative (u вЙ•0) if and only if the inequality Su(h) вЙ•0 holds whatever is h вИИD (L2((0, T); Y )). The definition of the operator Q and Definition 7.3 imply the following result. Proposition 7.3 A generalized random element u from L2,Q(W) is non- negative if and only if Qu вЙ•0. For example, the solution of equation (7.7) is non-negative because Qu(t) = exp п£Ђ п£≠ kвЙ•1 (qkwk(t) вИТ(1/2)q2 k) п£ґ п£Є. We conclude this section with one technical remark. Definition 7.2 expresses the S-transform in terms of the generalized Fourier coefficients. The following results makes it possible to recover generalized Fourier coefficients from the corresponding S-transform. Proposition 7.4 If u belongs to some L2,Q(W; X) or I ќ≥вЙ•0(S)вИТѕБ,вИТќ≥(X) with 0 вЙ§ѕБ вЙ§1, then uќ± = 1 вИЪ ќ±! п£Ђ п£≠( i,k вИВќ±k i Su(h) вИВh ќ±k i k,i п£ґ п£Є

    h=0 . (7.13)

    466 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Proof. For each ќ± вИИJ with K non-zero entries, equality (7.12) and Lemma 7.1 imply that the function Su(h), as a function of K variables hk,i, is analytic in some neighborhood of zero. Then (7.13) follows after differentiation of the series (7.12). 8 General Properties of the Wiener Chaos Solutions Using notations and assumptions from Section 6, consider the linear evolution equation du(t) = (Au(t) + f(t))dt + (Mu(t) + g(t), dW(t))Y , u|t=0 = u0. (8.1) The objective of this section is to study how the Wiener Chaos compares with the traditional and white noise solutions. To make the presentation shorter, we shall call an X-valued generalized random element S-admissible if and only if it belongs to L2,Q(FW ; X) for some Q or to (S)ѕБ,q(X) for some ѕБ вИИ[вИТ1, 1] and q вИИR. It was shown in Section 7 that, for every S-admissible u, the S-transform Su(h) is defined when h = i,k hk,imiyk вИИD(L2((0, T); Y )) and is an analytic function of hk,i in some neighborhood of h = 0. The next result describes the S-transform of the Wiener Chaos solution. Theorem 8.1. Assume that: 1. There exists a unique w(A, X) Wiener Chaos solution u of (8.1) and u is S-admissible. 2. For each t вИИ[0, T], the linear operators A(t), Mk(t) are bounded from A to X. 3. The generalized random elements u0, f, gk are S-admissible. Then, for every h вИИD(L2((0, T); Y )) with вИ•hвИ•2 L2((0,T );Y ) sufficiently small, the function v = Su(h) is a w(A, X) solution of the deterministic equation v(t) = Su0(h) + t 0

    Av + Sf(h) + (Mkv + Sgk(h))hk

    (s)ds. (8.2) Proof. By assumption, Su(h) exists for suitable functions h. Then the S- transformed equation (8.2) follows from the definition of the S-transform (7.12) and the propagator equation (6.4) satisfied by the generalized Fourier coefficients of u. Indeed, continuity of operator A implies S(Au)(h) =

    ќ± hќ± вИЪ ќ±! Auќ± = A

    ќ± hќ± вИЪ ќ±! uќ± = A(Su(h)).

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 467 Similarly,

    ќ± hќ± вИЪ ќ±!

    i,k H ќ±k i Mkuќ±вИТ(i,k)mi =

    ќ±

    i,k hќ±вИТ(i,k)

    ќ±вИТ(i, k)! Mkuќ±вИТ(i,k)mihk,i =

    i,k

    ќ± hќ± вИЪќ±Mkuќ±

    mihk,i = Mk(Su(h))hk. Computations for the other terms are similar. Theorem 8.1 is proved. Remark 8.1 If h вИИD(L2((0, T); Y )) and Et(h) = exp t 0 (h(s), dW(s))Y вИТ1 2 t 0 вИ•h(t)вИ•2 Y dt

    , (8.3) then, by the ItЋЖo formula, dEt(h) = Et(h)(h(t), dW(t))Y . (8.4) If u0 is deterministic, f and gk are FW t -adapted, and u is a square-integrable solution of (8.1), then equality (8.2) is obtained by multiplying equations (8.4) and (8.1) according to the ItЋЖo formula and taking the expectation. Remark 8.2 Rewriting (8.4) as dEt(h) = Et(h)hk,imi(t)dwk(t) and using the relations Et(h) = E(ET (h)|FW t ), ќЊќ± = 1 вИЪ ќ±! п£Ђ п£≠( i,k вИВќ±k i ET (h) вИВh ќ±k i k,i п£ґ п£Є

    h=0 , we arrive at representation (5.5) for E(ќЊќ±|FW t ). A partial converse of Theorem 8.1 is that, under some regularity conditions, the Wiener Chaos solution can be recovered from the solution of the S- transformed equation (8.2). Theorem 8.2. Assume that the linear operators A(t), Mk(t), t вИИ[0, T], are bounded from A to X, the input data u0, f, gk are S-admissible, and, for every h вИИD(L2((0, T); Y )) with вИ•hвИ•2 L2((0,T );Y ) sufficiently small, there exists a w(A, X) solution v = v(t; h) of equation (8.2). We write h = hk,imiyk and consider v as a function of the variables hk,i. Assume that all the derivatives of v at the point h = 0 exists, and, for ќ± вИИJ , define uќ±(t) = 1 вИЪ ќ±! п£Ђ п£≠( i,k вИВќ±k i v(t; h) вИВh ќ±k i k,i п£ґ п£Є

    h=0 . (8.5) Then the generalized random process u(t) = ќ±вИИJ uќ±(t)ќЊќ± is a w(A, X) Wiener Chaos solution of (8.1).

    468 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Proof. Differentiation of (8.2) and application of Proposition 7.4 show that the functions uќ± satisfy the propagator (6.4). Remark 8.3 The central part in the construction of the white noise solution of (8.1) is proving that the solution of (8.2) is an S-transform of a suitable generalized random process. For many particular cases of equation (8.1), the corresponding analysis is carried out in [10, 12, 33, 40]. The consequence of Theorems 8.1 and 8.2 is that a white noise solution of (8.1), if exists, must coincide with the Wiener Chaos solution. The next theorem establishes the connection between the Wiener Chaos solution and the traditional solution. Recall that the traditional, or square- integrable, solution of (8.1) was introduced in Definition 2.2. Accordingly, the notations from Section 2 will be used. Theorem 8.3. Let (V, H, V вА≤) be a normal triple of Hilbert spaces. Take a deterministic function u0 and FW t -adapted random processes f and gk so that (2.3) holds. Under these assumptions we have the following two statements. 1. An FW t -adapted traditional solution of (8.1) is also a Wiener Chaos so- lution. 2. If u is a w(V, V вА≤) Wiener Chaos solution of (8.1) such that

    ќ±вИИJ T 0 вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 V dt + sup 0вЙ§tвЙ§T вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 H

    < вИЮ, (8.6) then u is an FW t -adapted traditional solution of (8.1). Proof. (1) If u = u(t) is an FW t -adapted traditional solution, then uќ±(t) = E(u(t)ќЊќ±) = E

    u(t)E(ќЊќ±|FW t )

    = E(u(t)ќЊќ±(t)). Then the propagator (6.4) for uќ± follows after applying the ItЋЖo formula to the product u(t)ќЊќ±(t) and using (5.5). (2) Assumption (8.6) implies that u вИИL2(вД¶√Ч (0, T); V ) ? L2(вД¶; C((0, T); H)). Then, by Theorem 8.1, for every ѕХ вИИV and h вИИD((0, T); Y ), the S-transform uh of u satisfies the equation (uh(t), ѕХ)H = (u0, ѕХ)H + t 0 вЯ®Auh(s), ѕХвЯ©ds + t 0 вЯ®f(s), ѕХвЯ©ds +

    ќ±вИИJ hќ± ќ±!

    i,k t 0 H ќ±k i mi(s)

    (Mkuќ±вИТ(i,k)(s), ѕХ)H +(gk(s), ѕХ)HI(|ќ±| = 1)

    ds.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 469 If I(t) = t 0(Mku(s), ѕХ)Hdwk(s), then E(I(t)ќЊќ±(t)) = t 0

    i,k H ќ±k i mi(s)(Mkuќ±вИТ(i,k)(s), ѕХ)Hds. (8.7) Similarly, E

    ќЊќ±(t) t 0 (gk(s), ѕХ)Hdwk(s)

    =

    i,k t 0 H ќ±k i mi(s)(gk(s), ѕХ)HI(|ќ±| = 1)ds. Therefore,

    ќ±вИИJ hќ± ќ±!

    i,k t 0 H ќ±k i mi(s)(Mkuќ±вИТ(i,k)(s), ѕХ)Hds = E

    E(h) t 0 ((Mku(s), ѕХ)H + (gk(s), ѕХ)H) dwk(s)

    . As a result, E (E(h)(u(t), ѕХ)H) = E (E(h)(u0, ѕХ)H) + E

    E(h) t 0 вЯ®Au(s), ѕХвЯ©ds

    • E

    E(h) t 0 вЯ®f(s), ѕХвЯ©ds

    • E

    E(h) t 0 ((Mku(s), ѕХ)H + (gk(s), ѕХ)H) dwk(s)

    . (8.8) Equality (8.8) and Remark 7.1 imply that, for each t and each ѕХ, (2.4) holds with probability one. Continuity of u implies that, for each ѕХ, a sin- gle probability-one set can be chosen for all t вИИ[0, T]. Theorem 9.3 is proved. 9 Regularity of the Wiener Chaos Solution Let F = (вД¶, F, {Ft}tвЙ•0, P) be a stochastic basis with the usual assumptions and wk = wk(t), k вЙ•1, t вЙ•0, a collection of standard Wiener processes on F. As in Section 2, let (V, H, V вА≤) be a normal triple of Hilbert spaces and A(t) : V вЖТV вА≤, Mk(t) : V вЖТH, linear bounded operators; t вИИ[0, T]. In this section we study the linear equation u(t) = u0 + t 0 (Au(s) + f(s))ds + t 0 (Mku(s) + gk(s))dwk(s), t вЙ§T, (9.1) under the following assumptions:

    470 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii A1 There exist positive numbers C1 and ќі such that вЯ®A(t)v, vвЯ©+ ќівИ•vвИ•2 V вЙ§C1вИ•vвИ•2 H, v вИИV, t вИИ[0, T]. (9.2) A2 There exists a real number C2 such that 2вЯ®A(t)v, vвЯ©+

    kвЙ•1 вИ•Mk(t)vвИ•2 H вЙ§C2вИ•vвИ•2 H, v вИИV, t вИИ[0, T]. (9.3) A3 The initial condition u0 is non-random and belongs to H; the process f = f(t) is deterministic and T 0 вИ•f(t)вИ•2 V вА≤dt < вИЮ; each gk = gk(t) is a deterministic processes and kвЙ•1 T 0 вИ•gk(t)вИ•2 Hdt < вИЮ. Note that condition (9.3) is weaker than (2.5). Traditional analysis of equation (9.1) under (9.3) requires additional regularity assumptions on the input data and additional Hilbert space constructions beyond the normal triple [42, Section 3.2]. In particular, no existence of a traditional solution is known under assumptions A1-A3, and the Wiener Chaos approach provides new existence and regularity results for equation (9.1). A different version of the following theorem is presented in [29]. Theorem 9.1. Under assumptions A1вАУA3, for every T > 0, equation (9.1) has a unique w(V, V вА≤) Wiener Chaos solution. This solution u = u(t) has the following properties: 1. There exists a weight sequence Q such that u вИИL2,Q(W; L2((0, T); V )) ? L2,Q(W; C((0, T); H)). 2. For every t вЙ§T, u(t) вИИL2(вД¶; H) and EвИ•u(t)вИ•2 H вЙ§3eC2t п£Ђ п£≠вИ•u0вИ•2 H + Cf t 0 вИ•f(s)вИ•2 V вА≤ds +

    kвЙ•1 t 0 вИ•gk(s)вИ•2 Hds п£ґ п£Є, (9.4) where the number C2 is from (9.3) and the positive number Cf depends only on ќі and C1 from (9.2). 3. For every t вЙ§T, u(t) = u(0) +

    nвЙ•1

    k1,...,knвЙ•1 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 ќ¶t,snMkn ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ќ¶s2,s1

    Mk1u(0) + gk1(s1)

    dwk1(s1) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј dwkn(sn), (9.5) where ќ¶t,s is the semigroup of the operator A.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 471 Proof. Assumption A2 and the properties of the normal triple imply that there exists a positive number CвИЧsuch that

    kвЙ•1 вИ•Mk(t)vвИ•2 H вЙ§CвИЧвИ•vвИ•2 V , v вИИV, t вИИ[0, T]. (9.6) Define the sequence Q such that qk = ¬µќі CвИЧ 1/2 := q, k вЙ•1, (9.7) where ¬µ вИИ(0, 2) and ќі is from Assumption A1. Then, by Assumption A2, 2вЯ®Av, vвЯ©+

    kвЙ•1 q2вИ•MkvвИ•2 H вЙ§вИТ(2 вИТ¬µ)ќівИ•vвИ•2 V + C1вИ•vвИ•2 H. (9.8) It follows from Theorem 2.1 that equation v(t) = u0 + t 0 (Av + f)(s)ds +

    kвЙ•1 t 0 q(Mkv + gk)(s)dwk(s) (9.9) has a unique solution v вИИL2(W; L2((0, T); V )) ? L2(W; C((0, T); H)). Comparison of the propagators for equations (9.1) and (9.9) shows that u = QвИТ1v is the unique w(V, V вА≤) solution of (9.1) and u вИИL2,Q(W; L2((0, T); V )) ? L2,Q(W; C((0, T); H)). (9.10) If CвИЧ< 2ќі, then equation (9.1) is strongly parabolic and q > 1 is an admissible choice of the weight. As a result, for strongly parabolic equations, the result (9.10) is stronger than the conclusion of Theorem 2.1. The proof of (9.4) is based on the analysis of the propagator uќ±(t) = u0I(|ќ±| = 0) + t 0

    Auќ±(s) + f(s)I(|ќ±| = 0)

    ds + t 0

    i,k H ќ±k i (Mkuќ±вИТ(i,k)(s) + gk(s)I(|ќ±| = 1))mi(s)ds. (9.11) We consider three particular cases: (1) f = gk = 0 (the homogeneous equa- tion); (2) u0 = gk = 0; (3) u0 = f = 0. The general case will then follow by linearity and the triangle inequality. Let us denote by (ќ¶t,s, t вЙ•s вЙ•0) the semigroup generated by the oper- ator A(t); ќ¶t := ќ¶t,0. One of the consequence of Theorem 2.1 is that, under Assumption A1, this semigroup exists and is strongly continuous in H.

(9.27) To derive (9.4), it remains to combine (9.19), (9.23), and (9.27) with the elementary inequality (a + b + c)2 вЙ§3(a2 + b2 + c2). Representation (9.5) of the Wiener Chaos solution as a sum of iterated ItЋЖo integrals now follows from Corollary 6.1. Theorem 9.1 is proved. Corollary 9.1 If

    ќ±вИИJ T 0 вИ•uќ±(s)вИ•2 V ds < вИЮ, then

    ќ±вИИJ sup 0вЙ§tвЙ§T вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 H < вИЮ. Proof. The proof of Theorem 9.1 shows that it is sufficient to consider the homogeneous equation. Then, by inequalities (9.15)вАУ(9.16), n1

    вДУ=n+1
