    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 475 T 0 вИ•uќ±(s)вИ•2 V ds =

    nвЙ•1

    k1,...,knвЙ•1 T 0 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 вИ•Mknќ¶t,snMkn . . . ќ¶s1u0вИ•2 V dsndt < вИЮ. (9.29) As a result, (9.6) and (9.29) imply that lim nвЖТвИЮ T 0 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 вИ•Mkn+1ќ¶t,snMkn . . . Mk1ќ¶s1u0вИ•2 Hdsndt = 0, which, by (9.28), implies uniform, with respect to t, convergence of the series

    ќ±вИИJ вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 H. Corollary 9.1 is proved. Corollary 9.2 Let aij, bi, c, ѕГik, ќљk be deterministic measurable functions of (t, x) such that |aij(t, x)| + |bi(t, x)| + |c(t, x)| + |ѕГik(t, x)| + |ќљk(t, x)| вЙ§K, i, j = 1, . . . , d, k вЙ•1, x вИИRd, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T;

    aij(t, x) вИТ1 2ѕГik(t, x)ѕГjk(t, x)

    yiyj вЙ•0, x, y вИИRd, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T; and

    kвЙ•1 |ќљk(t, x)|2 вЙ§Cќљ < вИЮ, x вИИRd, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T. Consider the equation du = (Di(aijDju) + biDiu + c u + f)dt + (ѕГikDiu + ќљku + gk)dwk. (9.30) Assume that the input data satisfy u0 вИИL2(Rd), f вИИL2((0, T); HвИТ1 2 (Rd)),

    kвЙ•1 вИ•gkвИ•2 L2((0,T )√ЧRd) < вИЮ, and there exists an ќµ > 0 such that aij(t, x)yiyj вЙ•ќµ|y|2, x, y вИИRd, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T. Then there exists a unique Wiener Chaos solution u = u(t, x) of (9.30). The solution has the following regularity: u(t, ¬Ј) вИИL2(W; L2(Rd)), 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T, (9.31) and EвИ•uвИ•2 L2(Rd)(t) вЙ§CвИЧ вИ•u0вИ•2 L2(Rd) + вИ•fвИ•2 L2((0,T );HвИТ1 2 (Rd)) +

    kвЙ•1 вИ•gkвИ•2 L2((0,T )√ЧRd)

    , (9.32) where the positive number CвИЧdepends only on Cќљ, K, T, and ќµ.

    476 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Remark 9.1 (1) If (2.5) holds instead of (9.3), then the proof of Theorem 9.1, in particular, (9.15)вАУ(9.16), shows that the term EвИ•u(t)вИ•2 H in the left-hand-side of inequality (9.4) can be replaced with E

    вИ•u(t)вИ•2 H + ќµ t 0 вИ•u(s)вИ•2 V ds

    . (2) If f = gk = 0 and the equation is fully degenerate, that is, 2вЯ®A(t)v, vвЯ©+

    kвЙ•1 вИ•Mk(t)vвИ•2 H = 0, t вИИ[0, T], then it is natural to expect conservation of energy. Once again, analysis of (9.15)вАУ(9.16) shows that equality EвИ•u(t)вИ•2 H = вИ•u0вИ•2 H holds if and only if lim nвЖТвИЮ T 0 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 вИ•Mkn+1ќ¶t,snMkn . . . Mk1ќ¶s1u0вИ•2 Hdsndt = 0. The proof of Corollary 9.1 shows that for the conservation of energy in a fully degenerate homogeneous equation the condition E T 0 вИ•u(t)вИ•2 V dt < вИЮis sufficient. One of applications of the Wiener Chaos solution is new numerical methods for solving the evolution equations. Indeed, an approximation of the solution is obtained by truncating the sum ќ±вИИJ uќ±(t)ќЊќ±. For the Zakai filtering equa- tion, these numerical methods were studied in [25, 26, 27]; see also Section 11 below. The main question in the analysis is the rate of convergence, in n, of the series nвЙ•1

    |ќ±|=n вИ•u(t)вИ•2 H. In general, this convergence can be arbitrarily slow. For example, consider the equation du = 1 2uxxdt + uxdw(t), t > 0, x вИИR, in the normal triple (H1 2(R), L2(R), HвИТ1 2 (R)), with the initial condition u|t=0 = u0 вИИL2(R). It follows from (9.12) that Fn(t) =

    |ќ±|=n вИ•uвИ•2 L2(R)(t) = tn n!

    R |y|2neвИТy2t|ЋЖu0|2dy, where ЋЖu0 is the Fourier transform of u0. If |ЋЖu0(y)|2 = 1 (1 + |y|2)ќ≥ , ќ≥ > 1/2,

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 477 then the rate of decay of Fn(t) is close to nвИТ(1+2ќ≥)/2. Note that, in this ex- ample, EвИ•uвИ•2 L2(R)(t) = вИ•u0вИ•2 L2(R). An exponential convergence rate that is uniform in вИ•u0вИ•2 H is achieved un- der strong parabolicity condition (2.5). An even faster factorial rate is achieved when the operators Mk are bounded on H. Theorem 9.2. Assume that there exist a positive number ќµ and a real number C0 such that 2вЯ®A(t)v, vвЯ©+

    kвЙ•1 вИ•Mk(t)vвИ•2 H + ќµвИ•vвИ•2 V вЙ§C0вИ•vвИ•2 H, t вИИ[0, T], v вИИV. Then there exists a positive number b such that, for all t вИИ[0, T],

    |ќ±|=n вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 H вЙ§вИ•u0вИ•2 H (1 + b)n . (9.33) If, in addition, kвЙ•1 вИ•Mk(t)ѕХвИ•2 H вЙ§C3вИ•ѕХвИ•2 H, then

    |ќ±|=n вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 H вЙ§(C3t)n n! eC1tвИ•u0вИ•2 H. (9.34) Proof. If CвИЧis from (9.6) and b = ќµ/CвИЧ, then the operators вИЪ 1 + bMk satisfy the inequality 2вЯ®A(t)v, vвЯ©+ (1 + b)

    kвЙ•1 вИ•Mk(t)вИ•2 H вЙ§C0вИ•vвИ•2 H. By Theorem 9.1, (1 + b)n

    k1,...,knвЙ•1 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 вИ•ќ¶t,snMkn . . . Mk1ќ¶s1u0вИ•2 Hdsn вЙ§вИ•u0вИ•2 H, and (9.33) follows. To establish (9.34), note that, by (9.2), вИ•ќ¶tfвИ•2 H вЙ§eC1tвИ•fвИ•2 H, and therefore the result follows from (9.12). Theorem 9.2 is proved. The Wiener Chaos solution of (9.1) is not, in general, a solution of the equation in the sense of Definition 2.2. Indeed, if u ћЄвИИL2(вД¶√Ч (0, T); V ), then the expressions вЯ®Au(s), ѕХвЯ©and (Mku(s), ѕХ)H are not defined. On the other hand, if there is a possibility to move the operators A and M from the solution process u to the test function ѕХ, then equation (9.1) admits a natural analog of the traditional weak formulation (2.4).

    478 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Theorem 9.3. In addition to A1вАУA3, assume that there exist operators AвИЧ(t), MвИЧ k(t) and a dense subset V0 of the space V such that: 1. AвИЧ(t)(V0) вКЖH, MвИЧ k(t)(V0) вКЖH, t вИИ[0, T]. 2. For every v вИИV , ѕХ вИИV0, and t вИИ[0, T], вЯ®A(t)v, ѕХвЯ©= (v, AвИЧ(t)ѕХ)H, (Mk(t)v, ѕХ)H = (v, MвИЧ k(t)ѕХ)H. If u = u(t) is the Wiener Chaos solution of (9.1), then, for every ѕХ вИИV0 and every t вИИ[0, T], the equality (u(t), ѕХ)H = (u0, ѕХ)H + t 0 (u(s), AвИЧ(s)ѕХ)Hds + t 0 вЯ®f(s), ѕХвЯ©ds + t 0 (u(s), MвИЧ k(s)ѕХ)Hdwk(s) + t 0 (gk(s), ѕХ)Hdwk(s) (9.35) holds in L2(W). Proof. The arguments are identical to the proof of Theorem 8.3(2). As was mentioned earlier, the Wiener Chaos solution can be constructed for anticipating equations, that is, equations with FW T -measurable input data. With obvious modifications, inequality (9.4) holds if each of the input func- tions u0, f, and gk in (9.1) is a finite linear combination of the basis elements ќЊќ±. The following example demonstrates that inequality (9.4) is impossible for a general anticipating equation. Example 9.1 Let u = u(t, x) be a Wiener Chaos solution of an ordinary dif- ferential equation du = udw(t), t вЙ§1, (9.36) with u0 = ќ±вИИJ aќ±ќЊќ±. For n вЙ•0, denote by (n) the multi-index with ќ±1 = n and ќ±i = 0, i вЙ•2, and assume that a(n) > 0, n вЙ•0. Then Eu2(1) вЙ•C

    nвЙ•0 e вИЪna2 (n). (9.37) Indeed, the first column of propagator for ќ± = (n) is u(0)(t) = a(0) and u(n)(t) = a(n) + вИЪn t 0 u(nвИТ1)(s)ds, so that u(n)(t) = n

    k=0 вИЪ n!

    (n вИТk)!k! a(nвИТk) вИЪ k! tk. Then u2 (n)(1) вЙ•n k=0 n k

    a2 (nвИТk) k! and

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 479

    nвЙ•0 u2 (n)(1) вЙ•

    nвЙ•0 п£Ђ п£≠ kвЙ•0 1 k! n + k n п£ґ п£Єa2 (n). Since

    kвЙ•0 1 k! n + k n

    вЙ•

    kвЙ•0 nk (k!)2 вЙ•Ce вИЪn, the result follows. The consequence of Example 9.1 is that it is possible, in (9.1), to have u0 вИИLn 2(W; H) for every n, and still get EвИ•u(t)вИ•2 H = +вИЮfor all t > 0. More generally, the solution operator for (9.1) is not bounded on any L2,Q or (S)вИТѕБ,вИТќ≥. On the other hand, the following result holds. Theorem 9.4. In addition to Assumptions A1, A2, let u0 be an element of DвА≤(W; H), f, an element of DвА≤(W; L2((0, T), V вА≤)), and each gk, an element of DвА≤(W; L2((0, T), H)). Then the Wiener Chaos solution of equation (9.1) satisfies L M M N ќ±вИИJ вИ•uќ±(t)вИ•2 H ќ±! вЙ§C

    ќ±вИИJ 1 вИЪ ќ±!

    вИ•u0ќ±вИ•H + t 0 вИ•fќ±(s)вИ•2 V вА≤ds 1/2 + п£Ђ п£≠ kвЙ•1 t 0 вИ•gk,ќ±(s)вИ•2 Hds п£ґ п£Є 1/2 , (9.38) where C > 0 depends only on T and the numbers ќі, C1, and C2 from (9.2) and (9.3). Proof. To simplify the presentation, assume that f = gk = 0. For fixed ќ≥ вИИJ , denote by u(t; ѕХ; ќ≥) the Wiener Chaos solution of the equation (9.1) with initial condition u(0; ѕХ; ќ≥) = ѕХќЊќ≥. Denote by (0) the zero multi-index. The structure of the propagator implies the following relation: uќ±+ќ≥(t; ѕХ; ќ≥)

    (ќ± + ќ≥)!¶

Remark 9.2 Using Proposition 7.1 and the CauchyвАУSchwartz inequality, (9.38) can be rewritten in a slightly weaker form to reveal continuity of the solution operator for equation (9.1) from (S)вИТ1,ќ≥ to (S)вИТ1,0 for every ќ≥ > 1: вИ•u(t)вИ•2 (S)вИТ1,0(H) вЙ§C

    вИ•u0вИ•2 (S)вИТ1,ќ≥(H) + t 0 вИ•f(s)вИ•2 (S)вИТ1,ќ≥(V вА≤)ds +

    kвЙ•1 t 0 вИ•gk(s)вИ•2 (S)вИТ1,ќ≥(H)ds

    . 10 Probabilistic Representation of Wiener Chaos Solutions The general discussion so far has been dealing with the abstract evolution equation du = (Au + f)dt +

    kвЙ•1 (Mku + gk)dwk. By further specifying the operators A and Mk, as well as the input data u0, f, and gk, it is possible to get additional information about the Wiener Chaos solution of the equation. Definition 10.1 For r вИИR, the space L2,(r) = L2,(r)(Rd) is the collection of real-valued measurable functions such that f вИИL2,(r) if and only if

    Rd |f(x)|2(1 + |x|2)rdx < вИЮ. The space H1 2,(r) = H1 2,(r)(Rd) is the collection of real-valued measurable func- tions such that f вИИH1 2,(r) if and only if f and all the first-order generalized derivatives Dif of f belong to L2,(r). It is known, for example, from Theorem 3.4.7 in [42], that L2,(r) is a Hilbert space with the norm вИ•fвИ•2 0,(r) =

    Rd |f(x)|2(1 + |x|2)rdx,

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 481 and H1 2,(r) is a Hilbert space with the norm вИ•fвИ•1,(r) = вИ•fвИ•0,(r) + d

    i=1 вИ•DifвИ•0,(r). Denote by HвИТ1 2,(r) the dual of H1 2,(r) with respect to the inner product in L2,(r). Then (H1 2,(r), L2,(r), HвИТ1 2,(r)) is a normal triple of Hilbert spaces. Let F = (вД¶, F, {Ft}tвЙ•0, P) be a stochastic basis with the usual assump- tions and wk = wk(t), k вЙ•1, t вЙ•0, a collection of standard Wiener processes on F. Consider the linear equation du = (aijDiDju + biDiu + cu + f)dt + (ѕГikDiu + ќљku + gk)dwk (10.1) under the following assumptions: B0 All coefficients, free terms, and the initial condition are non-random. B1 The functions aij = aij(t, x) and their first-order derivatives with respect to x are uniformly bounded in (t, x), and the matrix (aij) is uniformly positive definite, that is, there exists ќі > 0 such that, for all vectors y вИИRd and all (t, x), aijyiyj вЙ•ќі|y|2. B2 The functions bi = bi(t, x), c = c(t, x), and ќљk = ќљk(t, x) are measurable and bounded in (t, x). B3 The functions ѕГik = ѕГik(t, x) are continuous and bounded in (t, x). B4 The functions f = f(t, x) and gk = gk(t, x) belong to L2((0, T); L2,(r)) for some r вИИR. B5 The initial condition u0 = u0(x) belongs to L2,(r). Under Assumptions B2вАУB4, there exists a sequence Q = {qk, k вЙ•1} of positive numbers with the following properties: P1 The matrix A with Aij = aijвИТ(1/2) kвЙ•1 qkѕГikѕГjk satisfies the inequality Aij(t, x)yiyj вЙ•0, x, y вИИRd, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T. P2 There exists a number C > 0 such that

    kвЙ•1

    sup t,x |qkќљk(t, x)|2 + T 0 вИ•qkgkвИ•p 0,(r)(t)dt

    вЙ§C. For the matrix A and each t, x, we have Aij(t, x) = ЋЬѕГik(t, x)ЋЬѕГjk(t, x), where the functions ЋЬѕГik are bounded. This representation might not be unique; see, for example, [7, Theorem III.2.2] or [44, Lemma 5.2.1]. Given any such representation of A, consider the following backward ItЋЖo equation

    482 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Xt,x,i (s) = xi + t s Bi (ѕД, Xt,x (ѕД)) dѕД +

    kвЙ•1 qkѕГik (ѕД, Xt,x (ѕД)) вЖРвИТвИТ dwk (ѕД) + t s ЋЬѕГik (ѕД, Xt,x (ѕД)) вЖРвИТ d ЋЬwk (ѕД) ; s вИИ(0, t), t вИИ(0, T], t вИТfixed, (10.2) where Bi = bi вИТ kвЙ•1 q2 kѕГikќљk and ЋЬwk, k вЙ•1, are independent standard Wiener processes on F that are independent of wk, k вЙ•1. This equation might not have a strong solution, but does have a weak, or martingale, solution due to Assumptions B1вАУB3 and properties P1 and P2 of the sequence Q; this weak solution is unique in the sense of probability law [44, Theorem 7.2.1]. The following result is a variation of Theorem 4.1 in [29]. Theorem 10.1. Under assumptions B0вАУB5 equation (10.1) has a unique w(H1 2,(r), HвИТ1 2,(r)) Wiener Chaos solution. If Q is a sequence with properties P1 and P2, then the solution of (10.1) belongs to L2,Q

    W; L2((0, T); H1 2,(r)) ? L2,Q

    W; C((0, T); L2,(r))

    and has the following representation: u(t, x) = QвИТ1E t 0 f(s, Xt,x(s))ќ≥(t, s, x)ds +

    kвЙ•1 t 0 qkgk(s, Xt,x(s))ќ≥(t, s, x)вЖРвИТвИТ dwk(s) + u0(Xt,x(0))ќ≥(t, 0, x) FW t

    , t вЙ§T, (10.3) where Xt,x(s) is a weak solution of (10.2), and ќ≥(t, s, x) = exp t s c(ѕД, Xt,x(ѕД))dѕД +

    kвЙ•1 t s qkќљk(ѕД, Xt,x(ѕД))вЖРвИТвИТ dwk(ѕД) вИТ1 2 t s

    kвЙ•1 q2 k|ќљk(ѕД, Xt,x(ѕД))|2dѕД

    . (10.4) Proof. It is enough to establish (10.3) when t = T. Consider the equation dU = (aijDiDjU +biDiU +cU +f)dt+

    kвЙ•1 (ѕГikDiU +ќљkU +gk)qkdwk (10.5) with initial condition U(0, x) = u0(x). Applying Theorem 2.1 in the normal triple (H1 2,(r), L2,(r), HвИТ1 2,(r)), we conclude that there is a unique solution

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 483 U вИИL2

    W; L2((0, T); H1 2,(r)) ? L2

    W; C((0, T); L2,(r))

    of this equation. By Proposition 7.2, the process u = QвИТ1U is the corre- sponding Wiener Chaos solution of (10.1). To establish representation (10.3), consider the S-transform Uh of U. According to Theorem 8.1, the function Uh is the unique w(H1 2,(r), HвИТ1 2,(r)) solution of the equation dUh = (aijDiDjUh + biDiUh + cUh + f)dt +

    kвЙ•1 (ѕГikDiUh + ќљkUh + gk)qkhkdt (10.6) with initial condition Uh|t=0 = u0. We also define Y (T, x) = T 0 f(s, XT,x(s))ќ≥(T, s, x)ds +

    kвЙ•1 T 0 gk(s, XT,x(s))ќ≥(T, s)qk вЖРвИТвИТ dwk(s) + u0(XT,x(0))ќ≥(T, 0, x). (10.7) By direct computation, E

    E

    E(h)Y (T, x)|FW T

    = E (E(h)Y (T, x)) = EвА≤Y (T, x), where EвА≤ is the expectation with respect to the measure dPвА≤ T = E(h)dPT and PT is the restriction of P to FW T . To proceed, let us first assume that the input data u0, f, and gk are all smooth functions with compact support. Then, applying the FeynmannвАУKac formula to the solution of equation (10.6) and using GirsanovвАЩs theorem (see, e.g., Theorems 3.5.1 and 5.7.6 in [15]), we conclude that Uh(T, x) = EвА≤Y (T, x) or E

    E(h)EY (t, x)|FW T

    = E (E (h) U(T, x)) . By Remark 7.1, the last equality implies U (T, ¬Ј) = E

    as elements of L2

    вД¶; L2,(r)(Rd)

    . To remove the additional smoothness assumption on the input data, let un 0, f n, and gn k be sequences of smooth compactly supported functions such that lim nвЖТвИЮ

    вИ•u0 вИТun 0вИ•2 L2,(r)(Rd) + T 0 вИ•f вИТf nвИ•2 L2,(r)(Rd)(t)dt +

    kвЙ•1 T 0 q2 kвИ•gk вИТgn k вИ•2 L2,(r)(Rd)(t)dt

    = 0. (10.8) Denote by U n and Y n the corresponding objects defined by (10.5) and (10.7) respectively. By Theorem 9.1, we have

    dPT . By GirsanovвАЩs theorem, equation (10.2) can be rewritten as XT,x,i (s) = xi + T s

    kвЙ•1 q2 kѕГikќљk) (ѕД, XT,x (ѕД)) dѕД + t s

    kвЙ•1 qkѕГik (ѕД, XT,x (ѕД)) вЖРвИТвИТ dwвА≤вА≤ k (ѕД) + t s ЋЬѕГik (ѕД, XT,x (ѕД)) вЖРвИТвИТ d ЋЬ wвА≤вА≤k (ѕД) , (10.11) where wвА≤вА≤ k and ЋЬ wвА≤вА≤k are independent Wiener processes with respect to the measure PвА≤вА≤ T .     Rd f(x)ѕХ(x)dx вЙ•0 for every non-negative ѕХ вИИCвИЮ 0 (Rd). Then Theorem 10.1 implies the following result. Corollary 10.1 In addition to Assumptions B0вАУB5, let u0 вЙ•0, f вЙ•0, and gk = 0 for all k вЙ•1. Then u вЙ•0. Proof. This follows from (10.3) and Proposition 7.3. Example 10.1 (KrylovвАУVeretennikov formula) Consider the equation du = (aijDiDju + biDiu) dt + d

    k=1 ѕГikDiudwk, u (0, x) = u0 (x) . (10.16)

    486 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Assume B0вАУB5 and suppose that aij(t, x) = 1 2ѕГik(t, x)ѕГjk(t, x). By Theorem 9.1, equation (10.16) has a unique Wiener chaos solution such that EвИ•uвИ•2 L2(Rd)(t) вЙ§CвИЧвИ•u0вИ•2 L2(Rd) and u (t, x) = вИЮ

    n=1

    |ќ±|=n uќ±(t, x)ќЊќ± = u0 (x) + вИЮ

    n=1 d

    k1,...,kn=1 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 ќ¶t,snѕГjknDj ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ќ¶s2,s1ѕГik1Diќ¶s1,0u0(x)dwk1(s1) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј dwkn(sn), (10.17) where ќ¶t,s is the semigroup generated by the operator A = aijDiDju+biDiu. On the other hand, in this case, Theorem 10.1 yields u(t, x) = E

    u0(Xt,x(0)) FW t

    , where W = (w1, ..., wd) and Xt,x,i (s) = xi + t s bi (ѕД, Xt,x (ѕД)) dѕД + d

    k=1 ѕГik (ѕД, Xt,x (ѕД)) вЖРвИТвИТ dwk (ѕД) , s вИИ(0, t), t вИИ(0, T], t вИТfixed. (10.18) Thus, we have arrived at the KrylovвАУVeretennikov formula [20, Theorem 4] E

    u0 (Xt,x (0)) |FW t

    = u0 (x) + вИЮ

    n=1 d

    k1,...,kn=1 t 0 sn 0 . . . s2 0 ќ¶t,snѕГjknDj ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј ќ¶s2,s1ѕГik1Diќ¶s1,0u0(x)dwk1(s1) ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј dwkn(sn). (10.19) 11 Wiener Chaos and Nonlinear Filtering In this section, we discuss some applications of the Wiener Chaos expansion to numerical solution of the nonlinear filtering problem for diffusion processes; the presentation is essentially based on [25]. Let (вД¶, F, P) be a complete probability space with independent standard Wiener processes W = W(t) and V = V (t) of dimensions d1 and r respec- tively. Let X0 be a random variable independent of W and V . In the diffu- sion filtering model, the unobserved d-dimensional state (or signal) process X = X(t) and the r-dimensional observation process Y = Y (t) are defined by the stochastic ordinary differential equations

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 487 dX(t) = b(X(t))dt + ѕГ(X(t))dW(t) + ѕБ(X(t))dV (t), dY (t) = h(X(t))dt + dV (t), 0 < t вЙ§T; X(0) = X0, Y (0) = 0, (11.1) where b(x) вИИRd, ѕГ(x) вИИRd√Чd1, ѕБ(x) вИИRd√Чr, h(x) вИИRr. Denote by Cn(Rd) the Banach space of bounded, n times continuously differentiable functions on Rd with finite norm вИ•fвИ•Cn(Rd) = sup xвИИRd |f(x)| + max 1вЙ§kвЙ§n sup xвИИRd |Dkf(x)|. Assumption R1. The components of the functions ѕГ and ѕБ are in C2(Rd), the components of the functions b are in C1(R), the components of the func- tion h are bounded measurable, and the random variable X0 has a density u0. Assumption R2. The matrix ѕГѕГвИЧis uniformly positive definite: there exists ќµ > 0 such that d

    i,j=1 d1

    k=1 ѕГik(x)ѕГjk(x)yiyj вЙ•ќµ|y|2, x, y вИИRd. Under Assumption R1 system (11.1) has a unique strong solution [15, Theorems 5.2.5 and 5.2.9]. Extra smoothness of the coefficients in assumption R1 insure the existence of a convenient representation of the optimal filter. If f is a scalar measurable function on Rd with suptвЙ§T E|f(X(t))|2 < вИЮ, then the filtering problem for (11.1) is to find the best mean square estimate ЋЖft of f(X(t)), t вЙ§T, given the observations Y (s), s вЙ§t. Denote by FY t the ѕГ-algebra generated by Y (s), s вЙ§t. Then the properties of the conditional expectation imply that the solution of the filtering problem is ЋЖft = E

    f(X(t))|FY t

    . To derive an alternative representation of ЋЖft, some additional constructions will be necessary. Define on (вД¶, F) the probability measure d*P = ZвИТ1 T dP where Zt = exp t 0 hвИЧ(X(s))dY (s) вИТ1 2 t 0 |h(X(s))|2ds

    (here and below, if ќґ вИИRk, then ќґ is a column vector, ќґвИЧ= (ќґ1, . . . , ќґk), and |ќґ|2 = ќґвИЧќґ). If the function h is bounded, then the measures P and P are equivalent. The expectation with respect to the measure P will be denoted by E. The following properties of the measure P are well known [14, 42]:

    488 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii P1. Under the measure P, the distributions of the Wiener process W and the random variable X0 are unchanged, the observation process Y is a standard Wiener process, and, for t вЙ§T, the state process X satisfies: dX(t) = b(X(t))dt + ѕГ(X(t))dW(t) + ѕБ(X(t)) (dY (t) вИТh(X(t))dt) , X(0) = X0. P2. Under the measure P, the Wiener processes W and Y and the random variable X0 are independent of one another. P3. The optimal filter ЋЖft satisfies the relation ЋЖft = *E

    f(X(t))Zt|FY t

    E[Zt|FY t ] . (11.2) Because of property P2 of the measure P the filtering problem will be studied on the probability space (вД¶, F, P). In particular, we will consider the stochastic basis F = {вД¶, F, {FY t }0вЙ§tвЙ§T , P} and the Wiener Chaos space L2(Y) of FY T -measurable random variables ќЈ with E|ќЈ|2 < вИЮ. If the function h is bounded, then, by the CauchyвАУSchwarz inequality, E|ќЈ| вЙ§C(h, T) H E|ќЈ|2, ќЈ вИИ*L2(Y). (11.3) Next, consider the partial differential operators Lg(x) = 1 2 d

    i,j=1 ((ѕГ(x)ѕГвИЧ(x))ij + (ѕБ(x)ѕБвИЧ(x))ij) вИВ2g(x) вИВxiвИВxj + d

    i=1 bi(x)вИВg(x) вИВxi ; Mlg(x) = hl(x)g(x) + d

    i=1 ѕБil(x)вИВg(x) вИВxi , l = 1, . . . , r; and their adjoints LвИЧg(x) = 1 2 d

    i,j=1 вИВ2 вИВxiвИВxj ((ѕГ(x)ѕГвИЧ(x))ijg(x) + (ѕБ(x)ѕБвИЧ(x))ijg(x)) вИТ d

    i=1 вИВ вИВxi (bi(x)g(x)) ; MвИЧ l g(x) = hl(x)g(x) вИТ d

    i=1 вИВ вИВxi (ѕБil(x)g(x)) , l = 1, . . . , r. Note that, under the assumptions R1 and R2, the operators L, LвИЧare bounded from H1 2(Rd) to HвИТ1 2 (Rd), operators M, MвИЧare bounded from H1 2(Rd) to L2(Rd), and

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 489 2вЯ®LвИЧv, vвЯ©+ r

    l=1 вИ•MвИЧ l vвИ•2 L2(Rd)+ќµвИ•vвИ•2 H2 1(Rd) вЙ§CвИ•vвИ•2 L2(Rd), v вИИH1 2(Rd), (11.4) where вЯ®¬Ј, ¬ЈвЯ©is the duality between H1 2(Rd) and HвИТ1 2 (Rd). The following result is well known [42, Theorem 6.2.1]. Proposition 11.1 In addition to Assumptions R1 and R1 suppose that the initial density u0 belongs to L2(Rd). Then there is a random field u = u(t, x), t вИИ[0, T], x вИИRd, with the following properties: 1. u вИИL2(Y; L2((0, T); H1 2(Rd))) вИ©L2(Y; C([0, T], L2(Rd))). 2. The function u(t, x) is a traditional solution of the stochastic partial differential equation du(t, x) = LвИЧu(t, x)dt + r

    l=1 MвИЧ l u(t, x)dYl(t), 0 < t вЙ§T, x вИИRd; u(0, x) = u0(x). (11.5) 3. The equality *E

    f(X(t))Zt|FY t

    =

    Rd f(x)u(t, x)dx (11.6) holds for all bounded measurable functions f. The random field u = u(t, x) is called the unnormalized filtering density (UFD) and the random variable ѕХt[f] = *E

    f(X(t))Zt|FY t

    , the unnormalized optimal filter. A number of authors studied the nonlinear filtering problem using the multiple ItЋЖo integral version of the Wiener Chaos [2, 21, 39, 46, etc.]. In what follows, we construct approximations of u and ѕХt[f] using the CameronвАУ Martin version. By Theorem 8.3, u(t, x) =

    ќ±вИИJ uќ±(t, x)ќЊќ±, (11.7) where ќЊќ± = 1 вИЪ ќ±! ( i,k Hќ±k i (ќЊik), ќЊik = T 0 mi(t)dYk(t), k = 1, . . . , r; (11.8) as before, Hn(¬Ј) is the Hermite polynomial (3.3) and mi вИИm, an orthonormal basis in L2((0, T)). The functions uќ± satisfy the corresponding propagator вИВ вИВtuќ±(t, x) = LвИЧuќ±(t, x) +

    k,i H ќ±k i MвИЧ kuќ±вИТ(i,k)(t, x)mi(t), t вЙ§T, x вИИRd; u(0, x) = u0(x)I(|ќ±| = 0). (11.9)

    490 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Writing fќ±(t) =

    Rd f(x)uќ±(t, x)dx, we also get a Wiener chaos expansion for the unnormalized optimal filter: ѕХt[f] =

    ќ±вИИJ fќ±(t)ќЊќ±, t вИИ[0, T]. (11.10) For a positive integer N, define uN(t, x) =

    |ќ±|вЙ§N uќ±(t, x)ќЊќ±. (11.11) Theorem 11.1. Under Assumptions R1 and R2, there exists a positive num- ber ќљ, depending only on the functions h and ѕБ, such that EвИ•u вИТuNвИ•2 L2(Rd)(t) вЙ§ вИ•u0вИ•2 L2(Rd) ќљ(1 + ќљ)N , t вИИ[0, T]. (11.12) If, in addition, ѕБ = 0, then there exists a real number C, depending only on the functions b and ѕГ, such that EвИ•u вИТuNвИ•2 L2(Rd)(t) вЙ§(4hвИЮt)N+1 (N + 1)! eCtвИ•u0вИ•2 L2(Rd), t вИИ[0, T], (11.13) where hвИЮ= maxk=1,...,r supx |hk(x)|. For positive integers N, n, define a set of multi-indices J n N = {ќ± = (ќ±k i , k = 1, . . . , r, i = 1, . . . , n) : |ќ±| вЙ§N}. and let un N(t, x) =

    ќ±вИИJ n N uќ±(t, x)ќЊќ±. (11.14) Unlike Theorem 11.1, to compute the approximation error in this case we need to choose a special basis m вАФ to do the error analysis for the Fourier approximation in time. We also need extra regularity of the coefficients in the state and observation equations вАФ to have the semi-group generated by the operator LвИЧcontinuous not only in L2(Rd) but also in H2 2(Rd). The resulting error bound is presented below; the proof can be found in [25]. Theorem 11.2. Assume that 1. The basis m is the Fourier cosine basis m1(t)= 1 вИЪ T ; mk(t)=

    2 T cos ѕА(k вИТ1)t T

    , k > 1; t вЙ§T, (11.15)

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 491 2. The components of the functions ѕГ are in C4(Rd), the components of the functions b are in C3(R), the components of the function h are in C2(Rd); ѕБ = 0; u0 вИИH2 2(Rd). Then there exist a positive number B1 and a real number B2, both depending only on the functions b and ѕГ such that *EвИ•uвИТun NвИ•2 L2(Rd)(T)вЙ§B1eB2T (4hвИЮT)N+1 (N + 1)! eCtвИ•u0вИ•2 L2(Rd) + T 3 n вИ•u0вИ•2 H2 2(Rd)

    , (11.16) where hвИЮ= maxk=1,...,r supx |hk(x)|. 12 Passive Scalar in a Gaussian Field This section presents the results from [29] and [28] about the stochastic trans- port equation. The following viscous transport equation is used to describe the time evo- lution of a scalar quantity ќЄ in a given velocity field v: ЋЩќЄ(t, x) = ќљвИЖќЄ(t, x) вИТv(t, x) ¬Ј вИЗќЄ(t, x) + f(t, x); x вИИRd, d > 1. (12.1) The scalar ќЄ is called passive because it does not affect the velocity field v. We assume that v = v(t, x) вИИRd is an isotropic Gaussian vector field with zero mean and covariance E(vi(t, x)vj(s, y)) = ќі(t вИТs)Cij(x вИТy), where C = (Cij(x), i, j = 1, . . . , d) is a matrix-valued function such that C(0) is a scalar matrix; with no loss of generality we will assume that C(0) = I, the identity matrix. It is known from [22, Section 10.1] that, for an isotropic Gaussian vector field, the Fourier transform ЋЖC = ЋЖC(z) of the function C = C(x) is ЋЖC(y) = A0 (1 + |y|2)(d+ќ±)/2

    ayyвИЧ |y|2 + b d вИТ1

    I вИТyyT |y|2

    , (12.2) where yвИЧis the row vector (y1, . . . , yd), y is the corresponding column vector, |y|2 = yвИЧy; ќ≥ > 0, a вЙ•0, b вЙ•0, A0 > 0 are real numbers. Similar to [22], we assume that 0 < ќ≥ < 2. This range of values of ќ≥ corresponds to a turbulent velocity field v, also known as the generalized Kraichnan model [8]; the original Kraichnan model [18] corresponds to a = 0. For small x, the asymptotics of Cij(x) is (ќіij вИТcij|x|ќ≥) [22, Section 10.2]. By direct computation (cf. [1]), the vector field v = (v1, . . . , vd) can be written as vi(t, x) = ѕГi k(x) ЋЩwk(t), (12.3)

    492 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii where {ѕГk, k вЙ•1} is an orthonormal basis in the space HC, the reproducing kernel Hilbert space corresponding to the kernel function C. It is known from [22] that HC is all or a part of the Sobolev space H(d+ќ≥)/2(Rd; Rd). If a > 0 and b > 0, then the matrix ЋЖC is invertible and HC =

    f вИИRd :

    Rd ЋЖf вИЧ(y) ЋЖCвИТ1(y) ЋЖf(y)dy < вИЮ

    = H(d+ќ≥)/2(Rd; Rd), because вИ•ЋЖC(y)вИ•вИЉ(1 + |y|2)вИТ(d+ќ≥)/2. If a > 0 and b = 0, then HC =

    f вИИRd :

    Rd | ЋЖf(y)|2(1 + |y|2)(d+ќ≥)/2dy < вИЮ; yyвИЧЋЖf(y) = |y|2 ЋЖf(y)

    , the subset of gradient fields in H(d+ќ≥)/2(Rd; Rd), that is, the vector fields f for which ЋЖf(y) = y ЋЖF(y) for some scalar F вИИH(d+ќ≥+2)/2(Rd). If a = 0 and b > 0, then HC =

    f вИИRd :

    Rd | ЋЖf(y)|2(1 + |y|2)(d+ќ≥)/2dy < вИЮ; yвИЧЋЖf(y) = 0

    , the subset of divergence-free fields in H(d+ќ≥)/2(Rd; Rd). By the embedding theorems, each ѕГi k is a bounded continuous function on Rd; in fact, every ѕГi k is H¬®older continuous of order ќ≥/2. In addition, being an element of the corresponding space HC, each ѕГk is a gradient field if b = 0 and is divergence-free if a = 0. Equation (12.1) becomes dќЄ(t, x) = (ќљвИЖќЄ(t, x) + f(t, x))dt вИТ

    k ѕГk(x) ¬Ј вИЗќЄ(t, x)dwk(t). (12.4) We summarize the above constructions in the following assumptions: S1 There is a fixed stochastic basis F = (вД¶, F, {Ft}tвЙ•0, P) with the usual as- sumptions and (wk(t), k вЙ•1, t вЙ•0) is a collection of independent standard Wiener processes on F. S2 For each k, the vector field ѕГk is an element of the Sobolev space H(d+ќ≥)/2 2 (Rd; Rd), 0 < ќ≥ < 2, d вЙ•2. S3 For all x, y in Rd, k ѕГi k(x)ѕГj k(y) = Cij(xвИТy) such that the matrix-valued function C = C(x) satisfies (12.2) and C(0) = I. S4 The input data ќЄ0, f are deterministic and satisfy ќЄ0 вИИL2(Rd), f вИИL2((0, T); HвИТ1 2 (Rd)); ќљ > 0 is a real number.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 493 Theorem 12.1. Let Q be a sequence with qk = q < вИЪ 2ќљ, k вЙ•1. Under assumptions S1вАУS4, there exits a unique w(H1 2(Rd), HвИТ1 2 (Rd)) Wiener Chaos solution of (12.4). This solution is an FW t -adapted process and satisfies the inequality вИ•ќЄвИ•2 L2,Q(W;L2((0,T );H1 2(Rd))) + вИ•ќЄвИ•2 L2,Q(W;C((0,T );L2(Rd))) вЙ§C(ќљ, q, T)

    вИ•ќЄ0вИ•2 L2(Rd) + вИ•fвИ•2 L2((0,T );HвИТ1 2 (Rd))

    . Theorem 12.1 provides new information about the solution of equation (12.1) for all values of ќљ > 0. Indeed, if вИЪ 2ќљ > 1, then q > 1 is an admissible choice of the weights, and, by Proposition 7.2(1), the solution ќЄ has Malliavin derivatives of every order. If вИЪ 2ќљ вЙ§1, then equation (12.4) does not have a square-integrable solution. Note that if the weight is chosen such that q = вИЪ 2ќљ, then equa- tion (12.1) can still be analyzed using Theorem 9.1 in the normal triple (H1 2(Rd), L2(Rd), HвИТ1 2 (Rd)). If ќљ = 0, equation (12.4) must be interpreted in the sense of Stratonovich: du(t, x) = f(t, x)dt вИТѕГk(x) ¬Ј вИЗќЄ(t, x) вЧ¶dwk(t). (12.5) To simplify the presentation, we assume that f = 0. If (12.2) holds with a = 0, then each ѕГk is divergence free and (12.5) has an equivalent ItЋЖo form dќЄ(t, x) = 1 2вИЖќЄ(t, x)dt вИТѕГi k(x)DiќЄ(t, x)dwk(t). (12.6) Equation (12.6) is a model of non-viscous turbulent transport [5]. The prop- agator for (12.6) is вИВ вИВtќЄќ±(t, x) = 1 2вИЖќЄќ±(t, x) вИТ

    i,k H ќ±k i ѕГj kDjќЄќ±вИТ(i,k)(t, x)mi(t), t вЙ§T, (12.7) with initial condition ќЄќ±(0, x) = ќЄ0(x)I(|ќ±| = 0). The following result about solvability of (12.6) is proved in [29] and, in a slightly weaker form, in [28]. Theorem 12.2. In addition to S1вАУS4, assume that each ѕГk is divergence free. Then there exits a unique w(H1 2(Rd), HвИТ1 2 (Rd)) Wiener Chaos solution ќЄ = ќЄ(t, x) of (12.6). This solution has the following properties: (A) For every ѕХ вИИCвИЮ 0 (Rd) and all t вИИ[0, T], the equality (ќЄ, ѕХ)(t) = (ќЄ0, ѕХ) + 1 2 t 0 (ќЄ, вИЖѕХ)(s)ds + t 0 (ќЄ, ѕГi kDiѕХ)dwk(s) (12.8) holds in L2(FW t ), where (¬Ј, ¬Ј) is the inner product in L2(Rd). (B) If X = Xt,x is a weak solution of the equation

    494 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii Xt,x = x + t 0 ѕГk (Xs,x) dwk (s) , (12.9) then, for each t вИИ[0, T], ќЄ (t, x) = E

    ќЄ0 (Xt,x) |FW t

    . (12.10) (C) For 1 вЙ§p < вИЮand r вИИR, define Lp,(r)(Rd) as the Banach space of measurable functions with the norm вИ•fвИ•p Lp,(r)(Rd) =

    Rd |f(x)|p(1 + |x|2)pr/2dx < вИЮ. Then there exits a number K depending only on p, r such that, for each t > 0, EвИ•ќЄвИ•p Lp,(r)(Rd)(t) вЙ§eKtвИ•ќЄ0вИ•p Lp,(r)(Rd). (12.11) In particular, if r = 0, then K = 0. It follows that, for all s, t and almost all x, y, EќЄ (t, x) = ќЄќ± (t, x) I|ќ±|=0, EќЄ (t, x) ќЄ (s, y) =

    ќ±вИИJ ќЄќ± (t, x) ќЄќ± (s, y) . If the initial condition ќЄ0 belongs to L2(Rd) вИ©Lp(Rd) for p вЙ•3, then, by (12.11), higher-order moments of ќЄ exist. To obtain the expressions of the higher-order moments in terms of the coefficients ќЄќ±, we need some auxiliary constructions. For ќ±, ќ≤ вИИJ , define ќ± + ќ≤ as the multi-index with components ќ±k i + ќ≤k i . Similarly, we define the multi-indices |ќ± вИТќ≤| and ќ± вИІќ≤ = min(ќ±, ќ≤). We write ќ≤ вЙ§ќ± if and only if ќ≤k i вЙ§ќ±k i for all i, k вЙ•1. If ќ≤ вЙ§ќ±, we define ќ± ќ≤

    := ( i,k ќ±k i ! ќ≤k i !(ќ±k i вИТќ≤k i )!. Definition 12.1 We say that a triple of multi-indices (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) is complete and write (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) вИИвЦ≥if all the entries of the multi-index ќ± + ќ≤ + ќ≥ are even numbers and |ќ± вИТќ≤| вЙ§ќ≥ вЙ§ќ± + ќ≤. For fixed ќ±, ќ≤ вИИJ , we write вЦ≥(ќ±) := {ќ≥, ¬µ вИИJ : (ќ±, ќ≥, ¬µ) вИИвЦ≥} and вЦ≥(ќ±, ќ≤) := {ќ≥ вИИJ : (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) вИИвЦ≥}.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 495 For (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) вИИвЦ≥, we define ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) :=

    ќ±!ќ≤!ќ≥! ќ± вИТќ≤ + ќ≥ 2

    ! ќ≤ вИТќ± + ќ≥ 2

    ! ќ± + ќ≤ вИТќ≥ 2

    ! вИТ1 . (12.12) Note that the triple (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) is complete if and only if any permutation of the triple (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) is complete. Similarly, the value of ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) is invariant under permutation of the arguments. We also define C (ќ≥, ќ≤, ¬µ) := ќ≥ + ќ≤ вИТ2¬µ ќ≥ вИТ¬µ ќ≥ ¬µ ќ≤ ¬µ 1/2 , ¬µ вЙ§ќ≥ вИІќ≤. (12.13) It is readily checked that if f is a function on J , then for ќ≥, ќ≤ вИИJ ,

    ¬µвЙ§ќ≥вИІќ≤ C (ќ≥, ќ≤, p) f (ќ≥ + ќ≤ вИТ2¬µ) =

    ¬µвИИ(ќ≥,ќ≤) f (¬µ) ќ¶ (ќ≥, ќ≤, ¬µ) (12.14) The next theorem presents the formulas for the third and fourth moments of the solution of equation (12.6) in terms of the coefficients ќЄќ±. Theorem 12.3. In addition to S1вАУS4, assume that each ѕГk is divergence-free and the initial condition ќЄ0 belongs to L2(Rd) вИ©L4(Rd). Then EќЄ(t, x)ќЄ (tвА≤, xвА≤) ќЄ(s, y) =

    (ќ±,ќ≤,ќ≥)вИИвЦ≥ ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) ќЄќ±(t, x)ќЄќ≤(tвА≤, xвА≤)ќЄќ≥ (s, y) (12.15) and EќЄ(t, x)ќЄ(tвА≤, xвА≤)ќЄ (s, y) ќЄ (sвА≤, yвА≤) (12.16) =

    ѕБвИИвЦ≥(ќ±,ќ≤)вИ©вЦ≥(ќ≥,ќЇ) ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ѕБ) ќ® (ѕБ, ќ≥, ќЇ) ќЄќ± (t, x) ќЄќ≤(tвА≤, xвА≤)ќЄќ≥ (s, y) ќЄќЇ (sвА≤, yвА≤) . Proof. It is known, [30], that ќЊќ≥ќЊќ≤ =

    ¬µвЙ§ќ≥вИІќ≤ C (ќ≥, ќ≤, ¬µ) ќЊќ≥+ќ≤вИТ2¬µ. (12.17) Let us consider the triple product ќЊќ±ќЊќ≤ќЊќ≥. By (12.17), EќЊќ±ќЊќ≤ќЊќ≥ = E

    ¬µвИИвЦ≥(ќ±,ќ≤) ќЊќ≥ќЊ¬µќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ¬µ) =

    ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) , (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) вИИвЦ≥; 0, otherwise. (12.18) Equality (12.15) now follows. To compute the fourth moment, note that

    496 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii ќЊќ±ќЊќ≤ќЊќ≥ =

    ¬µвЙ§ќ±вИІќ≤ C (ќ±, ќ≤, ¬µ) ќЊќ±+ќ≤вИТ2¬µќЊќ≥ =

    ¬µвЙ§ќ±вИІќ≤ C (ќ±, ќ≤, ¬µ)

    ѕБвЙ§(ќ±+ќ≤вИТ2¬µ)вИІќ≥ C (ќ± + ќ≤ вИТ2¬µ, ќ≥, ѕБ) ќЊќ±+ќ≤+ќ≥вИТ2¬µвИТ2ѕБ. (12.19) Repeated applications of (12.14) yield ќЊќ±ќЊќ≤ќЊќ≥ =

    ¬µвЙ§ќ±вИІќ≤ C (ќ±, ќ≤, ¬µ)

    ѕБвИИвЦ≥(ќ±+ќ≤вИТ2¬µ,ќ≥) ќЊѕБќ® (ќ± + ќ≤ вИТ2¬µ, ќ≥, ѕБ) =

    ¬µвИИвЦ≥(ќ±,ќ≤)

    ѕБвИИвЦ≥(¬µ,ќ≥) ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ¬µ) ќ® (¬µ, ќ≥, ѕБ) ќЊѕБ Thus, EќЊќ±ќЊќ≤ќЊќ≥ќЊќЇ =

    ¬µвИИвЦ≥(ќ±,ќ≤)

    ѕБвИИвЦ≥(¬µ,ќ≥) ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ¬µ) ќ® (¬µ, ќ≥, ѕБ) I{¬µ=ќЇ} =

    ѕБвИИвЦ≥(ќ±,ќ≤)вИ©вЦ≥(ќ≥,ќЇ) ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ѕБ) ќ® (ѕБ, ќ≥, ќЇ) . Equality (12.16) now follows. In the same way, one can get formulas for fifth- and higher-order moments. Remark 12.1 Expressions (12.15) and (12.16) do not depend on the structure of equation (12.6) and can be used to compute the third and fourth moments of any random field with a known CameronвАУMartin expansion. The interested reader should keep in mind that the formulas for the moments of orders higher then two should be interpreted with care. In fact, they represent the pseudo- moments (for detail see [35]). We now return to the analysis of the passive scalar equation (12.4). By reducing the smoothness assumptions on ѕГk, it is possible to consider velocity fields v that are more turbulent than in the Kraichnan model, for example, vi(t, x) =

    kвЙ•0 ѕГi k(x) ЋЩwk(t), (12.20) where {ѕГk, k вЙ•1} is an orthonormal basis in L2(Rd; Rd). With v as in (12.20), the passive scalar equation (12.4) becomes ЋЩќЄ(t, x) = ќљвИЖќЄ(t, x) + f(t, x) вИТвИЗќЄ(t, x) ¬Ј ЋЩW(t, x), (12.21) where ЋЩW = ЋЩW(t, x) is a d-dimensional space-time white noise and the ItЋЖo sto- chastic differential is used. Previously, such equations have been studied using white noise approach in the space of Hida distributions [4, 40]. A summary of the related results can be found in [12, Section 4.3].

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 497 The Q-weighted Wiener chaos spaces allow us to state a result that is fully analogous to Theorem 12.1. The proof is derived from Theorem 9.1; see [29] for details. Theorem 12.4. Suppose that ќљ > 0 is a real number, each |ѕГi k(x)| is a bounded measurable function, and the input data are deterministic and satisfy u0 вИИL2(Rd), f вИИL2

    (0, T); HвИТ1 2 (Rd)

    . Fix ќµ > 0 and let Q = {qk, k вЙ•1} be a sequence so that, for all x, y вИИRd, 2ќљ|y|2 вИТ

    kвЙ•1 q2 kѕГi k(x)ѕГj k(x)yiyj вЙ•ќµ|y|2. Then, for every T > 0, there exits a unique w(H1 2(Rd), HвИТ1 2 (Rd)) Wiener Chaos solution ќЄ of the equation dќЄ(t, x) = (ќљвИЖќЄ(t, x) + f(t, x))dt вИТѕГk(x) ¬Ј вИЗќЄ(t, x)dwk(t), (12.22) The solution is an Ft-adapted process and satisfies the ineq21uality вИ•ќЄвИ•2 L2,Q(W;L2((0,T );H1 2(Rd))) + вИ•ќЄвИ•2 L2,Q(W;C((0,T );L2(Rd))) вЙ§C(ќљ, q, T)

    вИ•ќЄ0вИ•2 L2(Rd) + вИ•fвИ•2 L2((0,T );HвИТ1 2 (Rd))

    . If maxi supx |ѕГi k(x)| вЙ§Ck, k вЙ•1, then a possible choice of Q is qk = (ќіќљ)1/2/(d2kCk), 0 < ќі < 2. If ѕГi k(x)ѕГj k(x) вЙ§CѕГ < вИЮ, i, j = 1, . . . , d, x вИИRd, then a possible choice of Q is qk = ќµ (2ќљ/(CѕГd))1/2 , 0 < ќµ < 1. 13 Stochastic NavierвАУStokes Equation In this section, we review the main facts about the stochastic NavierвАУStokes equation and indicate how the Wiener Chaos approach can be used in the study of non-linear equations. Most of the results of this section come from the two papers [35] and [31]. A priori, it is not clear in what sense the motion described by KraichnanвАЩs velocity (see Section 12) might fit into the paradigm of Newtonian mechanics. Accordingly, relating the Kraichnan velocity field v to classic fluid mechanics naturally leads to the question whether we can compensate v (t, x) by a field u (t, x) that is more regular with respect to the time variable, so that there is a balance of momentum for the resulting field U (t, x) = u (t, x) + v (t, x) or, equivalently, that the motion of a fluid particle in the velocity field U (t, x) satisfies the Second Law of Newton.

    498 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii A positive answer to this question is given in [35], where it is shown that the equation for the smooth component u = (u1, . . . , ud) of the velocity is given by п£± п£і п£і п£і п£і п£і п£≤ п£і п£і п£і п£і п£і п£≥ dui = [ќљвИЖui вИТujDjui вИТDiP + fi]dt +

    gi k вИТDi ЋЬPk вИТDjѕГj kui dwk, i = 1, . . . , d, 0 < t вЙ§T; div u = 0, u(0, x) = u0(x). (13.1) where wk, k вЙ•1 are independent standard Wiener processes on a sto- chastic basis F, the functions ѕГj k are given by (12.3), the known functions f = (f 1, . . . , f d), gk = (gi k), i = i, . . . , d, k вЙ•1, are, respectively, the drift and the diffusion components of the free force, and the unknown functions P, ЋЬPk are the drift and diffusion components of the pressure. Remark 13.1 It is useful to study equation (13.1) for more general coefficients ѕГj k. So, in the future, ѕГj k are not necessarily the same as in Section 12. We make the following assumptions: NS1 The functions ѕГi k = ѕГi k(t, x) are deterministic and measurable,

    kвЙ•1 d

    i=1 |ѕГi k(t, x)|2 + |DiѕГi k(t, x)|2

    вЙ§K, and there exists ќµ > 0 such that, for all y вИИRd, ќљ|y|2 вИТ1 2ѕГi k(t, x)ѕГj k(t, x)yiyj вЙ•ќµ|y|2, t вИИ[0, T], x вИИRd. NS2 The functions f i, gi k are non-random and d

    i=1 п£Ђ п£≠вИ•f iвИ•2 L2((0,T );HвИТ1 2 (Rd)) +

    kвЙ•1 вИ•gi kвИ•2 L2((0,T );L2(Rd)) п£ґ п£Є< вИЮ. Remark 13.2 In NS1, the derivatives DiѕГi k are understood as Schwartz dis- tributions, but it is assumed that div ѕГ := d i=1 вИВiѕГi is a bounded L2-valued function. Obviously, the latter assumption holds in the important case when d i=1 вИВiѕГi = 0. Our next step is to use the divergence-free property of u to eliminate the pressure P and ЋЬP from equation (13.1). For that, we need the decomposition of L2(Rd; Rd) into potential and solenoidal components. Write S(L2(Rd; Rd)) = {V вИИL2(Rd; Rd) : div V = 0}. It is known (see e.g. [16]) that

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 499 L2(Rd; Rd) = G(L2(Rd; Rd)) вКХS(L2(Rd; Rd)), where G(L2(Rd; Rd)) is a Hilbert subspace orthogonal to S(L2(Rd; Rd)). The functions G(V) and S(V) can be defined for V from any Sobolev space Hќ≥ 2 (Rd; Rd) and are usually referred to as the potential and the divergence-free or solenoidal projections, respectively, of the vector field V. Now let u be a solution of equation (13.1). Since div u = 0, we have Di(ќљвИЖui вИТujDjui вИТDiP + f i) = 0; Di(ѕГj kDjujui + gi k вИТDi ЋЬPk) = 0, k вЙ•1. As a result, DiP = G(ќљвИЖui вИТujDjui +f i); Di ЋЬPk = G(ѕГj kDjui +gi k), i = 1, . . . , d, k вЙ•1. So, instead of equation (13.1), we can and will consider its equivalent form for the unknown vector u = (u1, . . . , ud): du = S(ќљвИЖu вИТujDju + f)dt + S(ѕГj kDju + gk)dwk, 0 < t вЙ§T, (13.2) with initial condition u|t=0 = u0. Definition 13.1 An Ft-adapted random process u вИИL2(вД¶√Ч[0, T]; H1 2(Rd; Rd)) is called a solution of equation (13.2) if: 1. With probability one, the process u is weakly continuous in L2(Rd; Rd). 2. For every ѕХ вИИCвИЮ 0 (Rd, Rd) with div ѕХ = 0 there exists a measurable set вД¶вА≤ вКВвД¶such that, for all t вИИ[0, T], the equality (ui, ѕХi)(t) = (ui 0, ѕХi) + t 0

    (ќљDjui, DjѕХi)(s) + вЯ®f i, ѕХiвЯ©(s)

    ds t 0

    ѕГj kDjui + gi, ѕХi)dwk(s) (13.3) holds on вД¶вА≤. In (13.3), (¬Ј, ¬Ј) is the inner product in L2(Rd) and вЯ®¬Ј, ¬Ј, вЯ©is the duality between H1 2(Rd) and HвИТ1 2 (Rd). The following existence and uniqueness result is proved in [31]. Theorem 13.1. In addition to NS1 and NS2, assume that the initial con- dition u0 is non-random and belongs to L2(Rd; Rd). Then there exist a sto- chastic basis F = (вД¶, F, {Ft}tвЙ•0, P) with the usual assumptions, a collection {wk, k вЙ•1} of independent standard Wiener processes on F, and a process u such that u is a solution of (13.2) and E

    sup sвЙ§T вИ•u(s)вИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd) + T 0 вИ•вИЗu(s)вИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd) ds

    < вИЮ. If, in addition, d = 2, then the solution of (13.2) exists on any prescribed stochastic basis, is strongly continuous in t, is FW t -adapted, and is unique, both path-wise and in distribution.

    500 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii When d вЙ•3, the existence of a strong solution as well as uniqueness (strong or weak) for equation (13.2) are important open problems. By the CameronвАУMartin theorem, u(t, x) =

    ќ±вИИJ uќ±(t, x)ќЊќ±. If the solution of (13.2) is FW t -adapted, then, using the ItЋЖo formula together with relation (5.5) for the time evolution of E(ќЊќ±|FW t ) and relation (12.17) for the product of two elements of the CameronвАУMartin basis, we can derive the propagator system for coefficients uќ± [31, Theorem 3.2]: Theorem 13.2. In addition to NS1 and NS2, assume that u0 вИИL2(Rd; Rd) and equation (13.2) has an FW t -adapted solution u such that sup tвЙ§T EвИ•uвИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd)(t) < вИЮ. (13.4) Then u (t, x) =

    ќ±вИИJ uќ± (t, x) ќЊќ±, (13.5) and the HermiteвАУFourier coefficients uќ±(t, x) are L2(Rd; Rd)-valued weakly continuous functions such that sup tвЙ§T

    ќ±вИИJ вИ•uќ±вИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd)(t) + T 0

    ќ±вИИJ вИ•вИЗuќ±вИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd√Чd)(t) dt < вИЮ. (13.6) The functions uќ± (t, x) , ќ± вИИJ , satisfy the (nonlinear) propagator вИВ вИВtuќ± = S

    вИЖuќ± вИТ

    ќ≥,ќ≤вИИвИЖ(ќ±) ќ® (ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) (uќ≥, вИЗuќ≤) + I{|ќ±|=0}f +

    j,k H ќ±k j

    ѕГk, вИЗ

    uќ±вИТ(j,k) + I{|ќ±|=1}gk mj (t)

    , 0 < t вЙ§T; uќ±|t=0 = u0I{|ќ±|=0}; (13.7) recall that the numbers ќ®(ќ±, ќ≤, ќ≥) are defined in (12.12). One of the questions in the theory of the NavierвАУStokes equation is com- putation of the mean value ¬ѓu = Eu of the solution. The traditional approach relies on the Reynolds equation for the mean вИВt¬ѓu вИТќљвИЖ¬ѓu + ( u, вИЗ) u = 0, (13.8) which is not really an equation with respect to ¬ѓu. Decoupling (13.8) has been an area of active research: Reynolds approximations, coupled equations for the

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 501 moments, Gaussian closures, and so on (see, e.g., [36], [45] and the references therein) Another way to compute ¬ѓu (t, x) is to find the distribution of v (t, x) us- ing the infinite-dimensional Kolmogorov equation associated with (13.2). The complexity of this Kolmogorov equation is prohibitive for any realistic appli- cation, at least for now. The propagator provides a third way: expressing the mean and other sta- tistical moments of u in terms of uќ±. Indeed, by CameronвАУMartin Theorem, Eu(t, x) = u0(t, x), Eui(t, x)u j (s, y) =

    ќ±вИИJ ui ќ±(t, x)uj ќ±(s, y). If exist, the third- and fourth-order moments can be computed using (12.15) and (12.16). The next theorem, proved in [31], shows that the existence of a solution of the propagator (13.7) is not only necessary but, to some extent, sufficient for the global existence of a probabilistically strong solution of the stochastic NavierвАУStokes equation (13.2). Theorem 13.3. Let NS1 and NS2 hold and u0 вИИL2(Rd; Rd). Assume that the propagator (13.7) has a solution {uќ± (t, x) , ќ± вИИJ } on the interval (0, T] such that, for every ќ±, the process uќ± is weakly continuous in L2(Rd; Rd) and the inequality sup tвЙ§T

    ќ±вИИJ вИ•uќ±вИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd)(t) + T 0

    ќ±вИИJ вИ•вИЗuќ±вИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd√Чd)(t) dt < вИЮ (13.9) holds. If the process ¬ѓU (t, x) :=

    ќ±вИИJ uќ± (t, x) ќЊќ± (13.10) is FW t -adapted, then it is a solution of (13.2). The process ¬ѓU satisfies E

    sup sвЙ§T вИ•¬ѓU(s)вИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd) + T 0 вИ•вИЗ¬ѓU(s)вИ•2 L2(Rd;Rd√Чd) ds

    < вИЮ and, for every v вИИL2(Rd; Rd), E ¬ѓU, v

    is a continuous function of t. Since ¬ѓU is constructed on a prescribed stochastic basis and over a pre- scribed time interval [0, T], this solution of (13.2) is strong in the probabilistic sense and is global in time. Being true in any space dimension d, Theorem 13.3 suggests another possible way to study equation (13.2) when d вЙ•3. Unlike the propagator for the linear equation, the system (13.7) is not lower-triangular and not solvable by induction, so the analysis of (13.7) is an open problem.

    502 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii 14 First-Order ItЋЖo Equations The objective of this section is to study the equation du(t, x) = ux(t, x)dw(t), t > 0, x вИИR, (14.1) and its analog for x вИИRd. Equation (14.1) was first encountered in Example 6.2; see also [9]. With a non-random initial condition u(0, x) = ѕХ(x), direct computations show that, if exists, the Fourier transform ЋЖu = ЋЖu(t, y) of the solution must satisfy dЋЖu(t, y) = вИЪ вИТ1yЋЖu(t, y)dw(t), or ЋЖu(t, y) = ЋЖѕХ(y)e вИЪвИТ1yw(t)+ 1 2 y2t. (14.2) The last equality shows that the properties of the solution essentially depend on the initial condition, and, in general, the solution is not in L2(W). The S-transformed equation, vt = h(t)vx, has a unique solution v(t, x) = ѕХ

    x + t 0 h(s)ds

    , h(t) = N

    i=1 himi(t). The results of Section 3 imply that a white noise solution of the equation can exist only if ѕХ is a real analytic function. On the other hand, if ѕХ is infinitely differentiable, then, by Theorem 8.2, the Wiener Chaos solution exists and can be recovered from v. Theorem 14.1. Assume that the initial condition ѕХ belongs to the Schwarz space S = S(R) of tempered distributions. Then there exists a generalized random process u = u(t, x), t вЙ•0, x вИИR, such that, for every ќ≥ вИИR and T > 0, the process u is the unique w(Hќ≥ 2 (R), Hќ≥вИТ1 2 (R)) Wiener Chaos solution of equation (14.1). Proof. The propagator for (14.1) is uќ±(t, x) = ѕХ(x)I(|ќ±| = 0) + t 0

    i вИЪќ±i(uќ±вИТ(i)(s, x))xmi(s)ds. (14.3) Even though Theorem 6.1 is not applicable, the system can be solved by induction if ѕХ is sufficiently smooth. Denote by CѕХ(k), k вЙ•0, the square of the L2(R)-norm of the kth derivative of ѕХ: CѕХ(k) = +вИЮ вИТвИЮ |ѕХ(k)(x)|2dx. (14.4) By Corollary 6.1, for every k вЙ•0 and n вЙ•0,

    |ќ±|=k вИ•(u(n) ќ± )xвИ•2 L2(R)(t) = tkCѕХ(n + k) k! . (14.5) The statement of the theorem now follows.

    Wiener Chaos for Stochastic Equations 503 Remark 14.1 Once interpreted in a suitable sense, the Wiener Chaos solution of (14.1) is FW t -adapted and does not depend on the choice of the CameronвАУ Martin basis in L2(W). Indeed, choose the weight sequence so that r2 ќ± = 1 1 + CѕХ(|ќ±|). By (14.5), we have u вИИRL2(W; L2(R)). Next, define ѕИN(x) = 1 ѕА sin(Nx) x . Direct computations show that the Fourier transform of ѕИN is supported in [вИТN, N] and

    R ѕИN(x)dx = 1. Consider equation (14.1) with initial condition ѕХN(x) =

    R ѕХ(x вИТy)ѕИN(y)dy. By (14.2), this equation has a unique solution uN such that uN(t, ¬Ј) is in L2(W; Hќ≥ 2 (R)) for every t вЙ•0, ќ≥ вИИR. Relation (14.5) and the definition of uN imply that lim NвЖТвИЮ

    |ќ±|=k вИ•uќ± вИТuN,ќ±вИ•2 L2(R)(t) = 0, t вЙ•0, k вЙ•0, so that, by the Lebesgue dominated convergence theorem, lim NвЖТвИЮвИ•u вИТuNвИ•2 RL2(W;L2(R))(t) = 0, t вЙ•0. In other words, the solution of the propagator (14.3) corresponding to any basis m in L2((0, T)) is a limit in RL2(W; L2(R)) of the sequence {uN, N вЙ•1} of FW t -adapted processes. The properties of the Wiener Chaos solution of (14.1) depend on the growth rate of the numbers CѕХ(n). In particular, вАҐ If CѕХ(n) вЙ§Cn(n!)ќ≥, C > 0, 0 вЙ§ќ≥ < 1, then u вИИL2 (W; L2((0, T); Hn 2 (R))) for all T > 0 and every n вЙ•0. вАҐ If CѕХ(n) вЙ§Cnn!, C > 0, then вАУ for every n вЙ•0, there is a T > 0 such that u вИИL2 (W; L2((0, T); Hn 2 (R))). In other words, the square-integrable solution exists only for sufficiently small T. вАУ for every n вЙ•0 and every T > 0, there exists a number ќі вИИ(0, 1) such that u вИИL2,Q (W; L2((0, T); Hn 2 (R))) with Q = (ќі, ќі, ќі, . . .). вАҐ If the numbers CѕХ(n) grow as Cn(n!)1+ѕБ, ѕБ вЙ•0, then, for every T > 0, there exists a number ќ≥ > 0 such that u вИИ(S)вИТѕБ,вИТќ≥ (L2(W); L2((0, T); Hn 2 (R))). If ѕБ > 0, then this solution does not belong to any L2,Q (W; L2((0, T); Hn 2 (R))). If ѕБ > 1, then this solution does not have an S-transform.

    504 S. Lototsky and B. Rozovskii вАҐ If the numbers CѕХ(n) grow faster than Cn(n!)b for any b, C > 0, then the Wiener Chaos solution of (14.1) does not belong to any (S)вИТѕБ,вИТќ≥ (L2((0, T); Hn 2 (R))), ѕБ, ќ≥ > 0, or L2,Q (W; L2((0, T); Hn 2 (R))). To construct a function ѕХ with the required rate of growth of CѕХ(n), consider ѕХ(x) = вИЮ 0 cos(xy)eвИТg(y)dy, where g is a suitable positive, unbounded, even function. Note that, up to a multiplicative constant, the Fourier transform of ѕХ is eвИТg(y), and so CѕХ(n) grows with n as вИЮ 0 |y|2neвИТ2g(y)dy. A more general first-order equation can be considered: du(t, x) = ѕГik(t, x)Diu(t, x)dwk(t), t > 0, x вИИRd. (14.6) Theorem 14.2. Assume that in equation (14.6) the initial condition u(0, x) belongs to S(Rd) and each ѕГik is infinitely differentiable with respect to x such that sup(t,x) |DnѕГik(t, x)| вЙ§Cik(n), n вЙ•0. Then there exists a generalized random process u = u(t, x), t вЙ•0, x вИИRd, such that, for every ќ≥ вИИR and T > 0, the process u is the unique w(Hќ≥ 2 (Rd), Hќ≥вИТ1 2 (Rd)) Wiener Chaos solution of equation (14.1). Proof. The arguments are identical to the proof of Theorem 14.1. Note that the S-transformed equation (14.6) is vt = hkѕГikDiv and has a unique solution if each ѕГik is a Lipschitz continuous function of x. Still, without additional smoothness, it is impossible to relate this solution to any generalized random process. References 1. Baxendale, P., Harris, T.E.: Isotropic stochastic flows.     A Martingale Equation of Exponential Type Michael MANIA1 вИЧand Revaz TEVZADZE2 1 A.Razmadze Mathematical Institute, 1 Alexidze Street, Tbilisi, 0193, Georgia. mania@rmi.acnet.ge 2 Institute of Cybernetics, 5 Euli Street, Tbilisi, 0186, Georgia. tevza@cybernet.ge Summary. We establish the existence of unique solution of an exponential martin- gale equation in the class of BMO martingales. The solution is used to characterize variance-optimal martingale measures. Key words: backward stochastic differential equation, exponential martingale, martingale measures Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 90A09, 60H30, 90C39 JEL Classification Numbers: G11 1 Introduction Let (вД¶, F, P) be a probability space with filtration F = (Ft)tвИИ[0,T ]. We assume that all local martingales with respect to F are continuous. Here T is a fixed time horizon and F = FT . Let M be a stable subspace of the space of square integrable martingales H2. Its ordinary orthogonal MвК•is a stable subspace of H2 and any element of M is strongly orthogonal to any element of MвК•(see, e.g., [4]). We consider the following exponential equation ET (m) ET (mвК•) = ceќЈ, (1.1) where ќЈ is a given FT -measurable random variable. Solution of equation (1.1) is a triple (c, m, mвК•), where c is a constant, m вИИM and mвК•вИИMвК•. Here E(X) is the Dol¬іeans-Dade exponential of X. вИЧResearch supported by Grant INTAS 99 00559.

    508 Michael Mania and Revaz Tevzadze If M and MвК•are stable subspaces of H2 generated by given local martin- gales M and N, strongly orthogonal to each other, then equation (1.1) takes the form ET ( . 0 ZsdMs) ET ( . 0 ZвК• s dNs) = ceќЈ (1.2) and solution of (1.2) is a triple (c, Z, ZвК•), where Z and ZвК•are predictable M and N integrable processes, respectively. Equations of such type are arising in mathematical finance. They are used to characterize the variance-optimal martingale measure (see [1], [12], [13] for such characterizations and also [3] and [14] for the definition of the variance-optimal martingale measure and related results). Note that the exponential equation of the form (1.1) can also be applied to the financial market models with infinitely many assets. Our aim is to prove the existence of (unique) solution of equation (1.1) in the class of BMO-martingales. The main statement of the paper is the following: Theorem 1. Let ќЈ вИИ LвИЮ(FT ). Then there exists a unique triple (c, m, mвК•), where c вИИR+, m вИИBMO вИ©M, mвК•вИИBMO вИ©MвК•, that sat- isfies equation (1.1). One can show that equation (1.1) is equivalent to the semimartingale back- ward equation Yt = Y0 вИТвЯ®LвЯ©t + вЯ®LвК•вЯ©t + Lt + LвК• t , YT = 1 2ќЈ. (1.3) We show that there exists a unique triple (Y, L, LвК•), where Y is a bounded continuous semimartingale, L вИИBMO вИ©M, LвК•вИИBMO вИ©MвК•, satisfying equation (1.3). If the filtration F is generated by a multidimensional Brownian motion ЋЬW = (W 1, ..., W n) and M, MвК•are stable subspaces of H2 generated by W = (W 1, ..., W k), W вК•= (W k+1, ..., W n) respectively, then equation (1.3) takes the form of the usual backward stochastic differential equation (BSDE) Yt = 1 2ќЈ + T t |Zs|2ds вИТ T t |ZвК• s |2ds вИТ T t ZsdWs вИТ T t ZвК• s dW вК• s . (1.4) The existence of a solution of equation (1.4) follows from the results of [9] and [10], where the BSDEs with drivers satisfying the quadratic growth conditions (and ќЈ вИИLвИЮ(FT )) were considered. To our knowledge, there are no general results on BSDEs driven by martingales and including drivers with quadratic growth. In [2] and [6] the well-posedness of BSDEs driven by martingales with drivers satisfying global Lipschitz type conditions was established. It is easy to see that if in front of square characteristics вЯ®LвЯ©and вЯ®LвК•вЯ©(of equation (1.3)) we were have the identical signs, then such an equation would admit an explicit solution. For example, a solution of the equation Yt = Y0 вИТвЯ®LвЯ©t вИТвЯ®LвК•вЯ©t + Lt + LвК• t , YT = 1 2ќЈ,

    Exponential Martingale Equation 509 (which corresponds to the exponential equation ET (m)ET (mвК•) = ceќЈ) is the triple (Y, L, LвК•): Lt = 1 2 t 0 1 E(eќЈ|Fs)dms(ќЈ), LвК• t = 1 2 t 0 1 E(eќЈ|Fs)dmвК• s (ќЈ), Yt = E 1 2ќЈ + вЯ®LвЯ©T вИТвЯ®LвЯ©t + вЯ®LвК•вЯ©T вИТвЯ®LвК•вЯ©t Ft

    , where the martingales m(ќЈ) and mвК•(ќЈ) are defined by the orthogonal decom- position E(eќЈ|Ft) = EeќЈ + mt(ќЈ) + mвК• t (ќЈ), m(ќЈ) вИИM, mвК•(ќЈ) вИИMвК•. Note that the problem to find the solution of equation (1.3) is caused here only by opposite signs at the square characteristics of martingales L and LвК•, but the method of the proof of Theorem 1 can be extended for semimartingale BSDEs with more general drivers (see, e.g., the remark at the end of the paper). The paper [12] seems to be the first one where the theory of BMO- martingales was used for BSDEs. For BSDEs similar to (1.3) it was shown that the martingale part of any bounded solution Y of (1.3) belongs to the class BMO. This fact shows, that it should be convenient to operate with BMO- norms in order to prove the existence of solution for equation (1.3) or for more general BSDEs with drivers satisfying the quadratic growth condition. Using the BMO-norms for martingales L, LвК•and the | ¬Ј |вИЮ-norm for the semimartingale Y , we apply the contraction principle to show the existence of a solution, first in case where the | ¬Ј |LвИЮ-norm of ќЈ is sufficiently small and then, applying a specific result (see Lemma 1) we construct a solution for an arbitrary ќЈ вИИLвИЮ. For all unexplained notations concerning the martingale theory used below we refer to [7], [4] and [11]. About BMO-martingales see [5] or [8]. 2 Proof of the Main Result First let us introduce some notations. We say that the process B strongly dominates the process A and write A вЙЇ B, if the difference B вИТA вИИA+ loc, i.e. B вИТA is a locally integrable increasing process. We shall use also the notation ѕИ ¬Ј X for the stochastic integral with respect to the semimartingale X. For the process of finite variation A we denote by vart s(A) the variation of A on the interval [s, t]. We use RвИЮto denote the space of all adapted cadlag processes Y such that |Y |вИЮ= |Y вИЧ T |LвИЮ< вИЮ, where Y вИЧ t = supsвЙ§t |Ys|. As stated before, we deal entirely with continuous local martingales and for convenience we shall use the following definition of BMO-martingales.

    510 Michael Mania and Revaz Tevzadze The square integrable martingale M belongs to the class BMO if there is a constant C > 0 such that E1/2(вЯ®MвЯ©T вИТвЯ®MвЯ©ѕД|FѕД) вЙ§C, P-a.s. for every stopping time ѕД. The smallest constant with this property (or +вИЮif it does not exist) is called the BMO-norm of M and is denoted by |M|BMO. Since the class BMO depends on the underlying probability measure, we shall use notation BMO(Q) if the measure Q is different from the basic probability measure P. Let N вИИBMO and dQ = ET (N)dP. Then Q is a probability measure equivalent to P by Theorem 2.3 of [8]. Denote by ѕИ = ѕИN(X) = вЯ®X, NвЯ©вИТX the Girsanov transformation. It is well known (see [8]) that if N вИИBMO, then both H2 and BMO are invariant under the transformation ѕИ. Let M(Q) and MвК•(Q) be the images of the mapping ѕИ for M and MвК•, respectively. Note that M(Q) and MвК•(Q) are stable orthogonal subspaces of the space H2(Q) of square integrable martingales with respect to Q. In the sequel we shall need the following Lemma 1. Suppose that there are m1, mвК• 1 вИИBMO, m1 вИИM, mвК• 1 вИИMвК• such that ET (m1) ET (mвК• 1 ) = c1eќЈ1. (1.5) Let Q be a probability measure defined by dQ = ET (m1 + mвК• 1 )dP and assume that there exist m2, mвК• 2 вИИBMO(Q), m2 вИИM(Q), mвК• 2 вИИMвК•(Q) such that ET (m2) ET (mвК• 2 ) = c2eќЈ2. (1.6) Then there exists a solution of the equation ET (m) ET (mвК•) = ceќЈ1+ќЈ2. (1.7) Proof. Note that dP dQ = EвИТ1 T (m1 + mвК• 1 ) = ET ( ЋЬm1 + ЋЬmвК• 1 ), where ЋЬm1 = вЯ®m1вЯ©вИТm1 and ЋЬmвК• 1 = вЯ®mвК• 1 вЯ©вИТmвК• 1 вИИBMO(Q). By the Girsanov theorem m2 and mвК• 2 are special semimartingales under P with the decomposition

    Exponential Martingale Equation 511 m2 = ЋЖm2 + вЯ®m2, ЋЬm1вЯ©, mвК• 2 = ЋЖmвК• 2 + вЯ®mвК• 2 , ЋЬmвК• 1 вЯ©, (1.8) where ЋЖm2 = m2вИТвЯ®m2, ЋЬm1вЯ©and ЋЖmвК• 2 = mвК• 2 вИТвЯ®mвК• 2 , ЋЬmвК• 1 вЯ©are BMO-martingales under P according to Theorem 3.6 of [8]. It is evident that вЯ®ЋЖm2, m1вЯ©= вИТвЯ®m2, ЋЬm1вЯ©, вЯ®ЋЖmвК• 2 , mвК• 1 вЯ©= вИТвЯ®mвК• 2 , ЋЬmвК• 1 вЯ©. (1.9) Multiplying now equations (1.5) and (1.6), using the Yor formula and decom- position (1.8) we obtain that ET (m1 + m2 + вЯ®ЋЖm2, m1вЯ©) ET (mвК• 1 + mвК• 2 + вЯ®ЋЖmвК• 2 , mвК• 1 вЯ©) = c1c2eќЈ1+ќЈ2. (1.10) By equality (1.9) and Theorem 3.6 of [8] m2 + вЯ®ЋЖm2, m1вЯ©and mвК• 2 + вЯ®ЋЖmвК• 2 , mвК• 1 вЯ© are BMO-martingales under P. It is easy to see that these martingales are strongly orthogonal to each other. Thus, c = c1c2, m = m1 + m2 + вЯ®ЋЖm2, m1вЯ© and mвК•= mвК• 1 + mвК• 2 + вЯ®ЋЖmвК• 2 , mвК• 1 вЯ©satisfy equation (1.7). вКУвКФ The proof of Theorem 1. Uniqueness. Let (c, m, mвК•) and (cвА≤, l, lвК•) be two solutions of (1.1) from the class BMO. Then (1.1) implies that cвА≤ ET (m) ET (mвК•) = c ET (l) ET (lвК•), (1.11) and, by YorвАЩs formula, cвА≤ET (m + lвК•) = cET (mвК•+ l). (1.12) Since m + lвК•and mвК•+ l are BMO-martingales, according to Theorem 2.3 of [8], E(m + lвК•) and E(mвК•+ l) are uniformly integrable martingales. Hence, equality (1.12) holds for any t вИИ[0, T]. Therefore, c = cвА≤ and m+lвК•= mвК•+l. Consequently, m = l and mвК•= lвК•. Existence. It is evident that equation (1.1) is equivalent to the following martingale equation вИТln cвА≤ вИТ1 2вЯ®mвЯ©T + 1 2вЯ®mвК•вЯ©T + mT вИТmвК• T = ќЈ. (1.13) Denoting cвА≤ = вИТ1 2 ln c, L = 1 2m, LвК•= вИТ1 2mвК•and ќЊ = 1 2ќЈ one can write this equation as c вИТвЯ®LвЯ©T + вЯ®LвК•вЯ©T + LT + LвК• T = ќЊ. (1.14) This equation can also be written in the following equivalent semimartingale form as a BSDE: Yt = Y0 вИТвЯ®L + LвК•, L вИТLвК•вЯ©t + Lt + LвК• t , YT = ќЊ. (1.15)

    512 Michael Mania and Revaz Tevzadze Let us show first that there exists a solution (c, L, LвК•) of equation (1.14) if |ќЊ|вИЮis small enough. For brevity we shall use the notation вЯ®mвЯ©tT = вЯ®mвЯ©T вИТвЯ®mвЯ©t for the incre- ment of square characteristic вЯ®mвЯ©of a martingale m. Let us consider the mapping Lt + LвК• t = E(ќЊ + вЯ®l + lвК•, l вИТlвК•вЯ©T |Ft) (1.16) вИТE(ќЊ + вЯ®l + lвК•, l вИТlвК•вЯ©T ), Yt = E(ќЊ + вЯ®l + lвК•, l вИТlвК•вЯ©tT |Ft), (1.17) which transforms BMO-martingales l and lвК•into a triple (Y, L, LвК•), where L and LвК•are BMO-martingales and Y is a semimartingale. Using |Y |вИЮ-norms for semimartingales and the BMO-norms for martingales, we shall show that if the norm |ќЊ|вИЮis sufficiently small, then there exists r > 0 such that the mapping (1.16) is a contraction in the ball Br = {(l, lвК•) : |l + lвК•|BMO вЙ§r}. Using the ItЋЖo formula for Y 2 T вИТY 2 t and (1.16), (1.17) we have Y 2 t вИТY 2 T = вИТ2 T t Ysd(Ls + LвК• s ) +2 T t YsdвЯ®l + lвК•, l вИТlвК•вЯ©s вИТвЯ®L + LвК•вЯ©tT . (1.18) Since ќЊ вИИLвИЮ, equations (1.16) and (1.17) imply that for any l, lвК•вИИBMO the process Y is bounded and the processes L and LвК•are square integrable martingales. Therefore, the stochastic integral Y ¬Ј (L + LвК•) is a martingale. Taking conditional expectations in (1.18) we have Y 2 t + E(вЯ®L + LвК•вЯ©tT |Ft) = E(ќЊ2|Ft) + 2E T t YsdвЯ®l + lвК•, l вИТlвК•вЯ©s Ft

    . Since вЯ®l+lвК•, lвИТlвК•вЯ©вЙЇвЯ®l+lвК•вЯ©, using the elementary inequality 1 2a2+2b2 вЙ•2ab, we get that Y 2 t + E(вЯ®L + LвК•вЯ©tT |Ft) вЙ§|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 2|Y |вИЮE(вЯ®l + lвК•вЯ©tT |Ft) вЙ§|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 1 2|Y |2 вИЮ+ 2E2(вЯ®l + lвК•вЯ©tT |Ft) вЙ§|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 1 2|Y |2 вИЮ+ 2|l + lвК•|4 BMO. (1.19) From (1.19) we have Y 2 t вЙ§|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 1 2|Y |2 вИЮ+ 2|l + lвК•|4 BMO;

    Exponential Martingale Equation 513 taking the | ¬Ј |вИЮ-norm of the left-hand side of this inequality we obtain the bound 1 2|Y |2 вИЮвЙ§|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 2|l + lвК•|4 BMO. (1.20) From (1.19) we also have E(вЯ®L + LвК•вЯ©tT |Ft) вЙ§|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 1 2|Y |2 вИЮ+ 2|l + lвК•|4 BMO. Therefore, from (1.20) we obtain that E(вЯ®L + LвК•вЯ©tT |Ft) вЙ§2|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 4|l + lвК•|4 BMO and, hence, |L + LвК•|2 BMO вЙ§2|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 4|l + lвК•|4 BMO. (1.21) If |ќЊ|вИЮвЙ§ 1 4 вИЪ 2 then there exists r вЙ•0 that satisfies the inequality 2|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 4r4 вЙ§r2 (1.22) It is easy to see that for such r (i.e. for r satisfying inequality (1.22)), from |l + lвК•|BMO вЙ§r it follows that |L + LвК•|BMO вЙ§r. Indeed, if |l + lвК•|BMO вЙ§r then from (1.21) we have |L + LвК•|2 BMO вЙ§2|ќЊ|2 вИЮ+ 4r4, which implies that |L + LвК•|2 BMO вЙ§r2 because r satisfies inequality (1.22). Now we shall show that the mapping (1.16) is a contraction on the ball Br. Let Yi, Li, LвК• i , i = 1, 2, correspond to li, li by the transformation (1.16), (1.17). Since Y1(T) вИТY2(T) = 0, we obtain similarly to (1.19), by applying the Ito formula to (Y1 вИТY2)2 that (Y1(t) вИТY2(t))2 + E(вЯ®L1 вИТL2 + LвК• 1 вИТL2 вК•вЯ©tT |Ft) = 2E T t (Y1(s) вИТY2(s)) √Чd(вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 , l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©вИТвЯ®l2 + lвК• 2 , l2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©) Ft

    (1.23) вЙ§1 2|Y1 вИТY2|2 вИЮ +2E2(varT t (вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 , l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©вИТвЯ®l2 + lвК• 2 , l2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©)|Ft). Note that the process вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 , l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©вИТвЯ®l2 + lвК• 2 , l2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ© coincides with the process вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 , l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©+ вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 , l2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©.

    514 Michael Mania and Revaz Tevzadze Using successively the elementary inequalities varT t (A + B) вЙ§varT t (A) + varT t (B), (a + b)2 вЙ§2(a2 + b2) and the KunitaвАУWatanabe inequality, we get that E2(varT t (вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 , l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©вИТвЯ®l2 + lвК• 2 , l2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©)|Ft) вЙ§2E2(varT t вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 , l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©|Ft) +2E2(varT t вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 , l2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©|Ft) вЙ§2E(вЯ®l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©tT |Ft)E(вЯ®ll + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©tT |Ft) +2E(вЯ®l2 вИТl2 вК•вЯ©tT |Ft)E(вЯ®ll + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©tT |Ft). (1.24) Since for any l + lвК•вИИBr we have the bound E(вЯ®l вИТlвК•вЯ©tT |Ft вЙ§r2, we obtain from (1.24) that for all l1 + lвК• 1 , l2 + lвК• 2 вИИBr E2(varT t (вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 , l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©вИТвЯ®l2 + lвК• 2 , l2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©)|Ft) вЙ§2E(вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©tT |Ft) √Ч[E(вЯ®l1 вИТlвК• 1 вЯ©tT |Ft) + E(вЯ®l2 вИТl2 вК•вЯ©tT |Ft)] вЙ§4r2E(вЯ®l1 + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 вЯ©tT |Ft) вЙ§4r2|l1 + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 |2 BMO. (1.25) Inequalities (1.23) and (1.25) imply that for all l1 + lвК• 1 , l2 + lвК• 2 вИИBr (Y1(t) вИТY2(t))2 + E(вЯ®L1 вИТL2 + LвК• 1 вИТL2 вК•вЯ©tT |Ft) вЙ§1 2|Y1 вИТY2|2 вИЮ+ 8r2|l1 + lвК• 1 вИТl2 вИТlвК• 2 |2 BMO. (1.26) Using similar arguments as above (see equations (1.19) вАУ (1.21) ) we obtain that the estimate |L1 вИТL2 + LвК• 1 вИТL2 вК•|BMO вЙ§4r|l1 вИТl2 + lвК• 1 вИТlвК• 2 |BMO holds. Finally, we remark that, if |ќЊ|вИЮвЙ§ 1 6 and 1 32 вЙ§r2 < 1 16, then the inequalities (1.22) and r < 1 4 are satisfied simultaneously. Thus, we obtain that if |ќЊ|вИЮis small enough (namely, if |ќЊ|вИЮ< 1 6), then the mapping (1.16) is a contraction in Br and by the fixed point theorem there exists a unique pair (ЋЬL, ЋЬLвК•) such that ЋЬLt + ЋЬLвК• t = E(ќЊ + вЯ®ЋЬL + ЋЬLвК•, ЋЬL вИТЋЬLвК•вЯ©T |Ft) (1.27) вИТE(ќЊ + вЯ®ЋЬL + ЋЬLвК•, ЋЬL вИТЋЬLвК•вЯ©T ) and Yt = E(ќЊ + вЯ®ЋЬL + ЋЬLвК•, ЋЬL вИТЋЬLвК•вЯ©tT |Ft). Taking t = T in (1.27) we obtain that the triple (c, ЋЬL, ЋЬLвК•), where the constant c = E(ќЊ + вЯ®ЋЬL + ЋЬLвК•, ЋЬL вИТЋЬLвК•вЯ©T ), satisfies equation (1.14) and, hence, equation

    Exponential Martingale Equation 515 (1.1) admits a unique solution. Namely, if |ќЊ|вИЮвЙ§1 6 then the BMO-norm of the solution is less than 1 4. To get rid of the assumption that |ќЊ|вИЮis sufficiently small, we shall use Lemma 1. Let us take an integer n вЙ•1 such that the equation ET (m) ET (mвК•) = c1e 1 n ќЊ (1.28) admits a solution. Let dQ = ET (m1 + mвК• 1 )dP, where (m1, mвК• 1 ) вИИBMO(P) be a solution of (1.28). Since the norm |ќЊ|вИЮis invariant with respect to an equivalent change of measure and since the Girsanov transformation is an isomorphism of BMO(P) onto BMO(Q), similarly as above one can show that there exists a pair m2, mвК• 2 вИИBMO(Q) that satisfies equation (1.28). Therefore, by Lemma 1, there exists a solution of equation ET (m) ET (mвК•) = c2e 2 n ќЊ. (1.29) Using now Lemma 1 to equation (1.29) by induction we obtain that there exists a solution of the equation (1.1) for an arbitrary ќЊ вИИLвИЮ. вКУвКФ Remark. By the same way one can show the solvability of the following, more general, BSDE Yt = Y0 + t 0 g(s)dвЯ®LвЯ©s + t 0 gвК•(s)вЯ®LвК•вЯ©s + Lt + LвК• t , YT = ќЊ, for given bounded predictable processes g, gвК•and ќЊ вИИLвИЮ(FT ).
