    On Local Martingale and its Supremum: Harmonic Functions and beyond. Jan OBL¬іOJ1,2 and Marc YOR1 1 Laboratoire de Probabilit¬іes et Modeles Al¬іeatoires, Universit¬іe Paris 6, 4 pl. Jussieu, BoЋЖƒ±te 188, 75252 Paris Cedex 05, France. 2 Wydzia^l Matematyki, Uniwersytet Warszawski Banacha 2, 02-097 Warszawa, Poland. obloj@mimuw.edu.pl Summary. We discuss certain facts involving a continuous local martingale N and its supremum N. A complete characterization of (N, N)-harmonic functions is given. This yields an important family of martingales, the usefulness of which is demon- strated, by means of examples involving the Skorohod embedding problem, bounds on the law of the supremum, or the local time at 0, of a martingale with a fixed terminal distribution, or yet in some Brownian penalization problems. In particular we obtain new bounds on the law of the local time at 0, which involve the excess wealth order. Key words: continuous local martingale, supremum process, harmonic function, SkorohodвАЩs embedding problem, excess wealth order. Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 60G44 (Primary), 60G42, 60G40, 60E15 Dedication. The first time I met Prof. A. Shiryaev was in January 1977, during a meeting dedicated to Control and Filtering theories, in Bonn. This was a time when meeting a Soviet mathematician was some event! Among the participants to that meeting, were, apart from A. Shiryaev, Prof. B. Grige- lionis, and M. Yershov, who was by then just leaving Soviet Union in hard circumstances. To this day, I vividly remember that A.S, M.Y. and myself spent a full Sunday together, trying to solve a succession of problems raised by A.S., who among other things, explained at length about TsirelвАЩsonвАЩs ex- ample of a one-dimensional SDE, with path dependent drift, and no strong solution ([32]; this motivated me to give - in [37] - a more direct proof than the original one by TsirelвАЩson, see also [38], and Revuz and Yor [24] p. 392). Each of my encounters with A.S. has had, roughly, the same flavor: A.S. would present, with great enthusiasm, some recent or not so recent result, and ask

    518 Jan Ob^l¬іoj and Marc Yor me for some simple proof, extension, etc... I have often been hooked into that game, which kept reminding me of one of my favorite pedagogical sentences by J. Dixmier: When looking for the 50th time at a well-known proof of some theorem, I would discover a new twist I had never thought of, which would cast a new light on the matter. I hope that the following notes, which discuss some facts about local martingales and their supremum processes, and are closely related to the thesis subject of the first author, may also have some this вАЬnew twistвАЭ character for some readers, and be enjoyed by Albert Shiryaev, on the occasion of his 70th birthday. Marc Yor 1 Introduction In this article we focus on local martingales, functions of two-dimensional processes, whose components are a continuous local martingale (Nt : t вЙ•0) and its supremum N t = supuвЙ§t Nu, i.e. on local martingales of the form (H(Nt, N t) : t вЙ•0), where H : R √Ч R+ вЖТR. We call functions H such that (H(Nt, N t) : t вЙ•0) is a local martingale, (N, N)-harmonic functions. Some examples of such local martingales are F(N t) вИТf(N t)(N t вИТNt), t вЙ•0, (1.1) where F вИИC1 and F вА≤ = f, introduced by Az¬іema and Yor [3]. We show that (1.1) defines a local martingale for any Borel, locally integrable function f. We conjecture that these are the only local martingales, that is that the only (N, N)-harmonic functions are of the form H(x, y) = F(y) вИТf(y)(y вИТx) + C, with f a locally integrable function, F(y) = y 0 f(u)du, and C a constant. We explain, in an intuitive manner, how these local martingales, which we call max-martingales, may be used to find the Az¬іemaвАУYor solution to the Skorohod embedding problem. We then go on and develop, with the help of these martingales, the well-known bounds on the law of the supremum of a uniformly integrable martingale with a fixed terminal distribution. Using the L¬іevy and DambisвАУDubinsвАУSchwarz theorems, we reformulate the results in terms of the absolute value |N| and the local time LN at 0, of the local mar- tingale N. This leads to some new bounds on the law of the local time of a uniformly integrable martingale with fixed terminal distribution. A recently introduced and studied stochastic order, called the excess wealth order (see Shaked and Shanthikumar [28]), plays a crucial role. We also point out that the max-martingales appear naturally in some Brownian penalization problems. Finally, we try to sketch a somewhat more general viewpoint linked with the balayage formula. The organization of this paper is as follows. We start in Sec- tion 3 with a discrete version of the balayage formula and show how to deduce from it DoobвАЩs maximal and Lp inequalities. In the subsequent Section 4, in Theorem 4.1, we formulate the result about the harmonic functions of (N, N)

    On Brownian Motion and its Supremum 519 and prove it in a regular case. Section 5 is devoted to some applications: it contains three subsections concentrating respectively on the Skorohod embed- ding problem, bounds on the laws of N and LN, and Brownian penalizations. The last section contains a discussion of the balayage formula. 2 Notation Throughout this paper (Nt : t вЙ•0) denotes a continuous local martingale with N0 = 0 and вЯ®NвЯ©вИЮ= вИЮa.s., and N t = supsвЙ§t Ns denotes its maximum process. We have adopted this notation so that there is no confusion with stock-price processes, which are often denoted St. The local time at 0 of N is denoted (LN t : t вЙ•0). For processes either in discrete or in continuous time, when we say that a process is a (sub/super) martingale without specifying the filtration, we mean the natural filtration of the process. B = (Bt : t вЙ•0) shall denote a one-dimensional Brownian motion, starting from 0, and Bt = supsвЙ§t Bs. The natural filtration of B is denoted (Ft) and is taken completed. The indicator function is denoted 1. We use the notations a вИ®b = max{a, b} and a вИІb = min{a, b}. The positive part is given by x+ = x вИ®0. For ¬µ a probability measure on R, ¬µ(x) := ¬µ([x, вИЮ)) is its tail distribution function; X вИЉ¬µ means X has distribution ¬µ. 3 Balayage in discrete time and some applications We start with the discrete time setting, and present a simple idea, which corresponds to balayage in continuous time, and which proves an efficient tool, as it allows, for example, to obtain easily DoobвАЩs maximal and Lp inequalities. Let (вД¶, F, (Fn)nвИИN, P) be a filtered probability space and (Yn : n вЙ•0) be some real-valued adapted discrete stochastic process. Let (ѕХn : n вЙ•0) be also an adapted process, which further satisfies ѕХn1YnћЄ=0 = ѕХnвИТ11YnћЄ=0, for all n вИИN. The last condition can be also formulated as вАЬthe process (ѕХn) is constant on excursions of (Yn) away from 0вАЭ. Lemma 3.1. Let (Yn, ѕХn) be as above, Y0 = 0. The following identities hold: ѕХnYn = ѕХnвИТ1Yn = n

    k=1 ѕХkвИТ1(Yk вИТYkвИТ1), n вЙ•1. (3.1) Proof. The first equality is obvious as ѕХnYn = ѕХnYn1YnћЄ=0 = ѕХnвИТ1Yn, and the second one is obtained by telescoping. вЬЈ To see how the above can be used, let us give some examples of pairs (Yn, ѕХn) involving in particular an adapted process Xn and its maximum Xn: вАҐ Yn = Xn вИТXn and ѕХn = f(Xn), for some Borel function f;

    520 Jan Ob^l¬іoj and Marc Yor вАҐ Yn = Xn, ѕХn = n k=0 1Xk=0 (note that Yn = |Xn| works as well); вАҐ Yn = XвИЧ nвИТ|Xn|, ѕХn = f(XвИЧ n), for some Borel function f, where the process XвИЧ n = maxkвЙ§n |Xk|; вАҐ Yn = Xn вИТXn, ѕХn = f n k=1 1(Xk=Xk)

    , for some Borel function f, where Xn = | minkвЙ§n Xk|. We now use the discrete balayage formula with the first of the above examples to establish a useful supermartingale property. Proposition 3.1. Let (Xn : n вИИN) be a submartingale in its natural filtra- tion (Fn), X0 = 0, and let f be some increasing, locally integrable, positive function. Assume that Ef(Xn) < вИЮand EF(Xn) < вИЮfor all n вИИN, where F(x) = x 0 f(s)ds. Then the process Sf n = f(Xn)(Xn вИТXn) вИТF(Xn) is a (Fn)-supermartingale. Proof. Since the pair (Xn вИТXn, f(Xn)) satisfies the assumptions of Lemma 3.1, we have: Sf n = n

    k=1 f(XkвИТ1)(Xk вИТXk вИТXkвИТ1 + XkвИТ1) вИТF(Xn) = n

    k=1 f(XkвИТ1)(Xk вИТXkвИТ1) вИТ n

    k=1 f(XkвИТ1)(Xk вИТXkвИТ1) вИТ Xn 0 f(x)dx = n

    k=1 Xk XkвИТ1

    f(XkвИТ1) вИТf(x)

    dx вИТ n

    k=1 f(XkвИТ1)(Xk вИТXkвИТ1). (3.2) Using (3.2), the fact that f is increasing, and (Xn) is a submartingale, we obtain the supermartingale property for Sf n. вЬЈ The above Proposition allows to recover DoobвАЩs maximal and Lp inequal- ities in a very easy way. Indeed, consider the function f(x) = 1xвЙ•ќї for some ќї > 0. Then the process Sf n = S(ќї) n = 1XnвЙ•ќї(ќї вИТXn) is a supermartingale, which yields DoobвАЩs maximal inequality ќїP

    Xn вЙ•ќї

    вЙ§E ) 1(XnвЙ•ќї)Xn

    . (3.3) To obtain the Lp inequalities we consider the function f(x) = pxpвИТ1 for some p > 1, and we suppose that (Xn : n вЙ•0) is a positive submartingale with EXp n < вИЮ. This implies, as X p n вЙ§n k=1 Xp k, that EX p n < вИЮ. The process Sf n = S(p) n = (p вИТ1)(Xn)p вИТp(Xn)pвИТ1Xn is then a supermartingale, which yields (p вИТ1)E ) (Xn)p вЙ§pE ) (Xn)pвИТ1Xn

    and hence, applying H¬®olderвАЩs ineq., E ) (Xn)p вЙ§

    p p вИТ1 p E ) Xp n

    , which is DoobвАЩs Lp ineq. (3.4)

    On Brownian Motion and its Supremum 521 To our best knowledge, this small wrinkle about DoobвАЩs inequalities for positive submartingales involving supermartingales does not appear in any of the books on discrete martingales, such as Neveu [17], Garsia [12] or Williams [36]. We point out also, that our method allows to obtain other variants of DoobвАЩs inequalities, such as L log L inequalities, etc. 4 The Markov process ((Bt, Bt) : t вЙ•0) and its harmonic functions In the rest of the paper we will focus on the continuous-time setup. It follows immediately from the strong Markov property of B, or rather the indepen- dence of its increments, that for s < t, and f : R√ЧR+ вЖТR+ a Borel function, one has: E ) f(Bt, Bt) Fs

    = ЋЬE ) f

    Bs + ЋЬBtвИТs, Bs вИ®sup uвЙ§tвИТs (Bs + ЋЬBu)

    , (4.1) where on the RHS, the notation ЋЬE indicates integration with respect to func- tionals of the Brownian motion ( ЋЬBu : u вЙ•0), which is assumed to be inde- pendent of (Bt : t вЙ•0). In particular, the two-dimensional process ((Bt, Bt) : t вЙ•0) is a nice Markov process, hence a strong Markov process, and its semigroup can be computed explicitly thanks to the well-known, and classical formula: P

    Bt вИИdx, Bt вИИdy

    = 2 ѕАt3 1/2 (2y вИТx) exp

    вИТ(2y вИТx)2 2t

    1(yвЙ•x+)dxdy. We are now interested in a description of the harmonic functions H of (B, B) that is of Borel functions such that (H(Bt, Bt) : t вЙ•0) is a local martingale. Note that this question is rather natural and interesting since H is (B, B)-harmonic if and only if, thanks to the DambisвАУDubinsвАУSchwarz theorem, for any continuous local martingale (Nt : t вЙ•0), H is also (N, N)- harmonic. The following proposition is an extension of Proposition 4.7 in Revuz and Yor [24]. Theorem 4.1. Let N = (Nt : t вЙ•0) be a continuous local martingale with вЯ®NвЯ©вИЮ= вИЮa.s., f a Borel, locally integrable function, and H defined through H(x, y) = F(y) вИТf(y)(y вИТx) + C, (4.2) where C is a constant and F(y) = y 0 f(s)ds. Then, the following holds: H(Nt, N t) = F(N t) вИТf(N t)(N t вИТNt) + C t 0 f(N s)dNs + C, t вЙ•0, (4.3) and (H(Nt, N t) : t вЙ•0) is a local martingale.

    522 Jan Ob^l¬іoj and Marc Yor Remarks. Local martingales of the form (4.3) were first introduced by Az¬іema and Yor [3] and used to solve the Skorohod embedding problem (cf. Sec- tion 5.1 below). In the light of the above theorem, we will call them max- martingales and the functions given in (4.2) will be called MM-harmonic functions (max-martingale harmonic) or (N, N)-harmonic. Note the resem- blance of (4.3) with the discrete time process Sf n given in Proposition 3.1. It is known (see Revuz and Yor [24, Prop. VI.4.7]) that if H вИИC2,1 then the reverse statement holds. That is, if H is (N, N)-harmonic then there exists a continuous function f such that (4.2) holds. We present below a proof of this fact. We conjecture that the same holds true if we only suppose that H finely-continuous3. Proof. As mentioned above, thanks to the DambisвАУDubinsвАУSchwarz theorem, it suffices to prove the theorem for N = B. We first recall how to prove the converse of the theorem for the regular case. We assume that H вИИC2,1, with obvious notation, and that H is (B, B)-harmonic. We denote by HвА≤ x and HвА≤ y the partial derivatives of H in the first and the second argument respectively, and HвА≤вА≤ x2 the second derivative of H in the first argument. Without loss of generality, we assume that H(0, 0) = 0. Under the present assumptions we can apply ItЋЖoвАЩs formula to obtain: H(Bt, Bt) = t 0 HвА≤ x(Bs, Bs)dBs + t 0 HвА≤ y(Bs, Bs)dBs + 1 2 t 0 HвА≤вА≤ x2(Bs, Bs)ds, where we used the fact that Bs = Bs, dBs-a.s. Now, since H(Bt, Bt) is a local martingale, the above identity holds if and only if: HвА≤ y(Bs, Bs)dBs + 1 2HвА≤вА≤ x2(Bs, Bs)ds = 0, s вЙ•0. (4.4) The random measures dBs and ds are mutually singular since we have dBs = 1(BsвИТBs=0)dBs and ds = dвЯ®BвЯ©s = 1(BsвИТBsћЄ=0)dвЯ®BвЯ©s. Equation (4.4) holds therefore if and only if HвА≤ y(y, y) = 0 and HвА≤вА≤ x2(x, y) = 0. (4.5) The second condition implies that H(x, y) = f(y)x + g(y) and the first one then gives f вА≤(y)y+gвА≤(y) = 0. Thus, g(y) = вИТ y 0 uf вА≤(u)du = y 0 f(u)duвИТf(y)y. This yields formula (4.2). Furthermore, the above reasoning grants us that the formula (4.3) holds for f of class C1. As C1 is dense in the class of locally integrable functions (in an appropriate norm), if we can show that the quantities given in (4.3) are well defined and finite for any locally integrable f on [0, вИЮ), then the formula (4.3) extends to such functions through monotone class theorems. For f a locally integrable function, F(x) is well defined and finite, so all we need to 3This conjecture is proved in Ob^l¬іoj [19].

    On Brownian Motion and its Supremum 523 show is that t 0 f(Bs)dBs is well defined and finite a.s. This is equivalent to t 0

    f(Bs) 2 ds < вИЮa.s., which we now show. Write Tx = inf{t вЙ•0 : Bt = x} for the first hitting time of x, which is a well defined, a.s. finite, stopping time. Thus t 0

    f(Bs) 2 ds < вИЮa.s., if and only if, for all x > 0, Tx 0

    f(Bs) 2 ds < вИЮ. However, the last integral can be rewritten as Tx 0 ds

    f(Bs) 2 =

    0вЙ§uвЙ§x Tu TuвИТ ds

    f(Bs) 2 =

    0вЙ§uвЙ§x f 2(u)

    Tu вИТTuвИТ

    = x 0 f 2(u)dTu. (4.6) Now it suffices to note that (see Ex. III.4.5 in Revuz and Yor [24]) E ) exp

    вИТ1 2 x 0 f 2(u)dTu

    = exp

    вИТ x 0 |f(u)|du

    , (4.7) to see that the last integral in (4.6) is finite if and only if x 0 |f(u)|du < вИЮ, which is precisely our hypothesis on f. Note that the function H given by (4.2) is locally integrable as both func- tions x вЖТf(x) and x вЖТxf(x) are locally integrable. вЬЈ L¬іevyвАЩs theorem guarantees that the processes ((Bt, Bt) : t вЙ•0) and ((Lt вИТ|Bt|, Lt) : t вЙ•0) have the same distribution, where Lt denotes local time at 0 of B. Theorem 4.1 yields therefore also a complete description of (L, |B|)-harmonic functions, which again through DambisвАУDubinsвАУSchwarz theorem, extends to any local continuous martingale. We have the following Corollary 4.1. Let N = (Nt : t вЙ•0) be a continuous local martingale with вЯ®NвЯ©вИЮ= вИЮa.s., and LN = (LN t : t вЙ•0) its local time at 0. Let g a Borel, locally integrable function, and H be defined through H(x, y) = G(y) вИТg(y)x + C, (4.8) where C is a constant and G(y) = y 0 g(s)ds. Then, the following holds: H(|Nt|, LN t ) = G(LN t )вИТg(LN t )|Nt|+C = вИТ t 0 g(LN s )sgn (Ns) dNs+C, t вЙ•0, (4.9) and (H(|Nt|, LN t ) : t вЙ•0) is a local martingale. 5 Some appearances of the MM-harmonic functions We now present some easy applications of the martingales described in the previous section. (Nt : t вЙ•0) denotes always a continuous local martingale

    524 Jan Ob^l¬іoj and Marc Yor with вЯ®NвЯ©вИЮ= вИЮa.s. We will show an intuitive way to obtain a solution to the Skorohod embedding problem, as given by Az¬іema and Yor [3]. We will also discuss relations between the law of N T and the conditional law of NT knowing N T , for some stopping time T. In the second subsection we will derive well-known bounds on the law of N T , when the law of NT is fixed. We will then continue in the same vein and describe the law of LN T , when the law of |NT | is fixed. We will end with a discussion of penalization of Brownian motion with a function of its supremum and some absolute continuity relations. 5.1 On the Skorohod embedding problem The classical Skorohod embedding problem can be formulated as follows: for a given centered probability measure ¬µ, find a stopping time T such that NT вИЉ¬µ and (NtвИІT : t вЙ•0) is a uniformly integrable martingale. Numerous solutions to this problem are known; for an extensive survey see Ob`l¬іoj [18]. Here we make a remark about the solution given by Az¬іema and Yor in [3]. Namely we point out how one can arrive intuitively to this solution using the max-martingales (4.3). Naturally, this might be extracted from the original paper, but it may not be so obvious to do so. The basic observation is that the max-martingales allow to express the law of the terminal value of N, that is N T , in terms of the conditional distribution of NT given N T . One then constructs a stopping time which actually binds both terminal values through a function and sees that the function can be obtained in terms of the target measure ¬µ. Proposition 5.1 (Vallois [35]). Let T be a stopping time, such that the stopped process (NtвИІT : t вЙ•0) is a uniformly integrable martingale. Write ќљ for the law of N T and suppose that ќљ is equivalent to the Lebesgue measure on its interval support [0, b], b вЙ§вИЮ. Then the law of N T is given by: P

    N T вЙ•y

    = exp

    вИТ y 0 ds s вИТѕХ(s)

    , 0 вЙ§y вЙ§b, (5.1) where ѕХ(x) = E[NT |N T = x], i.e. E[NT h(NT )] = E[ѕХ(N T )h(N T )], for any positive Borel function h. Remark. Note that the above formula in the special case when NT = ѕХ(N T ) a.s., and actually in the more general context of time-homogeneous diffusions, was obtained already by Lehoczky [16]. Vallois [35] studied this issues in detail and has some more general formulae. Proof. Suppose first that EN T < вИЮ. With the help of the max-martingales for any f : R+ вЖТR, bounded with compact support, we get that E ) F(N T ) вИТf(N T )(N T вИТNT )

    = 0. Upon conditioning with respect to N T we obtain:

    On Brownian Motion and its Supremum 525 E ) F(N T ) вИТf(N T )(N T вИТѕХ(N T ))

    = 0. (5.2) We can rewrite the above as a differential equation involving ќљ вИЉN T , which yields (5.1). When EN T is possibly infinite we can stop conveniently and pass to the limit. More precisely, let Rn = inf{t : N t = n} and ѕХn(x) = E[NT вИІRn|N T вИІRn = x], x вЙ§n. A refinement of the argumentation above shows that for any x < n, P(N T вИІRn вЙ•x) = exp{вИТ x 0 ds/(s вИТѕХn(s))}. Observe however that for any 0 вЙ§x < n, P(N T вИІRn вЙ•x) = P(N T вЙ•x) and ѕХn(x) = ѕХ(x). In consequence, letting n вЖТвИЮ, we see that (5.1) holds for all x > 0. вЬЈ Let us define the Az¬іemaвАУYor stopping time, as suggested above, through TѕХ = inf{t вЙ•0 : Nt = ѕХ(N t)}, for some strictly increasing, continuous function ѕХ : R+ вЖТR. Obviously NTѕХ = ѕХ(N TѕХ). We look for a function ѕХ = ѕХ¬µ such that NTѕХ¬µ вИЉ¬µ. To this end, we take x in the support of ¬µ and write ¬µ(x) = P(NTѕХ¬µ вЙ•x) = ќљ(ѕХвИТ1 ¬µ (x)) = exp

    вИТ ѕХвИТ1 ¬µ (x) 0 ds s вИТѕХ¬µ(s)

    , which may be considered as an equation on ѕХ¬µ in terms of ¬µ. Solving this equation, one obtains ѕХвИТ1 ¬µ (x) = ќ®¬µ(x) = 1 ¬µ(x)

    [x,вИЮ) s d¬µ(s), (5.3) the HardyвАУLittlewood maximal function, or barycentre function, of ¬µ. Proposition 5.2 (Az¬іemaвАУYor [3]). Let ¬µ be a centered probability measure. Define the function ќ®¬µ through (5.3) for x such that ¬µ(x) вИИ(0, 1) and put ќ®¬µ(x) = 0 for x such that ¬µ(x) = 1, ќ®¬µ(x) = x for x such that ¬µ(x) = 0. Then the stopping time T¬µ := inf{t вЙ•0 : N t вЙ•ќ®¬µ(Nt)} satisfies NT¬µ вИЉ¬µ and (NtвИІT¬µ : t вЙ•0) is a uniformly integrable martingale. The arguments presented above contain the principal ideas behind the Az¬іemaвАУ Yor solution to the Skorohod embedding problem. Naturally, they work well for measures with positive density on R. A complete proof of Proposition 5.2 requires some rigorous arguments involving, for example, a limit procedure, but this can be done, as shown by Michel Pierre [23]. We now develop a link between formula (5.1) and work of Rogers [25]. Let us carry out some formal computations. Write ѕБ for the law of the couple (N T , N T вИТNT ) вИИR+ √Ч R+, and ќљ for its first marginal (as above). Differen- tiating (5.1) we find dќљ(y) = вИТќљ(y)dy y вИТѕХ(y), hence ќљ(y)dy = (y вИТѕХ(y))dќљ(y), which we rewrite in terms of ѕБ

    (y,вИЮ)√ЧR+ ѕБ(ds, dx)

    dz =

    (0,вИЮ) zѕБ(ds, dz). (5.4)

    526 Jan Ob^l¬іoj and Marc Yor The last condition appears in Rogers [25] and is shown to be equivalent to the existence of a continuous, uniformly integrable martingale (NtвИІT : t вЙ•0) such that (N T , N T вИТNT ) вИЉѕБ. Our formulation in (5.1) is less general, as it is not valid when the law of BT has atoms. However, when it is valid, it provides an intuitive reading of (5.4). To close this section, we point out that arguments similar to the ones presented above, can be developed to obtain a solution to the Skorohod em- bedding problem for |N| based on LN: it suffices to use the martingales given by (4.9) instead of those given by (4.3). For a probability measure m on R+, define the dual HardyвАУLittlewood function (see Ob`l¬іoj and Yor [20]) through ѕИm(x) =

    [0,x] y m(y)dm(y), for x such that m(x) вИИ(0, 1), (5.5) and put ѕИm(x) = 0 for x such that m(xвИТ) = 1 and ѕИm(x) = вИЮfor x such that m(x+) = 0. Proposition 5.3 (Vallois [33], ObPl¬іoj and Yor [20]). Let m be a non- atomic probability measure on R+ and define the function ѕИm through (5.5). Let ѕХm(y) = inf{x вЙ•0 : ѕИm > y} be the right inverse of ѕИm. Then the stopping time T m := inf{t > 0 : |N|t = ѕХm(LN t )} satisfies |N|T m вИЉm. Furthermore, (NtвИІT m : t вЙ•0) is a uniformly integrable martingale if and only if вИЮ 0 xdm(x) < вИЮ. The theorem is valid for probability measures with atoms upon proper exten- sion of the definition of ѕИ¬µ. We note that the law of LN T m is given through P(LN T m вЙ•x) = exp

    вИТ x 0 ds ѕХm(s)

    (cf. (5.4) in [20]). An easy analogue of Proposition 5.1, is that this formula is also true for general stopping time T, such that the law of LT has a density, with the function ѕХm replaced by ѕХ(x) = E

    |NT | LN T = x

    . 5.2 Bounds on the laws of N T and LN T We present a classical bound on the law of N T , which was first obtained by Blackwell and Dubins [4] and Dubins and Gilat [7] (see also Az¬іema and Yor [2], Kertz and R¬®osler [14] and Hobson [13]). Proposition 5.4. Let ¬µ be a centered probability measure and T a stopping time, such that NT вИЉ¬µ and (NtвИІT : t вЙ•0) is a uniformly integrable martin- gale. Then the following bound is true: P(N T вЙ•ќї) вЙ§P(N T¬µ вЙ•ќї) = ¬µ(ќ® вИТ1 ¬µ (ќї)), ќї вЙ•0, (5.6) where T¬µ is given in Proposition 5.2, ќ®¬µ is displayed in (5.3) and its inverse is taken right-continuous.

    On Brownian Motion and its Supremum 527 In other words, for the partial order given by tails domination, the law of N T is bounded by the image of ¬µ through the HardyвАУLittlewood maximal function (5.3). Proof. Suppose for simplicity that ¬µ has a positive density, which is equiv- alent to ќ®¬µ being continuous and strictly increasing. We consider the max- martingale (4.3) for f(x) = 1(xвЙ•ќї), for some fixed ќї > 0, and apply the optional stopping theorem. We obtain: ќїP(N T вЙ•ќї) = E ) NT 1(N T вЙ•ќї)

    , (5.7) that is DoobвАЩs maximal equality for continuous-time martingales. Let p := P(N T вЙ•ќї). As NT вИЉ¬µ, then the RHS is smaller than E[NT 1(NT вЙ•¬µвИТ1(p))] which, by definition in (5.3), is equal to pќ®¬µ(¬µвИТ1(p)). We obtain therefore: ќїP(N T вЙ•ќї) = ќїp вЙ§E ) NT 1(NT вЙ•¬µвИТ1(p))

    = pќ®¬µ

    ¬µвИТ1(p)

    , hence ќї вЙ§ќ®¬µ

    ¬µвИТ1(p)

    , thus p вЙ§¬µ

    ќ® вИТ1 ¬µ (ќї)

    since ¬µ is decreasing. (5.8) To end the proof is suffice to note that P(BT¬µ вЙ•ќї) = ¬µ(ќ® вИТ1 ¬µ (ќї)), which is obvious from the definition of T¬µ. вЬЈ Investigation of similar quantities with N T replaced by T is also possible. Numerous authors studied the limit вИЪ ќїP(T вЙ•ќї). It goes back to Az¬іema, Gundy and Yor [1] with more recent works by Elworthy, Li and Yor [10] and Peskir and Shiryaev [21]4. Integrating (5.6) one obtains bounds on the expectation of N T . Another bound on EN T can be obtained using the max-martingales. Take f(x) = 2x, then by (4.3) the process N 2 t вИТ2N tNt = (N tвИТNt)2вИТN 2 t is a local martingale. For a stopping time T with EвЯ®NвЯ©T < вИЮ, we have then E(N T вИТNT )2 = EN 2 T , which yields: EN T = E(N T вИТNT ) вЙ§ H E(N T вИТNT )2 = H EN 2 T

    EвЯ®NвЯ©T . (5.9) The inequality EN T вЙ§

    EвЯ®NвЯ©T extends to any stopping time, through the monotone convergence theorem. This inequality was generalized for Bessel processes by Dubins, Shepp and Shiryaev [9] and for Brownian motion with drift by Peskir and Shiryaev [22]. These problems are also in close relation with the so-called Russian options developed mainly by L. Shepp and A. Shiryaev [29, 30, 31]. More elaborate arguments, using optimal stopping, yield: E ) sup sвЙ§T |Ns|

    вЙ§

    2EвЯ®NвЯ©T , (5.10) 4See also the note by Liptser and Novikov in this volume.

    528 Jan Ob^l¬іoj and Marc Yor as shown in Dubins and Schwarz [8]. We also learned from L. Dubins [6] that E ) sup sвЙ§T Ns вИТinf sвЙ§T Ns

    вЙ§

    3EвЯ®NвЯ©T , (5.11) and in (5.9), (5.10) and (5.11) the constants are optimal. Bounds on the law of the local time similar to (5.6) were studied in detail by Vallois [34]. He showed that the law of the local time of a uniformly in- tegrable continuous martingale with a fixed terminal distribution is bounded from above and below in the convex order. Vallois [34] also gave explicit con- structions which realize the upper and lower bounds. We derive now a complement to the study of Vallois [34]. Namely, we examine the possible laws of the local time, when the distribution of the absolute value of the terminal value of a martingale is fixed. We follow the same approach as above, only starting with the martingales given in Corollary 4.1. Proposition 5.5. Let m be a probability on R+ with вИЮ 0 xdm(x) < вИЮ, and let T be a stopping time, such that |NT | вИЉm and (NtвИІT : t вЙ•0) is a uniformly integrable martingale. Denote ѕБT the law of LN T . Then the following bound is true E ) LN T вИТѕБвИТ1 T (p) + вЙ§E ) LN T m вИТѕБвИТ1 T m(pвИЧ) + , p вИИ[0, 1], (5.12) where T m is given in Proposition 5.3, the inverses ѕБвИТ1 ¬Ј are taken left- continuous and pвИЧ= m

    mвИТ1(p)

    вЙ•p. Remarks. It follows from (5.14) in our proof that the RHS of (5.12) is inde- pendent of N and equal to вИЮ mвИТ1(p) xdm(x). For m with no atoms, pвИЧвЙ°p. In other words, for m with no atoms, we have ѕБT в™ѓѕБT m, where ѕБT m is the image of m through the dual HardyвАУLittlewood function ѕИm, and вАЬв™ѓвАЭ indicates the excess wealth order, defined through ѕБ1 в™ѓѕБ2 вЗФвИАp вИИ[0, 1]

    [ѕБ1вИТ1(p),вИЮ) xdѕБ1(x) вЙ§

    [ѕБ2вИТ1(p),вИЮ) xdѕБ2(x). (5.13) We point out that the excess wealth order, was introduced recently by Shaked and Shanthikumar in [28] (it is also called the right-spread or- der, cf. Fernandez-Ponce et al. [11]) and studied in Kochar et al. [15], and the above justifies some further investigation. Since in our case we have ELT = ELT m = вИЮ 0 xdm(x), the excess wealth order is equivalent to the TTT and NBUE orders and implies the convex order (see Kochar et al. [15]). We recall that Vallois [34] showed that when the law of NT is fixed, NT вИЉ¬µ, then the law of LT is bounded in the convex ordering of proba- bility measures and he gave an explicit construction of the stopping time T ¬µ V which realizes the upper bound. If we associate with m its symmetric exten- sion on R defined via ¬µm(x) = m(x)/2, x вЙ•0, then we have NT m вИЉ¬µm and

    On Brownian Motion and its Supremum 529 our stopping time T m coincides with the stopping time of Vallois, T m = T ¬µm V . However, typically, there exist many measures ¬µ on R such that if X вИЉ¬µ then |X| вИЉm. In consequence, our result which states that under |NT | вИЉm, T m maximizes the law of LT in the excess wealth order and hence in the convex order, complements the result of Vallois [34]. Proof. Our proof relies on the martingales given in (4.9). Assertion (5.12) is trivial for p = 1. It holds also for p = 0, as it just means that ELN T = ELN T m, which is true, as both quantities are equal to вИЮ 0 xdm(x). This follows from the fact that (LN t вИТ|Nt| : t вЙ•0) is a local martingale and ELN T вИІRn ÷А ELT by monotone convergence, and E|NT вИІRn| вЖТE|NT | by uniform integrability of (NT вИІt : t вЙ•0), where Rn is a localizing sequence for LN вИТ|N|. Take p вИИ(0, 1), z = ѕБвИТ1 T (p) and put g(x) = 1(x>z). Using the optional stopping theorem for the martingale in (4.9), we obtain: E ) LN T вИТz + = E ) |NT |1(LN T >z)

    , hence (5.14) E ) LN T вИТz + вЙ§E ) |NT |1(|NT |вЙ•mвИТ1(p))

    = E ) |NT m|1(ѕХm(LN T m)вЙ•mвИТ1(p))

    = E ) |NT m|1(LN T mвЙ•ѕБвИТ1 T m(pвИЧ))

    = E ) LN T m вИТѕБвИТ1 T m(pвИЧ) + , which ends the proof. вЬЈ 5.3 Penalizations of Brownian motion with a function of its supremum We sketch here yet another instance, where the MM-harmonic functions play a natural role. Let f : R+ вЖТR+ denote a probability density on R+, and consider the family of probabilities (Wf t : t вЙ•0) on вД¶= C(R+, R), where Xt(ѕЙ) = ѕЙ(t), and Fs = ѕГ(Xu : u вЙ§s), FвИЮ= . sвЙ•0 Fs, which are defined by: Wf t = f(Xt) EW ) f(Xt) ¬Ј W, (5.15) where W denotes the Wiener measure. Roynette, Vallois and Yor [27, 26] show that Wf t (w) вИТвИТвИТвЖТ tвЖТвИЮWf вИЮ, i.e.: вИАs > 0, вИАќУs вИИFs, Wf t (ќУs) вИТвИТвИТвЖТ tвЖТвИЮWf вИЮ(ќУs), (5.16) where the probability Wf вИЮmay be described as follows: for s > 0 and ќУs вИИFs, Wf вИЮ(ќУs) = EW

    1ќУsSf s ), where Sf s = 1 вИТF(Xs) + f(Xs)(Xs вИТXs) = 1 вИТ s 0 f(Xu)dXu. (5.17)

    530 Jan Ob^l¬іoj and Marc Yor We recognize instantly in the process Sf the max-martingale given by (4.3). Another description of Wf вИЮis that, under this measure the process Xt satisfies: Xt = Xf t вИТ t 0 f(Xu)du 1 вИТF(Xu) + f(Xu)(Xu вИТXu), (5.18) where Xf is a Wf вИЮ-Brownian motion, and F(x) = x 0 f(u)du. Naturally, we see the max-martingales (4.3) intervene again. Further descriptions of Wf вИЮ are given in Roynette, Vallois and Yor [26]. 6 A more general viewpoint: the balayage formula To end this paper, we propose a slightly more general viewpoint on results mentioned sofar. In order to present the (B, B)-harmonic functions (4.2), we relied on ItЋЖoвАЩs formula. However, it is possible to obtain these functions (and the corresponding martingales) as a consequence of the so-called balayage formula (see, e.g. Revuz and Yor [24] pp. 260-264 and a series of papers in [5]). Let (ќ£t : t вЙ•0) denote a continuous semimartingale, with ќ£0 = 0, and define gt = sup{s вЙ§t : ќ£s = 0}, dt = inf{s > t : ќ£s = 0}. Then, the balayage formula is: for any locally bounded predictable process (ku : u вЙ•0), one has: kgtќ£t = t 0 kgsdќ£s, t вЙ•0. (6.1) The intuitive meaning of this formula is that a вАЬglobal multiplicationвАЭ of ќ£ over its excursions away from 0 coincides with the stochastic integral of the multiplicator with respect to (dќ£s). As applications, we give some examples: вАҐ for ќ£t = N t вИТNt and ku = f(N u), f a locally integrable function, (6.1) reads f(N t)(N t вИТNt) = t 0 f(N s)d(N s вИТNs), which yields (4.3); вАҐ for ќ£t = Nt and ku = f(LN u ), f a locally integrable function, we obtain f(LN t )Nt = t 0 f(LN s )dNs; вАҐ for ќ£t = |N|t and ku = f(LN u ), f a locally integrable function, we obtain f(LN t )|Nt| = t 0 f(LN s )d|Ns|. This in turn is equal to t 0 f(LN s )sgn(Ns)dNs вИТF(LN t ) by ItЋЖoвАУTanakaвАЩs formula, and so we ob- tain (4.9). 7 Closing remarks Max-martingales, or max-harmonic functions, described in (4.2) and (4.3), occur in a number of studies of either Brownian motion, or martingales. They often lead to simple calculations, and/or formulae, mainly due to the (obvious,

    On Brownian Motion and its Supremum 531 but crucial) fact that dN t is carried by {t : Nt = N t}. This has been used again and again by a number of researchers, e.g: Hobson and co-workers, and, of course, Albert Shiryaev and co-workers. We tried to present in this article several such instances. More generally, this leads to a вАЬfirst order stochastic calculusвАЭ, as in Section 6, which is quite elementary in comparison with ItЋЖoвАЩs second order calculus. References 1.     On the Fundamental Solution of the KolmogorovвАУShiryaev Equation Goran PESKIR вИЧ Department of Mathematical Sciences, University of Aarhus, Ny Munkegade, 8000 Aarhus, Denmark. goran@maths.manchester.ac.uk Summary. We derive an integral representation for the fundamental solution of the Kolmogorov forward equation ft = вИТ((1+¬µx)f)x + (ќљ x2f)xx associated with the Shiryaev process X solving the linear SDE dXt = (1+¬µXt) dt + ѕГXt dBt where ¬µ вИИIR, ќљ = ѕГ2/2 > 0 and B is a standard Brownian motion. The method of proof is based upon deriving and inverting a Laplace transform. Basic properties of X needed in the proof are reviewed. Key words: Shiryaev process, Kolmogorov forward equation, integral of geometric Brownian motion, parabolic partial differential equation, Laplace transform, conflu- ent hypergeometric function, modified Bessel function, HartmanвАУWatson distribu- tion, HankelвАЩs contour integral. Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 60J60, 35K15, 60J65, 35C15 1 Introduction We consider the Kolmogorov forward equation: ft = вИТ((1+¬µx)f)x + (ќљ x2f)xx (1.1) associated with the Shiryaev process X = (Xt)tвЙ•0 solving: вИЧNetwork in Mathematical Physics and Stochastics (funded by the Danish Na- tional Research Foundation) and Centre for Analytical Finance (funded by the Dan- ish Social Science Research Council).

    536 G. Peskir dXt = (1+¬µXt) dt + ѕГXt dBt (1.2) with X0 = x0 in IR where ¬µ вИИIR, ќљ = ѕГ2/2 > 0 and B = (Bt)tвЙ•0 is a standard Brownian motion. The problem of finding the fundamental solution f = f(t, x) of (1.1) appears naturally in a number of fields (most notably in sequential analysis and financial mathematics). The unique (strong) solution of (1.2) is given by: Xt = Yt

    x0 + t 0 1 Ys ds

    (1.3) where Y = (Yt)tвЙ•0 is a geometric Brownian motion solving: dYt = ¬µYt dt + ѕГYt dBt (1.4) with Y0 = 1. The unique (strong) solution of (1.4) is given by: Yt = exp

    ѕГBt + (¬µвИТќљ)t

    . (1.5) Inserting (1.5) into (1.3) one obtains an explicit representation of X in terms of B. From this representation and the invariance of B on time reversal one sees that the following identity in law is satisfied: Xt law = t 0 Ys ds (1.6) when x0 = 0. This shows that the problem of finding the fundamental solution of (1.1) when x0 = 0 is equivalent to the problem of finding the distribution of the random variable t 0 Ys ds. The latter problem has been intensively studied in the last 10-15 years (see [20], [4], [15] and the references therein) but none of these approaches attempts to tackle the forward equation (1.1) directly (see [14] for numerical results of a related approach). The purpose of the present paper is to search for the fundamental so- lution of (1.1) by simple probabilistic and analytic means (cf. [5]). It will be seen below that this approach readily leads to the Laplace transform of t вЖТ x 0 f(t, y) dy expressed in terms of confluent hypergeometric functions (modified Bessel functions) providing a link to the Hartman-Watson distrib- ution [9]. The problem thus reduces to inverting the Laplace transform. This can be done using HankelвАЩs contour integrals for these functions (cf.[19]) lead- ing to representations of the solution in terms of single or double integrals. For simplicity and comparison we only treat a particular case of the equation (1.1) in complete detail. A treatment of other cases is briefly indicated and it is hoped that their study will be continued. A disadvantage of the previous inversion approach is that the analytic expressions obtained are numerically unstable for small t. This fact was ob- served independently by several authors (see e.g. [2]). While this may not be

    The KolmogorovвАУShiryaev Equation 537 such a big drawback for applications to Asian options of European type (cf. [3]), in the case of Asian options of American type such a numerical stability becomes fundamentally important (see [13]). A similar need for stable analytic expressions arises in quickest detection problems (sequential analysis) when the horizon is finite (see [8]). Further research of the KolmogorovвАУShiryaev equation (1.1) thus appears to be necessary. The stochastic differential equation (1.2) has been derived by Shiryaev [16, Eq. (9)] in the context of quickest detection problems (sequential analy- sis). These problems play a prominent role in diverse applications ranging from quality control in industry to structural analysis of DNA in medicine. Applications in financial data analysis (detection of arbitrage) are recently discussed in [17]. The Kolmogorov backward and forward equation (of which (1.1) is a particular case) have been derived in [11]. In the physical literature the forward equation is often referred to as the FokkerвАУPlanck equation (cf. [7], [12]). 2 The Shiryaev process In this section we present basic properties of the Shiryaev process X solving (1.2). Note that the initial point x0 of X belongs to IR and may be negative as well. 1. The Shiryaev process X is a strong Markov process with continuous sample paths (a diffusion process). The drift of X is given by ¬µ(x) = 1 вИТ¬µx and the diffusion coefficient of X is given by ѕГ(x) = ѕГx. Recall that ¬µ вИИIR and ќљ = ѕГ2/2 > 0. 2. Since ѕГ(0) = 0 we see that the state space of X splits into (вИТвИЮ, 0] and [0, вИЮ). From the representation (1.3) it is evident that: The point 0 is an entrance boundary point for [0, вИЮ). (2.1) Likewise it will be formally verified below that: The point 0 is an exit boundary point for (вИТвИЮ, 0]. (2.2) 3. The scale function of X is given by: s(x) = x 1 zвИТ¬µ/ќљ e1/ќљz dz for x > 0 (2.3) s(x) = 1 вИТx zвИТ¬µ/ќљ eвИТ1/ќљz dz for x < 0. (2.4) Hence s(0+) = вИТвИЮalways, and s(вИЮ) = вИЮif and only if ¬µ вЙ§ќљ. This shows that X is recurrent in [0, вИЮ) if and only if ¬µ вЙ§ќљ. Note also that s(вИТвИЮ) = вИТвИЮ if and only if ¬µ вЙ§ќљ, and s(0вИТ) < вИЮalways. This shows that X exists (вИТвИЮ, 0]

    538 G. Peskir almost surely at 0 if and only if ¬µ вЙ§ќљ. We also see that X can never be recurrent in (вИТвИЮ, 0]. 4. The speed measure of X is given by: m(dx) = ќљвИТ1 xвИТ2+¬µ/ќљ eвИТ1/ќљx dx for x > 0 (2.5) m(dx) = ќљвИТ1 (вИТx)вИТ2+¬µ/ќљ eвИТ1/ќљx dx for x < 0. (2.6) Since вИЮ 0 m(dx) = ќљ¬µ/ќљ ќУ(1вИТ¬µ/ќљ) < вИЮif and only if ¬µ < ќљ, it follows that X has an invariant density function on [0, вИЮ) given by: f(x) = 1 ќљ1вИТ¬µ/ќљ ќУ(1вИТ¬µ/ќљ) 1 x2вИТ¬µ/ќљ eвИТ1/ќљx for x > 0 (2.7) if and only if ¬µ < ќљ. Noting that 0 вИТвИЮm(dx) = вИЮwe see that X cannot have an invariant density function on (вИТвИЮ, 0] as already indicated above. 5. By the law of iterated logarithm for B one easily sees that вИЮ 0 Ys ds < вИЮ almost surely if and only if ¬µ < ќљ. Hence when ¬µ < ќљ we find using (1.3) and (1.6) that: Xt d вИТвЖТ вИЮ 0 Ys ds (2.8) as t вЖТвИЮwhere the density function of вИЮ 0 Ys ds is given by (2.7) above. Likewise one sees that вИЮ 0 (1/Ys) ds < вИЮalmost surely if and only if ¬µ > ќљ. Hence when ¬µ > ќљ we find using (1.3) that: Xt вЖТ+вИЮ if x0 + вИЮ 0 (1/Ys) ds > 0 (2.9) Xt вЖТвИТвИЮ if x0 + вИЮ 0 (1/Ys) ds < 0 (2.10) as t вЖТвИЮ. The probabilities of the latter two events can readily be computed upon noting that the density function of вИЮ 0 (1/Ys) ds is given by: g(x) = 1 ќљ¬µ/ќљвИТ1 ќУ(¬µ/ќљвИТ1) 1 x¬µ/ќљ eвИТ1/ќљx for x > 0 (2.11) when ¬µ > ќљ. This follows from the identity in law stated after (2.8) above with a new drift ЋЖ¬µ = 2ќљ вИТ¬µ and a new Brownian motion ЋЖB = вИТB. Another way to compute these probabilities is to make use of the scale function in (2.4). This gives that the probability of the event in (2.9) equals one minus the probability of the event in (2.10) which, in turn, is equal to the ratio (S(0вИТ)вИТS(x0))/(S(0вИТ)вИТS(вИТвИЮ)). Finally, when ¬µ = ќљ then X is recurrent in [0, вИЮ) no matter if x0 is positive or negative. Recall that X hits zero almost surely if x0 < 0 never returning to zero again. 6. A formal verification of (2.1) and (2.2) can be made upon invoking the standard boundary classification for one-dimensional diffusions (cf. [6]).

    The KolmogorovвАУShiryaev Equation 539 Firstly, since mвА≤ вИИL1((0, вИЮ)) and s mвА≤ вИИL1((0, вИЮ)) but sвА≤ /вИИL1((0, вИЮ)) we see that (2.1) follows. Secondly, since mвА≤ /вИИL1((вИТвИЮ, 0)) and sвА≤m вИИ L1((вИТвИЮ, 0)) we see that (2.2) follows as claimed. 7. We will conclude this section by deriving boundary conditions which will be used in the next section. For this, let F denote the transition distribution function of X, and let f denote the transition density function of X. Since X is a time-homogeneous Markov process, it is no restriction to assume that the initial time point equals zero. We thus have: F(0, x0; t, x) = P(Xt вЙ§x | X0 = x0) (2.12) f(0, x0; t, x) = Fx(0, x0; t, x). (2.13) In the sequel we will only study the case when x0 вЙ•0. From the facts exposed above we then know that the state space of X equals [0, вИЮ) and that X can only start at 0 and never arrive at it (recall (2.1) above). Hence the following boundary conditions at 0 are in agreement with what we would expect to hold: f(0, x0; t, 0+) = 0 (2.14) fx(0, x0; t, 0+) = 0. (2.15) In fact, all higher derivatives of f with respect to x satisfy the same zero condition, but we will only make use of the conditions (2.14) and (2.15) below. 8. A formal proof of (2.14) and (2.15) is simple. Denote Xt from (1.3) by Xx0 t to indicate its dependence on x0, note that Xx0 t

    0, and set Z = 1/Xx0 t . Then for any p > 0 given and fixed we find by the Markov inequality that: F(0, x0; t, h) = P(Xt вЙ§h | X0 = x0) = P(Xx0 t вЙ§h) (2.16) = P(Z вЙ•1/h) = P(Zp вЙ•1/hp) вЙ§hp E(Zp) where E(Zp) < вИЮby the well-known properties of B. From (2.16) we see that: F(0, x0; t, h) = O(hp) (2.17) as h вЖТh0 for h0 вЙ•0 whenever p > 0 is given and fixed. Taking p = 3 and using (2.17) one finds that (2.14) and (2.15) hold as claimed. 3 The fundamental solution In this section we study the problem of finding the fundamental solution of the KolmogorovвАУShiryaev equation (1.1). For simplicity we will only examine the case when x0 вЙ•0 (cf. Section 2). By the fundamental solution we thus mean a non-negative solution f = f(t, x) for t > 0 and x > 0, satisfying вИЮ 0 f(t, x) dx = 1 for each t > 0, and f(t, x) вЖТќі(xвИТx0) weakly as t вЖУ0 (where ќі denotes the Dirac delta function).

    540 G. Peskir 1. Recall that X solving (1.2) is time-homogeneous so that there is no restriction to assume that the initial time point equals zero. We will moreover suppress the dependence on 0 and x0 in (2.12) and (2.13) and simply write: F(t, x) = P(Xt вЙ§x | X0 = x0) (3.1) f(t, x) = Fx(t, x). (3.2) Standard Markovian arguments (cf. [11]) imply that the transition density function (3.2) solves the equation (1.1), and thus the initial problem is equiv- alent to the problem of finding the transition density function (3.2). 2. Let us set: g = вИТ(1+¬µx)f + (ќљ x2f)x. (3.3) Then (1.1) can be written as: ft = gx. (3.4) In view of taking the Laplace transform with respect to t and making use of the initial condition for t = 0 we shall integrate both sides of (3.4) from 0 to x upon using that: F(t, x) = x 0 f(t, y) dy. (3.5) Since g(t, 0+) = 0 by (2.14) and (2.15) this gives: Ft = g(t, x) вИТg(t, 0+) = g(t, x) = вИТ(1+¬µx)f + (ќљ x2f)x (3.6) = вИТ(1+¬µx)Fx + (ќљ x2Fx)x = ((2ќљвИТ¬µ)xвИТ1)Fx + ќљ x2Fxx. Setting ќ± = 2ќљвИТ¬µ we see that (3.6) reads: Ft = (ќ±xвИТ1)Fx + ќљ x2Fxx. (3.7) 3. To simplify technicalities we will assume that x0 = 0 in the sequel. Then F satisfies the following initial condition: F(0, x) = 1 (3.8) for all x вЙ•0. Moreover, since Xt remains positive almost surely for all t > 0, we see that F satisfy the following boundary conditions: F(t, 0+) = 0 (3.9) F(t, вИЮ) = 1 (3.10) for all t > 0. 4. Taking the Laplace transform in (3.7) with respect to t upon setting: ¬ѓF(ќї, x) = вИЮ 0 eвИТќїtF(t, x) dt (3.11)

    The KolmogorovвАУShiryaev Equation 541 we obtain the following ordinary differential equation: ќї ¬ѓF вИТF(0, x) = (ќ±xвИТ1) ¬ѓFx + ќљ x2 ¬ѓFxx. (3.12) (Note that by taking the Laplace transform with respect to x, we would arrive instead to a new second-order partial differential equation. This is in sharp contrast with the equation studied in [5] where one has x instead of x2 in (1.1) which makes such a transform profitable since the new partial differential equation is of the first order.) Making use of (3.8) we see that the equation (3.12) reads: ќљ x2 ¬ѓFxx + (ќ±xвИТ1) ¬ѓFx вИТќї ¬ѓF = вИТ1. (3.13) By (3.9) and (3.10) we obtain the following boundary conditions: ¬ѓF(ќї, 0+) = 0 (3.14) ¬ѓF(ќї, вИЮ) = 1/ќї. (3.15) 5. Note that a particular solution of the equation (3.13) is given by ¬ѓF вЙ°1/ќї. To find the general solution we need to consider the homogeneous equation which reads: x2yвА≤вА≤ + (Ax+B)yвА≤ + C y = 0 (3.16) where A = ќ±/ќљ = 2вИТ¬µ/ќљ, B = вИТ1/ќљ and C = вИТќї/ќљ. A standard substitution for this equation (cf. (2.188) in [10, p. 447]) is given by: y(x) = (1/xp) z(B/x). (3.17) Inserting (3.17) into (3.16) one finds that z = z(x) solves the Kummer equa- tion: xzвА≤вА≤ + (b вИТx)zвА≤ вИТax = 0 (3.18) where a and b are given by: a = p (3.19) b = 2(p+1)вИТA (3.20) and p > 0 solves the quadratic equation: p2 + (1вИТA)p + C = 0. (3.21) Solving (3.21) we find that: a = 1 2

    1 вИТ¬µ ќљ +

    1 вИТ¬µ ќљ 2 + 4ќї ќљ

    (3.22) b = 1 +

    1 вИТ¬µ ќљ 2 + 4ќї ќљ . (3.23)

    542 G. Peskir 6. Two linearly independent solutions of the Kummer equation (3.18) are the confluent hypergeometric function of the first kind: M(a, b, x) = 1 + a b x + a(a+1) b(b+1) x2 2! + ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј (3.24) and the confluent hypergeometric function of the second kind U(a, b, x). (We refer to [1, pp. 504-510] for basic properties of these functions.) Summarizing the preceding facts about (3.16) and (3.17) it follows that the equation (3.13) has the general solution given by: ¬ѓF(ќї, x) = C1 xвИТa M(a, b, вИТ1/ќљx) + C2 xвИТa U(a, b, вИТ1/ќљx) + 1/ќї. (3.25) 7. Letting x вЖТвИЮand using that xвИТa M(a, b, вИТ1/ќљx) вЖТ0 it follows from (3.15) that we may take C2 = 0. Using the known relation (cf. (13.1.5) in [1, p. 504]): xa M(a, b, вИТx) = ќУ(b) ќУ(bвИТa)

    1 + O(xвИТ1)

    (3.26) as x вЖТвИЮ, we find that: xвИТa M(a, b, вИТ1/ќљx) вЖТќљa ќУ(b) ќУ(bвИТa) (3.27) as x вЖУ0. Hence by (3.14) we get: C1 = вИТќУ(bвИТa) ќї ќљa ќУ(b). (3.28) Inserting this into (3.25) upon recalling that C2 = 0, we obtain the following closed-form expression for the Laplace transform (3.11) above: ¬ѓF(ќї, x) = 1 ќї

    1 вИТќУ(bвИТa) ќУ(b) (ќљx)вИТa M(a, b, вИТ1/ќљx)

    (3.29) where a = a(ќї) and b = b(ќї) are given by (3.22) and (3.23) respectively. 8. By the inversion formula we have: F(t, x) = 1 2ѕАi c+iвИЮ cвИТiвИЮ etz ¬ѓF(z, x) dz (3.30) for any c > 0 given and fixed. The initial problem is thus reduced to com- puting the complex integral (3.30). The representation (3.29) possesses a rich structure which opens various ways to tackle the inversion problem. Some of these possibilities will now be addressed. 9. By the convolution theorem we see that: F(t, x) = 1 вИТ t 0 G(s, x) ds (3.31)

    The KolmogorovвАУShiryaev Equation 543 where the Laplace transform of s вЖТG(s, x) is given by: ¬ѓG(ќї, x) = вИЮ 0 eвИТќїs G(s, x) ds = ќУ(bвИТa) ќУ(b) (ќљx)вИТa M(a, b, вИТ1/ќљx) (3.32) upon recalling that a = a(ќї) and b = b(ќї) are given by (3.22) and (3.23) respectively. The problem thus reduces to inverting the Laplace transform on the right-hand side of (3.32). 10. Consider the case when ¬µ = 0 and ќљ = 1/2 i.e. ѕГ = 1. Then from (3.22) and (3.23) we see that a = (1/2)(1+ вИЪ 1+8ќї) and b = 2a so that: ¬ѓG(ќї, x) = ќУ(a) ќУ(2a) (x/2)вИТa M(a, 2a, вИТ2/x). (3.33) Using the well-known relation (cf. (13.6.3) in [1, p. 509]): M(p +1/2, 2p +1, 2z) = ќУ(1+p) ez (z/2)вИТp Ip(z) (3.34) where Ip(z) is the modified Bessel function of the first kind (cf.     dz ds when ¬µ = 0 and ќљ = 1/2. Clearly the formula (3.41) extends along the same lines to the case of general ќљ > 0 when ¬µ = 0. 11.     C ezzвИТa dz (3.44) where the path of integration C starts at вИТвИЮon the real axis, circles the origin in the anticlockwise direction, and returns to the starting point.
