    dz.

    The KolmogorovвАУShiryaev Equation 545 Recalling the well-known identity: LвИТ1 ќї

    eвИТwвИЪ ќ±+ќ≤ќї (t) = вИЪќ≤ w eвИТќ±t/ќ≤вИТќ≤w2/4t 2 вИЪ ѕАt3 (3.47) that is valid for all complex numbers w = w1 +iw2 such that Re(w) = w1 > 0 and Re(w2) = w2 1 вИТw2 2 > 0, letting z = reiѕХ in (3.46) and choosing C not too close to the origin in the sense that r вЙ•R where R > 0 is taken large enough, we see that it is possible to perform the inversion in (3.45) by expressing the result in terms of a double integral. A more systematic study of the expressions obtained appears worthy of further consideration. References 1. Abramowitz M., Stegun I.A.: Handbook of Mathematical Functions. The Na- tional Bureau of Standards 1964. 2. Barrieu P., Rouault A., Yor M.: A study of the HartmanвАУWatson distri- bution motivated by numerical problems related to Asian options pricing. Pr¬іepublication PMA 813, Universit¬іe Pierre et Marie Curie, Paris (2003). 3. Carr P., Schr¬®oder M.: Bessel processes, the integral of geometric Brownian mo- tion, and Asian options. Theory Probab. Appl. 48, 400вАУ425 (2004). 4. Dufresne D.: The integral of geometric Brownian motion. Adv. Appl. Probab. 33, 223вАУ241 (2001). 5. Feller W.: Two singular diffusion problems. Ann. of Math. 54, 173вАУ182 (1951). 6. Feller W.: The parabolic differential equations and the associated semi-groups of transformations. Ann. of Math. 55, 468вАУ519 (1952). 7. Fokker A.D.: Die mittlere Energie rotierender elektrischer Dipole im Strahlungs- feld. Ann. Phys. 43, 810вАУ820 (1914). 8. Gapeev P.V., Peskir G.: The Wiener disorder problem with finite horizon. Re- search Report No. 435, Dept. Theoret. Statist. Aarhus (2003). 9. Hartman P., Watson G.S.: вАЭNormalвАЭ distribution functions on spheres and the modified Bessel functions. Ann. Probab. 2, 593вАУ607 (1974). 10. Kamke E.: Differentialgleichungen. Chelsea 1948. 11. Kolmogorov A.N. ¬®Uber die analytischen Methoden in der Wahrscheinlichkeit- srechnung. Math. Ann. 104, 415вАУ458 (1931). 12. Planck M.: ¬®Uber einen Satz der statistischen Dynamik and seine Erweiterung in der Quantentheorie. Sitzungsber. Preu√Я. Akad. Wiss. 24, 324вАУ341 (1917). 13. Peskir G., Uys N.: On Asian options of American type. Research Report No. 436, Dept. Theoret. Statist. Aarhus (2003). 14. Rogers L.C.G., Shi Z.: The value of an Asian option. J. Appl. Probab. 32, 1077вАУ 1088 (1995). 15. Schr¬®oder M.: On the integral of geometric Brownian motion. Adv. Appl. Probab. 35, 159вАУ183 (2003). 16. Shiryaev A.N.: The problem of the most rapid detection of a disturbance in a stationary process. Soviet Math. Dokl. 2, 795вАУ799 (1961). 17. Shiryaev A.N.: Quickest detection problems in the technical analysis of the fi- nancial data. Math. Finance Bachelier Congress (Paris 2000), 487вАУ521, Springer 2002.

    546 G. Peskir 18. Whittaker E.T., Watson G.N.: A Course of Modern Analysis. Cambridge Univ. Press 1927. 19. Yor M.: Loi de lвАЩindice du lacet Brownien, et distribution de HartmanвАУWatson. Z. Wahrsch. Verw. Gebiete 53, 71вАУ95 (1980). 20. Yor M.: On some exponential functionals of Brownian motion. Adv. Appl. Probab. 24, 509вАУ531 (1992).

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model with a Stochastic Production Capacity HuyЋЖen PHAM Laboratoire de Probabilit¬іes et Mod`eles Al¬іeatoires CNRS, UMR 7599, Universit¬іe Paris 7, and CREST, France. pham@math.jussieu.fr Summary. This paper studies the problem of a company which expands its sto- chastic production capacity in irreversible investments by purchasing capital at a given price. The profit production function is of a very general form satisfying min- imal standard assumptions. The objective of the company is to find optimal pro- duction decisions to maximize its expected total net profit in an infinite horizon. The resulting dynamic programming principle is a singular stochastic control prob- lem. The value function is analyzed in great detail relying on viscosity solutions of the associated Bellman variational inequality: we state several general properties and in particular regularity results on the value function. We provide a complete solution with explicit expressions of the value function and the optimal control: the firm invests in capital so as to maintain its capacity above a certain threshold. This boundary can be computed quite explicitly. Key words: singular stochastic control, viscosity solutions, Skorohod problem, ir- reversible investment, production. Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 93E20, 60G40, 91B28 1 Introduction This paper focuses on the problem of a company which wants to expand its stochastic production capacity. The investments in capital for expanding the capacity are irreversible in the sense that the company cannot recover the investment by reducing the capacity. In addition, there is a transaction cost for purchasing capital. We refer to the book by Dixit and Pindick (1994) for a review where such problems occur. There are several papers in the litera- ture dealing with irreversible investments models. For instance, Kobila (1993) consider a model with deterministic capacity in an uncertain market and with- out transaction costs on buying capital. Recently, Chiarolla and Haussmann

    548 H. Pham (2003) studied an irreversible investment model in a finite horizon and ob- tained an explicit solution for a power type production function. We consider a concave production function of very general form, satisfying minimal standard assumptions. The buying capital decision is modelled by a singular control. This allows for instantaneous purchase of capital of arbitrary large amounts and various other sorts of behavior. The companyвАЩs objective is to maximize the expected net production profit over an infinite horizon, with choice of control of its buying. The resulting dynamic programming principle leads to a singular stochastic control problem. There is by now a number of papers on singular controls related to financial problems, see, e.g., Davis and Norman (1990) and Jeanblanc-Picqu¬іe and Shiryaev (1995). We solve mathematically this problem by a viscosity solution approach. This contrasts with the classical approach on investment models where the principal activity is to construct by ad hoc methods a solution to the HamiltonвАУJacobiвАУBellman equation, and validate the optimality of the so- lution by a verification theorem argument for smooth functions. We, on the other hand, start by studying and deriving the general properties via the dy- namic programming principle and viscosity arguments. Using the concavity property of the value function, we prove that it satisfies in fact the HJB in the classical C2-sense. Similar approach is done in the paper by Shreve and Soner (1994) for optimal consumption models with transaction costs. The rest of the paper goes as follows. In the next section, we give a math- ematical formulation of the problem. We analyze and derive some general properties of the value function in Section 3. By means of viscosity solutions arguments, we state in Section 4 the C2-smoothness of the value function that satisfies then in a classical sense the associated HJB equation. Section 5 is devoted to the explicit construction of the solution to this singular control problem and the optimal control. 2 Formulation of the problem Let (вД¶, F, P) be a complete probability space equipped with a filtra- tion (Ft)tвЙ•0 satisfying the usual conditions, and carrying a standard one- dimensional Brownian motion W. We consider a firm producing some output from stochastic capacity pro- duction Kt and possibly also from other inputs. The firm can buy capital at any time t at constant price p > 0. The production rate process is then described by a control L вИИA, set of right-continuous with left-hand limits adapted processes, nonnegative and nondecreasing, with L0вИТ= 0. Here, Lt represents the cumulative purchase of capital until time t. Given the initial capital k вЙ•0, and control L вИИA, the firmвАЩs capacity production evolves according to the linear SDE dKt = Kt (вИТќіdt + ќ≥dWt) + dLt, K0вИТ= k. (2.1)

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 549 Here ќі вЙ•0 is the depreciation rate of the capacity production and ќ≥ > 0 represents its volatility. The instantaneous operating profit of the firm is a function ќ†(Kt) of the capacity production. The production profit function ќ† is assumed to be continuous on R+, nondecreasing, concave and C1 on (0, вИЮ), with ќ†(0) = 0 and satisfying the standing usual Inada conditions : ќ†вА≤(0+) := lim kвЖУ0 ќ†вА≤(k) = вИЮand ќ†вА≤(вИЮ) := lim kвЖТвИЮќ†вА≤(k) = 0. (2.2) We define the FenchelвАУLegendre transform of ќ†, which is finite on (0, вИЮ) under the Inada conditions: ЋЬќ†(z) := sup kвЙ•0 [ќ†(k) вИТkz] < вИЮ, вИАz > 0. (2.3) A typical example arising from the CobbвАУDouglas production function leads to a profit function of the form ќ†(k) = Ckќ±, with C > 0, 0 < ќ± < 1. (2.4) The firmвАЩs objective is to maximize the expected profit on the infinite time horizon J(k, L) = E вИЮ 0 eвИТrt (ќ†(Kt)dt вИТpdLt)

    (2.5) over all controls L вИИA. Here r > 0 is a fixed positive discount factor. Without loss of generality, one may consider the strategies L in A for which E вИЮ 0 eвИТrtdLt

    < вИЮ, (2.6) Accordingly, we define the value function v(k) = sup LвИИA J(k, L), k вЙ•0. (2.7) Notice that since J(k, 0) вЙ•0, the value function v takes value in [0, вИЮ]. 3 Some properties of the value function Problem (2.7) is a singular stochastic control problem and its associated HamiltonвАУJacobiвАУBellman equation is min {rv вИТLv вИТќ† , вИТvвА≤ + p} = 0, (3.1) where L is the second order operator

    550 H. Pham LѕХ = 1 2ќ≥2k2ѕХвА≤вА≤ вИТќіkѕХвА≤ for any C2-function ѕХ. We first state a standard comparison theorem, which says that any smooth function, being a supersolution of the HJB equation (3.1), dominates v. To this end, we first recall in our context how ItЋЖoвАЩs formula for cadlag semimartingales (see, e.g., [8]) is written. Let ѕХ вИИC2(0, вИЮ) and let ѕД be a finite stopping time, k > 0 and L вИИA. Then, we have: eвИТrѕДѕХ(KѕД) = ѕХ(k) + ѕД 0 eвИТrt (вИТrѕХ + LѕХ) (Kt)dt + ѕД 0 eвИТrtќ≥KtѕХвА≤(Kt)dWt + ѕД 0 eвИТrtѕХвА≤(Kt)dLc t +

    0вЙ§tвЙ§ѕД eвИТrt [ѕХ(Kt) вИТѕХ(KtвИТ)] , (3.2) where Lc t = Lt вИТ

    0вЙ§sвЙ§t вИЖLs, is the continuous part of L. Proposition 3.1. Let ѕХ be a nonnegative C2-function which is a supersolu- tion on (0, вИЮ) to (3.1), i.e.: min {rѕХ вИТLѕХ вИТќ†(k) , вИТѕХвА≤ + p} вЙ•0, k > 0. (3.3) Then, v(k) вЙ§ѕХ(k), вИАk > 0. Proof. For L вИИA define the stopping time ѕДn = inf{t вЙ•0 : Kt вЙ•n} вИІn and apply ItЋЖoвАЩs formula (3.2) between 0 and ѕДn. Then, taking expectation and noting that the integrand in the stochastic integral is bounded on [0, ѕДn), we get that E

    eвИТrѕДnѕХ(KѕДn)

    = ѕХ(k) + E ѕДn 0 eвИТrt (вИТrѕХ + LѕХ) (Kt)dt

    • E ѕДn 0 eвИТrtѕХвА≤(Kt)dLc t

    • E п£Ѓ п£∞ 0вЙ§tвЙ§ѕДn eвИТrt [ѕХ(Kt) вИТѕХ(KtвИТ)] п£є п£ї. Since ѕХвА≤ вЙ§p, and Kt вИТKtвИТ= вИЖLt, the mean-value theorem implies that ѕХ(Kt) вИТѕХ(KtвИТ) вЙ§pвИЖLt.

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 551 Using again the inequality ѕХвА≤ вЙ§p in the integrals with respect to dLc and taking into account that вИТrѕХ + LѕХ вЙ§вИТќ†, we obtain: E

    eвИТrѕДnѕХ(KѕДn)

    вЙ§ѕХ(k) вИТE ѕДn 0 eвИТrtќ†(Kt)dt

    • E ѕДn 0 eвИТrtpdLc t

    • E п£Ѓ п£∞ 0вЙ§tвЙ§ѕДn eвИТrtpвИЖLt п£є п£ї = ѕХ(k) вИТE ѕДn 0 eвИТrtќ†(Kt)dt

    • E ѕДn 0 eвИТrtpdLt

    , and so E ѕДn 0 eвИТrt (ќ†(Kt)dt вИТpdLt)

    • E

    eвИТrѕДnѕХ(KѕДn)

    вЙ§ѕХ(k). Since ѕХ is nonnegative, ѕХ(k) вЙ•E ѕДn 0 eвИТrtќ†(Kt)dt

    вИТE вИЮ 0 eвИТrtpdLt

    . Applying FatouвАЩs lemma we get that E вИЮ 0 eвИТrt (ќ†(Kt)dt вИТpdLt)

    вЙ§ѕХ(k), and so, finally, v(k) вЙ§ѕХ(k) from the arbitrariness of L. вЬЈ We now give some properties on the value function v. Lemma 3.1. For all k вЙ•0 and l вЙ•0, we have: v(k) вЙ•вИТpl + v(k + l). (3.4) Proof. For L вИИA we consider the control ЋЬL with ЋЬL0вИТ= 0 and ЋЬLt = Lt +l, for t вЙ•0. Let ЋЬK be the solution of (2.1) with the control ЋЬL and initial condition ЋЬK0вИТ= k. Then, ЋЬKt = Kt + l for t вЙ•0, and so ЋЬL вИИA. Thus, v(k) вЙ•J(k, ЋЬL) = E вИЮ 0 eвИТrt ќ†( ЋЬKt)dt вИТpdЋЬLt

    = J(k + l, L) вИТpl. We obtain the required result from the arbitrariness of L. вЬЈ Moreover, recalling the standing assumption (2.3), we have: Lemma 3.2. The value function v is finite and for any q вИИ[0, p] 0 вЙ§v(k) вЙ§ ЋЬќ†((r + ќі)q) r + kq, k вЙ•0. (3.5)

    552 H. Pham Proof. The zero lower bound has been already noticed in Section 2. To prove the upper bound, consider for q вИИ[0, p] the nonnegative function ѕХ(k) = kq + ЋЬќ†((r + ќі)q) r . Then, ѕХвА≤ вЙ§p and rѕХ вИТLѕХ вИТќ† = ЋЬќ†((r + ќі)q) + (r + ќі)kq вИТќ†(k) вЙ•0, вИАk вЙ•0, by definition of ЋЬќ† in (2.3). This implies that the nonnegative function ѕХ is a super-solution to (3.1), and we conclude with Proposition 3.1. вЬЈ Lemma 3.3. a) The value function v is nondecreasing, concave and contin- uous on (0, вИЮ). b) We have the inequalities: 0 вЙ§v(0+) вЙ§ ЋЬ ќ†((r+ќі)p) r . Proof. a) The nondecreasing monotonicity of v follows from the nondecreas- ing property of the process K with respect to the initial condition k given an admissible control L, and from the nondecreasing monotonicity of ќ†. The proof of concavity of v is standard: it is established by considering convex combinations of initial states and controls and using the linearity of dynamics (2.1) and concavity of ќ†. b) The limit v(0+) exists from the nondecreasing property of v. By taking q = p in the inequality of Lemma 3.2, we obtain the required estimation on this limit. вЬЈ Since v is concave on (0, вИЮ), it admits a right derivative vвА≤ +(k) and a left derivative vвА≤ вИТ(k) at any k > 0, and vвА≤ +(k) вЙ§vвА≤ вИТ(k). Moreover, inequality (3.4) shows that vвА≤ вИТ(k) вЙ§p, вИАk > 0. (3.6) We then define the so-called no-transaction region : NT = 1 k > 0 : vвА≤ вИТ(k) < p 2 . Lemma 3.4. There exists kb вИИ[0, вИЮ] such that: NT = (kb, вИЮ), (3.7) v is differentiable on (0, kb) and vвА≤(k) = p on B = (0, kb). (3.8) Proof. Put kb = inf{k вЙ•0 : vвА≤ +(k) < p}. Then p вЙ§vвА≤ +(k) вЙ§vвА≤ вИТ(k) if k < kb. Together with (3.6), this proves (3.8). Finally, the concavity of v shows (3.7). вЬЈ Remark 3.1. We shall see later that 0 < kb < вИЮ, and the optimal strategy for the firm consists in doing nothing when it is in the region NT = (kb, вИЮ), and in buying capital when it is below kb in order to reach the threshold kb. The region B = (0, kb) will be then called the buy region.

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 553 4 Viscosity solutions and regularity of the value function The concept of viscosity solutions is known to be a general power tool for characterizing the value function of a stochastic control problem, see, e.g., [4]. It is based on the dynamic programming principle which we now recall in our context. Dynamic programming principle: Assume that v is continuous on (0, вИЮ). Then for all k > 0, we have v(k) = sup LвИИA E B ќЄ 0 eвИТrt (ќ†(Kt)dt вИТpdLt) + eвИТrќЄv(KќЄ)1ќЄ<вИЮ C , (4.1) where ќЄ = ќЄ(L) is any stopping time, possibly depending on the control L вИИ A. The precise meaning of this assertion is: v(k) = sup LвИИA sup ѕДвИИT E B ќЄ 0 eвИТrt (ќ†(Kt)dt вИТpdLt) + eвИТrќЄv(KќЄ)1ќЄ<вИЮ C = sup LвИИA inf ѕДвИИT E B ќЄ 0 eвИТrt (ќ†(Kt)dt вИТpdLt) + eвИТrќЄv(KќЄ)1ќЄ<вИЮ C . Here T denotes the set of stopping times in [0, вИЮ]. The DPP is frequently used in this form in the literature. However, many proofs cannot be considered as rigorous. Clearly, DPP holds for the case where вД¶is a path space. However, it is difficult to give a precise reference which covers the situation we consider here. We use this result for granted and left the detailed discussion of this issue for further studies. We recall the definition of viscosity solutions for a PDE of the form F(x, v, Dxv, D2 xxv) = 0, x вИИO, (4.2) where O is an open subset in Rn and F is a continuous function and nonin- creasing in its last argument (with respect to the order of symmetric matrices). Definition 1. Let v be a continuous function on O. We say that v is a vis- cosity solution to (4.2) on O if it is (i) a viscosity supersolution to (4.2) on O: for any x0 вИИO and any C2- function ѕХ in a neighborhood of x0 such that x0 is a local minimum of v вИТѕХ and (v вИТѕХ)(x0) = 0, we have: F(x0, ѕХ(x0), DxѕХ(x0), D2 xxѕХ(x0)) вЙ•0; (ii) a viscosity subsolution to (4.2) on O: for any x0 вИИO and any C2-function ѕХ in a neighborhood of x0 such that x0 is a local maximum of v вИТѕХ and (v вИТѕХ)(x0) = 0, we have: F(x0, ѕХ(x0), DxѕХ(x0), D2 xxѕХ(x0)) вЙ§0.

    554 H. Pham Theorem 4.1. The value function v is a continuous viscosity solution of the HamiltonвАУJacobiвАУBellman equation (3.1) on (0, вИЮ). Proof. The argument is based on the dynamic programming principle and ItЋЖoвАЩs formula. It is standard, but somewhat technical in this singular control context. We give it in the appendix. вЬЈ Based on the property that the value function is a concave viscosity solu- tion of the HJB equation, we can now prove that it belongs to C2. Theorem 4.2. The value function v is a classical C2-solution on (0, вИЮ) to the HamiltonвАУJacobiвАУBellman equation min {rv вИТLv вИТќ†(k) , вИТvвА≤(k) + p} = 0, k > 0. Proof. Step 1. We first prove that v is a C1-function on (0, вИЮ). Since v is concave, the left and right derivatives vвА≤ вИТ(k) and vвА≤ +(k) exist for any k > 0 and vвА≤ +(k) вЙ§vвА≤ вИТ(k). We argue by contradiction and suppose that vвА≤ +(k0) < vвА≤ вИТ(k0) for some k0 > 0. Fix some q in (vвА≤ +(k0), vвА≤ вИТ(k0)) and consider the function ѕХќµ(k) = v(k0) + q(k вИТk0) вИТ1 2ќµ(k вИТk0)2, with ќµ > 0. Then k0 is a local maximum of (v вИТѕХќµ) with ѕХќµ(k0) = v(k0). Since ѕХвА≤ ќµ(k0) = q < p by (3.6) and ѕХвА≤вА≤ ќµ(k0) = 1/ќµ, the subsolution property for v to (3.1): min {rѕХ(k0) вИТLѕХ(k0) вИТќ†(k0) , вИТѕХвА≤(k0) + p} вЙ§0, implies that we must have the inequality rѕХ(k0) + ќіk0q + 1 ќµ вИТќ†(k0) вЙ§0. (4.3) With ќµ sufficiently small, this leads to a contradiction and, hence, proves that vвА≤ +(k0) = vвА≤ вИТ(k0). Step 2. By Lemma 3.4, v belongs to C2 on (0, kb) and satisfies vвА≤(k) = p, k вИИ(0, kb). From Step 1, we have NT = (kb, вИЮ) = {k > 0 : vвА≤(k) < p}. We now check that v is a viscosity solution of : rv вИТLv вИТќ† = 0, on (kb, вИЮ). (4.4) Let k0 вИИ(kb, вИЮ) and ѕХ be a C2-function on (kb, вИЮ) such that k0 is a local maximum of v вИТѕХ, with (v вИТѕХ)(k0) = 0. Since ѕХвА≤(k0) = vвА≤(k0) < p, the subsolution property for v to (3.1): min {rѕХ(k0) вИТLѕХ(k0) вИТќ†(k0) , вИТѕХвА≤(k0) + p} вЙ§0, implies the inequality

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 555 rѕХ(k0) вИТLѕХ(k0) вИТќ†(k0) вЙ§0. Thus, v is a viscosity subsolution of (4.4) on (kb, вИЮ). The proof of the vis- cosity supersolution property is similar. Now for arbitrary k1 вЙ§k2 вИИ(kb, вИЮ), consider the Dirichlet boundary problem rV вИТLV вИТќ†(k) = 0, on (k1, k2), (4.5) V (k1) = v(k1), V (k2) = v(k2). (4.6) Classical results provide the existence and uniqueness of a C2-function V on (k1, k2) which is a solution to (4.5)-(4.6). In particular, this smooth function V is a viscosity solution of (4.4) on (k1, k2). From standard uniqueness results on viscosity solutions (here for a linear PDE in a bounded domain), we deduce that v = V on (k1, k2). From the arbitrariness of k1, k2, it follows that v is in C2 on (kb, вИЮ) and satisfies (4.4) in the classical sense. Step 3. It remains to prove the C2-condition at kb in the case 0 < kb < вИЮ. Let k вИИ(0, kb). Since v is in C2 on (0, kb) with vвА≤(k) = p, the supersolution property for v to (3.1) applied at the point k and the test function ѕХ = v: min {rѕХ(k) вИТLѕХ(k) вИТќ†(k) , вИТѕХвА≤(k) + p} вЙ•0, implies that v satisfies (in the classical sense) the inequality: rv(k) вИТLv(k) вИТќ†(k) вЙ•0, 0 < k < kb. The derivative of v being constant equal to p on (0, kb), this yields: rv(k) + ќіkp вИТќ†(k) вЙ•0, 0 < k < kb, and, therefore, rv(kb) + ќіkbp вИТќ†(kb) вЙ•0. (4.7) On the other hand, from the C1-smooth fit at kb, we have by sending k downwards to kb into (4.4): rv(kb) + ќіkbp вИТќ†(kb) = 1 2ќ≥2k2 bvвА≤вА≤(k+ b ). (4.8) From the concavity of v, the right-hand side of (4.8) is nonpositive, and this fact, combined with (4.7), implies that vвА≤вА≤(k+ b ) = 0. This proves that v is C2 at kb with vвА≤вА≤(kb) = 0. вЬЈ 5 Solution of the optimization problem 5.1 Some preliminary results on an ODE We recall some useful results on the second order linear differential equation

    556 H. Pham rv вИТLv вИТќ† = 0. (5.1) arising from the HJB equation (3.1). It is well-known that the general solution to the ODE (5.1) with ќ† = 0 is given by the formula ЋЖV (k) = Akm + Bkn, where m = ќі ќ≥2 + 1 2 вИТ

    ќі ќ≥2 + 1 2 2 + 2r ќ≥2 , < 0 n = ќі ќ≥2 + 1 2 + ќі ќ≥2 + 1 2 2 + 2r ќ≥2 > 1 are the roots of 1 2ќ≥2m(m вИТ1) + ќіm вИТr = 0. Moreover, the ODE (5.1) admits a twice continuously differentiable particular solution on (0, вИЮ) given, accordingly, e.g. [6], by the formula ЋЖV0(k) = J(k, 0) = E вИЮ 0 eвИТrtќ†( ЋЖKk t )dt

    , where ЋЖKk is the solution to the linear SDE d ЋЖKt = ЋЖKt (вИТќіdt + ќ≥dWt) , ЋЖK0 = k. In other words, ЋЖV0 is the expected profit corresponding to the zero control L = 0. Remark 5.1. The function ЋЖV0 can be expressed analytically as ЋЖV0(k) = knG1(k) + kmG2(k), with G1(k) = 2 ќ≥2(n вИТm) вИЮ k sвИТnвИТ1ќ†(s)ds, k > 0, G2(k) = 2 ќ≥2(n вИТm) k 0 sвИТmвИТ1ќ†(s)ds, k > 0. Under assumption (2.2), the limiting behavior of the derivative ЋЖV вА≤ 0 as k tends to zero and infinity is described as follows.

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 557 Lemma 5.1. ЋЖV вА≤ 0(0+) := lim kвЖУ0 ЋЖV вА≤ 0(k) = вИЮand ЋЖV вА≤ 0(вИЮ) := lim kвЖТвИЮ ЋЖV вА≤ 0(k) = 0. Proof. We rewrite ЋЖV0 as ЋЖV0(k) = E вИЮ 0 eвИТrtќ†(kYt)dt

    , k > 0, where Yt = eвИТќіtMt, and M is the martingale Mt = exp(ќ≥WtвИТќ≥2 2 t). It is easily checked by the Lebesgue theorem that one can differentiate the expression of ЋЖV0 inside the expectation and the integral so that its derivative is given by the equality ЋЖV вА≤ 0(k) = E вИЮ 0 eвИТrtYtќ†вА≤(kYt)dt

    , k > 0. Using the positivity and nonincreasing monotonicity of ќ†вА≤, we may apply the monotone convergence theorem as k tends to zero and obtain from the Inada condition ќ†вА≤(0+) = вИЮthat limkвЖУ0 ЋЖV вА≤ 0(k) = вИЮ. On the other hand, we may also apply the dominated convergence theorem as k tends to infinity and obtain from the other Inada condition ќ†вА≤(вИЮ) = 0 that limkвЖТвИЮЋЖV вА≤ 0(k) = 0. вЬЈ 5.2 Explicit form of the value function Lemma 5.2. The buying threshold satisfies the inequalities 0 < kb < вИЮ. Proof. We first check that kb > 0. If it is not the case, the buying region is empty, and we would have from Lemma 3.4 and Theorem 4.2 that rv вИТLv вИТќ† = 0, k > 0. Hence, v would be of the form v(k) = Akm + Bkn + ЋЖV0(k), k > 0. Since m < 0 and |v(0+)| < вИЮ, this implies that A = 0. Now, since n > 1, we get that vвА≤(0+) = ЋЖV вА≤ 0(0+) = вИЮ, a contradiction with the bound vвА≤(k) вЙ§p for all k > 0. We also have kb < вИЮ. Otherwise, v would be on the form v(k) = kp + v(0+), вИАk > 0. This contradicts to the growth condition (3.5). вЬЈ We can now explicitly determine the value function v.

    558 H. Pham Theorem 5.1. The value function v has the following structure: v(k) = kp + v(0+), k вЙ§kb, Akm + ЋЖV0(k), kb < k, (5.2) where the three constants v(0+), A and kb are determined by the continuity, C1- and C2-smooth fit conditions at kb: Akm b + ЋЖV0(kb) = kbp + v(0+), (5.3) mAkmвИТ1 b + ЋЖV вА≤ 0(kb) = p, (5.4) m(m вИТ1)AkmвИТ2 b + ЋЖV вА≤вА≤ 0 (kb) = 0. (5.5) Proof. We already know from Lemma 3.4 that on the interval (0, kb), which is nonempty by Lemma 5.2, v has the structure described in (5.2). Moreover, on (kb, вИЮ), the derivative vвА≤ < p in virtue of Lemma 3.4. Therefore, by Theorem 4.2, v satisfies the equation rv вИТLv вИТќ† = 0, and so, according to Subsection 5.1, it is of the form v(k) = Akm + Bkn + ЋЖV0(k), k > kb. Since m < 0, n > 1, ЋЖV вА≤ 0(k) вЖТ0 as k вЖТвИЮ, and вЙ§vвА≤(k) вЙ§p, we must have necessarily B = 0, and so v has the form written in (5.2). Finally, the three conditions resulting from the continuity, C1- and C2-smooth fit conditions at kb determine the constants A, kb and v(0+). вЬЈ Remark 5.2. By the viscosity solutions method adopted here we know the existence of a triple (v(0+), A, kb) вИИR+ √Ч R √Ч (0, вИЮ) which is solution to the system of equations (5.3)-(5.4)-(5.5). Indeed, this results from the continuity, C1- and C2-properties of v at kb that we proved to hold a priori. This contrasts with the classical verification approach where one tries to find a C2-solution to (3.1), so of the form ЋЬv(k) = kp + ЋЬv(0+), k вЙ§ЋЬkb, ЋЬAkm + ЋЖV0(k), ЋЬkb < k, (5.6) and, hence, to prove the existence of a triple (ЋЬv(0+), ЋЬA, ЋЬkb) вИИR+ √Ч R √Ч (0, вИЮ) which is a solution to (5.3)-(5.4)-(5.5). By a verification argument, one then shows that ЋЬv = v proving a posteriori the C2-property of v. On the other hand, it is easily seen that we have uniqueness of a solution (ЋЖv(0+), A, kb) вИИR+ √Ч R √Ч (0, вИЮ) to the system of equations (5.3) вАУ (5.5). Indeed, otherwise we could find another smooth C2-function ЋЬv of the form (5.6), with the linear growth condition, and solving (3.1). This contradicts the standard uniqueness results for PDE (3.1). Remark 5.3. The value function v satisfies in (kb, вИЮ) the second order ODE

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 559 rv(k) + ќіkvвА≤(k) вИТ1 2ќ≥2k2vвА≤вА≤(k) вИТќ†(k) = 0, k вИИ(kb, вИЮ). From the continuity and C1- and C2-conditions of v at kb, i.e. the relations v(kb) = kbp + v(0+), vвА≤(kb) = p and vвА≤вА≤(kb) = 0, we then deduce that (r + ќі)kbp + rv(0+) = ќ†(kb). (5.7) Remark 5.4. Computation of v From a computational viewpoint, the constants A, kb, v(0+) can be determined as follows. From equations (5.4)-(5.5), we obtain an equation for kb and express A in terms of kb : F(kb) := (1 вИТm) ЋЖV вА≤ 0(kb) + kb ЋЖV вА≤вА≤ 0 (kb) = p(1 вИТm), (5.8) A = k1вИТm b m

    p вИТЋЖV вА≤ 0(kb)

    . (5.9) The value v(0+) is then computed from relation (5.3) or, equivalently, (5.7). Note that a straightforward calculation provides the explicit expression of F: F(k) = n(n вИТm)knвИТ1G1(k) вИТ2 ќ≥2 ќ†(k) k , k > 0. Example 1. Special case of the power profit function We consider the case where ќ† is the CobbвАУDouglas profit function, and we assume, without loss of generality, that ќ†(k) = kќ± with 0 < ќ± < 1. Then ЋЖV0(k) = Ckќ±, with C = 1 r + ќ±ќі + ќ≥2 2 ќ±(1 вИТќ±) . Then, from (5.8), kb is explicitly written as : kb = p(1 вИТm) ќ±C(ќ± вИТm)

    1 ќ±вИТ1 . 5.3 Optimal control We recall the following well-known Skorohod lemma, see, e.g., [7]. Lemma 5.3. For any initial state k вЙ•0 and given a boundary kb вЙ•0, there exist unique cadlag adapted processes KвИЧand nondecreasing processes LвИЧsat- isfying the following Skorohod problem S(k, kb) : dKвИЧ t = KвИЧ t (вИТќіdt + ќ≥dWt) + dLвИЧ t , t вЙ•0, KвИЧ 0вИТ= k, (5.10) KвИЧ t вИИ[kb, вИЮ) a.e., t вЙ•0, (5.11) вИЮ 0 1KвИЧ u>kbdLвИЧ u = 0. (5.12) Moreover, if k вЙ•kb, then LвИЧis continuous. When k < kb, LвИЧ 0 = kb вИТk, and

    560 H. Pham KвИЧ 0 = kb. Remark 5.5. The solution KвИЧto the above equations is a reflected diffusion at the boundary kb and the process LвИЧis the local time of KвИЧat kb. Condition (5.12) means that LвИЧincreases only when KвИЧhits the boundary kb. It is also known that the r-potential of LвИЧis finite, i.e. E вИЮ 0 eвИТrtdLвИЧ t

    < вИЮ, see Chapter X in [9], so that E вИЮ 0 eвИТrtKвИЧ t dt

    < вИЮ. (5.13) Theorem 5.2. For k вЙ•0, let (KвИЧ, LвИЧ) be the solution to the Skorohod problem S(k, kb). Then v(k) = J(k, LвИЧ), k вЙ•0. Proof. 1) We first consider the case where k вЙ•kb. Then, the processes KвИЧ, LвИЧare continuous. In view of (5.11) and Theorem 4.2, we have rv(KвИЧ t ) вИТLv(KвИЧ t ) вИТќ†(KвИЧ t ) = 0, a.e. t вЙ•0. By applying ItЋЖoвАЩs formula to eвИТrtv(KвИЧ t ) between 0 and T, we thus get: E

    eвИТrT v(KвИЧ T )

    = v(k) вИТE B T 0 eвИТrtќ†(KвИЧ t )dt C + E B T 0 eвИТrtvвА≤(KвИЧ t )dLвИЧ t C . (5.14) (Notice that the stochastic integral appearing in the ItЋЖo formula has zero expectation because of (5.13)). Now, in view of (5.12), we have E B T 0 eвИТrtvвА≤(KвИЧ t )dLвИЧ t C = E B T 0 eвИТrtvвА≤(KвИЧ t )1KвИЧ t =kbdLвИЧ t C = E B T 0 eвИТrtpdLвИЧ t C , since vвА≤(kb) = p. Plugging into (5.14) yields: v(k) = E

    eвИТrT v(KвИЧ T )

    • E B T 0 eвИТrtќ†(KвИЧ t )dt C вИТE B T 0 eвИТrtpdLвИЧ t C . (5.15) From (5.13), we have that limT вЖТвИЮE[eвИТrT KвИЧ T ] = 0. Since v satisfies a linear growth condition in k, this implies that also lim T вЖТвИЮE[eвИТrT v(KвИЧ T )] = 0.

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 561 By sending T to infinity into (5.15), we obtain, by the dominated convergence theorem, the required result: v(k) = J(k, LвИЧ) = E вИЮ 0 eвИТrt (ќ†(KвИЧ t ) вИТpdLвИЧ t )

    . 2) If k < kb, and since then LвИЧ 0 = k вИТkb, we have: J(k, LвИЧ) = J(kb, LвИЧ) вИТp(k вИТkb) = v(kb) вИТp(k вИТkb) = v(k), by recalling that vвА≤ = p on (0, kb). вЬЈ Conclusion. The main results of this paper in Theorems 5.1 and 5.2 provide a complete and explicit solution to our irreversible investment under uncertainty. They mathematically formulate the economic intuition that a company will invest in buying capital in order to maintain its production capacity above a threshold kb, which can be computed quite explicitly. Appendix : Proof of Theorem 4.1 (i) Viscosity supersolution property. Fix k0 > 0 and C2-function ѕХ such that v(k0) = ѕХ(k0) and ѕХ(k) вЙ§v(k) for all k in a neighborhood ¬ѓBќµ(k0) = [k0 вИТќµ, k0 + ќµ] of k0 (0 < ќµ < k0). Consider the admissible control L вИИA defined by Lt = 0, t = 0 ќЈ, t вЙ•0, where 0 вЙ§ќЈ < ќµ. Define the exit time ѕДќµ = inf{t вЙ•0 : Kt /вИИ¬ѓBќµ(x0)}. Here K is the capacity production starting from k0 and controlled by L above. Notice that K has at most one jump at t = 0 and is continuous on (0, ѕДќµ]. By the dynamic programming principle (4.1) with ќЄ = ѕДќµ вИІh, h > 0, we have : ѕХ(k0) = v(k0) вЙ•E B ѕДќµвИІh 0 eвИТrt(ќ†(Kt)dt вИТpdLt) + eвИТr(ѕДќµвИІh)v(KѕДќµвИІh) C вЙ•E B ѕДќµвИІh 0 eвИТrt(ќ†(Kt)dt вИТpdLt) + eвИТr(ѕДќµвИІh)ѕХ(KѕДќµвИІh) C . (5.16) Applying ItЋЖoвАЩs formula to the process eвИТrtѕХ(Kt) between 0 and ѕДќµ вИІh, and taking the expectation, we obtain similarly as in the proof of Proposition 3.1 by noting also that dLc t = 0:

    562 H. Pham E[eвИТr(ѕДќµвИІh)ѕХ(KѕДќµвИІh)] = ѕХ(k0) + E B ѕДќµвИІh 0 eвИТrt (вИТrѕХ + LѕХ) (Kt)dt C + E п£Ѓ п£∞

    0вЙ§tвЙ§ѕДќµвИІh eвИТrt [ѕХ(Kt) вИТѕХ(KtвИТ)] п£є п£ї. (5.17) Combining relations (5.16) and (5.17), we see that E B ѕДќµвИІh 0 eвИТrt (rѕХ вИТLѕХ вИТќ†) (Kt)dt C + E B ѕДќµвИІh 0 eвИТrtpdLt C вИТE п£Ѓ п£∞

    0вЙ§tвЙ§ѕДќµвИІh eвИТrt [ѕХ(Kt) вИТѕХ(KtвИТ)] п£є п£ївЙ•0. (5.18) вЛЖTaking first ќЈ = 0, i.e. L = 0, we see that K is continuous, and only the first term in the left-hand side of (5.18) is non zero. By dividing the above inequality by h with h вЖТ0, we conclude by the dominated convergence theorem: rѕХ(k0) вИТLѕХ(k0) вИТќ†(k0) вЙ•0. (5.19) вЛЖNow, by taking ќЈ > 0 in (5.18), and noting that L and K jump only at t = 0 with the jump size ќЈ, we get that E B ѕДќµвИІh 0 eвИТrt (rѕХ вИТLѕХ вИТќ†) (Kt)dt C + pќЈ вИТѕХ(k0 + ќЈ) + ѕХ(k0) вЙ•0. (5.20) Taking h вЖТ0, then dividing by ќЈ and letting ќЈ вЖТ0, we obtain the inequality p вИТѕХвА≤(k0) вЙ•0. (5.21) This proves the required viscosity supersolution property: min {rѕХ(k0) вИТLѕХ(k0) вИТќ†(k0), вИТѕХвА≤(k0) + p} вЙ•0. (5.22) (ii) Viscosity sub-solution property. We prove this part by contradiction. Suppose the claim is not true. Then, there is k0 > 0, ќµ вИИ(0, k0), a ѕХ C2-function with ѕХ(k0) = v(k0) and ѕХ вЙ•v in ¬ѓBќµ(k0) = [k0 вИТќµ, k0 + ќµ], and ќљ > 0 such that for all k вИИ¬ѓBќµ(k0) we have: rѕХ(k) вИТLѕХ(k) вИТќ†(k) вЙ•ќі, (5.23) ѕХвА≤(k) вЙ§p вИТќљ. (5.24)

    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 563 For a control L вИИA, consider the exit time ѕДќµ = inf{t вЙ•0 : Kt /вИИ¬ѓBќµ(x0)}. (Here K is the capacity production starting from k0 and controlled by L). By applying ItЋЖoвАЩs formula to eвИТrtѕХ(Kt), we get : E ) eвИТrѕДќµѕХ(KѕД вИТ ќµ )

However, there is some random variable ќ± taking values in [0, 1] such that kќ± := KѕД вИТ ќµ + ќ±вИЖKѕДќµ = KѕД вИТ ќµ + ќ±вИЖLѕДќµ вИИвИВ¬ѓBќµ(k0) = {k0 вИТќµ, k0 + ќµ}.     вЙ•g0. (5.31) Indeed, one can always find some constant G0 > 0 such that the C2-function ѕИ(k) = G0((k вИТk0)2 вИТќµ2), satisfies the relations min {rѕИ вИТLѕИ + 1, 1 вИТ|ѕИ|} вЙ•0, on ¬ѓBќµ(k0), ѕИ = 0, on вИВ¬ѓBќµ(k0). For instance, we can choose: G0 = min
