    Explicit Solution to an Irreversible Investment Model 565 Hence, the claim (5.31) holds with g0 = G0ќµ2. вЛЖFinally, by taking supremum over all (L, M) вИИA in (5.30), and invoking the dynamic programming principle (4.1), we have that v(k0) вЙ•v(k0) + ќљg0, which is the required contradiction. References 1. Chiarolla, M. and Haussmann, U.: Explicit solution of a stochastic irreversible investment problem and its moving threshold. Preprint, 2003. 2. Davis, M., Norman, A.: Portfolio selection with transaction costs. Math. Oper. Res., 15, 676вАУ713, 1990. 3. Dixit, A.K., Pindick, R.: Investment under Uncertainty. Princeton University Press, 1994. 4. Fleming, W., Soner, M.: Controlled Markov Processes and Viscosity Solutions. Springer-Verlag, New York, 1993. 5. Jeanblanc-Picqu¬іe, M. and Shiryaev, A.: Optimization of the flow of dividends. Russian Math. Surveys, 50, 257вАУ277, 1995. 6. Kobila, T.O.: A class of solvable investment problems involving singular controls. Stoch. and Stoch. Reports., Vol. 43, 20вАУ63, 1993. 7. Lions, P.L., Snitzman, A.: Stochastic differential equations with reflecting boundary conditions. Comm. Pure. Appl. Math., Vol. 37, 511вАУ537. 8. Meyer, P.A.: S¬іeminaire de Probabilit¬іes, Lect. Notes in Math., 511, Springer- Verlag, 1976. 9. Revuz, D., Yor, M.: Continuous Martingale and Brownian Motion¬њ Springer- Verlag, 1991. 10. Rockafellar, T.: Convex Analysis. Princeton University Press. 11. Shreve, S. and Soner, M.: Optimal investment and consumption with transaction costs. Annals of Appl. Prob., Vol. 4, 609вАУ692, 1994.

    Gittins Type Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs Ernst PRESMAN1 and Isaac SONIN2,1 1 Central Economics and Mathematics Institute, Nakhimovskii prospect, 47, Moscow, Russia. presman@cemi.rssi.ru 2 Department of Mathematics, University of North Carolina at Charlotte, Charlotte,NC, 28223, USA. imsonin@email.uncc.edu Summary. We consider the problem which informally can be described as follows. Initially a finite set of independent trials is available. If a Decision Maker (DM) chooses to test a specific trial she receives a reward, and with some probability, the process of testing is terminated or the tested trial becomes unavailable but some random finite set (possibly empty) of new independent trials is added to the set of initial trials, and so on. The total number of potential trials is finite. A DM knows the rewards and transition probabilities depending on the trials. On each step she can either quit (i.e. stop the process of testing), or continue. Her goal is to select an order to test trials and an quitting (stopping) time to maximize the expected total reward. We simplify and generalize some results obtained earlier for similar problems, we prove that an index can be assigned to each possible trial and an optimal strategy uses on each step the trial with maximal index between available ones. We present a recursive procedure with a transparent interpretation to calculate the index. We discuss the connection between introduced index and Gittins index. Key words: Markov decision process, graph, Gittins index, priority rules. Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 90B36, 90C40, 62L05 1 Introduction The goal of this paper is twofold. First, to generalize the main result and to simplify the proof of the paper by Denardo et al. [3]. In that paper a model of R&D projects is considered. Each stage of a project in the model is represented by an edge of a directed forest. To activate an edge e one needs to pay a certain amount r(e). Each activated edge can pass or fail. The successful completion of a path from a root to a leaf brings certain reward and terminates the activity. In case of failure all edges which follow the failed edge become unavailable. The

    568 I. Sonin and E. Presman goal is to maximize the expected reward. The optimal strategy in the model is an index strategy. Each time one should use an edge with the highest value of the index among the available indices. An index for an edge is specified only by the parameters of the directed tree above this edge. We consider more general model where an optimal strategy is also an index strategy. The notion of the index in both papers is a generalization of the corresponding notion in the model, which we call below a binary elementary (BE) model, studied in early sixties in Mitten (1960) [9]. The second goal of our paper is to show that the index described above is a generalization of the well-known Gittins index (GI). Thus GI, beside the original papers of Gittins [6] and Gittins and Jones [7], has the second root of its origin in the mentioned paper by Mitten [9]. It seems that the proper credit never was given to Mitten and his model. The strategies of the type, when for selecting an action on each stage it suffices to solve much simpler problem, for example the one-step optimization problem, are called myopic or greedy. They are very popular and intensively studied though in contrast to model above they usually are not optimal. We call a strategy a Priority Rule (PR) if an index is calculated for each action and an action with the highest value of index among available is selected. The myopic strategies form a nucleus of developed later so called Multi- armed Bandit (MAB) Theory (for independent (!) arms) (see Gittins [6], Whit- tle [15], and Berry and Fristedt [1]), where the corresponding strategy is called Gittins index strategy. The Gittins index, denoted by G(x), where x is a state of Markov chain, plays an important role in theory of MAB with independent arms but it also appears in other problems like the optimal replacement problems. The main result of this theory states that if there are a finite number of independent MC and a decision maker at each moment can engage (test) one of these MC while all other remain frozen then the optimal strategy is to test MC whose state xj at this moment has the largest value Gj(xj), where Gj(xj) is the value of GI of MC j at state xj. Note also that the same term Multi-armed bandit problem is used also in the classical papers by R. Bellman [2], D. Feldman [4] as well as in the book of Presman and Sonin [10] and in some sections of the book by Berry and Fristedt where arms are dependent, i.e. a trial of one arm provides an information about the parameters of other arms also. In this case a myopic Gittins index strategy is not optimal in general. The traditional Gittins index G(x) for a Markov chain (MC) is defined as the maximal value of a discounted expected reward per expected discounted length of a cycle starting from x, i.e. G(x) = sup ѕД Ex ѕДвИТ1 n=o ќ≤nr(Zn) Ex ѕДвИТ1 n=o ќ≤n , (1.1) where ќ≤ is a discount factor, 0 < ќ≤ < 1, ѕД is a stopping time, ѕД вЙ•1, r(¬Ј) is a reward function, and Zn is the state of Markov chain at time n.

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 569 Note, that as usual in the theory of Markov Decision Processes, one can consider the discount factor ќ≤ as a probability of survival of a MC at each step. Formally one can introduce an absorbing state and to introduce new probabilities such that the probability of transition to an absorbing state is equal to 1вИТќ≤ and all other transition probabilities are multiplied by the factor ќ≤. Then the denominator in formula (1.1) multiplied by (1 вИТќ≤) is equal to the probability of absorption during the time interval (0, ѕД), QѕД(x) = 1 вИТExќ≤ѕД. (1.2) In our paper we will consider the specific Markov decision process on a forest with one absorption state, when probability of absorption q(A) depends on chosen action A. We introduce notion of index for control actions as follows. For fixed strategy ѕА with stopping time ѕД and control process (Ai), with A0 = e, we consider the reward RѕА(e), and the probability of absorption QѕА(e). Following the footsteps of Mitten [9], Granot and Zuckerman [8] and Denardo et al. [3], we define the index ќ±(e) = sup RѕА(e) QѕА(e) , (1.3) where supremum is taken over some set of strategies. Note that the reward RѕА(e) can be represented in the form RѕА(e) = EѕА BѕДвИТ1

    i=0 r(Ai) C = ЋЬEѕА п£Ѓ п£∞ ѕДвИТ1

    i=0 r(Ai) iвИТ1 ( j=0 (1 вИТq(Aj)) п£є п£ї, where ЋЬE denote the expectation with respect to corresponding Markov chain without absorbing state. The probability of absorption QѕА(e) can be repre- sented in the same way with q(¬Ј) instead of r(¬Ј). In case q(Ai) = 1 вИТќ≤ for all i, the denominator in (1.3) coincides with (1.2). So, (1.3) generalizes (1.1) to the case of Markov decision process with probability of absorption depending on the current state. In the sequel we consider only the case of finite forest but most of the results can be extended to the case of an infinite forest with some extra con- ditions. The plan of our paper is as follows. In Section 2 and 3 we consider cor- respondingly the BE-model and the model studied in Denardo et al. [3]. In Section 4 we formulate our model and present the main result. In Section 5 we discuss main ideas of the proofs. In Section 6 we present and prove some auxiliary results leaving the proof of one lemma to the Appendix (Section 9). In Section 6 we give the proof of the main result. In Section 8 we present an algorithm for calculating the index. In Section 9 we discuss connection with Gittins index and some open problems.

    570 I. Sonin and E. Presman 2 A binary elementary (BE) model of independent trials Suppose that there is a finite set of independent Bernoulli trials e1, e2, ..., em, with two possible outcomes in each trial, вАЬcontinuationвАЭ with probability pi, in the i-th trial, and вАЬterminationвАЭ, with probability qi. A decision maker (DM) can choose an order in which to conduct (test) the trials. Each trial can be tested only once. The test of the i-th trial brings a reward ri, and in the case of вАЬcontinuationвАЭ she may continue testing or quit. In the case of вАЬterminationвАЭ the testing has to be terminated. The goal of DM is to select the optimal order to maximize the expected total reward. Such formulation is equivalent to a formulation where DM has to pay an amount ci in advance, obtains ai with probability pi, and bi with probability qi, and ri = вИТci + aipi + biqi. This problem is a reformulation of a вАЬleast cost testing sequencingвАЭ prob- lem solved independently by a few authors in 1960 (see Mitten [9]). We call it BE-model (Binary Elementary model). A rather simple proof shows that the optimal strategy has a remarkably simple structure and is based on an index ќ± calculated for each trial ei, ќ±(ei) equal to expected profit divided by probability of termination, i.e. ќ±(ei) = ri qi . (2.1) The optimal strategy has the following form: test the trials with positive index in the order of decreasing. If all trials must be tested then all they should be tested in the above order. Mitten analyzed the model when ci < 0, ai = 0, and bi > 0 but this makes no difference for the analysis of the problem. 3 Independent trials on a forest, binary forest (BF) model A model described above was generalized by Granot and Zuckerman [8] in the context of multi-stage R&D models. That paper has many interesting developments but contrary to their claim the Theorem 1 in their paper can be obtained from the Mitten result by transforming semi-Markov discounting into absorption probabilities. This model in turn was recently generalized in a paper by Denardo et al. [3]. The latter model can be described briefly as follows. At initial moment a set of independent trials with two possible outcomes are available. For some of trials the nature of two outcomes is the same as in BE model - вАЬcontinuationвАЭ and вАЬterminationвАЭ. For other trials for both of outcomes one can continue but differently. to pone of outcomes leads to a possibility to continue the process of testing. In the case of one outcome a вАЬcontinuationвАЭ is the same as above, but the second of outcomes adds to the set of available trial a set of new trials, some of them with a similar feature and

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 571 so on, and so on. Each trial e of the second kind and all trials that вАЬfollowвАЭ e in one or more steps can be represented by edges of a directed tree T(e). A tree corresponding to the trial of the first kind consists of one edge. The total set of potentially available trials is finite and is represented by a union of directed trees, i.e. by a directed forest F0. The trials of the first kind correspond to the leaves of this forest, i.e. to the edges such that no edges follows. All other edges are called stems. The initially available edges are called the roots of F0. If edge e is tested (used) it can pass with some probability or fail with complimentary probability. These events are independent of similar events for other edges. If an edge e вАЬfailsвАЭ than e and all edges that follow e are not available any more, but other available edges can be tested. If a stem e passed then it becomes unavailable but all edges that immediately follow e are added to the set of available edges. If a leaf e passed then the testing has to be terminated. An edge eвА≤ can be tested only once and only if all edges on the path from one of the roots of F0 to eвА≤ вАЬpassedвАЭ before. The reward on stems (costs) are negative, positive rewards (prizes) are available only on leaves, i.e. on edges such that no edge follows. The testing can be conducted till the termination, when a prize is obtained, i.e. a leaf is reached and вАЬpassedвАЭ, or till the moment when DM decides to quit, i.e. to stop testing. The goal of a DM is to maximize the expected value of either linear or exponential function of the profit (total reward) over all possible strategies to test edges. We call this model BF-model (Binary Forest model) since the result of each trial has two outcomes. The main result of paper [3] is that the optimal strategy is based again on an index generalizing (2.1). This index ќ±(e) is defined as ќ±(e) = sup ѕА RѕА(e) QѕА(e), where RѕА(e) and QѕА(e) are correspondingly the expected total reward and the probability of termination (to obtain a prize) in linear case and corresponding function in exponential case. Supremum is taken over some class of strategies, which authors call вАЬcandidatesвАЭ. The authors also noted that their problem can be described in terms of so called MAB processes and their index is similar to the Gittins index. We gratefully acknowledge the possibility to read the manuscript of [3] before its publication. The proof of the main theorem in [3] is complicated and long. Responding to their hope вАЬthat someone will devise a simpler proof than theirsвАЭ we ob- tained in the linear case a different, shorter and more transparent inductive proof of this important and interesting result. We found also that our proof covers also more general situation when: 1) a binary result of testing of an edge (a trial) can be replaced by a finite number of outcomes in the spirit of general theory of Markov Decision Processes (MDP); 2) two separate functions, the prize function b(e) > 0 for leaves and the cost function c(e) < 0 for all other edges are replaced by a general reward

    572 I. Sonin and E. Presman function r(e), which can take any finite values (positive, negative or zero) for any edge; 3) the termination when a prize is obtained, is replaced by a possibility of termination with probability depending on the trial tested at any stage. The last possibility implies also that the discounting with coefficient ќ≤, 0 < ќ≤ < 1 can be considered as a special case of our model since it is equivalent to a termination with a fixed probability 1 вИТќ≤. We will consider only the linear function of the profit. Note also that the optimal strategy in BF-model takes the form of a series of вАЬdepth firstвАЭ searches of paths to leaves. In our model this property is not true generally due to generalization 2. In the MAB literature the term arm is usually understood as a stochastic process which can be engaged again and again. In the BE, BF models and the model presented below each edge can be used only once so we prefer not to use the term arm at all. 4 Multiple forest (MF) model: formulation and results We present our model in a standard frame of Markov Decision Processes (MDP). A MDP model is given (see e.g. Feinberg and Schwartz [5]) by a tuple M = (S, A(x), p(y|x, a), L), where S is a state space, x вИИS represents a state of a system under consideration, A(x) is a set of actions a available at state x, p(y|x, a) is a probability that the next state is y if at state x an action a was chosen (transition operator), and L is a functional defined on the trajectories of a system. By hn = (x0, a0, x1, . . . , xnвИТ1, anвИТ1, xn) we denote a trajectory of length n, n вЙ§вИЮ, hвИЮ= h. A general (randomized) strategy ѕА in MDP is a se- quence ѕАn(¬Ј|hn), n = 0, 1, 2, ... of distributions on action set A(xn) possibly depending on the whole past history. An initial state x and a strategy ѕА de- fine a measure P ѕА x in the space of infinite trajectories, i.e. the distribution of the state-action process (Xn, An), Xn(h) = xn, An(h) = an, n = 0, 1, . . .. We denote by EѕА x the corresponding expectation. If a distribution ѕАn(¬Ј|hn) is a function ѕА(xn) with values in A(xn), a strategy ѕА is a stationary (nonran- domized) strategy. A stationary strategy ѕА defines the transition probabilities p(y|x, ѕА(x)) for the (homogeneous) Markov chain (Xn) describing the evo- lution of the system. The goal of the DM is to maximize the expected total reward RѕА(x) = EѕА xL = EѕА x вИЮ i=0 r(Xi, Ai). From the general theory of MDP it follows that for such a functional it suffices to consider only the station- ary strategies. The value function R(x) = supѕА RѕА(x) satisfies the Bellman (optimality) equation R(x) = sup aвИИA(x) B r(x, a) +

    y p(y|x, a)R(y) C . Let some initial forest F0 be given. We say that edge eвА≤ follows e, if e is on a unique path from a root of a tree to eвА≤. Denote by N(e) the edges from

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 573 T(e) that immediately follow e. Leaves are edges such that no edge follows. Other edges are stems. The state space S = {x} in MF-model consists of absorbing state xвИЧ, empty set вИЕ, and all subsets of edges of F0 which do not contain any two edges such that one follows other, i.e. if e, eвА≤ вИИx for some x and e ћЄ= eвА≤ then T(e) = T(eвА≤) = вИЕ. The action set A(x) = x вИ™{eвИЧ} for x ћЄ= xвИЧ, A(xвИЧ) = eвИЧ, where eвИЧis a quit action, i.e. at each stage a DM can test any of edges in x or select an action eвИЧwhich at the next moment moves a system to xвИЧ. The following parameters are defined for every edge e: 1) a number q(e), 0 вЙ§q(e) вЙ§1, 2) for each subset D of the set N(e) (including empty set and the full set N(e)) a number pD(e) вЙ•0 such that DвКВN(e) pD(e) = 1 вИТq(e), 3) a reward r(e) such that r(eвИЧ) = 0. The meaning of these parameters is as follows. Edges correspond to trials. If edge e is tested, it becomes unavailable, and with probability q(e) the system moves to the absorbing state xвИЧ, and with probability pD(e) all edges from the set D are added to the set of edges available for testing. Formally, the transitional probabilities have the following form: p(xвИЧ|x, eвИЧ) = 1; if e ћЄ= eвИЧthen p(y|x, e) = pD(e) for y = {x \ e} вИ™D and p(xвИЧ|x, e) = q(e). Note that the independence of arms (edges e) is manifested by the property that p(y|x, e) depends only on e вИИx, and does not depend on other eвА≤ from x, and that the вАЬcoordinatesвАЭ of a new state y for edges eвА≤ ћЄ= e remain the same. Given an initial state x and strategy ѕА, the goal is to maximize the expected total reward, RѕА(x) = EѕА x вИЮ

    i=0 r(Ai), where Ai is the edge tested at moment i. Main Problem A: Given an initial state x, maximize RѕА(x) over all strategies. Main Problem B: Given an initial state x, maximize RѕА(x) over all strategies such that a quit action eвИЧis available only if x = вИЕ, or x = xвИЧ. As we mentioned, the general theory of MDP implies that for these prob- lem the stationary nonrandomized strategies form a sufficient class. Still, stationary strategies may have rather complicated structure. For example, a strategy can test edge e if edges e, eвА≤, and eвА≤вА≤ are available and test edge eвА≤ if only edges e, and eвА≤ are available. We can expect that the optimal strategy will be among stationary strategies having the following simpler structure. Let us consider an ordered list of different edges ѕА = (e1, ..., ek). We shall say that ei is senior than ej for ѕА if ei is listed earlier i.e. if i < j. We shall denote {ѕА} = {e1, ..., ek}, i.e. the set of elements of ѕА. List ѕА defines a (nonrandomized) stationary strategy, which we denote also ѕА, as follows: if there is no available edges, i.e. if x вИ©{ѕА} = вИЕ, then ѕА(x) = eвИЧ, otherwise ѕА(x) equals to the most senior element in xвИ©{ѕА}. Such strategy is called a priority rule (PR).

    574 I. Sonin and E. Presman Note that if ei is senior than ej, it does not imply that edge ei for a particular history will be used earlier then ej. It may happens because ei may be not available when ej is already available. More than that, it is possible that two different lists define the same PR because the same states have positive probabilities and both lists define the same order for each state that has positive probability. Example. Consider the forest given on Fig.1. a10= -2 9 10 11 12 13 14 15 a9=8 a11=11 a12= -4 a13=1 a14=3 a15= -3 a4=6 a7=2 3 4 5 7 a3=6.4 a5= -1 a6=9 a8=10 a1¬™5.05 a2=4 1 2 6 8 Fig. 1. Example of a forest with ќ≥(i) = ќ±i. Edges 1 - 3, 5, 7 are stems, N(1) = {3, 4, 5}, N(2) = {6, 7, 8}, N(3) = {9, 10, 11}, N(5) = {12, 13}, N(7) = {14, 55}. Edges 4, 6, 8 - 15 are leafs, so that N(j) = вИЕfor j = 4, 6, 8 вИТ15. p{3,4}(1) > 0, p{5}(1) > 0, p{6,7}(2) > 0, p{8}(2) > 0, p{9,10}(3) > 0, p{11}(3) > 0, p{12,13}(5) > 0, p{14}(7) > 0, p{15}(7) > 0, pвИЕ(j) > 0 for all j = 1, . . . , 15, pD(j) = 0 for all other subsets of N(j), j = 1, 2, 3, 5, 7. Let ѕА0 = (11, 8, 6, 9, 3, 4, 1, 2, 14, 7, 13, 5, 10, 15, 12). Although 11, 8, 6, 3, 9 are senior then 1 for ѕА0, DM will use 1 earlier than these edges because at the initial state {1, 2} edge 1 is senior among available. All trajectories of maximal length corresponding to ѕА0 and having positive probabilities are given on Fig.2. In each state an exit action eвИЧis also available so there are also shortened trajectories. In Fig. 2 edges in states are listed in the order of seniority in ѕА0.

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 575 {14,5} {5} {6,7,5} {7,5} {5,15} {13,15,12} {13,15,12} {2,5} {5} {13,12} {15,12} {15,12} {8,5} {5} {13,12} {12} {12} {12} {12} {5} {13,12} {12} {2} {6,7} {7} {14} вИЖ вИЖ вИЖ вИЖ вИЖ {8} {15} {15} {7} {1,2} вИЖ вИЖ вИЖ {8} {14} {15} {8} {6,7} {7} {14} {10} {4,2} {2} {6,7} {10} {10} {14,10} {15} {3,4,2} {11,4,2} {4,2} {2} {8,10} {7,10} {10,15} {9,4,2,10} {4,2,10} {2,10} {6,7,10} x0 x1 x2 x3 x4 x7 x6 x5 x8 x9 Fig. 2. Possible trajectories of maximal length corresponding to ѕА0 It follows from Fig. 2 that a list ѕА1 = (6, 8, 9, 3, 11, 4, 1, 7, 2, 14, 10, 5, 13, 15, 12) defines the same PR as ѕА0. Each PR can also be specified as follows. Let ќ≥ = ќ≥(e) be a function defined on edges from F0. Then by definition an edge e is senior than eвА≤ if ќ≥(e) > ќ≥(eвА≤). For simplicity we assume that if e, eвА≤ вИИx for some state x and e ћЄ= eвА≤ then ќ≥(e) ћЄ= ќ≥(eвА≤). In opposite case we assume that from the very beginning all edges are numbered and for the edges with equal values of ќ≥(¬Ј) a senior is with greater initial number. We call a strategy ѕА a (ќ≥, c)-PR if {ѕА} = {e : ќ≥(e) вЙ•c}. In other words ѕА assigns to use each time the edge with highest value of ќ≥(e) among all available with values greater or equal to c, and use eвИЧif there is no available edges with ќ≥(e) вЙ•c. The value c is called a cutoffvalue. Below in Section 8 we consider concrete values of p, q and r for all edges in the Example. We show that the PR ѕА0 is an optimal strategy in problem B and it corresponds in particular to ќ≥(i) = ќ±i, where ќ±i are given in Fig 1, ќ±11 = 11, ќ±8 = 10, ќ±6 = 9, ќ±9 = 8, ќ±3 = 6.4, ќ±4 = 6, ќ±1 вЙИ5, 05, ќ±2 = 4, ќ±14 = 3, ќ±7 = 2, ќ±13 = 1, ќ±5 = вИТ1, ќ±10 = вИТ2, ќ±15 = вИТ3, ќ±12 = вИТ4. Denote the class of all PRs by ќ†. For any x вИИS, x ћЄ= вИЕor xвИЧlet us define F(x) = I eвИИx T(e). Given x вИИS and ѕА вИИќ† let us define F ѕА(x) = / e : P ѕА x {An = e} > 0 for some n вЙ•0 0 . (4.1)

    576 I. Sonin and E. Presman Note that F ѕА(x) is also a forest, but some of its leaves can be stems for the initial forest F0. If x = {e} then F ѕА(e) is a tree and we will denote it T ѕА(e). Here and in what follows we use the same notation for a forest F and for the set of edges of F. We say that ѕА вИИќ†(x) if {ѕА} = F ѕА(x). Given x вИИS and ѕА вИИќ† we always can assume that ѕА вИИќ†(x) eliminating вАЬinaccessibleвАЭ edges, i.e. such e вИИ{ѕА} that P ѕА x {An = e} = 0 for all n. If x = {e}, i.e. x consists only of one edge, we use notation e instead of {e}, for example we write ќ†(e), RѕА(e), P ѕА e and so on. Therefore if ѕА is a (ќ≥, c)-PR and ѕА вИИќ†(e) it means that {ѕА} contains only those edges eвА≤ with ќ≥(eвА≤) вЙ•c which are accessible from e. For example, PR ѕА2 = (1, 3, 10) in Fig. 1 defines the same PR as ѕА3 = (1, 3, 10, 12) but only ѕА2 вИИќ†(x) for x = (1, 2). On a set of trajectories h = (x0, e0, x1, . . . , ) let us define a stopping time ѕДвИЧ= ѕДвИЧ(h) = min(n : An = eвИЧor Xn = xвИЧ). Since forest F0 is finite and any PR uses quit action eвИЧif there is no available actions, we always have P ѕА x {AѕДвИЧ= eвИЧor XѕДвИЧ= xвИЧ} = 1, for any x вИИS and ѕА вИИќ†(x). Thus ѕДвИЧcan be described as a random time when either the system runs out of edges in F ѕА(x), and therefore at this moment an action eвИЧwas chosen (a quit moment), or at a previous moment some edge e ћЄ= eвИЧfrom F ѕА(x) was chosen and the transition to xвИЧhas occurred now (at a termination moment). For the sake of brevity we call ѕДвИЧan exit time. Since r(eвИЧ) = 0, we have obviously RѕА(x) = EѕА x ѕДвИЧвИТ1 i=0 r(Ai). For any initial state x and PR ѕА let us define QѕА(x) = P ѕА x {XѕДвИЧ= xвИЧ}, ќ±ѕА(x) = RѕА(x) QѕА(x), (4.2) where ќ±ѕА(x) = вИТвИЮif QѕА(x) = 0. Note that the probability of final absorption, i.e. limn P ѕА x (Xn = xвИЧ) equals to 1 for any PR ѕА. The value QѕА(x) is the probability of termination, i.e. probability of transition to xвИЧwithout using a quit action eвИЧ. Thus QѕА(x) вЙ•0 and вИТвИЮвЙ§ќ±ѕА(x) вЙ§вИЮ. Now we define index ќ±(e) for all e. As it was done in [3], we could define it ќ±(e) = supѕА RѕА(e)/QѕА(e) over all ѕА вИИќ†(e), but it is more convenient to specify ќ±(e) recursively as follows. For any leaf e we set ќ±(e) = r(e)/q(e) if q(e) > 0. If q(e) = r(e) = 0 then we set ќ±(e) = 0. If q(e) > 0, r(e) > 0 or r(e) < 0 we set ќ±(e) = +вИЮ(or вИТвИЮcorrespondingly. For stems we define ќ±(e) as follows. If ќ±(¬Ј) is not defined for e but is defined for all other elements of T(e) we set ќ±(e) = supc ќ±ѕАc, where ѕАc вЙ°ѕАc(e) is a PR which first tests e and after that uses (ќ±, c) -PR from ќ†(N(e)). Let us denote by ѕАвИЧ(e) the PR where ќ±(e) is attained. We also will call such PR ќ± -optimizer. Auxiliary Problem C(e): For an edge e to find ѕАвИЧ(e) and ќ±(e). Later we present an algorithm to calculate ќ±(e). It requires no more than n2 operations. To slightly simplify our proofs sometimes we will assume

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 577 A uniqueness assumptions U: ќ±(e) ћЄ= 0 for all e, and if e ћЄ= eвА≤ then ќ±(e) ћЄ= ќ±(eвА≤). Theorem 1. (a) An (ќ±, 0)-PR is an optimal strategy in the Main Problem A; (b) an (ќ±, вИТвИЮ)-PR is an optimal strategy in the Main Problem B; (c) an (ќ±, ќ±(e))-PR ѕА, ѕА вИИќ†(e) is an optimal strategy in the Auxiliary Problem C(e). Under the assumption U the optimal strategies in (a), (b), and (c) are unique. If assumption U is not true we can modify the notion of ќ±-PR so that statements (a)-(c) of Theorem 1 will still hold. 5 One simple idea and three elementary situations In this section we describe heuristically the key elements of the proof. There are different proofs of Gittins result (see an interesting paper [14]) but it seems none of them can be immediately applied to our case. At the same time our solution is based on a simple key idea, though its implementation in the case of a random forest is technically cumbersome, and will be presented in the next section. We describe this idea using as illustrations three elementary sit- uations, which can be described as three elementary forests. For the simplicity we will assume that all rewards are positive so a quit action is not at all. The first situation (a) describes in fact the simplest case of Mitten ele- mentary model when there are two interchangeable actions a1 and a2. If used, an action ai brings a reward ri and after that with probability qi the other action becomes unavailable (the process is terminated), with complimentary probability decision process may continue. This situation can be described by a forest consisting of two trees {e1} and {e2}. We must compare two PR ѕАij, i, j = 1, 2, i ћЄ= j with corresponding expected rewards Rij. In this case it is optimal to use first an action with highest index ќ±i = ri/qi. This state- ment can be checked easily algebraically, but we prefer to demonstrate this as follows. First, note that the corresponding probability of termination is the same for the both orderings, i.e. we have Q12 = q1 + (1 вИТq1)q2 = q2 + (1 вИТq2)q1 = Q21. (5.1) This important property in a general situation is proved in Lemma 1 in Section 6. This property implies that to maximize Rij is the same as to maximize ќ±ij = Rij/Qij. Let us consider ќ±12 = r1 + (1 вИТq1)r2 q1 + (1 вИТq1)q2 = ќ±1q1 + ќ±2(1 вИТq1)q2 q1 + (1 вИТq1)q2 . (5.2)

    578 I. Sonin and E. Presman It is easy to see that this is a formula for a center of gravity of two masses q1 and (1 вИТq1)q2 located on a horizontal axis with coordinates ќ±1 and ќ±2. The formula for ќ±21 corresponds to a center of gravity for masses (1 вИТq2)q1 and q2 with the same coordinates ќ±1 and ќ±2. Since the sum of masses is the same for both cases, the center of gravity will have higher value when larger mass will be placed into higher position, i.e. ќ±12 > ќ±21 iffќ±1 > ќ±2. (5.3) We described situation (a) for two actions but this case implies also that the similar statement is true for any m interchangeable actions, i.e. for BE model. This property for a general situation corresponds to Corollary 2, pre- sented at Section 6. It is important to observe that the reasoning above does not depend on whether each actions ai is really one time action or consists of a series of actions. In the latter case we must calculate corresponding quantities R and Q for the whole series. Let us explain heuristically how the index ќ±(e) should be calculated for the situation (b), when some action is followed by a set of actions, i.e. when a forest consists of a tree T1 = {e0, e1, e2, ..., em}, where N(e0) = {e1, e2, ..., em}, N(ei) = вИЕ, i = 1, ..., m, and p0 := pN(e0)(e0) = 1вИТq(e0)вИТpвИЕ(e0). The indices for the leaves of this tree, ќ±i := ќ±(ei), i = 1, 2, .., m are known, ќ±(ei) = ri/qi, where ri := r(ei), qi := q(ei). Without loss of generality we assume that edges are numbered in such a way that ќ±1 > ќ±2 > ... > ќ±m. According to definition, to find ќ±(e0) we have to choose kвИЧ, possibly equal to zero, that maximizes ќ±k = Rk/Qk, where Rk and Qk are the reward and termination probability for a PR ѕАk = (e0, e1, e2, ..., ek). Using the notation ќ≤0 = r0/q0, we obtain ќ±k = r0 + r1p0 + r2p0p1 + . . . + rk KkвИТ1 i=0 pi q0 + q1p0 + q2p0p1 + . . . + qk KkвИТ1 i=0 pi = ќ≤0m0 + ќ±1m1 + . . . + ќ±kmk m0 + m1 + . . . + mk , (5.4) where m0 = q0, mi = (p0 ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј piвИТ1)qi, i = 1, ..., k. Thus expression ќ±k also represents a position of a center of gravity for a system of masses and to find the value k which brings the maximum value to (5.4) we can use the following Proposition 1. Suppose that mi are the masses and ќ±i the positions of these masses on the real line, i = 0, 1, 2, ..., N, and ќ±1 > ќ±2 > ... > ќ±N. Suppose that our goal is to select a subset Jmax of a set {0, 1, ..., N} which contains a subset J0 = {0} and has the largest possible center of gravity. Then a) Jmax can be obtained by adding sequentially masses m1, m2, ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј , to a set J0 = {0} till the center of gravity of a system Jk = {0, 1, ..., k} will stop to increase; b) Jmax = {0} вИ™{i : ќ±вИЧ< ќ±i}, where ќ±вИЧis the center of gravity of Jmax.

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 579 If there are ќ±i = ќ±вИЧthen Jmax is not unique in an obvious way. Note that both points of Proposition 1 describe the optimal set: b) de- scribes it in inexplicit form, since ќ±вИЧis not known yet, and a) describes it algorithmically and allows one to calculate ќ±(e0) in situation b) sequentially step by step. The proof of Proposition 1 follows from the elementary properties of pro- portions. (A similar statement was used in a paper by Sonin [11]). The simplest version of situation b) for m = 1 gives ќ±1 > ќ≤0 iffќ±1 > ќ≤0. (5.5) The proof of Theorem 1 in Section 7 is based on the induction with re- spect to the number of edges, and on Lemma 1, which corresponds to (5.1), Corollary 1, which corresponds to (5.3), and Corollary 2, which corresponds to (5.5). These statements are more general than (5.1), (5.3), (5.5) because each action in Lemma and corollaries consists of some series of actions and after application some action (which corresponds to some PR) the system transits to a random set and the choice of the next action depends on this set. To illustrate this fact and an algorithm of calculation of ќ±(e) consider the more complicated situation c), when in situation b) one of leaves e1, e2, ..., em, let say an edge e3, is replaced by a tree T(e3). Then the first two steps of our procedure of maximization of center of gravity will be the same. Suppose that the value of ќ±(e3) is achieved on some PR ѕА = (e3, v1, ..., vk) and ќ±(e3) = R3/Q3. Then in formula (5.4) the value r3 should be replaced by R3 = ќ±(e3)Q3 and correspondingly the mass m3 will be also modified. After that the set N(e3) will be added to the set of available edges, where N(e3) is the set of elements of T(e3) which does not belong to ѕА, but follows immediately elements of ѕА. By the property of ќ± optimizer, all elements of N(e3) have the values of index less then ќ±(e3), and on the next step we will choose an edge with maximal value of ќ± in enlarged set of available edges. 6 Auxiliary results To prove Theorem 1 we introduce some new notations and prove some auxil- iary statements. Let ѕА1 and ѕА2 are PR and ѕА1 вИИќ†(x). Let us define a new PR from ќ†(x) - we denote it ѕА = (ѕА1, ѕА2) - which uses first all available edges from ѕА1 and after that switches to ѕА2, i.e. all edges in the list ѕА1 are defined now as senior than all edges in ѕА2. The list ѕА can be obtained as follows. First, list all elements of ѕА1 in their order and after that list those elements of ѕА2 - in their order - which does not belong to ѕА1 and which are accessible from x. We call PR ѕА2 a continuation of ѕА1. The similar meaning has notation ѕА = (ѕА1, ѕА2, ѕА3) and so on.

    580 I. Sonin and E. Presman Remark 1. Let ѕА be a (ќ≥, c)-PR and ѕА1 be a (ќ≥, c1)-PR, where c1 > c. Then obviously ѕА can be represented as ѕА = (ѕА1, ѕА2), where ѕА2 is a (ќ≥, c)-PR. For a PR ѕА = (ѕА1, ѕА2) let us define a random time ѕГ = min(n : Xn = xвИЧ or An вИИ{ѕА2}), i.e. a time of termination or first usage of edges from ѕА2. For the sake of brevity we call time ѕГ a time of switching from ѕА1 to ѕА2. Remark 2. Note that for any trajectory ѕГ вЙ§ѕДвИЧ, but at the same time P ѕА1 x {XѕДвИЧ= y} = P ѕА x {XѕГ = y} for any y. Equivalently, a moment of termi- nation for ѕА1 is a moment of switching from ѕА1 to ѕА2 in ѕА. Using strong Markov property and the total probabilities formula it is easy to obtain for a ѕА = (ѕА1, ѕА2) RѕА(x) = EѕА1 x BѕГвИТ1

    i=0 ri + RѕА2(XѕГ) C = RѕА1(x) +

    y P ѕА1 x (XѕГ = y)RѕА2(y). (6.1) Lemma 1. If ѕА1, ѕА2 вИИќ†(x) and {ѕА1} = {ѕА2}, then P ѕА1 x {XѕДвИЧ= y} = P ѕА2 x {XѕДвИЧ= y} (6.2) for all y вИИS, and, in particular, for y = xвИЧ, i.e. QѕА1(x) = QѕА2(x). This lemma is an analog of the simple statement that for a set of inde- pendent trials the probability of at least one success does not depend on the order in which these trials are tested. We prove this lemma in an Appendix. Let us call PRs ѕА1 and ѕА2 disjoint if ѕА1 вИИќ†(x1), ѕА2 вИИќ†(x2), and F(x1)вИ© F(x2) = вИЕ. Let ѕА1 вИИќ†(x1) and ѕА2 вИИќ†(x2) are disjoint and ѕА вИИќ†. Then for any x, x1вИ™x2 вКВx we can define PRs ѕА12 = (ѕА1, ѕА2, ѕА) and ѕА21 = (ѕА2, ѕА1, ѕА) such that both belong to ќ†(x). Where no confusion is possible we will use shorthand notations RѕАi(x) = Ri, QѕАi(x) = Qi, ќ±ѕАi(x) = ќ±i and so on. Lemma 2. Consider two PRs ѕАij = (ѕАi, ѕАj, ѕА) вИИќ†(x), i, j = 1, 2, i ћЄ= j, where ѕА1, ѕА2 are disjoint, and ѕАi вИИќ†(xi). Then for any x, x1 вИ™x2 вКВx Rij = Ri + diRj + R, (6.3) where di = 1 вИТQi, and the term R is the same for both ѕА12 and ѕА21. Proof. Given PR ѕАij = (ѕАi, ѕАj, ѕА) let us define ѕГi as the switching moment from (ѕАi, ѕАj) to ѕА. Since ѕА1 and ѕА2 are disjoint we have {(ѕА1, ѕА2)} = {(ѕА2, ѕА1)} and therefore by Lemma 1 the distributions P ѕАij x {XѕГi = y} coincide. Hence, according to (6.1) the term R is the same for both ѕА12 and ѕА21. The equality in Lemma 3 follows from formula (6.1) applied to the moments ѕДi of switching from ѕАi to (ѕАj, ѕА) and the fact that for disjoint PRs the second factor of each term in the sum y P ѕАi x (XѕДi = y)RѕА(y) is the same for all y such that y ћЄ= xвИЧ and P ѕАi x (XѕДi = y) ћЄ= 0. Notice that any equality for R always implies similar a equality for Q because QѕА = RѕА if all rewards r(e) are put equal r(e) = q(e). Indeed,

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 581 let us consider a reward function rвА≤(e, x) defined by rвА≤(ei, xi+1) = 1 if ei ћЄ= eвИЧ, xi+1 = xвИЧ, and rвА≤(ei, xi+1) = 0 otherwise. Then for such func- tion we have QѕА(x) = RѕА(x). It remains to note that averaging of such rвА≤ gives r(ei) = q(ei). Therefore, we have an equality similar to (6.3) for Q, and hence ќ±ij = ќ±iQi + ќ±jdiQj + R Qi + diQj + Q . (6.4) Corollary 1. If under assumptions of Lemma 2 ќ±1 > ќ±2 then ќ±12 > ќ±21 (and therefore R12 > R21). Proof. The assertion follows from (6.3) and (6.4), using the obvious equal- ity Q1 + (1 вИТQ1)Q2 = Q2 + (1 вИТQ2)Q1. The next lemma shows how the вАЬisolated tailвАЭ of a PR ѕА contributes to the value of RѕА. If ѕА вИИќ†(x) we will omit sometimes the dependence on x of R, Q and ќ±. Lemma 3. Let ѕА1 вИИќ†(x), ѕА2 вИИќ†(e), e /вИИ{ѕА1}, ѕА = (ѕА1, ѕА2). Then RѕА(x) = RѕА1(x) + d1RѕА2(e), (6.5) where d1 = P ѕА1 x {e вИИXѕГ}. Proof follows directly from the second equality in (6.1) and the relations RѕА2(y) = RѕА2(e) for e вИИy, and RѕА2(y) = 0 if XѕГ = y and e /вИИy. Note that the assumption ѕА2 вИИќ†(e) is crucial for validity of (6.5). According to our remark after Lemma 2, Lemma 3 implies that the formula similar to (6.1) (with replacement R by Q) holds for QѕА, and hence we have ќ±ѕА = R1 + d1R2 Q1 + d1Q2 = ќ±1Q1 + ќ±2d1Q2 Q1 + d1Q2 . (6.6) Formula (6.6) and elementary properties of proportions imply Corollary 2. Under the assumptions of Lemma 3 either ќ±ѕА1 = ќ±ѕА2 = ќ±ѕА or min{ќ±ѕА1, ќ±ѕА2} < ќ±ѕА < max{ќ±ѕА1, ќ±ѕА2}. (6.7) 7 Proof of Theorem 1 We prove theorem 1 by induction on the number k of edges in the forest F(x) of an initial state x. We denote by |C| the number of elements in a finite set C. For k = 1 the theorem is trivial. Suppose it is proved for all x with |F(x)| вЙ§k, and suppose an initial state is x with |F(x)| = k + 1. We consider separately two cases: (A) when |x| > 1, and (B) when |x| = 1. In both cases we will use a well-known Bellman Optimality Principle, a corollary of a Bellman equation for the expected total reward: if ѕА is an optimal strategy (for the problem A

    582 I. Sonin and E. Presman or B) for an initial state x, then after the first step it remains optimal for all states that follow x. We prove theorem under the Uniqueness assumption U. The proof for the general case is similar. Case (A). In this case point (c) of the theorem is trivial since each |T(e)| вЙ§ k for each e вИИF(x) so, it remains to prove (a) and (b). For any e вИИx let ѕА0 be an ќ±-PR (with cutoffvalue c = 0 in Problem A and cutoffvalue c = вИТвИЮin Problem B). According to the induction assumption it is an optimal PR for any state in F(x)\e. So, if ѕА is optimal on F(x), and applies e on the first step, by Optimality Principle, PR (e, ѕА0) is also optimal. Let ќ±1 = ќ±(e1) = maxeвИИx ќ±(e). Let us show that ѕА = (e, ѕА0) is not optimal if ќ± = ќ±(e) < ќ±1. Using the description of ѕА0 by point (a) of Theorem 1 and Remark 1 we have ѕА = (e, ќљ1, ѕА1, ќљ), where ќљ1 is an ќ±-PR defined on a set T(e)\e with cutoff value c1 = mineвА≤вИИT (e)\e {eвА≤ : ќ±(eвА≤) > ќ±1} > ќ±1; PR ѕА1 is an ќ±-PR with cutoff value c = ќ±1, and ќљ is a continuation of ќ±-PR (with cutoffvalue c(ќљ) = 0 in Problem A and cutoffvalue c = вИТвИЮin Problem B). Note that it is possible that ќљ1 = вИЕ. According to the definitions of ќ±-PR and the value c1, all edges used by ѕА1 belong to T(e1). Note that PRs ѕА1 and ѕА2 = (e, ќљ1) are disjoint because they are defined on different trees T(e1) and T(e), and that ќ±ѕА2(e) вЙ§ќ± = ќ±(e) because PR (e, ќљ1) can be different than ѕАe which gives a solution to the Auxiliary Problem. Let us show that PR ѕХ = (ѕА1, ѕА2, ќљ) is better than ѕА = (e, ќљ1, ѕА1, ќљ) = (ѕА2, ѕА1, ќљ). According to the induction assumption ќ±ѕА1(e1) = ќ±1, so ќ±ѕА1(e1) = ќ±1 > ќ± вЙ• ќ±ѕА2(e). Applying Corollary 1 to ѕА1 and ѕА2 we obtain that RѕХ > RѕА, i.e. ѕА is not an optimal strategy. It means that an optimal strategy either coincides with (e1, ѕА0) or appoints to quit from the very beginning. Case (B). In this case x consists only of one edge and we denote it e0. The first step for any policy is defined uniquely and the resulting state has a forest with no more than k edges, so by the Optimality Principle the points (a) and (b) of the Theorem are trivial but point (c) is trivial for all edges except e0. Let ѕАe0 = (e0, ќљ), where ѕАe0 be a solution of an Auxiliary Problem for e0, ќ±-PR ќљ вИИќ†(N(e0)) and c is a corresponding cutoffvalue. Let us show that 1) if e вИИF ќљ(e0), then ќ±(e) вЙ•ќ±(e0), 2) if e /вИИF ќљ(e0) and e вИИN(eвА≤) for some eвА≤ which is a leaf of F ќљ(e0) then ќ±(e) < ќ±(e0). This will prove that c can be taken equal to ќ±(e0), i.e. satisfying point (c). Suppose that 1) is not true and e вИИF ќљ(e0) is such that ќ±(e) < ќ±(eвА≤) for all eвА≤ вИИF ќљ(e0), and ќ±(e) < ќ±(e0.) By the definition of (ќ±, ќ±(e))-PR all edges that can be used in ќљ after e belong to T(e). So, PR (e0, ќљ) can be represented in a form ѕА = (ѕА1, ѕА2) where ѕА2 вИИќ†(e) is an ќ±-PR. Consequently ќ±ѕА2(e) вЙ§ќ±(e) < ќ±(e0) = ќ±(e0,ќљ). But Lemma 3 and Corollary 2 applied to PR (e0, ќљ) = (ѕА1, ѕА2) imply that ќ±(e0,ќљ) < ќ±ѕА1. This contradicts to the definition of ѕА(e0).

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 583 Suppose that 2) is not true and we select e вИИN(eвА≤) such that eвА≤ is a leaf of F ќљ(e0), ќ±(e) > ќ±(e0) and e is the smallest among such e. Let ѕА2 is (ќ±, ќ±(e)) -PR, ѕА2 вИИќ†(e). Consider PR ѕА = (ѕА1, ѕА2), where ѕА1 = (e0, ќљ). Then ѕА is a PR with c = ќ±(e). Applying Lemma 1 and Corollary 2 to PR ѕА and using that ќ±ѕА1(e0) = ќ±(e0) < ќ±(e) = ќ±ѕА2(e) we obtain that ќ±(ѕА) > ќ±ѕА1. This contradicts to the definition of ѕА1. 8 A recursive algorithm to calculate ќ±(e) and ѕАвИЧ(e) To formulate the algorithm we first consider the structure of (ќ±, c)-PR ѕАc вИИ ќ†(x) for an initial state x. Recall that for any PR ѕА and initial state x we can consider RѕА(x), QѕА(x), F ѕА(x) (or T ѕА(e) if x consists of one edge e) (see (4.1)). We will consider also N ѕА(x) = N(F ѕА(x)), where N(F) for any subforest of initial forest F0 denotes the set of all edges that follow immediately вАЬleafsвАЭ of F, i.e. the set of all edges that do not belong to F, but follow immediately elements of F. For any D вКВN ѕА(x) (including empty set) we will consider also the probability pѕА D(x) = P ѕА x {XѕДвИЧ= D}, i.e. the probability that our decision to quit was taken at the state D. Proposition 2. For any x вИИS there exist a natural number k(x), non- increasing (decreasing in case of Assumption U) numbers ck = ck(x), with c0 = +вИЮ, and edges gk = gk(x) вИИF(x), k = 0, 1, ¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј , k(x), such that for (ќ±, c)-PR ѕАc вИИќ†(x) ѕАc = ѕАck for ck+1 < c вЙ§ck, ck+1 = ќ±(gk), ѕАck+1 = (ѕАck, ѕАвИЧ(gk)), for 0 вЙ§k < k(x); ѕАc = ѕАck(x) for c вЙ§ck(x), (8.1) where ѕАвИЧ(gk) is ќ±-optimizer of gk. Using indices вАЬkвАЭ and вАЬ*вАЭ instead of index ѕА for ѕА = ѕАck and ѕА = ѕАвИЧcorrespondingly we get: ѕА0(x) = (вИЕ), R0(x) = 0, Q0(x) = 0, F 0(x) = (вИЕ), N 0(x) = x, p0 x(x) = 1 and if N k(x) ћЄ= вИЕthen F k+1(x) = F k(x) A TвИЧ(gk), (8.2) N k+1(x) =

    N k(x) \ gk

    A NвИЧ(gk), (8.3) Rk+1(x) = Rk(x) + RвИЧ(gk)

    D: gkвИИDвКВNk(x), pk D(x), (8.4) Qk+1(x) = Qk(x) + QвИЧ(gk)

    D: gkвИИDвКВNk(x) pk D(x). (8.5) If D вКВN k+1(x) then there exist unique D1 вКВN k(x){gk} and D2 вКВNвИЧ(gk) such that D = D1 I D2, and if D1 = вИЕ, D2 ћЄ= вИЕ, then pk+1 D (x) = pk {gk}(x)pвИЧ D2(gk), (8.6)

    584 I. Sonin and E. Presman if D2 = вИЕ, then pk+1 D (x) = pk D1(x) + pk {gk}I D1(x)pвИЧ вИЕ(gk), (8.7) if D1 ћЄ= вИЕ, D2 ћЄ= вИЕ, then pk+1 D (x) = pk {gk}I D1(x)pвИЧ D2(gk). (8.8) Proof. For the sake of simplicity we will prove Proposition 2 under Assump- tion U. The changes for the general case is straightforward. Let for some k вЙ•0 we know ck, ѕАck, Rk(x), Qk(x), F k(x), N k(x), and pk D(x) for any D вКВN k(x). The set N k(x) corresponds to all potentially available edges after application of ѕАck. If N k(x) = вИЕthen k = k(x) and evidently we obtain the last equal- ity in (8.1). If N k(x) = вИЕthen according to the definition of (ќ±, c)-PR, all elements of N k(x) have the value of ќ± less or equal to ck. Consider the edge in N k(x) with maximal value of ќ± and denote it gk. Denote ck+1 = ќ±(gk). Since there is no edges in N k(x) with ck+1 < ќ±(e) < ck we have proved the first equality in (8.1). According to Remark 1 ѕАck+1(x) = (ѕАck, ѕА2), where ѕА2 вИИќ†(gk) is (ќ±, ќ±(gk))-PR. according to statement c) of Theorem 1 this PR coincides with ѕАвИЧ(gk). It proves third equality in (8.1) and equalities (8.2), (8.3. Equalities (8.4)-(8.8) are the results of application of total probability formula. It completes the proof of Proposition 2. Note that if ќ±(e) is known for all e вИИF(x) then Proposition 2 gives the algorithm for calculation of optimal value of functional in Main Problems A and B. In case of Problem B it coincides with Rk(x)(x), and in case of Problem A it coincides with Rk0(x), where k0 = inf{k : ќ±(gkвИТ1) > 0}. Now we can formulate algorithm for finding ќ±(e). Recall that we defined ќ±(e) as r(e)/q(e) for leaves, and if ќ±(eвА≤) is defined for all eвА≤ вИИT(e)\e then as a maximum of RѕАc(e)/QѕАc(e) over c, where ѕАc вЙ°ѕАc(e) is a PR which first tests e and after that uses (ќ±, c) -PR from ќ†(N(e)). It is evident that Proposition 2 is valid also for ѕАc(e) with initial values c0 = +вИЮ, ѕА0(e) = (e), R0(e) = r(e), Q0(e) = q(e), ќ±0(e) = R0(e)/Q0(e), T 0(e) = {e}, N 0(e) = N(e), p0 D(e) = pD(e) for all D вКВN 0(e). Define ќ±k(e) = Rk(e)/Qk(e). According to Corollary 2 (see also Proposition 1 and (5.5)) there exists kвИЧ= kвИЧ(e) such that ќ±k(e) increases for k < kвИЧand decreases for k > kвИЧand kвИЧ= inf{k : ќ±(gk) вЙ§ќ±k}. It means that for finding ќ±(e) we need to conduct calculations (8.4)-(8.8) sequentially from k = 0 till the time when ќ±(gk) < ќ±k and set ќ±(e) = ќ±kвИЧ. Note that if e вИИѕАвИЧ(eвА≤) for some eвА≤, then we do not need to remember all data for e. We need remember only the data for eвА≤. Consider now example 1 with q(1) = 0.2, pвИЕ(1) = 0.1 p{3,4}(1) = 0.4, p{5}(1) = 0.3, r(1) = 0.8; q(2) = 0.08, pвИЕ(2) = 0.17, p{6,7}(2) = 0.5, p{8}(2) = 0.25, r(2) = 0.1; q(3) = 0.1, pвИЕ(3) = 0.24, p3,{9,10}(3) = 0.5, p{11}(3) = 0.16, r3 = 0.2; q(4) = 0.3, pвИЕ(4) = 0.7, r(4) = 1.8; q(6) = 0.04, pвИЕ(6) = 0.96, r(6) = 0.36; q(5) = 0.24, pвИЕ(5) = 0.71, p{12,13}(5) = 0.05, r(5) = вИТ0.3; q(7) = 0.05, pвИЕ(7) = 0.45, p{14}(7) = 0.5, p{15}(7) = 0.3, r(7) = 0.05; q(8) = 0.08, pвИЕ(8) = 0.92, r(8) = 0.8; q(9) = 0.09, pвИЕ(9) = 0.91, r(9) = 0, 72;

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 585 q(10) = 0.7, pвИЕ(10) = 0.3, r(10) = вИТ1.4; q(11) = 0.5, pвИЕ(11) = 0.5, r(11) = 5.5; q(12) = 0.2, pвИЕ(12) = 0.8, r(12) = вИТ0.8; q(13) = 0.6, pвИЕ(13) = 0.4, r(13) = 0.6; q(14) = 0.01, pвИЕ(14) = 0.99, r(14) = 0.3; q(15) = 0.4, pвИЕ(15) = 0.6, r(15) = вИТ1.2. For leaves we have: ќ±(4) = r(4) q(4) = 6, ќ±(6) = r(6) q(6) = 9, ќ±(8) = r(8) q(8) = 10, ќ±(9) = r(9) q(9) = 8, ќ±(10) = r(10) q(10) = вИТ2, ќ±(11) = r(11) q(11) = 11, ќ±(12) = r(12) q(12) = вИТ4, ќ±(13) = r(13) q(13) = 1, ќ±(14) = r(14) q(14) = 3, ќ±(15) = r(15) q(15) = вИТ3. To calculate values of ќ± for stems we use the algorithm. ќ±0(3) = r(3) q(3) = 2. Since N(3) = {9, 10, 11} and ќ±(11) = 11 > ќ±(9) = 8 > ќ±0(3) > ќ±(10) = вИТ2, we set g0(3) = 11. Since N(11) = вИЕwe have from (8.3)- (8.5): N 1(3) = {9, 10}, R1(3) = r(3)+p{11}(3)r(11) = 0.2+0.16вИЧ5.5 = 1.08, Q1 3 = q3 +p3,{11}q11 = 0.1+0.16вИЧ0.5 = 0.18. Using (8.7) we get: p1 {9,10}(3) = p{9,10}(3) = 0.5, p1 вИЕ(3) = pвИЕ(3) + p{g}(3)pвИЧ вИЕ(11) = 0.24 + 0.16 вИЧ0.5 = 0.32, ќ±1(3) = R1(3) Q1(3) = 1.08 0.18 = 6. Since N 1(3) = {9, 10} and ќ±(9) = 8 > ќ±1(3) > ќ±(10) = вИТ2, we set g1 = 9. Since N(9) = вИЕwe have from (8.3)-(8.5): N 2(3) = {10}, R2(3) = R1(3) + p1 {9,10}(3)r(9) = 1.08 + 0.5 вИЧ0.72 = 1.44, Q2(3) = Q1(3) + p1 {9,10}(3)q(9) = 0.18 + 0.5 вИЧ0.09 = 0.225. Using (8.7) we get: p2 {10}(3) = p1 {9,10}(3)pвИЕ(9) = 0.5 вИЧ0.91 = 0.455, p2 вИЕ(3) = p1 вИЕ(3) = 0.32, ќ±2(3) = R2(3) Q2(3) = 1.44 0.225 = 6.4. Since N 2(3) = {10} and ќ±(10) = вИТ2 < ќ±2(3) = 6.4 we have: ѕАвИЧ(3) = ѕА8(3) = (3, 11, 9), NвИЧ(3) = N 2(3) = {10}, RвИЧ(3) = R2(3) = 1, 44, QвИЧ(3) = Q2(3) = 0.225, pвИЧ {10}(3) = p2 {10}(3) = 0.455, pвИЧ вИЕ(3) = p2 вИЕ(3) = 0.32, ќ±(3) = ќ±2(3) = 6.4. Calculations for the edges 5,7,1, and 2 are absolutely analogous and we omit them. This calculations give: ѕАвИЧ(5) = ѕА1(5) = (5, 13), NвИЧ(5) = {12}, RвИЧ(5) = вИТ0.27, QвИЧ(5) = 0.27, pвИЧ {12}(5) = 0.02, pвИЧ вИЕ(5) = 0.71, ќ±(5) = вИТ1; ѕАвИЧ(7) = ѕА3(7) = (7, 14), NвИЧ(7) = {15}, RвИЧ(7) = 0.2, QвИЧ(7) = 0.1, pвИЧ {15}(7) = 0.3, pвИЧ вИЕ(7) = 0.6, ќ±(7) = 2; ѕАвИЧ(1) = ѕА6.4(1) = (1, 3, 11, 9, 4), NвИЧ(1) = {5, 10}, RвИЧ(1) = 1, 934, QвИЧ(1) = 0.383, pвИЧ {10}(1) = 0.1274, pвИЧ {5}(1) = 0.3, pвИЧ вИЕ(1) = 0.1896, ќ±(1) =вЙИ5.05; ѕАвИЧ(2) = ѕА9(2) = (2, 8, 6), NвИЧ(2) = {7}, RвИЧ(2) = 0, 48, QвИЧ(2) = 0.12, pвИЧ {7}(2) = 0.48, pвИЧ вИЕ(2) = 0.4, ќ±(2) = 4.

    586 I. Sonin and E. Presman 9 Connection with the Gittins index and concluding remarks Now we outline how to obtain the proof of the celebrated Gittins result from Theorem 1. Suppose that there is a fixed number m of finite Markov chains with transition probabilities pk(i, j), j = 1, 2, ..., m, and a discount factor ќ≤, 0 < ќ≤ < 1. Each time a DM can engage one of these MC and a reward rk(i) is obtained if k-th MC was engaged at state i. Without loss of gen- erality these MCs have common state space S = {1, 2, ..., N} and we can describe the possible transitions of these MCs using infinite forest F0 which consists of m trees T1, ..., Tm. The set N(e) = {e1, ..., eN} and partitions of N(e) = {e1} вИ™{e2} вИ™...{eN} are the same for each e вИИF0. The probability p(Nj) for an edge ei вИИTk is equal to ќ≤pk(i, j), and q(e) = (1вИТќ≤), i.e. we use a standard way to replace a discount by a transition to an absorbing state. The reward r(e) = rk(i) if e = ei вИИTk. We can prove that for any given ќµ > 0 we can specify n sufficiently large so that the value function for an initial problem and a problem with finite forest Fn will be different less than in ќµ. For such finite forest we can apply Theorem 1 where the optimality of PR based on indices all ќ±n(e) was established. It can be proved also that if if e = ei вИИTk then limnвЖТвИЮќ±n(e) = ќ±k(i), where ќ±k(i) is the value of the classical Gittins index (GI) for the k-th MC at state i. This proves the optimality of PR based on GI. Note also that the value of GI will be obtained as a limit. At the same time there are algorithms that calculate GI for finite case in a finite number of steps, e.g in [13]. A new recursive algorithm to calculate GI even in a more general model is proposed in [12]. Not also that the idea of an infinite forest can be applied to the case of a countable state space under assumption e.g. that the ratio r(e)/q(e), e вИИF is bounded by a constant c. Note that this assumption holds for the classical Gittins case if Markov chain is finite or r(e) is bounded if it is countable. 10 Appendix Proof of Lemma 1. We prove lemma 1 by induction on n = |{ѕА}|. For n = 1 lemma is trivial. For n = 2 we have {ѕАi} = {e1, e2}. If x contains only one of these edges then both PRs use this edge on the first step and the other one on the second, so they coincide. Let ei вИИx for i = 1, 2, then there are two possible PRs, ѕА1 = (e1, e2), and ѕА2 = (e1, e2). From the definition of transition probabilities P ѕАi x {XѕДвИЧ= y} > 0 only if either y = xвИЧ, or y has a form ykQ = ((x \ (e1, e2)) вИ™Nk(e1) вИ™NQ(e1)) for some 0 вЙ§k вЙ§j(e1), 0 вЙ§ Q вЙ§j(e2), and P ѕАi x {xѕДвИЧ= yiQ} = pi(e1)pQ(e2) for i = 1, 2. For y = xвИЧwe have P ѕАi x {xѕДвИЧ= xвИЧ} = 1 вИТ yћЄ=eвИЧP ѕА1 x {xѕДвИЧ= y} for i = 1, 2. This completes the proof of Lemma 1 for the case |{ѕА}| = 2.

    Gittins Index Theorem for Randomly Evolving Graphs 587 Suppose now that (6.2) is proved for n = k, k вЙ•2, and |{ѕАi}| = k + 1. Given x вИИS, denote ei the senior edge among edges in x for a PR ѕАi. Then each ѕАi can be represented as ѕАi = (ei, ќљi), where ќљi is a continuation of ѕАi and |{ќљ1}| = k. Note that if e1 = e2 then {ќљ1} = {ќљ2} and lemma 1 holds because the first step for both PRs will be the same and after the first step we can apply an induction assumption to PRs ќљi. Suppose that e1 ћЄ= e2. Then let us introduce two new PRs ѕАвА≤ 1 = (e1, e2, ќљ) and ѕАвА≤ 2 = (e2, e1, ќљ), where ќљ is a PR with {ќљ} = {ѕА} \ {e1, e2}. For two pairs of PRs; ѕА1 and ѕАвА≤ 1, and for ѕА2 and ѕАвА≤ 2 lemma 1 holds because each pair has the same first edge and we discussed this case earlier. Thus we have to show that Lemma 1 holds for a pair of PRs ѕАвА≤ 1 and ѕАвА≤ 2. This pair of PRs is different only for the first two steps but according to our proof for the case of n = 2 the distributions of X2 coincide. After that we can apply an induction assumption. This completes the proof of Lemma 1. Acknowledgement This work was partly supported by RFBR (grant 03вАУ01вАУ00479). References 1. Berry, D.A., Fristedt, B.: Bandit problems. Res. 29, no. 1, 162вАУ181 (2004) 4. Feldman, D.: Contributions to the вАЬtwo-armed banditвАЭ problem. Ann. Math. Statist. 33, 847вАУ856 (1962) 5. Feinberg E., Schwartz A. (eds): Handbook of Markov Decision Processes. Kluwer Acad. Publ. (2002) 6. Gittins, J. C.: A Multi-armed Bandit Allocation indices. Wiley , Ney York (1989) 7. Gittins, J.C., Jones, D.M.: A dynamic allocation index for the sequential design experiments. In: Gani, J., Sarkadi, K., Vince, I. (eds) Progress in Statistics, Eu- ropean Meeting of Statisticians I. North Holland, Amsterdam, 241вАУ266 (1974). 8. Granot, D., Zuckerman, D.,: Optimal sequencing and resource allocation in re- search and development projects. Management Science 37, 140вАУ156 (1991) 9. Mitten, L.G.: An analytic solution to the least cost testing sequence problem. J. of Industr. Eng., 11, no. 1, 17 (1960) 10.
