    588 I. Sonin and E. Presman 12. Sonin, I.M.: A Generalized Gittins index for Markov chain and its recursive calculation. Manuscript (2004) 13. Varaiya, P., Walrand J., Buyukkoc, C.: Extensions of the multiarmed bandit problem: the discounted case. IEEE Trans. Autom. Control AC-30, no. 5, 426вАУ 439 (1985) 14. Weiss, G.: Branching bandit processes. Probability in the Engineering and In- formation Sciences 2, 269вАУ278 (1988) 15. Whittle, P.: Arm-acquiring bandits. Annals of Probability 9, 284вАУ292 (1981)

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios for the Utility Maximization Problem in Discrete Time Financial Market Models вИЧ Mikl¬іos R¬іASONYI and `Lukasz STETTNER Computer and Automation Institute Hungarian Academy of Sciences, 1111 Budapest, Kende utca 13-17, Hungary. rasonyi@sztaki.hu Institute of Mathematics, Polish Academy of Sciences, ¬іSniadeckich 8, 00-950 Warsaw, Poland. stettner@impan.gov.pl Summary. We consider an investor whose preferences are described by a concave nondecreasing function U : (0, вИЮ) вЖТR and prove that in an arbitrage-free discrete- time market model there is a strategy attaining the supremum of expected utility at the terminal date provided that this supremum is finite. Key words: utility function, portfolio optimization, dynamic programming Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 93E20, 91B28, 91B16 1 Introduction and main result In this paper we study the existence of optimal portfolios for maximizing expected utility of the terminal wealth. His or her preferences are described by a concave nondecreasing function U : (0, вИЮ) вЖТR, trading dates occur at discrete time instants. Recently, [8, 9] have treated the same problem, concentrating rather on the construction of pricing operators using optimal strategies. In this paper we apply the machinery which was developed in [7] for utility functions U : R вЖТR and establish the existence of optimal strategies under minimal conditions (U is concave nondecreasing, absence of arbitrage, the value function is finite). This general theorem has already been anticipated in Section 3.1 of [3] where the authors proved it for a one-step model and nonnegative price process. вИЧL. Stettner was supported by PBZ KBN 016/P03/99; M. R¬іasonyi by OTKA grant T 047193 and F049094.

    590 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner A usual setting for discrete-time market models is considered: a probability space (вД¶, F, P); a filtration (Ft)0вЙ§tвЙ§T such that F0 contains P-null sets and a d-dimensional adapted process (St)0вЙ§tвЙ§T describing the prices of d risky assets in a given economy. It is implicitly assumed that investors also dispose of a risk-free asset S0 t := 1, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T; hence trading strategies can be arbitrary d-dimensional predictable processes (ѕХt)1вЙ§tвЙ§T , where ѕХi t denotes the investorвАЩs holding in asset i at time t. Predictability means that ѕХt is FtвИТ1-measurable, i.e. the portfolio is chosen before new prices St are revealed. Let ќ¶ denote the family of all predictable trading strategies. The value of a portfolio ѕХ starting from initial capital c is given by V c,ѕХ t = c + t

    i=1 вЯ®ѕХi, вИЖSiвЯ©, where вЯ®¬Ј, ¬ЈвЯ©denotes scalar product in Rd, вИЖSi := Si вИТSiвИТ1 and c > 0. Introduce for each t = 1, ..., T a random subset Dt(ѕЙ) of Rd: the affine hull of the support of the (regular) conditional distribution of вИЖSt given FtвИТ1, see Proposition 4.1. In this paper we impose the following (fairly natural) trading constraint: portfolio value should not become negative. Define for c > 0 the set of admis- sible trading strategies as A(c) := {ѕХ вИИќ¶ : V c,ѕХ t вЙ•0 a.s., 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T}. (1.1) In what follows, ќЮt will denote the set of Ft-measurable d-dimensional random variables. When a date t is fixed, ѕХt is called admissible for the initial capital x if ѕХt вИИќЮx tвИТ1, where ќЮx t := {ќЊ вИИќЮt : x + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖSt+1вЯ©вЙ•0 a.s.}, x вИИ[0, вИЮ). Define for any Ft-measurable nonnegative random variable H ќЮt(H) := {ќЊ вИИќЮt : H + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖSt+1вЯ©вЙ•0 a.s.}, and also ЋЬќЮt := {ќЊ вИИќЮt : |ќЊ(ѕЙ)| = 1, ќЊ(ѕЙ) вИИDt+1(ѕЙ) a.s.}. Assumption 1.1 U : (0, вИЮ) вЖТR is a concave nondecreasing function. We extend U by continuity to zero (U(0) = U(0+) may be вИТвИЮ) and set U(x) = вИТвИЮ, x < 0. By convention, U вА≤(x) denotes the left-hand derivative of U at x; U + is the positive part of U. We are dealing with maximizing the expected utility of the terminal wealth: EU(V c,ѕХ T ) вЖТmax, ѕХ вИИA(c). (1.2)

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 591 So as to have a well-posed problem the following absence of arbitrage (NA) property will be imposed: (NA) вИАc > 0 вИАѕХ вИИA(c) (V c,ѕХ T вЙ•c a.s. =вЗТV c,ѕХ T = c a.s.). (1.3) Theorem 1.1. Let Assumption 1.1 hold and let S satisfy (1.3). Suppose that the expectations in the definition below exist (though might take the value вИТвИЮ) u(c) := sup ѕХвИИA(c) EU(V c,ѕХ T ), (1.4) and u(c) < вИЮfor all c вИИ(0, вИЮ). (1.5) Then for each c вИИ(0, вИЮ) there exists a strategy ѕХвИЧ(c) satisfying u(c) = EU(V c,ѕХвИЧ(c) T ), moreover one has ѕХвИЧ t (c) вИИDt a.s. We will present the proof of Theorem 1.1 in Sections 2 and 3. A possible extension (Theorem 3.1) to random utility functions is sketched in Remarks 2.2 and 3.1. Remark 1.1. In fact, it is sufficient to suppose that there exists c > 0 such that u(c) < вИЮ. In this case Lemma 2.2 entails that for any strategy ѕХ and any ќї вЙ•1 we have the bound U +(V ќїc,ѕХ T ) вЙ§2ќї[U +(V c,ѕХ/ќї T ) + U(2)], with the right-hand side having a finite expectation as u(c) < вИЮ. This means that for any cвА≤ > c the expectations in the definition (1.4) of u(cвА≤) exist. It is easy to see that u(¬Ј) is concave, hence if we had u(cвА≤) = вИЮfor some cвА≤ > c then u (c/2) = u(ќ±cвА≤ + (1 вИТќ±)c/4) вЙ•ќ±u(cвА≤) + (1 вИТќ±)u(c/4) = вИЮ, where ќ± вИИ(0, 1) is a suitable number. But this is impossible, as by monotonic- ity u (c/2) вЙ§u(c) < вИЮ. Remark 1.2. Theorem 1.1 fails to be true in general semimartingale models. As it was shown by counterexamples of [6], in the continuous-time case certain additional properties have to be imposed on U to guarantee the existence of optimal strategies. We mention a uniqueness result whose proof is omitted as it is identical to that of Theorem 2.8 in [7].

    592 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner Theorem 1.2. If U is strictly concave then there is a unique optimal strategy ѕХвИЧsatisfying ѕХвИЧ t вИИDt a.s. We will need an alternative characterization of (NA), see the Proposition below. This statement is implicit in Theorem 3 of [4], where it is shown that absence of arbitrage is equivalent to the fact that the origin lies in the relative interior of the convex hull of the support of conditional distribution of вИЖSt given FtвИТ1. We make this more explicit and вАЬquantitativeвАЭ: Proposition 1.1. Under (NA) the set Dt(ѕЙ) is a linear subspace of Rd, al- most surely. The (NA) condition implies the existence of Ft-measurable ran- dom variables ќ≤t, ќЇt > 0, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T вИТ1, such that for any p вИИЋЬќЮt P(вЯ®p, вИЖSt+1вЯ©< вИТќ≤t|Ft) вЙ•ќЇt (1.6) almost surely. Proof. The вАЬstandardвАЭ absence of arbitrage property is the following (NAвАЩ) вИАѕХ вИИќ¶ (V 0,ѕХ T вЙ•0 a.s. вЗТV 0,ѕХ T = 0 a.s.) It follows from Theorem 3 of [4] and Proposition 3.3 of [7] that if (NAвАЩ) holds then Dt is a linear subspace and (1.6) holds. So it suffices to establish that (NA) and (NAвАЩ) are equivalent. The (NAвАЩ) condition trivially implies (NA) since if we had a ѕХ violating (NA) we would immediately get V 0,ѕХ T = V c,ѕХ T вИТc вЙ•0, P(V 0,ѕХ T

    0) > 0, which contradicts (NAвАЩ). The other direction is also clear: if there is ѕХ such that (NAвАЩ) fails then we know from the implication (b) вЗТ(a) of Theorem 3 in [4] that there is ѕИ such that V 0,ѕИ t вЙ•0, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T and P(V 0,ѕИ T 0) > 0. For such a strategy V c,ѕИ t вЙ•c a.s., 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T, P(V c,ѕИ T c) > 0, so ѕИ вИИA(c) and (NA) is violated. 2 Optimal strategy in the one-step case Let V : [0, вИЮ) √Ч вД¶вЖТR вИ™{вИТвИЮ} be a function such that for almost all ѕЙ, V (¬Ј, ѕЙ) is a nondecreasing continuous concave function, V (x, ѕЙ) is finite for x вИИ(0, вИЮ) and V (x, ¬Ј) is F-measurable for any fixed x. Let H вКВF be a ѕГ-algebra containing P-null sets. Let Y be a d-dimensional random variable. Denote by ќЮ the family of H-measurable d-dimensional random variables. Put

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 593 ЋЬќЮ := {ќЊ вИИќЮ : |ќЊ(ѕЙ)| = 1, ќЊ(ѕЙ) вИИD(ѕЙ) a.s.}, ќЮx := {ќЊ вИИќЮ : x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©вЙ•0 a.s.}, x вИИ[0, вИЮ), here D denotes the smallest affine subspace containing the support of the conditional distribution of Y with respect to H (see Section 4). We suppose that D is actually a linear subspace a.s. and that P(вЯ®p, Y вЯ©< вИТќі|H) вЙ•ќЇ, for all p вИИЋЬќЮ, (2.1) with some H-measurable random variables ќЇ, ќі > 0. Introduce also ќЮH := {ќЊ вИИќЮ : H + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©вЙ•0 a.s.}, for each H-measurable nonnegative random variable H. This setting will be applied in Section 3 with H = FtвИТ1, D = Dt, and Y = вИЖSt; V will be the supremum of conditional expected utility if trading begins at time t. Assume that V (1) вЙ•0 a.s. (2.2) and for all x вИИ[0, вИЮ) ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) < вИЮ a.s. (2.3) We need some preparatory results. Proposition 2.1. Let ќЊ вИИќЮx be fixed. There exists a version of y вЖТE(V (y + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H), y вЙ•x, such that it is a nondecreasing upper semicontinuous concave function (per- haps taking the value вИТвИЮ), for almost all ѕЙ. Proof. Fix a version of F(q, ѕЙ) := E(V (q +вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) for q вИИQ+. The follow- ing inequalities hold almost surely for any pairs q1 вЙ§q2 of rational numbers: F(q1) вЙ§F(q2), F(q1 + q2 2 ) вЙ•F(q1) + F(q2) 2 . Let us fix a P-zero set N such that outside this set the above inequalities hold. Fix y вИИ[x, вИЮ) and take rationals qn ÷Б y. The monotone convergence theorem yields F(y+) = lim n F(qn) = lim n E(V (qn + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) = E(V (y + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H), a.s. showing that the right-continuous pathwise extension of F is as required.

    594 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner Remark 2.1. If E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) is almost surely finite then, by concavity, we get an almost surely continuous version from the above proposition. Proposition 2.2. Let x > 0, ќЊ вИИќЮx. Let ЋЖќЊ(ѕЙ) be the orthogonal projection of ќЊ(ѕЙ) on the subspace D(ѕЙ). Then ЋЖќЊ вИИќЮx. Furthermore, E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЖќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) = E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H), almost everywhere. Proof. To check that x + вЯ®ЋЖќЊ, Y вЯ©вЙ•0 a.s. (2.4) we proceed as follows: take a regular version ¬µ(dx, ѕЙ) of P(Y вИИdx|H). Notice that for almost all ѕЙ: supp ¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ) вКВD(ѕЙ), ¬µ({y : x + вЯ®ќЊ(ѕЙ), yвЯ©вЙ•0}, ѕЙ) = 1, so necessarily ¬µ({y : x + вЯ®ЋЖќЊ(ѕЙ), yвЯ©вЙ•0}, ѕЙ) = 1, which shows (2.4). For the rest of this technical proof we refer to Proposition 4.6 of [7]. Lemma 2.1. Let us fix x0 > 0. There exists a H-measurable random variable K = K(x0) > 0 such that for any x вЙ§x0 and ќЊ вИИќЮx satisfying ќЊ вИИD we have |ќЊ| вЙ§K almost surely. Proof. Indeed, we know from (2.1) that if |ќЊ| > x0/ќі then necessarily for any x вЙ§x0 P(x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©< 0|H) вЙ•ќЇ > 0, which means that ќЊ /вИИќЮx, hence we may set K := x0/ќі. When showing the existence of an optimal strategy we will use a Fatou- lemma argument for which we need the two lemmata below. Lemma 2.2. Let V : (0, вИЮ) вЖТR be a concave nondecreasing function such that V (1) вЙ•0. Then for all x > 0 and ќї вЙ•1 V +(ќїx) вЙ§2ќї[V +(x) + V (2)]. Proof. First let us suppose x вЙ•2. In this case V +(ќїx) = V (ќїx) вЙ§V (x) + V вА≤(x)(ќїx вИТx) вЙ§ V (x) + V (x) вИТV (1) x вИТ1 x(ќї вИТ1) вЙ§V (x) + 2(ќї вИТ1)(V (x) вИТV (1)) вЙ§ 2ќїV (x),

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 595 where we used the concavity and the inequalities x вЙ•2 and V (x) вЙ•V (1) вЙ•0. For x < 2 by monotonicity V +(ќїx) вЙ§V (2ќї) вЙ§2ќїV (2). Putting these estimations together, we get, for any x > 0, that V +(ќїx) вЙ§2ќї max{V (2), V +(x)} вЙ§2ќї[V +(x) + V (2)], as desired. Lemma 2.3. Fix x > 0. There exists a nonnegative random variable L such that for any ќЊ вИИќЮx, ќЊ вИИD V +(x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©) вЙ§L, E(L|H) < вИЮa.s. (2.5) Proof. Take the random set M(ѕЙ, x) of Proposition 4.2 and its linear span R(ѕЙ, x), see Proposition 4.3. It suffices to carry out the majoration separately on the sets Ak := {ѕЙ : dim R(ѕЙ) = k} вИИH, 0 вЙ§k вЙ§d, i.e. finding Lk such that V +(x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)IAk вЙ§Lk, E(Lk|H) < вИЮ. The case k = 0 being trivial we may and will suppose that dim R = m вЙ•1 is a fixed constant. Let the Rd-valued random variables ќґj, 1 вЙ§j вЙ§m, be such that they form a (random) orthonormal bases of R, almost surely. Define W := {вИТ1, +1}m and introduce the vectors ќЄi := m

    j=1 i(j)ќґj, i вИИW. It is clear from Lemma 2.1 that M(x) is contained in the m-dimensional cube with edges KќЄi, i вИИW, almost surely. As a linear function defined on a polyhedral set attains its maximum on the extreme points, we immediately have for all selectors ќЊ вИИM(x), i.e. for any ќЊ вИИќЮx, ќЊ вИИD x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©вЙ§ @ iвИИW (x + KвЯ®ќЄi, Y вЯ©) a.s. So by monotonicity V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©) вЙ§ @ iвИИW V (x + KвЯ®ќЄi, Y вЯ©) a.s. Thus, V +(x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©) вЙ§

    iвИИW V +(x + KвЯ®ќЄi, Y вЯ©) a.s. (2.6)

    596 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner The relative interior ri M is also a random set by Proposition 4.3. Let ѕБ be an H-measurable selector of ri M. Then the projection on вД¶of each set Bi := {(ѕЙ, a) вИИвД¶√Ч (0, 1] : ѕБ + a(KќЄi вИТѕБ) вИИM(x)} вИИH вКЧB((0, 1]), i вИИW, is of full measure. Hence Bi admit H-measurable selectors ѕИi. Now Lemma 2.2 implies that V +(x + KвЯ®ќЄi, Y вЯ©) = V +(x + вЯ®ѕБ, Y вЯ©+ вЯ®KќЄi вИТѕБ, Y вЯ©) вЙ§ (2.7) 2 1 ѕИi [V +(ѕИi(x + вЯ®ѕБ, Y вЯ©) + ѕИiвЯ®KќЄi вИТѕБ, Y вЯ©) + V (2)] вЙ§ 2 ѕИi [V +(x + вЯ®ѕБ, Y вЯ©+ вЯ®ѕИi(KќЄi вИТѕБ), Y вЯ©) + V (2)], i вИИW. where we used Lemma 2.2, monotonicity of V , ѕИi вЙ§1 and ѕБ вИИќЮx. Define L := 2

    iвИИW 1 ѕИi [V +(x + вЯ®ѕБ, Y вЯ©+ вЯ®ѕИi(KќЄi вИТѕБ), Y вЯ©) + V (2)]. As ѕИi is chosen in such a manner that ѕБ + ѕИi(KќЄi вИТѕБ) вИИM(x), i вИИW, we have, using (2.3) E(L|H) = 2

    iвИИW 1 ѕИi E(V +(x + вЯ®ѕБ, Y вЯ©+ вЯ®ѕИi(KќЄi вИТѕБ), Y вЯ©)|H)+ +2m+1E(V (2)|H) < вИЮ. The bounds (2.6) and (2.7) imply (2.5). Now a regular version of the essential supremum is shown to exist. Proposition 2.3. There is a function G : [0, вИЮ) √Ч вД¶вЖТR вИ™{вИТвИЮ} which is a version of ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) for each fixed x вИИ[0, вИЮ); nondecreasing, concave, continuous on [0, вИЮ) and finite valued for x вИИ(0, вИЮ), for almost all ѕЙ. Proof. Take a version G(q, ѕЙ) of the essential supremum, for q вИИQ+. As 0 вИИќЮx for all x, E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) is almost surely finite-valued for each x вИИ(0, вИЮ). Outside a P-null set the monotonicity and convexity relations G(q1) вЙ§G(q2), if q1 вЙ§q2, G 1 2 (q1 + q2)

    вЙ•G(q1) + G(q2) 2 , q1, q2 вИИQ+,

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 597 hold, hence on a set of probability one we may extend G by monotonicity to a nondecreasing concave function on (0, вИЮ) which is finite-valued (and hence continuous). Take any x вИИ(0, вИЮ) and two sequences of rationals qn ÷А x, rn ÷Б x. As for y вЙ§z the relation ќЮy вКЖќЮz holds, we get that ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ•lim sup n G(qn) = G(x), ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ§lim inf n G(rn) = G(x), showing that G(x) is a version of the essential supremum for each x вИИ(0, вИЮ). By construction G(0) is a version of the essential supremum at x = 0, so it remains to check the continuity of G at zero, i.e. the equality lim lвЖТвИЮess. sup ќЊвИИќЮ1/l E(V (1/l + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) = ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮ0 E(V (вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H). (2.8) The limit exists by monotonicity on a set of probability one and certainly greater than or equal to the right-hand side above. The particular structure of the family whose essential supremum is taken guarantees that for each l вИИN there exists ќЈl вИИќЮ1/l such that |ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮ1/l E(V (1/l + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) вИТE(V (1/l + вЯ®ќЈl, Y вЯ©)|H)| вЙ§1/l a.s. We may supppose ќЈl вИИD by Proposition 2.2. Then Lemmata 2.1 and 4.1 imply that a random subsequence ќЈlk exists such that ќЈlk вЖТЋЬќЈ a.s., as k вЖТвИЮ and ЋЬќЈ вИИвИ©x>0ќЮx = ќЮ0. The continuity of V , Lemma 2.3 and the Fatou lemma guarantee that lim kвЖТвИЮE(V (1/lk + вЯ®ќЈlk, Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ§E(V (вЯ®ЋЬќЈ, Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ§ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮ0 E(V (вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H), hence assertion (2.8) follows. We construct a sequence of strategies converging to the optimal value for all x вИИ(0, вИЮ). Lemma 2.4. There exist B(R+) вКЧH-measurable functions ќЊn(x, ѕЙ) and suit- able versions Gn(x, ѕЙ) of E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊn(x), Y вЯ©)|H), such that outside a fixed P-null set we have for all x вИИ(0, вИЮ) lim nвЖТвИЮGn(x) = G(x), (2.9) and the limit is attained in a nondecreasing way.

    598 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner Proof. It suffices to prove this for x вИИ[1, 2); in an analogous way we get sequences ќЊn for all the intervals [n, n + 1), [1/(n + 1), 1/n), n вИИN, and then by вАЭpasting togetherвАЭ we finally get an approximation along all the positive axis. Fix a version G(¬Ј, ѕЙ) of the essential supremum given by Proposition 2.3. First notice that, for fixed x вИИ(0, вИЮ), the family of functions E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H), ќЊ вИИќЮx, (2.10) is directed upwards, so there is a sequence ќЈn(x) вИИќЮx such that lim nвЖТвИЮвЖСE(V (x + вЯ®ќЈn(x), Y вЯ©)|H) = ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H), almost surely. Let us fix such a sequence for each dyadic rational q вИИ[1, 2). Now set ќЊ0(x, ѕЙ) := 0. Let us suppose that ќЊ0, . . . , ќЊnвИТ1 have been defined, as well as ќЊn(x, ѕЙ) for x вИИ[1, 1 + k/2n) for some 0 вЙ§k вЙ§2n вИТ1. If k = 0 we set ќЊn(x, ѕЙ) := ќЇ0 n for x вИИ[1, 1 + 1/2n), where ќЇ0 n is chosen such that E(V (1 + вЯ®ќЇ0 n, Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ•E (V (1 + вЯ®ќЊnвИТ1(1), Y вЯ©) |H) вИ®E (V (1 + вЯ®ќЈn(1), Y вЯ©) |H) . If 1 вЙ§k вЙ§2n вИТ1 we set ќЊn(x, ѕЙ) := ќЇk n(ѕЙ), x вИИ

    1 + k 2n , 1 + k + 1 2n

    , where ќЇk n вИИќЮ1+k/2n is chosen in such a way that almost everywhere E(V (1 + k/2n + вЯ®ќЇk n, Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ•uk n вИ®vk n вИ®wk n. (2.11) Here we use the notations uk n := E

    V

    1 + k 2n + O ќЊn

    1 + k вИТ1 2n

    , Y P H

    , vk n := E

    V

    1 + k 2n + O ќЈn

    1 + k 2n

    , Y P H

    , wk n := E

    V

    1 + k 2n + O ќЊnвИТ1

    1 + k 2n

    , Y P H

    . This is possible, as the family (2.10) is directed upwards and ќЮy вКЖќЮz for y вЙ§z. The latter fact implies also that actually ќЇk n вИИќЮy for y from the interval [1 + k/2n, 1 + (k + 1)/2n), so ќЊn(x) вИИќЮx for all x вИИ[1, 2). Using Propositions 2.1 and 2.3 as well as (2.11) it is easy to see that there is a P-null set N such that outside this set G(¬Ј, ѕЙ) is continuous and suitable versions Gn(¬Ј, ѕЙ) of

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 599 E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊn(x), Y вЯ©)|H)(ѕЙ) are nondecreasing and continuous on subintervals of the form [1 + k/2n, 1 + (k + 1)/2n), 0 вЙ§k вЙ§2n вИТ1, for all n вИИN. By the definitions of ќЈn(x) and ќЊn(x) we see immediately that (outside another P-null set N вА≤) for all dyadic rationals q вИИ[1, 2) G(q) = lim nвЖТвИЮвЖСGn(q). Consequently, outside N вИ™N вА≤ the sequence Gn(x) is nondecreasing in n, for all x вИИ[1, 2). For any x вИИ[1, 2) and dyadic rationals q1 < x < q2, Gn(q1) вЙ§Gn(x) вЙ§Gn(q2) outside N, so necessarily G(q1) вЙ§lim inf n Gn(x) вЙ§lim sup n Gn(x) вЙ§G(q2), outside N вИ™N вА≤. The function G being continuous at x, we get almost sure convergence to G in all points x вИИ[1, 2). The following lemma contains the actual construction of the one-step op- timal strategy. Lemma 2.5. There exists a B(R+)вКЧH-measurable function ЋЬќЊ(x, ѕЙ) such that for each x вИИ(0, вИЮ) E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЬќЊ(x), Y вЯ©)|H) = ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H). Proof. It suffices to prove this, e.g., when x вИИ[1, 2), then one can вАЭpaste togetherвАЭ the optimal strategy for x вИИ(0, вИЮ). We take an approximating sequence ќЊn as provided by Lemma 2.4, then change to the projections ЋЖќЊn figuring in Proposition 2.2. Using Proposition 2.1 and the structure of the approximating sequence one can see that Gn is a version of E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЖќЊn, Y вЯ©)|H), and almost surely E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЖќЊn, Y вЯ©)|H) вЖТG(x), for all x вИИ[1, 2). Then take x0 := 2 and apply Lemma 2.1. It follows that, almost surely, |ЋЖќЊn(x)| вЙ§K(x0), for all x вИИ[1, 2). Now use Lemma 4.1 to find a random subsequence ЋЬќЈk := ЋЖќЊnk of ЋЖќЊn con- verging to some ЋЬќЊ. Apply the Fatou lemma (we shall justify its use in a while): E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЬќЊ(x), Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ•lim sup kвЖТвИЮ E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЬќЈk(x), Y вЯ©)|H).

    600 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner By the structure of the random subsequence in Proposition 4.1 E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЬќЈk(x), Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ•E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊnk(x), Y вЯ©)|H), so the construction of the approximating sequence in Lemma 2.4 implies that for all x E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЬќЊ(x), Y вЯ©)|H) вЙ•G(x) a.s. hence by the definition of G E(V (x + вЯ®ЋЬќЊ(x), Y вЯ©)|H) = G(x) a.s. It remains to check that we were allowed to invoke the Fatou lemma. This follows from Lemma 2.3, the random variable L figuring there is a suitable majorant. Proposition 2.4. The ЋЬќЊ constructed in the proof of Lemma 2.5 is such that ЋЬќЊ(H) вИИќЮH and G(H) = E(V (H + вЯ®ЋЬќЊ(H), Y вЯ©)|H) = ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮH E(V (H + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) a.s., for any H-measurable [0, вИЮ)-valued random variable H; here G is the function constructed in Proposition 2.3. Proof. By the piecewise constant structure of the approximating sequence of Lemma 2.4 we have that P(вИАx вИАn x + вЯ®ЋЖќЊn(x, ѕЙ), Y вЯ©вЙ•0) = 1. Random subsequences do not change this, so P(вИАx x + вЯ®ЋЬќЊ(x, ѕЙ), Y вЯ©вЙ•0) = 1, which implies that ЋЬќЊ(H) вИИќЮH. For the proof of вАЬвЙ§вАЭ in the first equality we refer to Proposition 4.10 of [7]. The left-hand side of the second equality is clearly not greater than the right-hand side, so we need only to show that for fixed ќЊ вИИќЮH we have: G(H, ѕЙ) вЙ•E(V (H + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) a.s. (2.12) For step functions H (2.12) is clearly true. Now for general H take a decreasing step-function approximation Hn of H. Then ќЊ вИИќЮH вКЖќЮHn for all n, hence G(Hn) вЙ•E(V (Hn + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) a.s., the left-hand side converges by path regularity of G, the right-hand side by monotone convergence, so (2.12) is proved.

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 601 Remark 2.2. Results of the present section may be extended to a slightly more general setting. We briefly sum up the major modifications. Let V : [0, вИЮ) √Ч вД¶вЖТR вИ™{вИТвИЮ} be a function such that V (x, ¬Ј) is F- measurable for each x and for almost all ѕЙ the function V (¬Ј, ѕЙ) is nondecreas- ing, concave and upper semicontinuous. Put ќШ(ѕЙ) := 0 вИ®sup{q вИИQ+ : V (q, ѕЙ) = вИТвИЮ}. Assume that ќШ is a bounded random variable and introduce the random variable ќЄ := ess. inf{X : ѕГ(X) вКВH, вИГѕХ вИИќЮ s.t. X + вЯ®ѕХ, Y вЯ©вЙ•ќШ a.s.}. Redefine ќЮH for each H-measurable H вЙ•ќЄ as ќЮH := {ќЊ вИИќЮ : H + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©вЙ•ќШ a.s.}. Replace (2.3) by вИАx вИИ[0, вИЮ) ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮќЄ+x E(V (x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) < вИЮ and (2.2) by V (F) вЙ•0, (2.13) where F > 0 is some constant. Otherwise let the notations and hypotheses at the beginning of this section be valid. One needs to construct regular versions of y вЖТE(V (ќЄ + y + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H), y вЙ•x, for ќЊ вИИќЮќЄ+x in Proposition 2.1. Proposition 2.2 and Lemma 2.1 remain almost unchanged except for re- placing ќЮx by ќЮx+ќЄ. The estimation of Lemma 2.2 is slightly modified due to (2.13), Lemma 2.3 remains practically the same. Instead of Proposition 2.3 one has to establish the following: Proposition 2.5. There is a function G : [0, вИЮ) √Ч вД¶вЖТR вИ™{вИТвИЮ} such that G(ќЄ + y) is a version of ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮќЄ+y E(V (ќЄ + y + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H) for each fixed y вИИ[0, вИЮ); G(x, ѕЙ) = вИТвИЮif x < ќЄ(ѕЙ), G(¬Ј, ѕЙ) is a nondecreas- ing, concave, continuous function on [ќЄ(ѕЙ), вИЮ) and finite-valued on (ќЄ(ѕЙ), вИЮ), for almost all ѕЙ. In Lemma 2.4 the approximating sequence should be constructed on the random interval (ќЄ, вИЮ). Then along the same arguments we finally get: Proposition 2.6. There exists a B(R) вКЧH-measurable function ЋЬќЊ such that for any H-measurable random variable H вЙ•ќЄ we have ЋЬќЊ(H) вИИќЮH and G(H) = E(V (H + вЯ®ЋЬќЊ(H), Y вЯ©)|H) = ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮH E(V (H + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©)|H), almost surely.

    602 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner 3 Dynamic programming From now on we suppose that U(1) = 0. (3.1) This is to assure (2.2), which plays a role in Lemma 2.2. Obviously there is no loss of generality here: by adding a constant to the utility function one may always have (3.1) without changing the optimal strategy. Define by recursion the following random functions. The existence of the conditional expectations will be shown in Proposition 3.1 below. Set UT (x, ѕЙ) := U(x), x вИИ[0, вИЮ), ѕЙ вИИвД¶, (3.2) and, for t < T, Ut(x, ѕЙ) := ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx t E(Ut+1(x + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖSt+1вЯ©)|Ft)(ѕЙ), x вИИ[0, вИЮ), ѕЙ вИИвД¶; (3.3) later on we shall omit the dependence on ѕЙ in notations. Set Ut(x) := вИТвИЮ, x < 0. Proposition 3.1. The functions Ut, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T, have versions which are almost surely nondecreasing, concave and continuous on [0, вИЮ), finite-valued on (0, вИЮ) and Ut(1) вЙ•0, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T, (3.4) ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx tвИТ1 E(Ut(x + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖStвЯ©)|FtвИТ1) < вИЮ, x вИИ[0, вИЮ), 1 вЙ§t вЙ§T, (3.5) where the expectations are well-defined. There exist B(R+) вКЧFt-measurable functions ЋЬќЊt+1, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T вИТ1, such that for all x вИИ(0, вИЮ) Ut(x, ѕЙ) = E(Ut+1(x + вЯ®ЋЬќЊt+1(x), вИЖSt+1вЯ©)|Ft). (3.6) Proof. Going backwards from T to 0, we apply Lemma 2.5 with V := Ut, H = FtвИТ1, D := Dt, Y := вИЖSt. We need to verify the conditions of Section 2: D is a random subspace by Propositions 1.1 and 4.1; (2.1) follows from (1.6); (2.2) and (2.3) will come from (3.4) and (3.5). We will check (3.4) and (3.5) in a little while. Expectations exist by (2.3), a good version for Ut is provided by Proposi- tion 2.3. Denote the resulting ЋЬќЊ of Lemma 2.5 by ЋЬќЊt, 1 вЙ§t вЙ§T; it certainly satisfies (3.6). It remains to establish (3.4) and (3.5). The first statement is true, since Ut(x) вЙ•E(Ut+1(x)|Ft) вЙ•¬Ј ¬Ј ¬Ј вЙ•E(UT (x)|Ft) = U(x), (3.7) and U(1) = 0 by Assumption 1.1. As to the second statement, it holds for t = T by (1.5). For t = T вИТ1 consider

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 603 UT вИТ1(x + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖST вИТ1вЯ©) = E(UT (x + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖST вИТ1вЯ©+ вЯ®ЋЬќЊT вИТ1(x + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖST вИТ1вЯ©), вИЖST вЯ©)|FT вИТ1), so the statement holds by (1.5) again. For other values of t the notation gets more and more complicated but the same argument applies. Now set ѕХвИЧ 1(c) := ЋЬќЊ1(c) and define recursively: ѕХвИЧ t+1(c) := ЋЬќЊt+1(c + t

    j=1 вЯ®ѕХвИЧ j, вИЖSjвЯ©), 1 вЙ§t вЙ§T вИТ1. Joint measurability of ЋЬќЊt assures that ѕХвИЧ= ѕХвИЧ(c) is a predictable process with respect to the given filtration. Proposition 3.2. We have ѕХвИЧвИИA(c) and for any strategy ѕХ вИИA(c) E(U(V c,ѕХ T )|F0) вЙ§E(U(V c,ѕХвИЧ T )|F0) = U0(c). (3.8) Proof. Notice that ѕХвИЧ t вИИќЮtвИТ1(V c,ѕХвИЧ tвИТ1 ), so ѕХвИЧвИИA(c). Remembering UT = U and using Proposition 2.4, we may rewrite the right-hand side of (3.8) as follows: E(UT (V c,ѕХвИЧ T )|F0) = E(E(UT (V c,ѕХвИЧ T вИТ1 + вЯ®ѕХвИЧ T , вИЖST вЯ©)|FT вИТ1)|F0) = = E(UT вИТ1(V c,ѕХвИЧ T вИТ1 )|F0). Continuing the procedure, we finally arrive at ѕХвИЧвИИA(c) and E(U(V c,ѕХвИЧ T )|F0) = E(U1(V c,ѕХвИЧ 1 )|F0) = E(U1(c + вЯ®ѕХвИЧ 1, вИЖS1вЯ©)|F0) = U0(c). (3.9) We remark that all conditional expectations below exist by Proposition 3.1. By the definition of UT вИТ1 and ѕХ вИИA(c) one has ѕХT вИИќЮT вИТ1(V c,ѕХ T вИТ1) and E(UT (V c,ѕХ T )|FT вИТ1) = E(UT (V c,ѕХ T вИТ1 + вЯ®ѕХT , вИЖST вЯ©)|FT вИТ1) вЙ§UT вИТ1(V c,ѕХ T вИТ1) a.s. Iterate the same argument and obtain E(U(V c,ѕХ T )|F0) вЙ§U0(c) a.s. (3.10) Putting (3.9) and (3.10) together, one gets exactly (3.8). Proof (of Theorem 1.1). Proposition 3.2 shows that u(c) = EU0(c) and the ѕХвИЧconstructed in the last two sections is a maximizer such that ѕХвИЧ t вИИDt. Remark 3.1. We indicate how Theorem 1.1 can be generalized. Let B вЙ•0 be a bounded random variable, interpreted as a contingent claim. Define recursively the superhedging prices as follows:

    604 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner ѕАT (B) := B, ѕАt(B) := ess. inf{X : ѕГ(X) вКВFt, вИГѕХ вИИќЮt X + вЯ®ѕХ, вИЖSt+1вЯ©вЙ•ѕАt+1(B) a.s.}, for 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T вИТ1. Define for c > ѕА0(B) A(B, c) := {ѕХ вИИќ¶ : V c,ѕХ t вЙ•ѕАt(B) a.s., 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T}, and redefine for each Ft-measurable H вЙ•ѕАt(B) ќЮt(H) := {ќЊ вИИќЮt : H + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖSt+1вЯ©вЙ•ѕАt+1(B) a.s.} Theorem 3.1. Suppose that the conditions of Theorem 1.1 hold and F0 is trivial. Then for all c > ѕА0(B) u(B, c) := sup ѕХвИИA(B,c) EU(V c,ѕХ T вИТB) < вИЮ, (3.11) and there exists ѕХвИЧ(c) вИИA(B, c) such that u(B, c) = EU(V c,ѕХвИЧ(c) T вИТB). Proof. As F0 is trivial, ѕА0(B) is a constant; (3.11) follows from (1.5) and the boundedness of B. Since B is bounded, by Assumption 1.1 there exists F > 0 such that UT (F) вЙ•0, and this will remain true for each Ut by (3.7). Replace (3.2) by UT (x, ѕЙ) := U(x вИТB(ѕЙ)), x вЙ•B(ѕЙ), UT (x, ѕЙ) = вИТвИЮ, x < B(ѕЙ), set for y вИИ[0, вИЮ) Ut(ѕАt(B) + y, ѕЙ) := ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮt(ѕАt(B)+y) E(Ut+1(ѕАt(B) + y + вЯ®ќЊ, вИЖSt+1вЯ©)|Ft), and Ut(x, ѕЙ) = вИТвИЮ, x < ѕАt(B)(ѕЙ), instead of (3.3) and follow the argument of this section. Use the extended setting of section 2 as explained in Remark 2.2. Apparently, ќШ, ќЄ will corre- spond to ѕАt+1(B), ѕАt(B) in the backward induction. The rest of the argument is essentially unchanged. 4 Auxiliary results We shall often rely on the measurable selection theorem, see III. 70-73 of [2]. Let H вКВF be a ѕГ-algebra containing P-null sets. An H-measurable random set or measurable multifunction A is an element of H вКЧB(Rd), where B(Rd) denotes the Borel sets of Rd. A random affine subspace A is an H-measurable random set such that A(ѕЙ) is an affine subspace of Rd for each ѕЙ. Let Y be a d-dimensional random variable and ¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ) := P(Y вИИ¬Ј|H) a regular version of its conditional distribution. Let D(ѕЙ) be the smallest affine subspace of Rd containing the support of ¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ).

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 605 Proposition 4.1. D is an H-measurable random affine subspace. Proof. We begin by showing that supp ¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ) or, equivalently, its complement suppC¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ) is a random set. Let G be a countable base for the topology of Rd. Then suppC¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ) := A {G вИИG : ¬µ(G, ѕЙ) = 0}, which proves the assertion. Actually, Z(ѕЙ) := conv(supp¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ)) is a random set, where conv(¬Ј) denotes closed convex hull, this follows from Theorem III. 40 on p. 87 of [1]. Take a measurable selector ќљ(ѕЙ) of Z(ѕЙ); Z вИТZ contains the origin in its relative interior and B A nвИИN {nz : z вИИZ(ѕЙ) вИТZ(ѕЙ)} C + ќљ(ѕЙ), equals D(ѕЙ), which proves the proposition. Proposition 4.2. Fix x > 0. There exists M(x) вИИHвКЧB(Rd) which is convex, compact (a.s.) and ќЊ вИИќЮx and ќЊ вИИD a.s. вЗРвЗТќЊ вИИM(x) a.s. Proof. Take a sequence of H-measurable random variables ѕГi such that for almost all ѕЙ the sequence ѕГi(ѕЙ), i вИИN, is dense in supp¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ). Such a sequence exists by Theorem III. 22 on p. 74 of [1]. Define the convex closed random set ЋЬ M(x) := ? iвИИN {(ѕЙ, p) : x + вЯ®p, ѕГi(ѕЙ)вЯ©вЙ•0}. The following series of equivalences is clear: ќЊ вИИќЮx вЗРвЗТP(x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©вЙ•0) = 1 вЗРвЗТP(x + вЯ®ќЊ, Y вЯ©вЙ•0|H) = 1, a.s. вЗРвЗТ¬µ({y вИИRd : x + вЯ®ќЊ(ѕЙ), yвЯ©вЙ•0}, ѕЙ) = 1 a.s. вЗРвЗТ {y вИИRd : x + вЯ®ќЊ(ѕЙ), yвЯ©вЙ•0} вКЗsupp¬µ(¬Ј, ѕЙ) a.s. вЗРвЗТ {y вИИRd : x + вЯ®ќЊ(ѕЙ), yвЯ©вЙ•0}ѕГi(ѕЙ) a.s., i вИИN, and this last one means precisely ќЊ вИИЋЬ M(x) a.s. Define M(x) := ЋЬ M(x) вИ©D. The argument of Lemma 2.1 implies that M(x) is compact, almost surely, so M(x) is as desired. Let ri M(x, ѕЙ) denote the relative interior of M(x, ѕЙ) and let R = R(x, ѕЙ) denote the linear span of M(x, ѕЙ). Proposition 4.3. Both ri M(x) and R(x) are H-measurable random sets.

    606 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner Proof. The set M вИТM contains zero in its relative interior, hence R = A nвИИN {nz : z вИИM вИТM} and this is indeed a random set. Take H-measurable random variables ќґi(ѕЙ), 1 вЙ§i вЙ§d, which are orthogonal and generate R(x) (some of them might be 0), this follows easily from the measurable selection theorem. The function [dim R(x)](ѕЙ) := d

    j=1 I{ќґjћЄ=0}(ѕЙ) is H-measurable. It suffices to prove the proposition separately on the events {ѕЙ : dim R(x, ѕЙ) = m} вИИH, for each m вЙ§d. The case m = 0 is trivial, so we suppose, without loss of generality, that dim R(x, ѕЙ) = m вЙ•1 for a fixed m. We may assume that ќґi(ѕЙ), 1 вЙ§i вЙ§m is an orthonormed basis of R(x, ѕЙ). The interior points are precisely those, around which a little cube can be drawn in R(x) which still belongs to M(x). As M(x) is convex, this is equivalent to the fact that the edges of that cube belong to M(x). Hence ri M(x) = A nвИИN п£± п£≤ п£≥(ѕЙ, p) : p + 1 n m

    j=0 i(j)ќґj(ѕЙ) вИИM(ѕЙ, x), вИАi вИИ{вИТ1, +1}m п£Љ п£љ п£Њ, which is clearly a measurable multifunction. Lemma 4.1. Let a, b вИИR, a < b. Let ќЈn : [a, b] √Ч вД¶вЖТRd be a sequence of B([a, b]) вКЧH-measurable functions such that for almost all ѕЙ вИАx lim inf nвЖТвИЮ|ќЈn(x, ѕЙ)| < вИЮ. Then there is a sequence nk of B([a, b]) вКЧH-measurable N-valued functions, nk < nk+1, k вИИN, such that ЋЬќЈk(x, ѕЙ) := ќЈnk(x, ѕЙ) converges for all x to some ЋЬќЈ(x, ѕЙ) as k вЖТвИЮ, for almost all ѕЙ. To put it more concisely, there is a convergent random subsequence. Proof. This is just a variant of Lemma 2 in [5]. 5 Conclusions Finally, we present a concrete model class where there exists an optimal in- vestment strategy. Let W denote the family of random variables with finite moments of all orders.

    On the Existence of Optimal Portfolios 607 Proposition 5.1. Let U satisfy Assumption 1.1. Let |St| вИИW, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T, and supppose that (1.6) holds with 1/ќ≤t вИИW, 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T вИТ1. Then (1.5) holds and the assertion of Theorem 1.1 is true. Proof. For notational simplicity let ќЊвИЖSt denote scalar product. We shall show by backward induction that there exists Jt вИИW such that Ut(x) вЙ§Jtx < вИЮ, x вИИ(0, вИЮ), 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T. Indeed, for t = T this is true with JT := U вА≤(1). Now suppose that this statement has been established for s вЙ•t + 1. Proposition 2.2 and Lemma 2.1 imply that ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx t E(Ut+1(x + ќЊвИЖSt+1)|Ft) = ess. sup ќЊвИИќЮx t ,ќЊвИИDt+1 E(Ut+1(x + ќЊвИЖSt+1)|Ft) вЙ§E(Ut+1(x + |вИЖSt+1|x/ќ≤t)|Ft) вЙ§E(Jt+1x + Jt+1x|вИЖSt+1|/ќ≤t|Ft), so we may set Jt := E(Jt+1(1 + |вИЖSt+1|/ќ≤t)|Ft). Finally we arrive at the bound U0(x) вЙ§J almost surely, where J вИИW so we get for all x > 0 u(x) = EU0(x) < вИЮ, i.e. (1.5) holds true. The proof of Theorem 1.1 shows that there exists an optimal ѕХвИЧ. Remark 5.1. The previous proposition applies, in particular, when ќ≤t = ќ≤ is a deterministic constant. The hypothesis that (1.6) holds with deterministic ќ≤ is called uniform no-arbitrage condition. This assumption has been introduced in [8]. Remark 5.2. One may consider concave nondecreasing functions U : R вЖТR. Under (NA), (1.5) and additional hypotheses on U there exists an optimal strategy in ќ¶, see [7]. We may also look at вАЬtameвАЭ portfolios, i.e. ѕХ such that there exists a вИИR satisfying V c,ѕХ t вЙ•a a.s., 0 вЙ§t вЙ§T. (5.1) Theorem 1.1 of the present paper immediately implies that (under (NA) and (1.5)) there exists an optimal strategy among ѕХ satisfying (5.1) with a fixed a. It is an intriguing question under what kind of conditions there is an optimal control among all tame strategies. References 1. Castaing, C., Valadier, M.: Convex Analysis and Measurable Multifunctions. Lec- ture Notes in Mathematics, 580, Springer, Berlin, 1977.

    608 M. R¬іasonyi and ^L. Stettner 2. Dellacherie, C., Meyer, P.-A.: Probabilities and Potential. Mathematical Studies 29, North-Holland, Amsterdam, 1978. 3. F¬®ollmer, H., Schied, A.: Stochastic Finance. Walter de Gruyter, Berlin, 2002. 4. Jacod, J., Shiryaev, A.N.: Local martingales and the fundamental asset pricing theorems in the discrete-time case. Finance and Stochastics, 2, 259вАУ273, 1998. 5. Kabanov, Yu. M., Stricker, Ch.: A teachersвАЩ note on no-arbitrage criteria. S¬іeminaire de Probabilit¬іes, XXXV, 149вАУ152, Springer, Berlin, 2001. 6. Kramkov, D.O., Schachermayer, W.: The asymptotic elasticity of utility functions and optimal investment in incomplete markets. Ann. Appl. Probab., 9, 904вАУ950, 1999. 7. R¬іasonyi, M., Stettner, L.: On the utility maximization problem in discrete-time financial market models. Forthcoming in Annals of Applied Probability. 8. Sch¬®al, M.: Portfolio optimization and martingale measures. Math. Finance, 10, 289вАУ303, 2000. 9. Sch¬®al, M.: Price systems constructed by optimal dynamic portfolios. Math. Meth- ods Oper. Res., 51, 375вАУ397, 2000.

    The Optimal Stopping of a Markov Chain and Recursive Solution of Poisson and Bellman Equations Isaac M. SONIN Department of Mathematics, University of North Carolina at Charlotte, Charlotte, NC, 28223, USA. imsonin@email.uncc.edu Summary. We discuss a modified version of the Elimination algorithm proposed earlier by the author to solve recursively the problem of optimal stopping of a discrete-time Markov chain with finite or countable state space. This algorithm and the idea behind it are applied to solve recursively discrete versions of the Poisson and Bellman equations. Key words: Markov chain, optimal stopping, Elimination algorithm, State Reduc- tion approach, Poisson equation, Bellman equation. Mathematics Subject Classification (2000): 60J22, 62L15, 65C40 1 Introduction The main goal of this paper is to present a unified recursive approach to the following two related but nevertheless different problems. Problem 1. Find the solution f of the discrete Poisson equation f = c + Pf, (1.1) where Pf(x) = y p(x, y)f(y) is the averaging operator, defined by a transi- tion matrix P, and c is a given function defined on a countable or finite state space X. Problem 2. Solve the problem of optimal stopping (OS) for a Markov chain (MC) with an immediate reward (one-step cost) function c and a ter- minal reward function g. This means to describe an optimal strategy (or ќµ-optimal strategies if there is no optimal strategy), and to find the value function v, which is the minimal solution of the corresponding Bellman (op- timality) equation

    610 I. Sonin v = max(g, c + Pv). (1.2) The main tool to study these problems in this article is the recursive algo- rithm for Problem 2, which we call the Elimination algorithm (EA), described in the papers [12] and [13] by the author (see also [11]). We present EA here in a modified form, and we prove also a new important Lemma 3. We limit our presentation to the case of a finite state space though one of the advantages of our approach is that in many cases it can be applied also to the countable state space. This algorithm is better understood in the context of a group of algorithms which are based on a similar idea and can be called the State Reduction algorithms. We will refer to this idea as the State Reduction (SR) approach. Problem 1 was analyzed on the basis of this approach in Sheskin (1999) [8], see also the references there to the earlier works of Kohlas (1986) and Heyman and Reeves (1989). Note, that formally, Problem 1 can be considered as a special case of Problem 2 when g(x) = вИТвИЮbut we will treat them separately. We start with Problem 2. The author would like to thank Robert Anderson who read the first version of this paper and made valuable comments. 2 Optimal stopping of a MC The optimal stopping problem (OSP) is one of the most developed and exten- sively studied fields of stochastic control. There are two different approaches to OSP, usually called вАЬthe martingale theory of OSP of general stochastic se- quences (processes)вАЭ (formulated by Snell) and вАЬthe OSP of Markov chainsвАЭ. The first one is is exposed in the well-known monograph by Chow, Robbins and Sigmund (1971) [2] (see also the book of T. Ferguson on his website for a modern presentation). The second approach is due to Albert Shiryaev is presented in his classical books (1969, 1978), [9], [10]. (See also Dynkin and Yushkevich (1969), [4]). There are also dozens of books and monographs with chapters or sections on OSP, see, e.g. [1], [7], and more than a thousand pa- pers on this topic. These two approaches basically represent nonstationary and stationary (nonhomogeneous versus homogeneous) situations and though formally they are equivalent, the second approach is more transparent for study and discussion. Formally, OSP of MC is specified by a tuple M = (X, P, c, g, ќ≤), where X is a finite (countable) state space, P = {p(x, y)} is a transition matrix, c(x) is a one step-cost function, g(x) is a terminal reward function, and ќ≤ is a discount factor, 0 < ќ≤ вЙ§1. We call such a model an OS model. A tuple without the terminal reward, M = (X, P, c, ќ≤), we call a reward model, and a tuple M = (X, P), we call a Markov model. A Markov chain (MC) from a family of MCs defined by a Markov model is denoted by (Zn). The probability measure and the expectation for the Markov chain with initial point x are denoted by

    Optimal Stopping and Solution of Poisson and Bellman Equations 611 Px and Ex, respectively. The value function v(x) for an OS model is defined as v(x) = supѕДвЙ•0 Ex[ѕДвИТ1 i=0 ќ≤ic(Zi) + ќ≤ѕДg(ZѕД)], where the sup is taken over all stopping times ѕД вЙ§вИЮ. If ѕД = вИЮwith positive probability we assume that g(ZвИЮ) = 0. It is well-known that the discounted case can be treated as not discounted if an absorbing point xвИЧand new transition probabilities are introduced: pќ≤(x, y) = ќ≤p(x, y) for x, y вИИX, pќ≤(x, xвИЧ) = 1 вИТќ≤, pќ≤(xвИЧ, xвИЧ) = 1. In other words, with probability ќ≤ the Markov chain вАЭsurvivesвАЭ and with complimen- tary probability it transits to an absorbing state xвИЧ. Further we will assume that this transformation is made and we skip the superscript ќ≤. More than that, all subsequent results are valid if the constant ќ≤ is replaced by a function ќ≤(x), 0 вЙ§ќ≤(x) вЙ§1, the probability of вАЭsurvivalвАЭ, ќ≤(x) = Px(Z1 ћЄ= xвИЧ). We will also assume that c(xвИЧ) = g(xвИЧ) = 0. Let Pf(x) be the averaging operator and let Ff(x) = c(x)+Pf(x) be the reward operator. If G вКЖX, let us denote by ѕДG the moment of the first visit to G, i.e., ѕДG = min(n вЙ•0 : xn вИИG). The following statement is the main result for OSP with finite and countable X. Theorem 1. (Shiryaev, [9]) (a) The value function v(x) is the minimal solution of Bellman (optimality) equation (2), i.e. the minimal function sat- isfying the inequalities v(x) вЙ•g(x), v(x) вЙ•Fv(x) for all x вИИX; (b) v(x) = limn vn(x), where vn(x) is the value function for the OSP on a finite time interval of length n; (c) for any ќµ > 0 the random time ѕДќµ = min{n вЙ•0 : g(Zn) вЙ•v(Zn)вИТќµ)}, is an ќµ-optimal stopping time; (d) if Px(ѕД0 < вИЮ) = 1 then ѕД0 = min{n вЙ•0 : g(Zn) = v(Zn)} is an optimal stopping time; (e) if the state space X is finite then set S = {x : g(x) = v(x)} is not empty and ѕД0 is an optimal stopping time. The classical tools to solve the OSP of a MC are: the direct solution of the Bellman equation, which is possible only for very specific MCs; the value iteration method based on the equality v(x) = limn vn(x), mentioned in the item (b) of Theorem 1; and for finite X, the value function v(x) can be found as the solution of a linear programming problem. See also the paper of Davis and Karatzas [3]. The Elimination Algorithm (EA) solves the finite space OS problem in no more than |X| steps, and allows us also to find the distribution of MC at the moment of stopping in the optimal stopping set S, and the expected number of visits to other states before stopping. Using the EA we also can prove in a new and shorter way Theorem 1. As a byproduct we also obtain a new recursive way to solve the Poisson equation. It works also for many OSP with countable X. Before describing the EA in Section 4, in the next section we describe a more general framework of the State Reduction (SR) approach. This is a brief version of a section from ([13]).

    612 I. Sonin 3 Recursive calculation of the MC characteristics and the SR approach Let M1 = (X1, P1) be a Markov model and let D вКВX1, X2 = X1 \ D. Let (Zn) be a Markov chain specified by the model M1 with the starting point x вИИX2. We introduce the sequence of Markov times ѕД0, ѕД1, ..., ѕДn, ..., the moments of zero, first, and so on, visits of (Zn) to the set X2 = X1 \ D, i.e. ѕД0 = 0, ѕДn+1 = min{k > ѕДn : Zk вИИ(X1\D)}, 0 < ѕД1 < ... Let us consider the random sequence Yn = ZѕДn, n = 0, 1, 2, ... For the sake of brevity we assume that ѕДn < вИЮfor all n = 0, 1, 2, ... with probability one. Otherwise we can complement X2 by an additional absorbing point xвИЧand correspondingly modify the transition probabilities participating in Lemma 1. Let us denote by u1(z, ¬Ј) the distribution of the Markov chain (Zn) for the initial model M1 at the moment ѕД1 of the first visit to set X2 (the first exit from D) starting at z вИИD. The strong Markov property and standard probabilistic reasoning imply the following basic lemma of the SR approach: Lemma 1. (Kolmogorov, Doeblin) (a) The random sequence (Yn) is a Markov chain in a model M2 = (X2, P2), where (b) the transition matrix P2 = {p2(x, y)} is given by the formula p2(x, y) = p1(x, y) +

    zвИИD p1(x, z)u1(z, y), (x, y вИИX2). (3.1) Part (a) is immediately implied by the strong Markov property for (Zn), while the proof of (b) is straightforward. Formula (3.1) can be represented in the matrix form (see, e.g., [6]). If the matrix P1 is decomposed as P1 = Q1 T1 R1 P вА≤ 1

    , (3.2) where sub-stochastic matrix Q1 describes the transitions inside of D, P вА≤ 1 de- scribes the transitions inside of X2 and so on, then P2 = P вА≤ 1 + R1U1 = P вА≤ 1 + R1N1T1. (3.3) In this formula U1 is the matrix of distribution of an MC at the moment of the first exit from D (exit probabilities matrix), and N1 is the fundamental matrix for the sub-stochastic matrix Q1, i.e. N1 = вИЮ

    n=0 Qn 1 = (I вИТQ1)вИТ1, (3.4) where I is the |D| √Ч |D| identity matrix. Formula (3.4) implies obviously: N1 = I + Q1N1 = I + N1Q1. (3.5)

    Optimal Stopping and Solution of Poisson and Bellman Equations 613 Both equalities in (3.5) have relatively simple probabilistic interpretations. The first is almost trivial statement while the second recalls the words of Kai Lai Chung вАЭLast exit is a deeper concept than first entranceвАЭ. Given the set D, the matrices N1 and U1 are related by the equality U1 = N1T1. (3.6) We call model M2 the X2-reduced model of M1. For the sake of brevity we will call two such models adjacent. An important case is when the set D consists of one not absorbing point z. In this case formula (3.1) obviously takes the form p2(x, ¬Ј) = p1(x, ¬Ј) + p1(x, z)n1(z)p1(z, ¬Ј), (x вИИX2), (3.7) where n1(z) = 1/(1 вИТp1(z, z)). According to this formula, each row-vector of the new stochastic matrix P2 is a linear combination of two rows of P1 (with the z-column deleted). For a given row of P2, these two rows are the corresponding row of P1 and the zth row of P1. This transformation corresponds formally to a step of the Gaussian elimination method for solving a linear system. If an initial Markov model M1 = (X1, P1), is finite, |X1| = k, and only one point is eliminated at each step, then a sequence of stochastic matrices (Pn), n = 2, ..., k, can be calculated recursively on the basis of formula (3.7), in which the subscripts вАЭ1вАЭ and вАЭ2вАЭ are replaced by вАЭnвАЭ and вАЭn+1вАЭ respectively. This sequence provides an opportunity to calculate many characteristics of the initial Markov model M1 recursively starting from some reduced model Ms, 1 < s вЙ§k. For this goal one needs also a relationship between a charac- teristic in a reduced model and a model with one more point. Sometimes this relationship is obvious or simple, sometimes it has a complicated structure. The EA algorithm for the problem of optimal stopping (OS) of a Markov chain was developed independently of other SR algorithms and shares with them the common idea of elimination. It also has very distinct specific fea- tures. The number of points to be eliminated and the order in which they are eliminated depend on some auxiliary procedure, and the value function of the problem is recovered on the second stage. For the problem of OS it is natural to try to find not only the optimal stopping set but as well the distribution of the stopping moment and the distribution of a MC at the moment of stopping. The next lemma provides tools for the sequential calculation of these characteristic. Lemma 2. (Lemma 3 in ([13])). Let the models M1, M2 be defined as in Lemma 1 and let G вКВX2 = X1 \ D,. Let ui(x, ¬Ј) be the distribution of the Markov chain (Zn) for the model Mi at the moment of the first visit to G in the model Mi, i = 1, 2, and let mi(x, v) be the mean time spent at point v till such a visit with an initial point x вИИX2 \ G. Then for any x вИИX2

    614 I. Sonin u1(x, y) = u2(x, y), y вИИG, (3.8) m1(x, v) = m2(x, v), v вИИX2 \ G. (3.9) 4 The Elimination Algorithm The Elimination algorithm for the OSP of a MC is based on the three following facts. 1. Though in the OSP it may be difficult to find the states where it is optimal to stop, it is easy to find a state (states) where it is optimal not to stop. Obviously, it is optimal to stop at z if g(z) вЙ•c(z) + Pv(z) вЙ°Fv(z), but v is unknown until the problem is solved. On the other hand, it is optimal not to stop at z if g(z) < Fg(z), i.e. the expected reward of doing one more step is larger than the reward from stopping. (Generally, it is optimal not to stop at any state where the expected reward of doing some, perhaps random number of steps, is larger than the reward from stopping). 2. After we have found states (state) which are not in the optimal stop- ping set, we can eliminate them and recalculate the transition matrix using (3.7) if one state is eliminated or (3.1) if a larger subset of the state space is eliminated. According to Lemma 2 this will keep the distributions at the moments of visits to any subset of the remaining states the same and the excluded states do not matter since it is not optimal to stop there. After that in the reduced model we can repeat the first step and so on. 3. Finally, though if g(z) вЙ•Fg(z) at a particular point z, we can not make a conclusion about whether this point belongs to the stopping set or not, but if this inequality is true for all points in the state space then we have the following well-known statement: Proposition 1. Let M = (X, P, g) be an optimal stopping problem, and g(x) вЙ•Fg(x) for all x вИИX. Then X is the optimal stopping set in the problem M, and v(x) = g(x) for all x вИИX. Proposition 1 follows immediately from Theorem 1 because the function g(x) in this case is its own excessive majorant. The formal justification of the transition from the initial model M1 to the reduced model M2 is given by Theorem 2 below. This theorem was formulated by the author in [11] and its proof was given in [12] when c(x) = 0 for all x. Here we prove this theorem in a shorter way and for any c(x) but, for simplicity, only for the case of finite X. Let us introduce a transformation of the cost function c1(x) (or any func- tion f(x)) defined on X1 into the cost function c2(x) defined on X2, under the transition from model M1 to model M2. Given the set D вКВX1, let ѕД be the moment of the first return to X2, i.e. ѕД = min(n вЙ•1 : Zn вИИX2). Then given a function c1(x) defined on X1 let us define the function c2(x) on X2 by the formula
